topics.title: Delta Modulation, Digital-to-Analog Conversion: ASK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Delta Modulation, Digital-to-Analog Conversion: ASK

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Delta Modulation (DM)

**Formal Definition:**
Delta Modulation is a simplified, 1-bit version of Differential Pulse Code Modulation (DPCM) in which the difference between two consecutive samples of an analog message signal is encoded using only **one bit** per sample. The output is a single-bit stream that indicates whether the current sample is **higher (+Δ)** or **lower (-Δ)** than the previous reconstructed sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Delta Modulation is a 1-bit DPCM scheme. The 'Delta' (Δ) refers to the *step-size* used to track the input signal. It replaces the multi-bit quantizer of DPCM with a 2-level (1-bit) hard limiter.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine you are **blindfolded** and asked to walk along the edge of a mountain ridge by only feeling the slope with your feet. Every second you take one step either **up** (if the ground rises) or **down** (if it falls). You never record your exact height — only the *direction of change*. Your friend watching from above can reconstruct your altitude by walking the same sequence of ups and downs.
- The **mountain path** = the analog message signal m(t)
- The **up/down decision each second** = the 1-bit DM output
- The **step size (up = down = 1 step)** = Δ (the quantization step)
- The **friend reconstructing the path** = the DM demodulator (integrator)

If the mountain rises too steeply, your steps can't keep up (you slip back) → **Slope Overload**.
If the mountain is almost flat, your steps create a noisy zig-zag around the true path → **Granular Noise**.

### 1.2 Digital-to-Analog Conversion: Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)

**Formal Definition:**
Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK), also called **On-Off Keying (OOK)**, is a digital modulation technique in which the **amplitude 'A' of a high-frequency carrier signal is varied in accordance with the digital binary data**, while the **frequency 'f' and phase 'θ' remain constant**. Binary '1' is transmitted by transmitting the carrier; Binary '0' is transmitted by suppressing the carrier (or transmitting zero amplitude).

**KTU Mathematical Statement:**
For binary data b(t) ∈ {0, 1}, the ASK signal is:

$$
s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & b(t) = 1 \\ 0, & b(t) = 0 \end{cases}
$$

This is equivalent to:

$$
s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \, \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a **flashlight** that you switch ON and OFF to send Morse code.
- **Flashlight ON** for 1 second = Binary '1' (carrier present)
- **Flashlight OFF** for 1 second = Binary '0' (no carrier)

The flashlight's color (frequency) and the direction it points (phase) never change — only its **brightness (amplitude)** switches between full and zero. A friend far away, observing the light through a window, can decode your message by simply counting the ON/OFF pattern. This is exactly how ASK works!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Parameters in KTU Problems:**
> - Bit Rate: **R_b** bits/second
> - Bit Duration: **T_b = 1/R_b** seconds
> - Bandwidth of ASK: **B_ASK = 2 × R_b** (same as BPSK)
> - Carrier frequency: **f_c** (typically much greater than R_b)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Delta Modulation — Operational Analysis

The Delta Modulator has **four essential components**:

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Comparator** | Subtracts previous sample estimate from current analog sample |
| **1-bit Quantizer (Hard-Limiter)** | Outputs '+1' if error > 0, otherwise '-1' |
| **Accumulator/Integrator** | Sums the quantized bits to form the staircase approximation |
| **Delay element** | Provides one-sample delay to feed back the previous estimate |

**Operation Step-by-Step:**
1. Let **m(t)** be the input analog signal and **m̂(t)** be the staircase reconstructed approximation.
2. The comparator computes the error: **e(t) = m(t) − m̂(t)**
3. The 1-bit quantizer outputs a binary bit **b_k ∈ {+Δ, −Δ}** depending on the sign of e(t).
4. The accumulator updates: **m̂(t_{k+1}) = m̂(t_k) + b_k**
5. The bit stream **b_k** is transmitted to the receiver.
6. The receiver is a similar accumulator that reconstructs m̂(t).

**The Two Critical Noises of DM:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **(a) Slope Overload Distortion** — occurs when **|dm/dt| > Δ/T_s**, i.e., the input signal changes faster than the staircase can follow. To avoid it: **Δ ≥ (dm/dt)_max × T_s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **(b) Granular (Idle) Noise** — occurs when the input is nearly constant; the staircase still oscillates between +Δ and −Δ around the flat signal. Reduced by smaller Δ — but smaller Δ worsens slope overload. Hence, **trade-off exists**.

**Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM):**
In ADM, the step-size **Δ** is **adapted** to the slope of the input signal — Δ increases when slope is steep (overload) and decreases when slope is flat (granular noise). This provides better SNR over a wider dynamic range.

### 2.2 Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) — Operational Analysis

**Generation (Transmitter):**
The binary bit stream **b(t)** is multiplied with the carrier **c(t) = A_c cos(2π f_c t)** using a **balanced modulator / multiplier**. A unipolar NRZ line code is typically used so that bit '1' = +1V and bit '0' = 0V, producing OOK.

**Detection (Receiver) — Two Methods:**

**(a) Coherent (Synchronous) Detection:**
1. Multiply received ASK signal by locally generated carrier **cos(2π f_c t)**
2. Pass through a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** to remove double-frequency components
3. Decision device compares with threshold to recover bits

**(b) Non-Coherent (Envelope) Detection:**
1. Pass the ASK signal through an **Envelope Detector** (diode + RC circuit)
2. Decision device compares envelope amplitude with threshold
3. No need for carrier synchronization — simpler but slightly higher BER

**Power Spectral Density (PSD):**
For a random binary sequence with equal probability of 0 and 1:

$$
S_{ASK}(f) = \frac{A_c^2}{4}\left[\delta(f - f_c) + \delta(f + f_c)\right] + \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4}\left[\text{sinc}^2((f-f_c)T_b) + \text{sinc}^2((f+f_c)T_b)\right]
$$

**Bit Error Probability (Coherent Detection):**

$$
P_e = \frac{1}{2}\, \text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{4N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{4N_0}}\right)
$$

**Bit Error Probability (Non-Coherent Detection):**

$$
P_e = \frac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{4N_0}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite:** You will most often be asked to compute ASK transmission bandwidth, draw the waveform for a given bit pattern, or compute the minimum transmission bandwidth.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | DM Output Bit Rate | $R_b = f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | bits/second, equals sampling rate |
| 2 | DM Step-Size Condition | $\Delta \geq \left(\dfrac{dm}{dt}\right)_{\max} \cdot T_s$ | To avoid slope overload |
| 3 | Granular Noise Power | $N_{gran} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{3}$ | Assumes uniform quant. error |
| 4 | ADM Step Update | $\Delta_k = \Delta_{k-1} \cdot G$ | G = step-size adaptation gain |
| 5 | ASK Bandwidth | $B_{ASK} = 2 R_b = \dfrac{2}{T_b}$ | Hz; same as BPSK |
| 6 | ASK Signal | $s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \, \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | b(t) ∈ {0, 1} for OOK |
| 7 | Average ASK Power | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2 R_b}\int_0^{T_b} b^2(t)\,dt$ | Equals $A_c^2/4$ for 50% duty |
| 8 | Coherent BER | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/4N_0}\right)$ | AWGN channel |
| 9 | Non-Coherent BER | $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/4N_0}$ | Simpler receiver |
| 10 | Carrier Frequency | $f_c \gg R_b$ | To avoid spectral overlap |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Delta Modulation** is widely used in **voice-over-IP (VoIP) codecs**, **military secure voice (LPC-10e vocoder)**, and **oversampling audio ADC systems** because it offers excellent robustness at low bit rates with simple hardware.
- **ASK** is the simplest digital modulation and is the foundation for **optical communication (Li-Fi)**, **RFID tag backscatter**, **AM radio control links**, and **low-cost remote controls (e.g., garage openers, 433 MHz ISM band devices)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DM Step-Size Condition for Slope Overload Avoidance

Let the analog input signal be $m(t)$ and its staircase reconstruction be $\hat{m}(t)$.

**Step 1:** The maximum rate of change of the staircase approximation occurs when the modulator produces consecutive identical bits (+Δ every $T_s$ seconds). The maximum slope of $\hat{m}(t)$ is therefore:

$$
\left(\frac{d\hat{m}}{dt}\right)_{\max} = \frac{\Delta}{T_s}
$$

**Step 2:** To avoid slope overload, this maximum staircase slope must be at least as large as the maximum slope of the analog input signal:

$$
\left(\frac{d\hat{m}}{dt}\right)_{\max} \geq \left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right)_{\max}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
\frac{\Delta}{T_s} \geq \left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right)_{\max}
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the minimum allowed step-size:

$$
\boxed{\Delta_{\min} = T_s \cdot \left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right)_{\max}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
A sinusoid $m(t) = A_m \sin(2\pi f_m t)$ with $A_m = 1$ V and $f_m = 1$ kHz is to be delta-modulated with sampling frequency $f_s = 32$ kHz. Find the minimum $\Delta$.

**Step A:** Compute $(dm/dt)_{\max}$:

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = 2\pi f_m A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \Rightarrow \left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right)_{\max} = 2\pi f_m A_m
$$

**Step B:** Evaluate numerically:

$$
\left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right)_{\max} = 2\pi \times 1000 \times 1 = 6283.2 \text{ V/s}
$$

**Step C:** Compute $T_s$:

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{32000} = 31.25 \text{ \mu s}
$$

**Step D:** Substitute into Step 4:

$$
\Delta_{\min} = 31.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 6283.2 = 0.1963 \text{ V}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: ASK Transmission Bandwidth

**Step 1:** Let the bit stream $b(t)$ have bit duration $T_b$, so its fundamental frequency is $f_b = 1/T_b = R_b$.

**Step 2:** Multiplication of $b(t)$ (band-limited to $R_b$) with $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ shifts the spectrum to $\pm f_c$. By the **frequency-shifting property**, the resulting spectrum has main-lobe width $2R_b$ centered at $f_c$.

**Step 3:** Therefore the null-to-null bandwidth of the ASK signal is:

$$
\boxed{B_{ASK} = 2R_b = \frac{2}{T_b}}
$$

**Step 4:** Using Nyquist's relationship $R_b = 2B$ for baseband, we see that ASK is **double-sideband** with both sidebands carrying identical information.

### 3.3 Derivation: Coherent BER for ASK

**Step 1:** In coherent detection, the received signal in a bit interval where '1' is transmitted is:

$$
r(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)
$$

where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 2:** Multiplication by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and LPF yields the decision variable:

$$
y = \frac{A_c}{2} + n_c
$$

where $n_c$ is Gaussian with zero mean and variance $\sigma^2 = N_0/4$.

**Step 3:** Energy per bit: $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 2$ (energy of carrier over one bit period).

**Step 4:** The probability of error is the probability that $y < 0$ when '1' is sent (or $y > 0$ when '0' is sent). Assuming symmetric equiprobable bits:

$$
P_e = \frac{1}{2}P(y<0 \mid 1) + \frac{1}{2}P(y>0 \mid 0)
$$

**Step 5:** Using Gaussian statistics and substituting the Q-function:

$$
P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{A_c/2}{\sqrt{N_0/4}}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{A_c}{2}\sqrt{\frac{4}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{A_c^2}{N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 6:** Since $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4 \cdot 2 = A_c^2/4 \cdot T_b$ (for OOK, effective amplitude is $A_c/2$), this simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{4N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{4N_0}}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "4N_0"?** ASK uses only half the symbol energy for one bit (since amplitude is half for OOK). This makes ASK ~3 dB **worse than BPSK**, which has $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.

### 3.4 Numerical Example: ASK Bandwidth

A binary source produces 8000 bits/sec. Find:
(i) ASK bandwidth  
(ii) Minimum Nyquist bandwidth  
(iii) Time duration of one bit

**Solution:**

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \text{ \mu s}
$$

$$
B_{ASK} = 2 R_b = 2 \times 8000 = 16 \text{ kHz}
$$

$$
B_{Nyquist} = \frac{R_b}{2} = 4 \text{ kHz}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Delta Modulator — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input m_t] --> B[Comparator + Sum Node]
    C[Feedback from Accumulator] --> B
    B --> D[1 bit Quantizer Hard Limiter]
    D --> E[Binary Bit Stream Output b_k]
    E --> F[Transmitter Channel]
    E --> G[Receiver Accumulator]
    G --> H[Staircase Approximation m_hat_t]
    H --> C
    H --> I[Low Pass Filter]
    I --> J[Reconstructed Signal m_prime_t]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Analog Input** enters the comparator on the left.
- The **Comparator** subtracts the *previous* reconstructed sample (fed back through the accumulator) from the current input sample.
- The **1-bit Quantizer** outputs either +Δ or −Δ, which is the transmitted bit.
- At the receiver, an identical **Accumulator** rebuilds the staircase approximation.
- An optional **Low-Pass Filter** smooths the staircase into a clean replica of the original signal.

### 4.2 Delta Demodulator — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received Bit Stream b_k] --> B[Accumulator Integrator]
    B --> C[Staircase m_hat_t]
    C --> D[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    D --> E[Reconstructed Output m_prime_t]
```

### 4.3 Adaptive Delta Modulator — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input m_t] --> B[Comparator]
    B --> C[Sign Output sign of e]
    C --> D[Step Size Adaptor Multiplier by G]
    D --> E[Variable Step Delta_k]
    E --> F[Accumulator]
    F --> G[Output Bit b_k]
    F --> H[Feedback to Comparator]
    G --> I[Channel]
    I --> J[Receiver Accumulator]
    J --> K[Reconstructed Signal]
```

### 4.4 ASK Transmitter — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Binary Data b_t 0 1] --> B[NRZ Encoder Unipolar]
    B --> C[Multiplier Balanced Modulator]
    D[Carrier c_t = A_c cos 2 pi f_c t] --> C
    C --> E[ASK Signal s_t at f_c]
    E --> F[Antenna and Channel]
```

### 4.5 ASK Receiver — Coherent Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received ASK r_t] --> B[Multiplier]
    C[Local Carrier cos 2 pi f_c t] --> B
    B --> D[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    D --> E[Integrator over T_b]
    E --> F[Decision Device Threshold]
    F --> G[Recovered Binary Output]
```

### 4.6 ASK Receiver — Non-Coherent (Envelope) Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received ASK r_t] --> B[Envelope Detector Diode + RC]
    B --> C[Threshold Comparator]
    C --> D[Sampler at T_b]
    D --> E[Recovered Binary Output]
```

### 4.7 ASK Power Spectral Density (Conceptual Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Baseband Spectrum of b_t centered at 0 Hz width 2 R_b] --> B[Multiplication by Carrier cos 2 pi f_c t]
    B --> C[Spectrum Shifted to plus minus f_c]
    C --> D[PSD at plus f_c with two delta impulses plus continuous sinc2]
    C --> E[PSD at minus f_c with two delta impulses plus continuous sinc2]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
**Q: Differentiate between PCM and Delta Modulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | PCM | Delta Modulation |
|---------|-----|------------------|
| Bits per sample | n bits (typically 8) | 1 bit |
| Quantizer | Multi-level (2ⁿ levels) | 2-level (hard limiter) |
| Transmission bit rate | $n f_s$ | $f_s$ (lower) |
| Complexity | Higher (A/D + encoder) | Lower (simple comparator) |
| Quantization noise | Function of step-size & bits | Granular + Slope-overload noise |
| Encoding type | Absolute sample values | Relative (difference) change |

**Memory tip (1 mark):** DM uses **1-bit differential encoding**, hence the bit rate equals the sampling rate $f_s$.

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
**Q: Define Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK). Why is it also called On-Off Keying (OOK)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** BASK is a digital modulation scheme in which the amplitude of a sinusoidal carrier is switched between two levels (ON and OFF) in accordance with the binary data, while the frequency and phase remain constant. Mathematically:

$$
s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & b(t) = 1 \\ 0, & b(t) = 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Reason for OOK name (1 mark):** When binary '1' is transmitted, the carrier is fully present (ON); when binary '0' is transmitted, the carrier amplitude becomes zero (OFF). Hence the scheme behaves like a simple ON/OFF switch on the carrier — called **On-Off Keying**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of Delta Modulation. Discuss the two types of distortions occurring in DM.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (already drawn in Section 4.1).**

**Generation (2 marks):**
- The analog signal $m(t)$ is fed to a comparator.
- The comparator subtracts the locally generated staircase approximation $\hat{m}(t)$ from $m(t)$.
- The resulting error $e(t) = m(t) - \hat{m}(t)$ is fed to a 1-bit quantizer.
- If $e(t) > 0$, the quantizer outputs $+1$ (or equivalently $+Δ$); else $-1$ (or $-Δ$).
- The bit stream is transmitted through the channel.

**Detection (2 marks):**
- The receiver uses an accumulator (integrator) which adds $\pm Δ$ to its previous output for each received bit.
- This produces a staircase reconstruction $\hat{m}(t)$.
- An LPF smooths the staircase into the recovered analog signal $m'(t)$.

**Two Distortions (3 marks):**

1. **Slope Overload Distortion:** When the analog signal changes *faster* than $\Delta/T_s$, the staircase cannot keep up and the output lags behind the input, producing a distortion error. Condition to avoid: $\Delta \geq (dm/dt)_{\max} \cdot T_s$.

2. **Granular Noise:** When the input is nearly constant (slowly varying), the staircase still oscillates between $+\Delta$ and $-\Delta$ around the true signal, producing a quantizing noise of average power $\Delta^2/3$.

---

**(b)** A sinusoidal signal $m(t) = 5 \sin(2000\pi t)$ is delta modulated with sampling rate $f_s = 32$ kHz. Determine:
   (i) The maximum slope of the message signal
   (ii) The minimum step-size to avoid slope overload
   (iii) The transmission bit rate

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum slope (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = 5 \times 2000\pi \cos(2000\pi t)
$$

$$
\left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right)_{\max} = 5 \times 2000\pi = 10000\pi = 31415.93 \text{ V/s}
$$

**(ii) Minimum step-size (3 marks):**

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{32000} = 31.25 \text{ \mu s}
$$

$$
\Delta_{\min} = T_s \cdot \left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right)_{\max} = 31.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 31415.93
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta_{\min} \approx 0.982 \text{ V}}
$$

**(iii) Transmission bit rate (2 marks):** Since DM uses 1 bit per sample:

$$
R_b = f_s = 32000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

**Q: (a)** Explain the generation and detection of Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK/ASK). Draw the waveform for the data stream `10110010`.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Generation (2 marks):**
The binary data stream is first encoded into **unipolar NRZ** format (e.g., +1V for '1' and 0V for '0'). This NRZ signal $b(t)$ is then multiplied with the carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ in a balanced modulator. The product is the ASK signal.

**Detection — Coherent (2 marks):**
- The received signal is multiplied by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- A Low-Pass Filter removes the double-frequency term.
- An integrator over one bit duration computes the decision variable.
- A threshold detector outputs '1' if the variable > 0, else '0'.

**Detection — Non-Coherent (1 mark):**
An envelope detector (diode + RC + smoothing) extracts the amplitude envelope. A sampler decides bit '1' if envelope > threshold, else '0'.

**Waveform (2 marks):**
For data `10110010` with each bit occupying 1 carrier cycle:

```
Data:        1   0   1   1   0   0   1   0
Carrier:     /\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\
ASK Out:     /\/\/\  0   /\/\/\/\/\  0   0   /\/\/\
             ^ON    OFF   ^ON        OFF        ^ON
```

A fully drawn waveform showing positive carrier bursts during '1' bits and zero during '0' bits — must be drawn with time axis.

---

**(b)** For a binary data stream at $R_b = 10$ kbps, an ASK signal is transmitted using a carrier of frequency $f_c = 100$ kHz.
   (i) Find the transmission bandwidth of the ASK signal.
   (ii) Find the minimum Nyquist bandwidth.
   (iii) If the receiver uses coherent detection and $E_b/N_0 = 10$, compute the probability of error.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) ASK Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
B_{ASK} = 2 R_b = 2 \times 10000 = 20 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Minimum Nyquist Bandwidth (2 marks):** For binary transmission:

$$
B_{Nyquist} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{10000}{2} = 5 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(iii) Probability of Error (3 marks):** For coherent ASK:

$$
P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{4N_0}}\right)
$$

With $E_b/N_0 = 10$:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{4N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{10}{4}} = \sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.581
$$

From standard Q-function table: $Q(1.581) \approx 5.7 \times 10^{-2}$

$$
\boxed{P_e \approx 0.057}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 
> **For Delta Modulation questions:**
> 1. **Common Mistake:** Confusing $(dm/dt)_{\max}$ with $A_m \cdot 2\pi f_m$ — students often forget the factor $2\pi$.
> 2. **Step-size formula:** Always write $\Delta \geq T_s \cdot (dm/dt)_{\max}$. Some students write $\Delta \geq (dm/dt)_{\max} / T_s$ — this is **wrong by a factor of $T_s^2$** and will cost full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always state final $\Delta$ in **Volts** with proper scientific notation.
> 4. **Confusing DM with DPCM:** DM uses 1-bit/sample, DPCM uses n-bits/sample.
> 
> **For ASK questions:**
> 1. **Bandwidth trap:** ASK bandwidth is $2R_b$, NOT $R_b$. Many students write $B = R_b$ (which is only true for SSB).
> 2. **Waveform drawing:** Students often draw the carrier continuously even during '0' bits — must show amplitude going to ZERO during '0'.
> 3. **BER formula:** Use $E_b/4N_0$ for ASK, NOT $E_b/N_0$ (that's BPSK). Getting this wrong is a 3-mark blunder.
> 4. **Threshold detection:** Don't forget to mention the threshold value (typically $A_c/4$ for OOK with equal prior probabilities).

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Delta Modulation** = 1-bit DPCM; bit rate = sampling rate $f_s$.
- Two errors in DM: **Slope Overload** (signal changes too fast) and **Granular Noise** (signal too flat).
- Trade-off: large $\Delta$ → less overload but more granular noise.
- **ADM (Adaptive DM)** dynamically adjusts $\Delta$ to mitigate both errors simultaneously.
- **ASK** = amplitude modulation of a carrier by binary data; carrier ON for '1', OFF for '0' (OOK).
- **ASK bandwidth** $B_{ASK} = 2R_b$.
- **ASK is ~3 dB worse than BPSK** at the same $E_b/N_0$ because effective symbol energy is halved in OOK.
- **Generation**: NRZ × Carrier → ASK. **Detection**: Coherent (multiplier + LPF) or Non-coherent (envelope detector).
- ASK is the **simplest digital modulation** but the **least power-efficient**; mostly used in optical and low-cost RF links.
- Always draw waveforms with **time on x-axis**, **amplitude on y-axis**, and bit boundaries clearly marked.
- For numericals, **always state units** (Hz, V, W, bps) — KTU examiners specifically check for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PSK, Transmission Modes: Parallel and Serial Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PSK, Transmission Modes: Parallel and Serial Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Phase Shift Keying (PSK) — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Shift Keying (PSK)** is a **digital modulation technique** in which the **phase of a constant-amplitude, constant-frequency carrier signal** is varied in discrete steps according to the digital data (binary or M-ary symbols), while the **amplitude and frequency** of the carrier are kept **constant**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, PSK belongs to the family of **bandpass digital modulation schemes** and is widely used in wireless communication standards such as **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **Bluetooth**, **satellite links**, and **cellular LTE/5G NR** systems because of its excellent **noise immunity** and **bandwidth efficiency**.

### Sub-Families of PSK (Required by KTU Syllabus)

| Variant | Full Form | Bits/Symbol | Phases Used |
|---------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **BPSK** | Binary PSK | 1 | 2 (0°, 180°) |
| **QPSK** | Quadrature PSK | 2 | 4 (45°, 135°, 225°, 315°) |
| **DPSK** | Differential PSK | 1 | 2 (with relative phase encoding) |
| **8-PSK** | 8-ary PSK | 3 | 8 (45° steps) |
| **M-PSK** | M-ary PSK | log₂M | M (uniformly spaced) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Carrier Recovery**: Because PSK conveys information purely in **phase**, the receiver must perform **coherent detection**, which requires **carrier synchronization** (carrier phase recovery using a **Phase-Locked Loop — PLL** or **Costas loop**). This is the **most critical operational challenge** of PSK systems.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Lighthouse and the Stop Sign"

Imagine you are a lighthouse keeper, and you must send the alphabet to a ship in fog.

- **FSK (Frequency Keying)** would be like flashing the light at **two different rates** (slow = dot, fast = dash). Useful, but slow.
- **ASK (Amplitude Keying)** would be like dimming the light to **two intensities** (bright = 1, faint = 0). Easily lost in fog.
- **PSK (Phase Keying)** is like **flipping the rotating mirror of the lighthouse** to one of two pre-decided angles. The **intensity and rotation speed stay identical**, but the **direction the beam swings** carries the message. Even in dense fog, the ship can precisely measure *where* the beam points, making PSK **far more robust against noise**.

For **QPSK**, the lighthouse can be pointed to **4 corners of a square** (NE, NW, SE, SW) — sending **2 letters per flash** instead of 1, **doubling the data rate** at the same bandwidth.

---

## 1.3 Transmission Modes — Parallel vs. Serial

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Mode** is the strategy used to transfer **data bits between a source and a destination** over a communication channel. The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes this into **Parallel Transmission** and **Serial Transmission**, with serial further divided into **Asynchronous** and **Synchronous** modes.

### Intuitive Analogy: "Trucks on a Highway"

- **Parallel Transmission** = **8 trucks driving side-by-side on an 8-lane highway**, each carrying 1 box. They all arrive at the same time, but you need **8 lanes** (expensive infrastructure).
- **Serial Transmission** = **1 truck driving on a single lane**, carrying boxes one-by-one. It is **slower per bit** but the road is **cheap and easy to build**.
- **Asynchronous Serial** = the truck **waits for a green light (start bit)** before each trip, then **stops (stop bit)** — inefficient but flexible.
- **Synchronous Serial** = a **continuous conveyor belt** runs at a fixed speed; boxes are placed at exact intervals — **fast and efficient**, but both ends must agree on the conveyor speed (shared clock).

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted by KTU

> [!NOTE]
> The following bolded metrics are **board-favorite numerical values** and standard KTU 2024 expectations:
> - **BPSK bandwidth = Bit Rate (R_b) Hz** (minimum Nyquist bandwidth).
> - **QPSK bandwidth = R_b / 2 Hz** (twice as bandwidth-efficient as BPSK).
> - **Standard baud rates (asynchronous)**: 300, 1200, 9600, **115200 bps**.
> - **Parallel port historical standard**: **8-bit** data bus (1 byte at a time).
> - **Universal Serial Bus (USB)**: Serial, **synchronous-style**, with embedded clock via NRZI + bit stuffing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Mathematical Model of a PSK Signal

The transmitted PSK signal for a symbol duration $T_s$ is given by the canonical bandpass form:

$$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_s}{T_s}} \cdot \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i\right), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s$$

where the parameters carry the following meaning:

- $E_s$ = **Symbol energy** (joules) — fixed for all PSK variants because amplitude is constant.
- $T_s$ = **Symbol duration** (seconds).
- $f_c$ = **Carrier frequency** (Hz).
- $\theta_i$ = **Phase offset** chosen from a finite set of $M$ equally-spaced angles.
- The leading factor $\sqrt{2E_s/T_s}$ normalizes the signal so that its energy over one symbol period is exactly $E_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Phase Only"?** Because **amplitude is constant** in PSK, all points in the signal constellation diagram lie on a **single circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$**. Information is encoded in the **angular position** of the symbol, not its distance from the origin. This makes PSK a **constant-envelope modulation**, giving it excellent resistance to **non-linear distortion** (e.g., passing through a saturated power amplifier).

---

## 2.2 The Five Logical Steps of PSK Operation

1. **Bit-to-Symbol Mapping**: Group the incoming bit stream into blocks of $\log_2 M$ bits (e.g., 2 bits per block for QPSK).
2. **Phase Selection**: Map each block to one of the $M$ allowed phase values $\theta_i = \frac{2\pi(i-1)}{M} + \theta_0$ where $\theta_0$ is an offset (often $\pi/M$ for symmetric constellations).
3. **Carrier Modulation**: Multiply the local carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ by the selected phase, producing $s_i(t)$.
4. **Channel Transmission**: $s_i(t)$ passes through an **AWGN channel** (Additive White Gaussian Noise) and may suffer **fading**.
5. **Coherent Demodulation & Decision**: The receiver multiplies $r(t)$ by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $-\sin(2\pi f_c t)$, integrates over $T_s$, and decides the transmitted phase by finding the **closest constellation point** (maximum likelihood decision).

---

## 2.3 BPSK Deep-Dive

Binary PSK uses **two antipodal phases** ($0$ and $\pi$), making it the **most noise-robust** of all PSK schemes. The two signal vectors are:

$$s_0(t) = +\sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

$$s_1(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Because $s_1(t) = -s_0(t)$, BPSK is an **antipodal signaling** scheme — the Euclidean distance between the two constellation points is the **maximum possible** ($d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Fact**: BPSK requires only **1 bit per symbol**, so $E_s = E_b$ and $T_s = T_b$. It is the **gold standard** for low-data-rate, ultra-reliable links (e.g., **deep-space communication**, **satellite telemetry**, **GPS navigation messages**).

---

## 2.4 QPSK Deep-Dive

QPSK encodes **2 bits per symbol** using **four phases** separated by 90°:

$$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_s}{T_s}} \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{(2i-1)\pi}{4}\right), \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

Using the **trigonometric identity** $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$, the QPSK signal decomposes into two orthogonal BPSK signals on the **in-phase (I)** and **quadrature (Q)** rails:

$$s_i(t) = \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2 E_s}{T_s}} \cos\!\left(\frac{(2i-1)\pi}{4}\right)}_{\text{I-component (in-phase)}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) \;-\; \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2 E_s}{T_s}} \sin\!\left(\frac{(2i-1)\pi}{4}\right)}_{\text{Q-component (quadrature)}} \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

The I-component carries one bit, the Q-component carries the other — both **independently demodulated** with no inter-symbol interference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why QPSK is the workhorse of 4G LTE/5G NR**: It provides the same **bit error probability as BPSK** (because the I and Q rails are orthogonal and each behaves like an independent BPSK channel), but uses **half the bandwidth** for the same bit rate. This is a **2× bandwidth-efficiency gain** at **no cost in error performance**.

---

## 2.5 DPSK — Differential PSK (The Non-Coherent Alternative)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DPSK** eliminates the need for **carrier phase recovery** by encoding data in the **phase *difference* between consecutive symbols**, not the absolute phase.

If the current symbol has phase $\theta_i$ and the previous symbol had phase $\theta_{i-1}$, then:

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_i - \theta_{i-1} = 0 \text{ (bit 0) or } \pi \text{ (bit 1)}$$

The receiver simply **delays the received signal by one symbol period** and **multiplies** by the current symbol — a phase difference of 0 indicates "no change", and $\pi$ indicates a "phase flip". The Costas loop is no longer required.

**Trade-off**: DPSK is **~3 dB worse** in noise performance than coherent BPSK at the same $E_b/N_0$, but it is **dramatically simpler to implement**, making it popular in **Bluetooth (older versions)**, **DECT cordless phones**, and **legacy modem standards**.

---

## 2.6 M-ary PSK and Bandwidth Efficiency

For **M-ary PSK**, the relationship between **bit rate** $R_b$, **symbol rate** $R_s$, and **minimum Nyquist bandwidth** $B$ is:

$$R_s = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 M}, \quad B = R_s = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 M} \text{ Hz}$$

As $M$ increases, **bandwidth efficiency rises** (more bits per Hz), but **adjacent constellation points move closer together**, increasing the **bit error rate (BER)**. The trade-off is fundamental and quantified in §3.3.

---

## 2.7 Transmission Modes — Deep Theory

### Parallel Transmission
- **n-bit bus** (typically n = 8, 16, 32, or 64) carries **n bits simultaneously** per clock pulse.
- **Clock skew** between parallel lines is the limiting factor: at high speeds, the bits arrive at slightly different times, causing **synchronization errors**.
- **Cost**: Requires $n$ separate physical wires — expensive for long distances.
- **Application**: **Internal computer buses** — PCI, ISA (legacy), data paths between CPU and RAM, older **printer ports (LPT/Centronics)**.

### Serial Transmission — Asynchronous
- **No shared clock** between sender and receiver.
- Each **byte (character)** is framed by a **start bit (0)**, **5–9 data bits**, an optional **parity bit**, and **1–2 stop bits (1)**.
- The receiver resynchronizes on every start bit using a **local clock running ~16× faster** than the data rate.
- **Advantage**: Simple, cheap, ideal for **low-speed peripherals** (keyboards, mice, legacy RS-232 ports).
- **Disadvantage**: **~20% overhead** from start/stop bits at standard 8-N-1 framing.

### Serial Transmission — Synchronous
- **Shared clock** (or clock recovered from the data stream itself, e.g., via **8b/10b encoding** in USB/Ethernet/PCIe).
- Data sent as **continuous frames or packets** (e.g., **HDLC, Ethernet, USB, SATA, PCIe**).
- **Throughput** is **much higher** because there is no per-byte start/stop overhead.
- **Disadvantage**: Requires **complex clock-recovery circuitry** (PLL).

---

## 2.8 🎯 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. **Every KTU board question** on PSK is solvable with one or more of these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **PSK general signal** | $s_i(t) = \sqrt{2E_s/T_s}\cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i)$ | $i = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$ |
| 2 | **BPSK phases** | $\theta_0 = 0,\ \theta_1 = \pi$ | Antipodal signaling |
| 3 | **QPSK phases** | $\theta_i = (2i-1)\pi/4$ for $i=1,2,3,4$ | Orthogonal I/Q rails |
| 4 | **Bits per symbol** | $k = \log_2 M$ | Universal |
| 5 | **Symbol period** | $T_s = k \cdot T_b$ | Slower signaling for larger $M$ |
| 6 | **Bit rate from symbol rate** | $R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2 M$ | bps |
| 7 | **BPSK / QPSK BER (AWGN)** | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | $Q(\cdot)$ is Gaussian Q-function |
| 8 | **M-ary PSK symbol error** | $P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\,\sin(\pi/M)\right)$ | Approx. for large $E_s/N_0$ |
| 9 | **M-ary PSK bit error (approx)** | $P_b \approx P_s / \log_2 M$ | Gray coding assumed |
| 10 | **Bandwidth efficiency** | $\eta = R_b / B = \log_2 M$ | bits/sec/Hz |
| 11 | **Minimum Nyquist BW** | $B_{\min} = R_s = R_b / \log_2 M$ | Hz |
| 12 | **Energy relationship** | $E_s = E_b \cdot \log_2 M$ | M-ary signaling |
| 13 | **Parallel throughput** | $T_{\parallel} = n \cdot R$ per $T_{\text{clk}}$ | $n$ = bus width |
| 14 | **Asynchronous efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{async}} = 8 / (8 + 2) = 80\%$ | 8-N-1 framing |
| 15 | **DPSK BER** | $P_b = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{-E_b/N_0}$ | Coherent BPSK equivalent $\to$ ~3 dB worse |

> **Notation legend**: $E_b$ = energy per bit, $E_s$ = energy per symbol, $N_0$ = noise spectral density (W/Hz), $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in real engineering**: The $E_b/N_0$ figure of merit is the **single most quoted performance metric** in every wireless standard (3GPP, IEEE 802.11). The **bandwidth efficiency** $\eta$ is what drives the choice of **higher-order PSK/QAM** in spectrum-scarce cellular systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: General PSK Constellation Geometry

**Starting premise**: All $M$ PSK points must lie on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ in the **I-Q plane** (signal space), and be **equally spaced angularly** for maximum minimum-distance performance.

The signal $s_i(t)$ over a symbol period can be written in the I-Q (quadrature) baseband form:

$$s_i(t) = I_i \cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_i \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the I-component coefficient.**

$$I_i = \int_0^{T_s} s_i(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt \cdot \frac{2}{T_s} = \sqrt{E_s} \cos\theta_i$$

**Logic**: Because the carrier and its quadrature are orthogonal over integer multiples of the carrier period ($\int_0^{T_s} \cos(2\pi f_c t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = 0$), only the matching cosine term survives the correlation.

**Step 2 — Compute the Q-component coefficient.**

$$Q_i = -\int_0^{T_s} s_i(t) \sin(2\pi f_c t)\, dt \cdot \frac{2}{T_s} = \sqrt{E_s} \sin\theta_i$$

**Step 3 — Verify the constellation lies on a circle.**

$$I_i^2 + Q_i^2 = E_s \cos^2\theta_i + E_s \sin^2\theta_i = E_s \quad \text{(always)}$$

This is the proof that **all M PSK points are equidistant from the origin** at radius $\sqrt{E_s}$, regardless of $\theta_i$.

**Step 4 — Compute the minimum Euclidean distance** between adjacent phases $\theta_i$ and $\theta_{i+1} = \theta_i + 2\pi/M$.

$$d_{\min} = 2 \sqrt{E_s} \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)$$

**Logic**: Use the chord-length formula for two points on a circle of radius $r = \sqrt{E_s}$ separated by angle $\Delta\theta = 2\pi/M$: $d = 2r \sin(\Delta\theta/2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This $d_{\min}$ expression is the **single most important quantity** in M-ary PSK — it directly controls the **symbol error probability**, as we will see in §3.3.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: BPSK Bit Error Probability in AWGN

**Setup**: Received signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$, where $n(t)$ is AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 1 — Correlator output at the receiver** (decision variable $Z$):

$$Z = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $r(t) = s_0(t) + n(t)$** assuming bit 0 was sent:

$$Z = \int_0^{T_b} \sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{T_b}} \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\, dt + \int_0^{T_b} n(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral** (signal term):

$$\int_0^{T_b} \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^{T_b} [1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)]\, dt = \frac{T_b}{2}$$

(assuming $f_c T_b \gg 1$, the high-frequency term averages to zero over a symbol period). So the signal contribution is:

$$Z_{\text{signal}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{T_b}} \cdot \frac{T_b}{2} = \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Characterize the noise contribution** $Z_n$:

$$Z_n = \int_0^{T_b} n(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt$$

Since $n(t)$ is Gaussian, $Z_n$ is also Gaussian. Its variance is:

$$\sigma^2 = \mathbb{E}[Z_n^2] = \frac{N_0}{2} \cdot \frac{T_b}{2} = \frac{N_0 T_b}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Decision rule**: Decide bit 0 if $Z > 0$, bit 1 if $Z < 0$ (because the two BPSK signals are antipodal and symmetric about zero).

**Step 6 — Error probability**: An error occurs when bit 1 was sent (mean $Z = -\sqrt{E_b/2}$) but $Z$ crosses zero. This is a Gaussian tail integral:

$$P_b = P(Z > 0 \mid \text{bit 1}) = Q\!\left(\frac{\vert -\sqrt{E_b/2} - 0 \vert}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b/2}}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b/4}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Simplify** (since $E_b/N_0$ is the standard SNR-per-bit):

$$P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check**: At $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB, $P_b \approx 10^{-5}$ — a value routinely cited in KTU textbook tables. **Students must be able to reproduce this number** when given $E_b/N_0$ in dB.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: M-ary PSK Symbol Error Probability

**Setup**: M points on a circle, angle spacing $2\pi/M$. The decision region for symbol $i$ is the angular sector $\theta_i \pm \pi/M$.

**Step 1 — Probability of correct decision** (assuming noise isotropically rotates the received phasor):

$$P_c = 1 - \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi/M}^{\pi/M} e^{-\rho^2/2} \, d\phi \quad \text{... (after standard Gaussian geometry)}$$

For **high SNR**, the dominant error mechanism is the phasor crossing the *nearest* decision boundary (the closest neighbor dominates).

**Step 2 — Distance to nearest decision boundary**:

$$d = \sqrt{E_s} \sin(\pi/M)$$

**Step 3 — Symbol error probability** (union bound, dominated by the two nearest neighbors):

$$P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\frac{d}{\sigma_n}\right) = 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_s}{N_0}}\sin(\pi/M)\right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bit error probability** using Gray coding assumption (only one bit flips per nearest-neighbor error):

$$P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{2}{\log_2 M}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_s}{N_0}}\sin(\pi/M)\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As $M$ grows, $\sin(\pi/M) \approx \pi/M$ shrinks**, so the BER rises rapidly. Going from BPSK ($M=2$) to QPSK ($M=4$) keeps $P_b$ constant (because $\log_2 M = 2$ in the denominator offsets the $2$ in the numerator, and $\sin(\pi/4) = 1/\sqrt{2}$ combined with the doubled $E_s$ gives the same effective $E_b$). Going to 8-PSK or 16-PSK requires **substantially higher SNR** to maintain the same BER — this is the fundamental spectral-vs-power trade-off.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: BPSK vs. QPSK vs. 8-PSK (Computed at $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB)

This matrix is the **kind of comparison KTU examiners love** for Part B 14-mark questions.

| Parameter | BPSK ($M=2$) | QPSK ($M=4$) | 8-PSK ($M=8$) | 16-PSK ($M=16$) |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Bits/symbol $k$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Symbol period $T_s$ | $T_b$ | $2 T_b$ | $3 T_b$ | $4 T_b$ |
| Symbol rate $R_s$ | $R_b$ | $R_b/2$ | $R_b/3$ | $R_b/4$ |
| Min. Bandwidth $B$ | $R_b$ | $R_b/2$ | $R_b/3$ | $R_b/4$ |
| Bandwidth efficiency $\eta$ (bits/s/Hz) | 1 | **2** | 3 | 4 |
| $E_s/E_b$ (dB) | 0 | 3.01 | 4.77 | 6.02 |
| $\sin(\pi/M)$ | 1 | 0.7071 | 0.3827 | 0.1951 |
| $P_b$ (ideal AWGN) | Lowest | Same as BPSK | Higher | Much higher |
| Constellation shape | 2 antipodal points | Square | Regular octagon | 16-point circle |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: "Bit-to-Phase Mapping" (QPSK)

**Problem**: Given the input bit stream `1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0`, plot the QPSK signal assuming Gray coding with mapping:
- `00` → 45°
- `01` → 135°
- `11` → 225°
- `10` → 315°

**Step 1 — Group into dibits**: `10 | 11 | 00 | 10`

**Step 2 — Look up phases**:
- `10` → 315°
- `11` → 225°
- `00` → 45°
- `10` → 315°

**Step 3 — Construct the I-Q baseband waveform**:
- Symbol 1: $I = \sqrt{E_s}\cos(315°) = +\sqrt{E_s/2}$, $Q = \sqrt{E_s}\sin(315°) = -\sqrt{E_s/2}$
- Symbol 2: $I = -\sqrt{E_s/2}$, $Q = -\sqrt{E_s/2}$
- Symbol 3: $I = +\sqrt{E_s/2}$, $Q = +\sqrt{E_s/2}$
- Symbol 4: $I = +\sqrt{E_s/2}$, $Q = -\sqrt{E_s/2}$

**Step 4 — Multiply with carrier** $c(t) = \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and quadrature $c_q(t) = \sin(2\pi f_c t)$ to obtain the bandpass QPSK waveform:

$$s(t) = I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

The waveform has **constant envelope** $|\sqrt{I^2+Q^2}| = \sqrt{E_s}$ at all times — the **defining feature** of PSK.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant envelope advantage**: PSK signals can be passed through **non-linear RF power amplifiers** (e.g., Class-C) without spectral regrowth — this is **why PSK was historically preferred over QAM** in satellite and mobile handsets. Modern OFDM systems use QAM because linearity is achieved via PAPR-reduction techniques and better amplifier design.

# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: BPSK Transmitter & Coherent Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Bit Source] --> encA[NRZ Encoder]
    encA --> mulA[Multiplier X cos2pifct]
    oscA[Local Oscillator cos2pifct] --> mulA
    mulA --> pgaA[Power Amplifier]
    pgaA --> antA[Channel AWGN]
    antA --> filtA[BPF Receive Filter]
    filtA --> mulB[Multiplier X cos2pifct]
    oscA --> mulB
    mulB --> intA[Integrate over Tb]
    intA --> smpA[Sample at Tb]
    smpA --> decA[Threshold Detector: z greater 0 or z less 0]
    decA --> outA[Recovered Bits]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: QPSK Modulator Using I/Q Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    bsA[Input Bit Stream] --> spA[Serial to Parallel Splitter]
    spA --> chA[I Channel even bits]
    spA --> chB[Q Channel odd bits]
    chA --> nrzA[NRZ Encode I]
    chB --> nrzB[NRZ Encode Q]
    nrzA --> muA[Multiplier with cos2pifct]
    nrzB --> muB[Multiplier with sin2pifct]
    loA[Local Oscillator cos2pifct] --> muA
    loA --> muC[Phase Shifter 90 deg]
    muC --> muB
    muA --> smA[SUMMER]
    muB --> smA
    smA --> outA[QPSK RF Output to Channel]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: M-ary PSK Constellation Geometry (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[M-PSK Constellation on Circle of radius sqrt Es]
    rootA --> aA[M = 2 BPSK antipodal points]
    rootA --> aB[M = 4 QPSK square corners]
    rootA --> aC[M = 8 Octagon]
    rootA --> aD[M = 16 16-Point Circle]
    aA --> noteA[Bandwidth eff 1 bit per s per Hz]
    aB --> noteB[Bandwidth eff 2 bit per s per Hz]
    aC --> noteC[Bandwidth eff 3 bit per s per Hz]
    aD --> noteD[Bandwidth eff 4 bit per s per Hz]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Transmission Mode Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootB[Data Transmission Mode]
    rootB --> branchA[Parallel Transmission]
    rootB --> branchB[Serial Transmission]
    branchA --> appA[Applications: Internal CPU bus, Older Printer Port, RAM Data Lines]
    branchA --> propA[Properties: 8 or 16 or 32 bit bus, Clock skew is a problem, Fast but expensive over long distance]
    branchB --> subA[Asynchronous Serial]
    branchB --> subB[Synchronous Serial]
    subA --> propB1[Start bit 1 stop bit framing, No shared clock, 8-N-1 convention, 80 percent efficient, Used in RS-232 UART keyboards]
    subB --> propB2[Continuous block transfer, Shared or recovered clock, No per-byte overhead, Used in USB PCIe SATA Ethernet HDLC]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: BPSK Constellation with Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisX[Horizontal axis I component] --> originA[Origin 0,0]
    originA --> ptA[Point S0 at plus sqrt Eb on I axis representing bit 0]
    originA --> ptB[Point S1 at minus sqrt Eb on I axis representing bit 1]
    ptA -.-> boundaryA[Decision boundary vertical line at I = 0]
    ptB -.-> boundaryA
    boundaryA --> decisionA[If received I greater 0 decode as bit 0]
    boundaryA --> decisionB[If received I less 0 decode as bit 1]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading these diagrams in the exam**: KTU board examiners expect you to draw a **PSK constellation diagram** with **labeled axes (I, Q), points, and decision boundaries** in Part A questions. The Mermaid diagrams above provide the **logical structure** — in the actual exam, **recreate them as hand-drawn (or AutoCAD-drawn) sketches** with a **clear arrow for each constellation point** and a **dashed line for each decision boundary**.
