The receiver uses a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** to recover the clock from the incoming bit stream, then demultiplexes samples into per-channel **jitter buffers** that release data at a steady, isochronous rate.

---

### 4.2 Timing Diagram of a 3-Channel TDM Isochronous Frame

```mermaid
gantt
    title TDM Frame Structure for 3 Isochronous Channels
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S.%L
    section Frame 1
    CH1 Slot 1 :a1, 0, 417us
    CH2 Slot 1 :a2, after a1, 417us
    CH3 Slot 1 :a3, after a2, 417us
    FRAME BIT    :a4, after a3, 139us
    section Frame 2
    CH1 Slot 2 :b1, after a4, 417us
    CH2 Slot 2 :b2, after b1, 417us
    CH3 Slot 2 :b3, after b2, 417us
    FRAME BIT    :b4, after b3, 139us
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each frame has three 417 μs data slots (one per channel) followed by a 139 μs framing bit, totaling **1390 μs = 1.39 ms per frame**. The pattern repeats strictly — the next frame starts exactly 1.39 ms after the previous. The time between two samples of CH1 is exactly 1.39 ms, satisfying the isochronous contract.

---

### 4.3 State Diagram of the Isochronous Transmitter

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> WAIT_CLOCK : Master Clock Detected
    WAIT_CLOCK --> SLOT_ACTIVE : Rising Edge of Clock Tick
    SLOT_ACTIVE --> SHIFT_OUT : Load Current Channel Sample
    SHIFT_OUT --> UPDATE_POINTER : All Bits Shifted
    UPDATE_POINTER --> CHECK_LAST_SLOT : Increment Slot Index
    CHECK_LAST_SLOT --> FRAME_END : Slot Index equals N
    CHECK_LAST_SLOT --> SLOT_ACTIVE : Slot Index less than N
    FRAME_END --> INSERT_FRAME_BIT : Emit Framing Bit
    INSERT_FRAME_BIT --> WAIT_CLOCK : Reset Slot Index to Zero
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The transmitter is a deterministic state machine locked to a master clock. Every clock tick advances the machine through sample → shift → pointer-update → next-channel-or-frame-wrap. The **determinism** of this state machine is the foundation of isochronous behavior.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between Asynchronous, Synchronous, and Isochronous transmission modes with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Asynchronous Transmission:** Data is transmitted character-by-character with start and stop bits. The time gap between characters is **variable and unbounded**. Example: **RS-232** serial communication.
> - **Synchronous Transmission:** Data is sent as a continuous stream of bits/blocks with a shared clock for bit alignment. No start/stop bits per character. Example: **HDLC** data link protocol.
> - **Isochronous Transmission:** Data is transmitted in a **continuous stream at fixed, equal time intervals**, with strict, bounded latency. Retransmission is not used. Example: **USB isochronous mode** or **PCM T1 voice link**.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Why is isochronous transmission preferred for real-time voice and video applications?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Isochronous transmission is preferred because it **guarantees that data units are delivered at fixed, predictable time intervals** with bounded latency and minimal jitter. Real-time voice and video are highly time-sensitive — a delayed or bursty frame causes audible glitches, frozen video, or loss of lip-sync. Isochronous transmission ensures that the decoder receives samples on time, even if a few are lost or corrupted, because the playback schedule is deterministic. The system prioritizes **timeliness over perfect accuracy**, which is the correct trade-off for perceptual media.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **A.** (a) Draw the block diagram of a Time Division Multiplexer for combining **$n = 4$** isochronous voice channels sampled at **$f_s = 8\,\text{kHz}$** with **$n_b = 8$ bits/sample**. Explain the function of each block. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **A.** (b) A TDM system combines **32 voice channels** sampled at **8 kHz** with **8 bits/sample**. There are **8 framing/overhead bits per TDM frame**. Compute:
> - (i) Frame duration
> - (ii) Total bits per frame
> - (iii) Line bit rate
> - (iv) Bit duration **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part A(a):**

```
[4 Voice Sources] → [Sample & Hold × 4] → [MUX controlled by
8 kHz Clock] → [Serial Output Line 1.544 Mbps]
                          ↑
              [Crystal Clock Source]
```

**Block Functions:**
- **Voice Sources (1–4):** Provide continuous analog voice signals.
- **Sample and Hold (×4):** Captures instantaneous amplitude of each voice signal at the 8 kHz clock edge, holds the value stable for the duration of one TDM slot.
- **Crystal Clock Source:** Generates the 8 kHz master timing reference, ensuring isochronous sampling.
- **TDM Multiplexer (MUX):** A digital switch that sequentially selects one channel at a time, in round-robin fashion, and forwards the held sample to the serial line.
- **Serial Output Line:** Carries the interleaved bit stream at the aggregate line rate.

**Model Solution — Part A(b):**

**(i) Frame duration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000}\,\text{s} \\[6pt]
T_{frame} &= 1.25 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 125\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total bits per frame:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bits} &= (n \times n_b) + N_{ovh} \\[6pt]
N_{bits} &= (32 \times 8) + 8 \\[6pt]
N_{bits} &= 256 + 8 = 264\,\text{bits/frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Line bit rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{line} &= N_{bits} \times f_s \\[6pt]
R_{line} &= 264 \times 8000 \\[6pt]
R_{line} &= 2{,}112{,}000\,\text{bps} = 2.112\,\text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Bit duration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{bit} &= \frac{1}{R_{line}} = \frac{1}{2.112 \times 10^6}\,\text{s} \\[6pt]
T_{bit} &= 4.7348 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s} = 473.48\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **B.** (a) Explain the concept of **jitter** in isochronous transmission. Derive the expression for **jitter buffer size** in terms of maximum observed jitter and sampling frequency. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **B.** (b) For a **CD-quality audio** stream with $f_s = 44.1\,\text{kHz}$ and $n_b = 16$ bits/sample in stereo (2 channels), compute:
> - (i) Per-channel bit rate
> - (ii) Total stereo bit rate
> - (iii) Size of 1 second of uncompressed audio
> - (iv) Maximum jitter buffer size if $J_{max} = 5\,\text{ms}$ **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part B(a):**

**Jitter Definition:** Jitter is the **short-term variation in the arrival time of isochronous data units** from their expected scheduled instant. Even with a perfect clock, the network introduces small random delays, causing the inter-arrival time to fluctuate around $\Delta t = 1/f_s$.

If jitter is uncompensated, the receiver may either **underflow** (no data ready for playout → glitch) or **overflow** (buffer full → sample dropped). The solution is a **jitter buffer (playout buffer)**.

**Derivation of Jitter Buffer Size:**

Let $J_{max}$ be the worst-case jitter (in seconds). To absorb this, the receiver must buffer at least $J_{max}$ seconds of samples **in advance** of the playout point. The number of samples buffered is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{samples} &= J_{max} \times f_s
\end{aligned}
$$

To allow for jitter on **both sides** of the nominal playout instant (early and late arrivals), we double this:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\text{Buffer Depth} = 2 \times J_{max} \times f_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding byte size (for 1 byte/sample) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\text{Buffer Size} = 2 \times J_{max} \times f_s \times 1\,\text{byte}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trade-off:** A larger buffer absorbs more jitter but adds **algorithmic (playout) delay**, which is detrimental for interactive applications (e.g., VoIP).

**Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**(i) Per-channel bit rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{ch} &= f_s \times n_b = 44100 \times 16 \\[6pt]
R_{ch} &= 705{,}600\,\text{bps} = 705.6\,\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total stereo bit rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{stereo} &= 2 \times R_{ch} = 2 \times 705{,}600 \\[6pt]
R_{stereo} &= 1{,}411{,}200\,\text{bps} = 1.4112\,\text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Size of 1 second of uncompressed audio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size} &= R_{stereo} \times 1\,\text{s} = 1.4112\,\text{Mb} \\[6pt]
\text{Size} &= \frac{1.4112}{8}\,\text{MB} = 0.1764\,\text{MB} = 176.4\,\text{kB}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Equivalent to the well-known **1411 kbps CBR** of Red Book CD-DA audio.)

**(iv) Maximum jitter buffer size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{samples} &= 2 \times J_{max} \times f_s \\[6pt]
B_{samples} &= 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 44100 \\[6pt]
B_{samples} &= 441\,\text{samples}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{size} &= 441 \times 2\,\text{bytes} = 882\,\text{bytes (stereo)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the framing/overhead bits** when calculating $N_{bits}$ — examiners dock **2 marks** for not including sync/overhead bits in TDM calculations.
> 2. **Mixing up TDM frame duration with bit duration.** $T_{frame} = 1/f_s$ (frame-level isochronous interval), while $T_{bit} = 1/R_{line}$ (single-bit time). Conflating them loses marks.
> 3. **Stating that isochronous transmission guarantees zero errors.** It does not — it guarantees **bounded latency**, not perfect accuracy. Use words like "bounded latency," "deterministic timing," "guaranteed bandwidth," and "no retransmission."
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in jitter buffer size.** The "2×" accounts for both pre- and post-playout jitter window. Missing this is a frequent **1-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Using the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper.** Use $c \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s for copper cable — a typical KTU trick question.
> 6. **Confusing sampling rate with line rate.** A 24-channel T1 PCM system samples at 8 kHz, but transmits at 1.544 Mbps — students sometimes report 8 kbps as the line rate.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This!)**
>
> - **Definition to remember:** Isochronous = *equal time* between data units; timing is guaranteed; bounded latency; **no retransmission**.
> - **Three-mode mantra:** *Async* = character+gap, *Sync* = clock-aligned stream, *Isochronous* = clock-aligned + periodic delivery.
> - **T1 carrier fact:** 24 voice channels × 8 kHz × 8 bits + 1 framing bit = **1.544 Mbps** — the canonical isochronous TDM example.
> - **E1 carrier fact:** 30 voice + 2 signaling = 32 slots × 8 bits × 8 kHz = **2.048 Mbps** (European counterpart of T1).
> - **Jitter buffer formula:** $\text{Depth} = 2 \times J_{max} \times f_s$ — *double the product of max jitter and sampling rate*.
> - **Playout delay trade-off:** Bigger buffer → less jitter, but more delay. Critical for VoIP (target one-way delay < 150 ms).
> - **Real-world adopters:** USB isochronous endpoints, IEEE 1394 FireWire, ATM AAL1, Bluetooth A2DP, HDMI, RTP/RTCP, FlexRay, EtherCAT.
> - **Error policy:** **Timing > Accuracy.** Lost or late samples are not retransmitted — the next sample continues. FEC may be used.
> - **Clock recovery:** Receivers use a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** to extract the sender's clock from the bit stream.
> - **Propagation constraint:** $L_{max} = c \times T_{bit}$ bounds physical reach without repeaters.
> - **SNR (quantization):** $\text{SNR} = 6.02 \cdot n_b + 1.76$ dB — every extra bit adds ~6 dB of dynamic range.

> **End of Module 1 — Isochronous Transmission Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplexing: TDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multiplexing: TDM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: Multiplexing — Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** is a digital multiplexing technique in which multiple low-speed digital signals are combined into a single high-speed transmission channel by allocating **discrete, sequential time slots** to each input signal on a **repetitive, rotating basis**. Each signal occupies the entire bandwidth of the channel, but only for the brief duration of its assigned time slot.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, TDM is positioned as a **Layer 1 (Physical Layer)** multiplexing mechanism used to maximize the utilization of expensive high-bandwidth trunk lines by interleaving samples from several lower-rate sources.

**Key Standard Metric**: In classical PCM carrier systems (e.g., the North American **T1** system), the standard sampling rate per voice channel is **8000 samples/second** (corresponding to the Nyquist rate for a **4 kHz** voice band), and each voice sample is encoded into **8 bits** using PCM → yielding the famous **64 kbps** per voice channel.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Round-Robin Toll Booth"

Imagine a single-lane highway toll booth (the shared high-speed channel) with **4 cars** (4 low-speed input lines) waiting to pass.

Instead of building 4 toll booths (which is expensive, just like running 4 separate cables), the system uses **one booth** and a strict **traffic controller**. The controller says:
- Second 1 → Car A passes.
- Second 2 → Car B passes.
- Second 3 → Car C passes.
- Second 4 → Car D passes.
- Then the cycle repeats.

Each car gets the **full road** for its brief turn, but they all share the road **in time**, not in space. That is exactly what TDM does with bits — it shares **one wire** by giving **turn-by-turn access** to each input stream.

### 1.3 Why TDM is Used in Modern Networks

> [!NOTE]
> - **Bandwidth Economics**: Leasing a single **1.544 Mbps** T1 line is far cheaper than leasing **24 separate 64 kbps** voice lines.
> - **Digital Native**: Unlike FDM, TDM directly accommodates digital signals without complex analog filtering.
> - **Low Crosstalk**: Because signals occupy the channel in separate time slots, cross-talk interference is minimized.
> - **Foundation of PSTN**: Almost every landline telephone call between exchanges is carried over a TDM backbone (T1, E1, T3, SONET/SDH).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Principle — How TDM Works

The TDM process occurs in **3 systematic stages**:

1. **Sampling Stage** — Each analog input is sampled at the Nyquist rate (≥ 2 × f_max). For voice, f_max ≈ 3400 Hz, hence sampling at **8 kHz**.
2. **Quantization & Encoding Stage** — Each sample is quantized and digitally encoded (typically into **8 bits** for PCM).
3. **Interleaving Stage** — A **commutator / multiplexer (MUX)** rotates at the sampling rate, connecting each input sequentially to the high-speed output line. At the receiver, a synchronized **demultiplexer (DEMUX)** de-interleaves the bits back to their original channels.

### 2.2 Two Principal Variants of TDM

| Variant | Full Name | Slot Allocation | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous TDM** (STDM / Classic TDM) | Time Division Multiplexing – Fixed Slot | Pre-assigned, fixed slot regardless of data presence | Lower (idle slots wasted) |
| **Asynchronous TDM** (ATDM / Statistical TDM) | Statistical Time Division Multiplexing | Dynamic slot allocation based on demand | Higher (no idle slots) |

### 2.3 Synchronous TDM — Detailed Mechanics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Synchronous TDM**, the multiplexer assigns a **fixed time slot** to each input line during **every frame**, even if that input has **no data to transmit**. The frame structure is rigid and predictable.

**Frame Structure**: One complete cycle of all input slots is called a **TDM Frame**. If there are `n` input sources and each contributes `b` bits, then:

> **Frame Size = n × b bits**

**Bit Rate of the Multiplexed Line**:

$$R_{TDM} = n \times f_s \times b \quad \text{(bps)}$$

Where:
- `n` = number of input channels
- `f_s` = sampling frequency per channel (**Hz**)
- `b` = number of bits per sample

### 2.4 Asynchronous (Statistical) TDM — Detailed Mechanics

In **Statistical TDM**, the MUX only allocates a slot to inputs that **actually have data**. This requires:
- **Addressing bits** prepended to each slot (to identify the source at the DEMUX).
- **Buffer memory** at the MUX to queue bursts of incoming data.
- A **variable-length frame** structure.

**Statistical Multiplexer Concentrator Concept**: When `n` inputs each operate at peak rate `r`, but with a duty cycle (activity factor) `α` (typically 0.1 to 0.4), the **average required output rate** is:

$$R_{stat} \approx \alpha \times n \times r$$

Because `α < 1`, the statistical TDM line can be **much slower** than the synchronous equivalent — a major economic advantage.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voice channel bit rate | $R_c = f_s \times b$ | **64 kbps** when $f_s = 8$ kHz, $b = 8$ |
| 2 | TDM Frame size | $L_f = n \times b$ | bits per frame |
| 3 | Synchronous TDM output rate | $R_{TDM} = n \times f_s \times b$ | bps |
| 4 | Frame duration | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds (one frame per sample) |
| 5 | Statistical TDM rate | $R_{stat} = \alpha \cdot n \cdot r$ | bps (where α = activity factor) |
| 6 | Number of voice channels in T1 | $n = 24$ | DS-1 standard |
| 7 | Number of voice channels in E1 | $n = 32$ (30 voice + 2 framing/sync) | E1 standard |
| 8 | T1 line bit rate (with framing) | $R_{T1} = 24 \times 8 \times 8000 + 8 \times 8000$ | **1.544 Mbps** |
| 9 | E1 line bit rate | $R_{E1} = 32 \times 8 \times 8000$ | **2.048 Mbps** |
| 10 | PCM sampling rate for voice | $f_s = 8000$ Hz | Nyquist for **4 kHz** band |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE**: The `|` character was deliberately avoided in formula cells (e.g., used `·` or written it out explicitly) to prevent breaking markdown table rendering. The mid-stroke `\vert` semantic remains clear from the LaTeX formatting.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Synchronous TDM Output Bit Rate

**Problem Setup**: We have `n` analog voice sources. Each source has a maximum voice frequency of $f_{max} = 4$ kHz. Each sample is encoded using PCM with `b` bits per sample.

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist Sampling Theorem**

According to Nyquist, to reconstruct a band-limited signal without aliasing, we must sample at:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

For voice, $f_{max} \approx 4$ kHz, hence:

$$f_s = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ samples/sec}$$

**Step 2 — Bit Rate of a Single PCM Channel**

Each sample produces `b` bits. Therefore:

$$R_c = f_s \times b = 8000 \times 8 = 64000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

This is the famous **DS-0 (Digital Signal level 0)** channel — the fundamental building block of digital telephony.

**Step 3 — Bit Rate of n Synchronously Multiplexed Channels**

Since synchronous TDM interleaves the bits of all `n` channels into a single output stream:

$$R_{TDM} = n \times R_c = n \times f_s \times b$$

**Step 4 — Verification with the T1 Standard (n = 24)**

For the North American **T1 / DS-1** system, $n = 24$ voice channels and a framing bit is added per frame:

$$R_{payload} = 24 \times 8000 \times 8 = 1{,}536{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

Adding the **1 framing bit per 193-bit frame** (1 frame = 24 × 8 + 1 = 193 bits; 8000 frames/sec):

$$R_{T1} = 1{,}536{,}000 + (1 \times 8000) = 1{,}544{,}000 \text{ bps} = 1.544 \text{ Mbps}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Statistical TDM Capacity Gain

**Problem Setup**: `n = 10` terminals each generating data at peak rate $r = 9.6$ kbps, but each terminal is only active `α = 30%` of the time.

**Step 1 — Synchronous TDM Requirement (Worst Case)**

$$R_{sync} = n \times r = 10 \times 9600 = 96{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 2 — Statistical TDM Requirement (Average Case)**

$$R_{stat} = \alpha \times n \times r = 0.30 \times 10 \times 9600 = 28{,}800 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 3 — Capacity Saving**

$$\text{Saving} = R_{sync} - R_{stat} = 96{,}000 - 28{,}800 = 67{,}200 \text{ bps}$$

$$\text{Saving \%} = \frac{67{,}200}{96{,}000} \times 100 = 70\%$$

This dramatic saving is why **Statistical TDM** is preferred in **data networks** (e.g., X.25 packet networks, frame relay multiplexers, and historically used in DSLAMs).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — T1 vs E1 Comparison

| Parameter | T1 (DS-1) — North America / Japan | E1 — Europe / India / KTU Region |
|---|---|---|
| Voice channels (n) | 24 | 30 (+ 2 signaling/framing) |
| Bits per sample (b) | 8 | 8 |
| Sampling rate (f_s) | 8000 Hz | 8000 Hz |
| Frame bits | 1 framing bit | 32 × 8 = 256 bits total |
| Frame duration | 125 µs | 125 µs |
| Total bit rate | **1.544 Mbps** | **2.048 Mbps** |
| Line coding | AMI / B8ZS | HDB3 |
| Used in India (BSNL/MTNL) | Rare | **Dominant standard** |

> [!NOTE]
> Indian telecom infrastructure predominantly uses **E1 (2.048 Mbps)** trunk lines between exchanges — a high-yield KTU fact.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Synchronous TDM — Transmitter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["INPUT 1\n8 bits per sample"] --> MUX1["ROTATING\nCOMMUTATOR"]
    A2["INPUT 2\n8 bits per sample"] --> MUX1
    A3["INPUT 3\n8 bits per sample"] --> MUX1
    A4["INPUT N\n8 bits per sample"] --> MUX1
    MUX1 -->|High Speed\nSerial Stream| TX["DIGITAL\nTRANSMISSION\nCHANNEL"]
    TX --> RX["DIGITAL\nRECEIVING\nCHANNEL"]
    RX --> DEMUX1["SYNCHRONIZED\nCOMMUTATOR"]
    DEMUX1 --> B1["OUTPUT 1\n8 bits per sample"]
    DEMUX1 --> B2["OUTPUT 2\n8 bits per sample"]
    DEMUX1 --> B3["OUTPUT 3\n8 bits per sample"]
    DEMUX1 --> B4["OUTPUT N\n8 bits per sample"]
    CLK["CLOCK 8 KHZ"] -.-> MUX1
    CLK -.-> DEMUX1
```

### 4.2 TDM Frame Structure (n = 4, b = 4 for illustration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["FRAME STRUCTURE\nEach slot = 4 bits"] --> S1["SLOT 1\nChannel 1\n(b3 b2 b1 b0)"]
    F --> S2["SLOT 2\nChannel 2\n(b3 b2 b1 b0)"]
    F --> S3["SLOT 3\nChannel 3\n(b3 b2 b1 b0)"]
    F --> S4["SLOT 4\nChannel 4\n(b3 b2 b1 b0)"]
    F --> SYNC["SYNCH\nFRAME BIT"]
```

### 4.3 Synchronous vs Statistical TDM — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYNC["SYNCHRONOUS TDM\nFixed Slot per Channel"] --> S1["Slot 1 = Channel 1\neven if IDLE"]
    SYNC --> S2["Slot 2 = Channel 2\neven if IDLE"]
    SYNC --> S3["Slot 3 = Channel 3\neven if IDLE"]
    SYNC --> S4["Slot 4 = Channel 4\neven if IDLE"]
    STAT["STATISTICAL TDM\nDynamic Slot Allocation"] --> T1["Slot 1 = Active Channel X"]
    STAT --> T2["Slot 2 = Active Channel Y"]
    STAT --> T3["Slot 3 = Active Channel X again"]
    STAT --> T4["Slot 4 = NO SLOT\nidle channel skipped"]
```

### 4.4 TDM Multiplexing Hierarchy (Digital Carrier System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DS0["DS-0\n64 kbps\n1 voice channel"] --> DS1["DS-1 T1\n1.544 Mbps\n24 voice channels"]
    DS1 --> DS2["DS-2\n6.312 Mbps\n96 voice channels"]
    DS2 --> DS3["DS-3\n44.736 Mbps\n672 voice channels"]
    DS3 --> DS4["DS-4\n274.176 Mbps\n4032 voice channels"]
    E0["E-0\n64 kbps"] --> E1["E-1\n2.048 Mbps\n30 voice channels"]
    E1 --> E2["E-2\n8.448 Mbps\n120 voice channels"]
    E2 --> E3["E-3\n34.368 Mbps\n480 voice channels"]
    E3 --> E4["E-4\n139.264 Mbps\n1920 voice channels"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note**: All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `MUX1`, `DEMUX1`) — no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) are used as node identifiers, and labels contain plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Time Division Multiplexing. Mention two advantages of TDM over FDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

> **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** is a multiplexing technique in which multiple digital signals share a common transmission channel by taking turns in **discrete time slots**. Each signal is sampled in sequence, and the samples are interleaved into a high-speed bit stream. **(2 marks)**

**Two advantages over FDM:**
1. **No guard bands required** — Since signals occupy separate time slots (not frequency bands), the bandwidth of TDM is used more efficiently. **(0.5 marks)**
2. **Lower crosstalk and better noise immunity** — Digital signals in time slots are less affected by inter-channel interference than analog carriers packed in frequency. **(0.5 marks)**

*(Examiner may also accept: simpler digital circuitry, direct compatibility with digital systems, easier encryption.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Synchronous TDM and Statistical TDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Feature | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Slot allocation | Fixed, pre-assigned | Dynamic, on demand |
| Idle channel slots | Wasted (still transmitted) | Skipped |
| Addressing required | No | Yes (source ID added) |
| Output line speed | Equal to n × peak rate | Lower than n × peak rate |
| Buffer needed | No | Yes |

**(3 marks for any three valid distinguishing points)**

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **4-channel Synchronous TDM system** with a neat block diagram. Assume each channel sends 8-bit PCM samples and the sampling rate is 8 kHz. Calculate the **duration of one TDM frame** and the **output bit rate**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **T1 (DS-1)** and **E1** digital carrier systems in terms of number of channels, frame structure, and bit rate. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q(a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (3 marks)**

A 4-channel Synchronous TDM system consists of:
- **4 analog input sources** → fed to **4 PCM encoders**.
- **PCM encoders** → output 8-bit parallel digital samples at 8 kHz each.
- **Multiplexer (MUX)** → rotating commutator at 8 kHz clock.
- **High-speed serial line** → carries interleaved bits.
- At the receiver: **DEMUX** + **PCM decoders** + **4 output channels**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1["INPUT 1\nAnalog Voice"] --> E1["PCM\nENCODER 1"]
    I2["INPUT 2\nAnalog Voice"] --> E2["PCM\nENCODER 2"]
    I3["INPUT 3\nAnalog Voice"] --> E3["PCM\nENCODER 3"]
    I4["INPUT 4\nAnalog Voice"] --> E4["PCM\nENCODER 4"]
    E1 --> M["MUX\nROTATING\nSWITCH"]
    E2 --> M
    E3 --> M
    E4 --> M
    M -->|"1.544 Mbps\nserial stream"| TX["TRANSMISSION\nLINE"]
    TX --> DM["DEMUX"]
    DM --> D1["PCM\nDECODER 1"]
    DM --> D2["PCM\nDECODER 2"]
    DM --> D3["PCM\nDECODER 3"]
    DM --> D4["PCM\nDECODER 4"]
    D1 --> O1["OUTPUT 1"]
    D2 --> O2["OUTPUT 2"]
    D3 --> O3["OUTPUT 3"]
    D4 --> O4["OUTPUT 4"]
```

**Step 2 — Frame Duration (2 marks)**

One TDM frame consists of **one sample from each of the 4 channels**. Frame size:

$$L_f = n \times b = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ bits}$$

Frame duration equals the sampling interval (since one sample per channel is collected per cycle):

$$T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3 — Output Bit Rate (2 marks)**

$$R_{TDM} = n \times f_s \times b = 4 \times 8000 \times 8 = 256{,}000 \text{ bps} = 256 \text{ kbps}$$

---

#### **Solution to Q(b) (7 marks)**

**T1 (DS-1) — North American/Japanese Standard:**

- Number of voice channels: **24**
- Each sample: **8 bits** PCM
- Sampling rate: **8000 Hz**
- Frame structure: **24 × 8 + 1 framing bit = 193 bits per frame**
- Frame duration: **125 μs**
- Bit rate: $24 \times 8 \times 8000 + 1 \times 8000$ = **1.544 Mbps**
- Line coding: **AMI / B8ZS**

**E1 — European/Indian Standard:**

- Number of voice channels: **30** (plus 2 channels used for framing and signaling)
- Each sample: **8 bits** PCM
- Sampling rate: **8000 Hz**
- Frame structure: **32 × 8 = 256 bits per frame** (8 bits/frame for framing, 8 bits for signaling, 240 bits for 30 voice channels)
- Frame duration: **125 μs**
- Bit rate: $32 \times 8 \times 8000$ = **2.048 Mbps**
- Line coding: **HDB3**

| Parameter | T1 (DS-1) | E1 |
|---|---|---|
| Voice channels | 24 | 30 |
| Total bit rate | 1.544 Mbps | 2.048 Mbps |
| Framing overhead | 1 bit/frame | 2 × 8 bits/frame |
| Used in India | Less common | **Standard** |

**(Full 7 marks awarded for complete comparison with calculations and at least 4 differentiating parameters)**

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **Statistical Time Division Multiplexer**. Why is its output bit rate lower than that of a synchronous TDM with the same number of inputs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Ten terminals are multiplexed using **Statistical TDM**. Each terminal has a peak data rate of **19.2 kbps**, but is active only **25% of the time** on average. Calculate:
  (i) The bit rate required by a **Synchronous TDM**.
  (ii) The bit rate required by a **Statistical TDM**.
  (iii) The **percentage saving** in line capacity. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q(a) (7 marks)**

A **Statistical TDM** (also called Asynchronous TDM or Intelligent TDM) dynamically allocates time slots only to those input lines that actually have data to send. It consists of:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1["INPUT 1"] --> B1["BUFFER\nMEMORY 1"]
    I2["INPUT 2"] --> B2["BUFFER\nMEMORY 2"]
    I3["INPUT 3"] --> B3["BUFFER\nMEMORY 3"]
    I4["INPUT N"] --> B4["BUFFER\nMEMORY N"]
    B1 --> SM["STATISTICAL\nMULTIPLEXER\n(SCANNER)"]
    B2 --> SM
    B3 --> SM
    B4 --> SM
    SM -->|Address + Data bits| OUT["HIGH SPEED\nOUTPUT LINE"]
    SM -.Control.-> B1
    SM -.Control.-> B2
    SM -.Control.-> B3
    SM -.Control.-> B4
```

**Working**: The scanner inspects buffer memories in a round-robin fashion. If a buffer has data, the MUX reads it, prepends the source address, and sends it as a compact packet on the high-speed line. If a buffer is empty, the MUX **skips** it — saving bandwidth.

**Why lower bit rate?** Since idle channels do NOT consume slot time, the line only carries active data. With activity factor `α < 1`, the required line rate is `α × n × r` instead of `n × r`.

**(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for explanation, 2 for "why lower" justification)**

---

#### **Solution to Q(b) (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- n = 10 terminals
- r = 19.2 kbps per terminal (peak)
- α = 0.25 (25% activity)

**(i) Synchronous TDM bit rate (3 marks):**

$$R_{sync} = n \times r = 10 \times 19{,}200 = 192{,}000 \text{ bps} = 192 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) Statistical TDM bit rate (2 marks):**

$$R_{stat} = \alpha \times n \times r = 0.25 \times 10 \times 19{,}200 = 48{,}000 \text{ bps} = 48 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iii) Percentage saving (2 marks):**

$$\text{Saving} = R_{sync} - R_{stat} = 192{,}000 - 48{,}000 = 144{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

$$\text{Saving \%} = \frac{144{,}000}{192{,}000} \times 100 = 75\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Students often write `n × r` for Synchronous TDM but forget to multiply by `α` for Statistical — losing 2 marks instantly.
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Confusing the **sampling rate** (8 kHz for voice) with the **data rate per channel** (64 kbps). They are NOT the same.
> - **Common Mistake #3**: In T1 calculations, forgetting the **+1 framing bit per frame** → answers come out as **1.536 Mbps** instead of the correct **1.544 Mbps** (a 0.5 mark deduction).
> - **Common Mistake #4**: Not labelling units explicitly (kbps vs Mbps vs bps) — KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — TDM:**

- ✅ **Definition**: TDM shares **one channel in TIME**, not in frequency.
- ✅ **Voice channel rate**: **64 kbps** = 8 kHz sampling × 8 bits PCM.
- ✅ **Frame size**: n × b bits. **Frame duration**: 1 / f_s = **125 μs**.
- ✅ **Synchronous TDM**: fixed slots; **Statistical TDM**: dynamic slots with addressing.
- ✅ **T1 (DS-1)**: 24 channels → **1.544 Mbps** (1.536 + 8 kbps framing).
- ✅ **E1**: 32 time slots (30 voice + 2 signaling) → **2.048 Mbps**.
- ✅ **Statistical gain**: $R_{stat} = \alpha \cdot n \cdot r$; saving factor = $(1 - \alpha)$.
- ✅ **Synchronization** between MUX and DEMUX is **essential** — without it, data lands in wrong channels.
- ✅ **Pulse stuffing** is used in higher-order multiplexers (Plesiochronous Digital Hierarchy — PDH) to synchronize slightly different source clocks.
- ✅ **Guard time / guard bits** are NOT needed in TDM (unlike FDM's guard bands) — a key advantage.
- ✅ **Indian telecom standard** = **E1 (2.048 Mbps)**.
- ✅ **Draw the MUX/DEMUX block diagram** in every TDM answer — it carries **2–3 marks alone**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Standalone Reference)

### 2.1 Three-Stage Operational Principle

1. **Sampling Stage** — Each analog input is sampled at the Nyquist rate (≥ 2 × f_max). For voice, f_max ≈ 3400 Hz, hence sampling at **8 kHz**.
2. **Quantization & Encoding Stage** — Each sample is quantized and digitally encoded (typically into **8 bits** for PCM).
3. **Interleaving Stage** — A **commutator / multiplexer (MUX)** rotates at the sampling rate, connecting each input sequentially to the high-speed output line. At the receiver, a synchronized **demultiplexer (DEMUX)** de-interleaves the bits back to their original channels.

### 2.2 Synchronous vs Asynchronous TDM (Detailed)

| Variant | Slot Allocation | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous TDM (STDM) | Pre-assigned, fixed slot | Lower (idle slots wasted) |
| Asynchronous TDM (ATDM) | Dynamic slot allocation | Higher (no idle slots) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Standalone)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voice channel bit rate | $R_c = f_s \times b$ | **64 kbps** when $f_s = 8$ kHz, $b = 8$ |
| 2 | TDM Frame size | $L_f = n \times b$ | bits per frame |
| 3 | Synchronous TDM output rate | $R_{TDM} = n \times f_s \times b$ | bps |
| 4 | Frame duration | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds (one frame per sample) |
| 5 | Statistical TDM rate | $R_{stat} = \alpha \cdot n \cdot r$ | bps (where α = activity factor) |
| 6 | Number of voice channels in T1 | $n = 24$ | DS-1 standard |
| 7 | Number of voice channels in E1 | $n = 32$ (30 voice + 2 framing/sync) | E1 standard |
| 8 | T1 line bit rate (with framing) | $R_{T1} = 24 \times 8 \times 8000 + 8 \times 8000$ | **1.544 Mbps** |
| 9 | E1 line bit rate | $R_{E1} = 32 \times 8 \times 8000$ | **2.048 Mbps** |
| 10 | PCM sampling rate for voice | $f_s = 8000$ Hz | Nyquist for **4 kHz** band |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network)** trunks worldwide use TDM over copper/fiber.
- **SONET/SDH** (Synchronous Optical Networking / Synchronous Digital Hierarchy) is a high-speed TDM-based optical carrier system used in modern long-haul telecom.
- **TDM-based access networks** are still dominant in 2G/3G mobile backhaul (e.g., E1/T1 links from BTS to BSC).
- **Statistical multiplexers** form the historical basis of X.25 packet switching networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Standalone Reference)

### 3.1 Derivation of the Synchronous TDM Output Bit Rate

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist Sampling Theorem**

According to Nyquist, to reconstruct a band-limited signal without aliasing, we must sample at:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

For voice, $f_{max} \approx 4$ kHz, hence:

$$f_s = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ samples/sec}$$

**Step 2 — Bit Rate of a Single PCM Channel**

Each sample produces `b` bits. Therefore:

$$R_c = f_s \times b = 8000 \times 8 = 64000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

This is the famous **DS-0 (Digital Signal level 0)** channel — the fundamental building block of digital telephony.

**Step 3 — Bit Rate of n Synchronously Multiplexed Channels**

Since synchronous TDM interleaves the bits of all `n` channels into a single output stream:

$$R_{TDM} = n \times R_c = n \times f_s \times b$$

**Step 4 — Verification with the T1 Standard (n = 24)**

For the North American **T1 / DS-1** system, $n = 24$ voice channels and a framing bit is added per frame:

$$R_{payload} = 24 \times 8000 \times 8 = 1{,}536{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

Adding the **1 framing bit per 193-bit frame** (1 frame = 24 × 8 + 1 = 193 bits; 8000 frames/sec):

$$R_{T1} = 1{,}536{,}000 + (1 \times 8000) = 1{,}544{,}000 \text{ bps} = 1.544 \text{ Mbps}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Statistical TDM Capacity Gain

**Step 1 — Synchronous TDM Requirement (Worst Case)**

$$R_{sync} = n \times r = 10 \times 9600 = 96{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 2 — Statistical TDM Requirement (Average Case)**

$$R_{stat} = \alpha \times n \times r = 0.30 \times 10 \times 9600 = 28{,}800 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 3 — Capacity Saving**

$$\text{Saving} = R_{sync} - R_{stat} = 96{,}000 - 28{,}800 = 67{,}200 \text{ bps}$$

$$\text{Saving \%} = \frac{67{,}200}{96{,}000} \times 100 = 70\%$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — T1 vs E1 Comparison

| Parameter | T1 (DS-1) — North America / Japan | E1 — Europe / India / KTU Region |
|---|---|---|
| Voice channels (n) | 24 | 30 (+ 2 signaling/framing) |
| Bits per sample (b) | 8 | 8 |
| Sampling rate (f_s) | 8000 Hz | 8000 Hz |
| Frame bits | 1 framing bit | 32 × 8 = 256 bits total |
| Frame duration | 125 µs | 125 µs |
| Total bit rate | **1.544 Mbps** | **2.048 Mbps** |
| Line coding | AMI / B8ZS | HDB3 |
| Used in India (BSNL/MTNL) | Rare | **Dominant standard** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Standalone Reference)

### 4.1 Synchronous TDM — Transmitter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["INPUT 1\n8 bits per sample"] --> MUX1["ROTATING\nCOMMUTATOR"]
    A2["INPUT 2\n8 bits per sample"] --> MUX1
    A3["INPUT 3\n8 bits per sample"] --> MUX1
    A4["INPUT N\n8 bits per sample"] --> MUX1
    MUX1 -->|High Speed\nSerial Stream| TX["DIGITAL\nTRANSMISSION\nCHANNEL"]
    TX --> RX["DIGITAL\nRECEIVING\nCHANNEL"]
    RX --> DEMUX1["SYNCHRONIZED\nCOMMUTATOR"]
    DEMUX1 --> B1["OUTPUT 1\n8 bits per sample"]
    DEMUX1 --> B2["OUTPUT 2\n8 bits per sample"]
    DEMUX1 --> B3["OUTPUT 3\n8 bits per sample"]
    DEMUX1 --> B4["OUTPUT N\n8 bits per sample"]
    CLK["CLOCK 8 KHZ"] -.-> MUX1
    CLK -.-> DEMUX1
```

### 4.2 TDM Frame Structure (n = 4, b = 4 for illustration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["FRAME STRUCTURE\nEach slot = 4 bits"] --> S1["SLOT 1\nChannel 1\n(b3 b2 b1 b0)"]
    F --> S2["SLOT 2\nChannel 2\n(b3 b2 b1 b0)"]
    F --> S3["SLOT 3\nChannel 3\n(b3 b2 b1 b0)"]
    F --> S4["SLOT 4\nChannel 4\n(b3 b2 b1 b0)"]
    F --> SYNC["SYNCH\nFRAME BIT"]
```

### 4.3 Synchronous vs Statistical TDM — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYNC["SYNCHRONOUS TDM\nFixed Slot per Channel"] --> S1["Slot 1 = Channel 1\neven if IDLE"]
    SYNC --> S2["Slot 2 = Channel 2\neven if IDLE"]
    SYNC --> S3["Slot 3 = Channel 3\neven if IDLE"]
    SYNC --> S4["Slot 4 = Channel 4\neven if IDLE"]
    STAT["STATISTICAL TDM\nDynamic Slot Allocation"] --> T1["Slot 1 = Active Channel X"]
    STAT --> T2["Slot 2 = Active Channel Y"]
    STAT --> T3["Slot 3 = Active Channel X again"]
    STAT --> T4["Slot 4 = NO SLOT\nidle channel skipped"]
```

### 4.4 TDM Multiplexing Hierarchy (Digital Carrier System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DS0["DS-0\n64 kbps\n1 voice channel"] --> DS1["DS-1 T1\n1.544 Mbps\n24 voice channels"]
    DS1 --> DS2["DS-2\n6.312 Mbps\n96 voice channels"]
    DS2 --> DS3["DS-3\n44.736 Mbps\n672 voice channels"]
    DS3 --> DS4["DS-4\n274.176 Mbps\n4032 voice channels"]
    E0["E-0\n64 kbps"] --> E1["E-1\n2.048 Mbps\n30 voice channels"]
    E1 --> E2["E-2\n8.448 Mbps\n120 voice channels"]
    E2 --> E3["E-3\n34.368 Mbps\n480 voice channels"]
    E3 --> E4["E-4\n139.264 Mbps\n1920 voice channels"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap (Standalone Reference)

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Time Division Multiplexing. Mention two advantages of TDM over FDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

> **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** is a multiplexing technique in which multiple digital signals share a common transmission channel by taking turns in **discrete time slots**. Each signal is sampled in sequence, and the samples are interleaved into a high-speed bit stream. **(2 marks)**

**Two advantages over FDM:**
1. **No guard bands required** — Since signals occupy separate time slots (not frequency bands), the bandwidth of TDM is used more efficiently. **(0.5 marks)**
2. **Lower crosstalk and better noise immunity** — Digital signals in time slots are less affected by inter-channel interference than analog carriers packed in frequency. **(0.5 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Synchronous TDM and Statistical TDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Feature | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Slot allocation | Fixed, pre-assigned | Dynamic, on demand |
| Idle channel slots | Wasted (still transmitted) | Skipped |
| Addressing required | No | Yes (source ID added) |
| Output line speed | Equal to n × peak rate | Lower than n × peak rate |
| Buffer needed | No | Yes |

**(3 marks for any three valid distinguishing points)**

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **4-channel Synchronous TDM system** with a neat block diagram. Assume each channel sends 8-bit PCM samples and the sampling rate is 8 kHz. Calculate the **duration of one TDM frame** and the **output bit rate**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **T1 (DS-1)** and **E1** digital carrier systems in terms of number of channels, frame structure, and bit rate. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q(a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (3 marks)**

A 4-channel Synchronous TDM system consists of:
- **4 analog input sources** → fed to **4 PCM encoders**.
- **PCM encoders** → output 8-bit parallel digital samples at 8 kHz each.
- **Multiplexer (MUX)** → rotating commutator at 8 kHz clock.
- **High-speed serial line** → carries interleaved bits.
- At the receiver: **DEMUX** + **PCM decoders** + **4 output channels**.

**Step 2 — Frame Duration (2 marks)**

One TDM frame consists of **one sample from each of the 4 channels**. Frame size:

$$L_f = n \times b = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ bits}$$

Frame duration equals the sampling interval (since one sample per channel is collected per cycle):

$$T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3 — Output Bit Rate (2 marks)**

$$R_{TDM} = n \times f_s \times b = 4 \times 8000 \times 8 = 256{,}000 \text{ bps} = 256 \text{ kbps}$$

---

#### **Solution to Q(b) (7 marks)**

**T1 (DS-1) — North American/Japanese Standard:**

- Number of voice channels: **24**
- Each sample: **8 bits** PCM
- Sampling rate: **8000 Hz**
- Frame structure: **24 × 8 + 1 framing bit = 193 bits per frame**
- Frame duration: **125 μs**
- Bit rate: $24 \times 8 \times 8000 + 1 \times 8000$ = **1.544 Mbps**
- Line coding: **AMI / B8ZS**

**E1 — European/Indian Standard:**

- Number of voice channels: **30** (plus 2 channels used for framing and signaling)
- Each sample: **8 bits** PCM
- Sampling rate: **8000 Hz**
- Frame structure: **32 × 8 = 256 bits per frame**
- Frame duration: **125 μs**
- Bit rate: $32 \times 8 \times 8000$ = **2.048 Mbps**
- Line coding: **HDB3**

| Parameter | T1 (DS-1) | E1 |
|---|---|---|
| Voice channels | 24 | 30 |
| Total bit rate | 1.544 Mbps | 2.048 Mbps |
| Framing overhead | 1 bit/frame | 2 × 8 bits/frame |
| Used in India | Less common | **Standard** |

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **Statistical Time Division Multiplexer**. Why is its output bit rate lower than that of a synchronous TDM with the same number of inputs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Ten terminals are multiplexed using **Statistical TDM**. Each terminal has a peak data rate of **19.2 kbps**, but is active only **25% of the time** on average. Calculate:
  (i) The bit rate required by a **Synchronous TDM**.
  (ii) The bit rate required by a **Statistical TDM**.
  (iii) The **percentage saving** in line capacity. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q(a) (7 marks)**

A **Statistical TDM** (also called Asynchronous TDM or Intelligent TDM) dynamically allocates time slots only to those input lines that actually have data to send. The multiplexer has:
- Buffer memories at each input.
- A scanner that inspects each buffer in round-robin.
- If buffer has data → read, prepend source address, send on high-speed line.
- If buffer is empty → skip the input, saving bandwidth.

**Why lower bit rate?** Since idle channels do NOT consume slot time, the line only carries active data. With activity factor `α < 1`, the required line rate is `α × n × r` instead of `n × r`.

**(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for explanation, 2 for "why lower" justification)**

---

#### **Solution to Q(b) (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- n = 10 terminals
- r = 19.2 kbps per terminal (peak)
- α = 0.25 (25% activity)

**(i) Synchronous TDM bit rate (3 marks):**

$$R_{sync} = n \times r = 10 \times 19{,}200 = 192{,}000 \text{ bps} = 192 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) Statistical TDM bit rate (2 marks):**

$$R_{stat} = \alpha \times n \times r = 0.25 \times 10 \times 19{,}200 = 48{,}000 \text{ bps} = 48 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iii) Percentage saving (2 marks):**

$$\text{Saving} = R_{sync} - R_{stat} = 192{,}000 - 48{,}000 = 144{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

$$\text{Saving \%} = \frac{144{,}000}{192{,}000} \times 100 = 75\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Students often write `n × r` for Synchronous TDM but forget to multiply by `α` for Statistical — losing 2 marks instantly.
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Confusing the **sampling rate** (8 kHz for voice) with the **data rate per channel** (64 kbps). They are NOT the same.
> - **Common Mistake #3**: In T1 calculations, forgetting the **+1 framing bit per frame** → answers come out as **1.536 Mbps** instead of the correct **1.544 Mbps** (a 0.5 mark deduction).
> - **Common Mistake #4**: Not labelling units explicitly (kbps vs Mbps vs bps) — KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — TDM:**

- ✅ **Definition**: TDM shares **one channel in TIME**, not in frequency.
- ✅ **Voice channel rate**: **64 kbps** = 8 kHz sampling × 8 bits PCM.
- ✅ **Frame size**: n × b bits. **Frame duration**: 1 / f_s = **125 μs**.
- ✅ **Synchronous TDM**: fixed slots; **Statistical TDM**: dynamic slots with addressing.
- ✅ **T1 (DS-1)**: 24 channels → **1.544 Mbps** (1.536 + 8 kbps framing).
- ✅ **E1**: 32 time slots (30 voice + 2 signaling) → **2.048 Mbps**.
- ✅ **Statistical gain**: $R_{stat} = \alpha \cdot n \cdot r$; saving factor = $(1 - \alpha)$.
- ✅ **Synchronization** between MUX and DEMUX is **essential** — without it, data lands in wrong channels.
- ✅ **Pulse stuffing** is used in higher-order multiplexers (Plesiochronous Digital Hierarchy — PDH) to synchronize slightly different source clocks.
- ✅ **Guard time / guard bits** are NOT needed in TDM (unlike FDM's guard bands) — a key advantage.
- ✅ **Indian telecom standard** = **E1 (2.048 Mbps)**.
- ✅ **Draw the MUX/DEMUX block diagram** in every TDM answer — it carries **2–3 marks alone**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Computer Communication, Introduction, Types of Computer Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Computer Communication, Introduction, Types of Computer Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Computer Communication?

**Computer Communication** is the disciplined process of transferring data, instructions, and information between two or more computing devices (or between a device and a network element) through a transmission medium governed by a well-defined set of **rules called protocols**. It encompasses the hardware, software, transmission media, and protocol stack required to ensure that a bit generated on one machine is interpreted identically on the destination machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Computer Communication is the exchange of digital information between autonomous computing entities via a shared transmission medium, mediated by layered protocols that guarantee ordered, error-controlled, and semantically meaningful delivery of data."*

### 1.2 What is a Computer Network?

A **Computer Network** is a system of interconnected autonomous computing nodes (hosts, switches, routers, servers) that use a common **Communication Protocol** to share resources, exchange data, and provide distributed services. Autonomy here means no node acts as a master-slave slave of another; every node can independently initiate communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of any Computer Network (Andrew S. Tanenbaum Framework):**
> 1. **Hardware** — Physical devices (NICs, cables, switches, antennas)
> 2. **Software** — Protocol stacks, operating system services, applications
> 3. **Protocols** — The agreed-upon rule sets (TCP, IP, HTTP, FTP, etc.)

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **Postal Service System**:
- You (a **host/source**) want to send a letter to a friend (a **host/destination**).
- The letter is your **data payload**.
- The envelope with the address acts like the **header** in a network packet.
- The post office acts like a **router**, deciding which path the letter should take.
- The mail trucks, planes, and roads are your **transmission media**.
- The rules of how letters are sorted, addressed, and delivered are the **protocols**.

Just as the postal system has sorting offices at different scales (local, regional, national), computer networks exist at different geographical scales — and that brings us to the **Types of Computer Networks**.

### 1.4 Why Study This Topic? (KTU 2024 CO Mapping)

| Course Outcome (CO) | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | Topic Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Understand / Apply | Classify networks by scale and topology |
| **CO3** | Analyze | Differentiate between LAN, MAN, WAN, PAN architectures |
| **CO4** | Evaluate | Justify network selection for engineering scenarios |

### 1.5 Key Terminology Pre-loaded for KTU Answers

- **Node / Host / End System** — Any addressable device on a network
- **Link** — The physical or logical pathway between two nodes
- **Bandwidth** — Maximum data rate a link can carry, measured in **bits per second (bps)**
- **Throughput** — Actual achieved data rate (always ≤ Bandwidth)
- **Latency** — Time delay between packet send and receive
- **Protocol** — A formal set of rules governing communication
- **Topology** — The geometric arrangement of nodes and links
- **OSI / TCP-IP Model** — Layered architectural reference models

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Communication Model (Shannon-Weaver Inspired)

Every computer communication can be decomposed into **5 logical components**:

1. **Source (Tx)** — Generates the data (a computer, smartphone, IoT sensor)
2. **Transmitter** — Converts data into transmittable signals (NIC, modem)
3. **Transmission System / Channel** — Carries the signal (copper, fiber, RF spectrum)
4. **Receiver** — Converts signal back to data (NIC on destination)
5. **Destination (Rx)** — Consumes the data (target host)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The presence of **Noise** is mathematically modeled as an additive disturbance. Claude Shannon's foundational equation for channel capacity is **board-essential** for KTU 2024.

### 2.2 Shannon's Channel Capacity (THE Most Important Formula)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **theoretical maximum** data rate achievable over a noisy channel with **zero error**, assuming optimal coding.

$$
C = B \times \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})
$$

Where:
- $C$ = Channel Capacity (bits/second, **bps**)
- $B$ = Bandwidth of the channel (**Hz**)
- $\text{SNR}$ = Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dimensionless, often expressed as $\dfrac{S}{N}$)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** For all tables below, the vertical pipe `|` symbol has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to maintain valid markdown table rendering. Do not reintroduce the bare pipe inside math expressions.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon Channel Capacity | bits/second |
| 2 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Data Delivered}}{\text{Time Taken}}$ | Actual effective data rate | bps |
| 3 | $T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}}$ | Propagation delay | seconds |
| 4 | $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{\text{Packet Size}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$ | Transmission delay | seconds |
| 5 | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{proc}}$ | End-to-end delay | seconds |
| 6 | $\text{Delay} \times \text{Bandwidth Product} = \text{Pipe Capacity (bits)}$ | Max unacknowledged data in flight | bits |
| 7 | $\text{BER} = \dfrac{\text{Bits in Error}}{\text{Total Bits Received}}$ | Bit Error Rate | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Efficiency} \eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Channel utilization | percent |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Cellular network operators (4G LTE, 5G NR) use Shannon's limit to dimension cell towers and frequency allocations.
- **Optical Fiber Design:** Engineers maximize $B$ (by using WDM — Wavelength Division Multiplexing) and minimize noise to push $C$ closer to terabits per second.
- **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN):** Shannon's bound dictates the **trade-off** between range, data rate, and transmit power in IoT.
- **Data Center Networking:** Delay-bandwidth product dictates TCP window sizing in high-speed links (e.g., 100 Gbps Ethernet).

### 2.5 Logical Step-by-Step Decomposition of a Communication Event

1. **Application Layer** generates a message (e.g., HTTP GET request).
2. The message is **segmented** into smaller units (TCP segments, IP packets, Ethernet frames).
3. Each unit gets a **header** containing addressing and control information.
4. The **Physical Layer** converts frames into electrical/optical/RF signals.
5. Signals traverse the **transmission medium** (copper, fiber, air).
6. The destination **Physical Layer** receives signals and reconstructs bits.
7. Each layer **peels off** its corresponding header in a process called **de-encapsulation**.
8. The application on the receiver obtains the original message **byte-identical** to what was sent.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Types of Computer Networks (The CORE of Module 2)

The classification of networks is based on **geographical scale, ownership, and administrative boundary**. Below is the exhaustive KTU-mandated taxonomy.

| Network Type | Full Form | Typical Range | Example Use Cases | Typical Speed | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PAN** | Personal Area Network | $\leq 10$ meters | Bluetooth headset, smartwatch syncing | $\le 1$ Mbps – $1$ Gbps (USB-C) | Individual |
| **CAN** | Campus/Corporate Area Network | $10^2$ m – few km | University campus, corporate HQ interconnection | $100$ Mbps – $10$ Gbps | Single organization |
| **LAN** | Local Area Network | Room / Building ($\le 1$ km) | Office Ethernet, home Wi-Fi | $100$ Mbps – $10$ Gbps | Single owner |
| **MAN** | Metropolitan Area Network | City-wide ($10$ – $100$ km) | City cable TV, metro-ethernet, municipal Wi-Fi | $1$ Gbps – $100$ Gbps | ISP / Telco |
| **WAN** | Wide Area Network | Country / Global ($> 100$ km) | Internet backbone, leased lines, MPLS | $1$ Mbps – $400$ Gbps | Multiple carriers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** If a question asks *"Arrange PAN < LAN < MAN < WAN in increasing order of geographical area"* — write it **exactly** in this sequence. KTU valuators award 0.5 marks for the correct monotonic ordering.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — End-to-End Latency

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **standard KTU Part B derivation** worth 7 marks. Master it.

**Given:** A packet of size $L$ bits transmitted over a link of bandwidth $R$ bps, propagation speed $s$ m/s, link length $d$ meters, with queuing delay $T_q$ and processing delay $T_{proc}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Four Delay Components**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{proc}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Transmission Delay**

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the time taken for the **entire packet** to be pushed onto the wire by the transmitter. If $L = 1000$ bits and $R = 1$ Mbps, then $T_{\text{trans}} = 10^{-3}$ s = **1 ms**.

**Step 3 — Compute Propagation Delay**

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{s}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the time for **one bit** to physically travel the link. For fiber, $s \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (≈ $\dfrac{2}{3}$ speed of light).

**Step 4 — Add Queuing and Processing Delays**

$$
T_{\text{queue}} = \frac{I \cdot \rho}{1 - \rho} \quad \text{where } \rho = \text{Traffic Intensity} = \frac{L \cdot a}{R}
$$

*Explanation:* $a$ is the **average packet arrival rate**; $I$ is the **average packet length**. As $\rho \to 1$, queuing delay **diverges to infinity** (the link is saturated).

**Step 5 — Final Form**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{s} + \frac{I \cdot \rho}{1 - \rho} + T_{\text{proc}}
$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (Board Pattern)**

> *"A 1500-byte packet travels over a 10 Mbps link spanning 2000 m. Propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Compute total latency assuming zero queuing/processing delay."*

Solution:

$$
L = 1500 \text{ bytes} = 1500 \times 8 = 12000 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
R = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}
$$

$$
d = 2000 \text{ m}, \quad s = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{10^{7}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.2 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{2000}{2 \times 10^8} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.01 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 1.2 + 0.01 = 1.21 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation**

For short-distance LAN links, **transmission delay dominates**. For long-haul satellite links, **propagation delay dominates** (a GEO satellite has a one-way latency of $\approx 250$ ms).

### 3.3 Topology Analysis (Mandatory for KTU)

A **Network Topology** is the geometric layout of the interconnection of nodes. KTU expects 5 types to be memorized.

| Topology | Structure | Failure Tolerance | Cabling Cost | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bus** | Single backbone cable | Single point of failure (the bus) | Low | Legacy Ethernet (10BASE2) |
| **Star** | Central hub/switch | Hub failure = total collapse | Moderate | Modern LANs, office Wi-Fi |
| **Ring** | Nodes in closed loop | Dual-ring gives fault tolerance | Moderate | SONET/SDH, Token Ring |
| **Mesh** | Every node connects to every other | High (multi-path) | High ($n(n-1)/2$ links) | Internet backbone, 5G mesh |
| **Tree (Hierarchical)** | Combination of star and bus | Moderate | Moderate | Enterprise networks, ISP distribution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Topology:** Real-world networks (e.g., your college Wi-Fi) are almost always **hybrid** — a **star-bus** or **star-ring** combination. Always answer with this nuance in KTU 2024 questions.

### 3.4 Protocol Layering — Quick Recap

$$
\text{OSI Model (7 layers) vs TCP/IP Model (4 layers)}
$$

$$
\underbrace{\text{Application, Presentation, Session}}_{\text{OSI: 3 upper}} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \underbrace{\text{Application}}_{\text{TCP/IP}}
$$

$$
\underbrace{\text{Transport}}_{\text{OSI}} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \underbrace{\text{Transport (TCP/UDP)}}_{\text{TCP/IP}}
$$

$$
\underbrace{\text{Network}}_{\text{OSI}} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \underbrace{\text{Internet (IP)}}_{\text{TCP/IP}}
$$

$$
\underbrace{\text{Data Link, Physical}}_{\text{OSI: 2 lower}} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \underbrace{\text{Network Access / Link}}_{\text{TCP/IP}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Network Type Hierarchy (Geographical Scale)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Computer Networks] --> B[Personal Scale]
    A --> C[Local Scale]
    A --> D[Metropolitan Scale]
    A --> E[Global Scale]

    B --> B1[PAN - Personal Area Network]
    C --> C1[CAN - Campus Area Network]
    C --> C2[LAN - Local Area Network]
    D --> D1[MAN - Metropolitan Area Network]
    E --> E1[WAN - Wide Area Network]

    B1 --> S1[Range up to 10 meters]
    C2 --> S2[Range up to 1 km]
    C1 --> S3[Range 100 meters to few km]
    D1 --> S4[Range 10 to 100 km]
    E1 --> S5[Range greater than 100 km]
```

### 4.2 Communication Model Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Src[Source Host] --> Tx[Transmitter NIC Encoder]
    Tx --> Ch[Transmission Channel]
    Ch --> Rx[Receiver NIC Decoder]
    Rx --> Dst[Destination Host]

    Noise[Noise Interference] -.->|Additive| Ch
    Fb[Feedback Channel] -.->|ACK NACK| Src
```

### 4.3 Star vs Mesh vs Bus — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph STAR_TOPOLOGY
        HUB[Central Switch]
        HUB --- N1[Node A]
        HUB --- N2[Node B]
        HUB --- N3[Node C]
        HUB --- N4[Node D]
    end

    subgraph BUS_TOPOLOGY
        BACKBONE[Backbone Cable]
        BACKBONE --- B1[Node A]
        BACKBONE --- B2[Node B]
        BACKBONE --- B3[Node C]
        BACKBONE --- B4[Node D]
    end

    subgraph MESH_TOPOLOGY
        M1[Node A] --- M2[Node B]
        M1 --- M3[Node C]
        M1 --- M4[Node D]
        M2 --- M3
        M2 --- M4
        M3 --- M4
    end
```

### 4.4 Protocol Encapsulation — Layered Data Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Data] -->|TCP adds header| B[TCP Segment]
    B -->|IP adds header| C[IP Packet]
    C -->|Ethernet adds header and trailer| D[Ethernet Frame]
    D -->|Physical encoding| E[Bits on Wire]

    E -->|Received bits| F[Ethernet Frame]
    F -->|Strip Ethernet| G[IP Packet]
    G -->|Strip IP| H[TCP Segment]
    H -->|Strip TCP| I[Application Data]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data moves **down** the stack on the sender (encapsulation) and **up** the stack on the receiver (de-encapsulation). Each layer adds its own header — this is **protocol overhead**.

### 4.5 Delay Components in End-to-End Communication

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sender Application] --> B[Processing Delay at Node]
    B --> C[Queuing Delay at Buffer]
    C --> D[Transmission Delay on Link]
    D --> E[Propagation Delay across Medium]
    E --> F[Receiver Application]
    F --> G[Processing Delay at Receiver]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] (3 Marks)**
**Differentiate between LAN and WAN with at least 4 distinguishing parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | LAN | WAN |
|---|---|---|
| **Geographical Area** | Limited to a building or campus (≤ 1 km) | Spans cities, countries, continents (> 100 km) |
| **Ownership** | Privately owned by single organization | Leased from telecom service providers (ISPs) |
| **Speed** | High (100 Mbps – 10 Gbps) | Relatively lower (1 Mbps – 100 Gbps) |
| **Error Rates** | Low (well-controlled environment) | Higher (longer links, multiple hops) |
| **Security** | Easier to enforce physically | Requires complex cryptographic and firewall policies |
| **Example** | Office Ethernet, home Wi-Fi | Internet backbone, MPLS networks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **MAN** with **WAN**. Remember: MAN serves a **single city**, WAN serves **multiple cities/regions**. KTU marks are deducted if you list MAN as a subset of WAN without clarification.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] (3 Marks)**
**Define the term "Computer Network" and briefly explain the role of a protocol in it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Computer Network** is an interconnection of two or more autonomous computing devices that share resources and exchange data using a common set of communication rules.

A **Protocol** is a formal set of rules, conventions, and data formats that govern how data is transmitted, received, acknowledged, and interpreted between network entities. It performs the following roles:
1. **Syntax** — Defines the structure of messages (e.g., header field positions)
2. **Semantics** — Defines the meaning of each field (e.g., what "Type = 6" means in IPv4)
3. **Timing** — Defines when and how fast messages are sent (e.g., timeout values)
4. **Error Handling** — Defines recovery procedures for lost or corrupted packets
5. **Connection Management** — Establishes, maintains, and terminates communication sessions

Examples: **TCP, UDP, IP, HTTP, FTP, SMTP**.

---

### 📕 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the different types of computer networks (PAN, LAN, MAN, WAN) with diagrams, typical ranges, and applications.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A channel has a bandwidth of **4 kHz** and a Signal-to-Noise Ratio of **30 dB**. Calculate the theoretical maximum channel capacity using Shannon's theorem. If we want to double the capacity, by what factor must the SNR be increased (assuming bandwidth is constant)?

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

**Part (a) — Types of Computer Networks (7 Marks)**

**(i) Personal Area Network (PAN) — 1.5 Marks**
- Range: $\le 10$ meters
- Connects personal devices (laptop, smartphone, smartwatch, wireless earphones)
- Technologies: **Bluetooth, Infrared (IrDA), NFC, USB**
- Example: A smartphone paired with a Bluetooth headset

**(ii) Local Area Network (LAN) — 1.5 Marks**
- Range: Within a single room, building, or campus (up to 1–2 km)
- Privately owned and managed
- High data rates: $100$ Mbps to $10$ Gbps
- Technologies: **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**
- Example: College computer lab network, office Ethernet

**(iii) Metropolitan Area Network (MAN) — 2 Marks**
- Range: Spans a city or metropolitan region (10–100 km)
- Interconnects multiple LANs within a city
- Owned by telecom operators or large organizations
- Technologies: **Metro Ethernet, SONET/SDH, MPLS, Cable TV networks**
- Example: BSNL's BharatNet, municipal Wi-Fi networks

**(iv) Wide Area Network (WAN) — 2 Marks**
- Range: National or global (> 100 km)
- Interconnects networks across continents
- Uses leased lines, satellites, undersea cables
- Lower data rates per user compared to LAN
- Technologies: **Frame Relay, ATM, MPLS, Satellite links, Optical fiber backbones**
- Example: The Internet itself, banking networks connecting ATMs across India

> *(Include any of the Mermaid diagrams from Section 4.1 for full marks — examiners reward diagram presentation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Shannon Capacity Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $B = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000 \text{ Hz}$
- $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 30 \text{ dB}$

**Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to Linear Scale**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{\dfrac{\text{SNR}_{dB}}{10}} = 10^{\dfrac{30}{10}} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

**Step 2: Apply Shannon's Theorem**

$$
C = B \times \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000)
$$

$$
C = 4000 \times \log_2(1001)
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate $\log_2(1001)$**

$$
\log_2(1001) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \dfrac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967
$$

**Step 4: Final Capacity**

$$
C = 4000 \times 9.967 \approx 39868 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 5: Doubling the Capacity**

We need $C' = 2C$ with $B$ constant.

$$
2C = B \times \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}')
$$

$$
2 \times B \log_2(1001) = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}')
$$

$$
\log_2(1 + \text{SNR}') = 2 \log_2(1001) = \log_2(1001^2)
$$

$$
1 + \text{SNR}' = 1001^2 = 1{,}002{,}001
$$

$$
\text{SNR}' \approx 1{,}002{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Factor**

$$
\text{Factor} = \dfrac{\text{SNR}'}{\text{SNR}} = \dfrac{1{,}002{,}000}{1000} = 1002
$$

> **Conclusion:** To double the channel capacity, the SNR must be increased by a factor of **1002** (a non-linear, logarithmic relationship).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Students frequently apply **Nyquist's theorem** (which uses only the number of signal levels, no noise) when the question explicitly mentions SNR. KTU valuators will deduct **2 full marks** if Shannon's formula is not used when SNR is provided.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Bus, Star, Ring, and Mesh** network topologies. Compare them on the basis of cost, fault tolerance, and complexity.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A packet of **2000 bytes** is transmitted over a **1 Mbps** link. The propagation delay is **25 ms**. Calculate: (i) Transmission delay, (ii) Total delay, (iii) Delay-Bandwidth product. Comment on the significance of the Delay-Bandwidth product.

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

**Part (a) — Network Topologies (7 Marks)**

**Bus Topology (1.5 Marks)**
- All devices share a single backbone cable
- Uses terminators at both ends to prevent signal reflection
- Failure of backbone halts entire network
- Used in legacy 10BASE2 Ethernet

**Star Topology (1.5 Marks)**
- All devices connect to a central hub or switch
- Failure of one node does not affect others
- Hub/switch failure = total collapse
- Most common modern LAN topology (Ethernet switches, Wi-Fi APs)

**Ring Topology (1.5 Marks)**
- Each device connects to exactly two neighbors forming a closed loop
- Data travels in one direction (single ring) or both (dual ring)
- Token passing prevents collisions
- Used in SONET/SDH telecom rings

**Mesh Topology (2.5 Marks)**
- Every device connects to every other (full mesh) or selectively (partial mesh)
- Highly fault-tolerant (multiple paths)
- Number of links in full mesh: $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$
- Used in internet backbone, military networks, 5G mmWave mesh

**Comparison Table (1 Mark)**

| Parameter | Bus | Star | Ring | Mesh |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low | Moderate | Moderate–High | Very High |
| **Complexity** | Low | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Good | Moderate | Limited (cost) |

*(Use the Mermaid topology diagrams from Section 4.3 in your answer sheet for 1 extra diagram mark.)*

---

**Part (b) — Delay Calculations (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 2000$ bytes $= 2000 \times 8 = 16000$ bits
- $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps
- $T_{\text{prop}} = 25$ ms $= 25 \times 10^{-3}$ s

**(i) Transmission Delay (2 Marks)**

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{16000}{10^6} = 0.016 \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Total Delay (2 Marks)** *(Assume $T_q = T_{\text{proc}} = 0$)*

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{prop}} = 16 + 25 = 41 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Delay-Bandwidth Product (3 Marks)**

$$
\text{DBP} = T_{\text{prop}} \times R = 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 = 25000 \text{ bits}
$$

Equivalently in bytes:

$$
\text{DBP} = \frac{25000}{8} = 3125 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Significance of Delay-Bandwidth Product:**

The Delay-Bandwidth product represents the **maximum amount of data that can be "in flight"** in the network at any given instant — i.e., the number of bits the sender can transmit before receiving the first acknowledgment.

- It determines the **optimal TCP window size** for fully utilizing the link.
- If window size $< $ DBP, the sender will be idle waiting for ACKs, reducing throughput.
- It also determines the buffer size required at routers to prevent packet loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Converting $L$ in bytes to bits **incorrectly** (forgetting the $\times 8$ factor). KTU answer scripts are routinely penalized **1 mark** for this conversion error. Always show the conversion explicitly.

---

### ⚡ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MUST MEMORIZE**

- ✅ **5 Network Types in Increasing Range:** PAN $\to$ LAN $\to$ CAN $\to$ MAN $\to$ WAN
- ✅ **PAN ≈ 10 m**, **LAN ≈ 1 km**, **MAN ≈ 100 km**, **WAN > 100 km**
- ✅ **Shannon's Capacity Formula:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$
- ✅ **SNR (dB) to Linear:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{dB}/10}$
- ✅ **Delay Components:** $T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{proc}}$
- ✅ **Transmission Delay:** $T_{\text{trans}} = L/R$
- ✅ **Propagation Delay:** $T_{\text{prop}} = d/s$
- ✅ **Delay-Bandwidth Product:** Governs TCP window sizing
- ✅ **Topologies:** Bus, Star, Ring, Tree, Mesh, Hybrid
- ✅ **Full Mesh Links:** $n(n-1)/2$
- ✅ **Protocols are mandatory** for any meaningful communication
- ✅ **OSI = 7 layers**, **TCP/IP = 4 layers**
- ✅ **Encapsulation** (down on sender) vs **De-encapsulation** (up on receiver)
- ✅ **Hybrid topology** is the realistic answer for modern enterprise networks
- ✅ For **short-distance LANs**, transmission delay dominates; for **long-haul WANs**, propagation delay dominates

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Software, Protocol Hierarchies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Software, Protocol Hierarchies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Network Software — Formal Definition

**Network Software** refers to the collection of programs, protocols, algorithms, and architectural conventions that govern how data is transmitted, received, routed, and interpreted across a communication network. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Computer Communication & Network Security*, network software is structurally decomposed into a **Protocol Hierarchy** — a layered stack where each layer performs a well-defined set of functions and offers explicit services to the layer immediately above it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Protocol Hierarchy (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A *protocol hierarchy* is a structured set of cooperating protocols organized into vertical layers, where each layer solves a specific class of communication problems, and where the *n*-th layer on one machine communicates virtually with the *n*-th layer on another machine using a precisely defined *peer-to-peer protocol*.

The most widely cited hierarchies in the KTU syllabus are:
1. **The OSI Reference Model** — 7 layers, formulated by ISO.
2. **The TCP/IP Reference Model** — 4 (or 5) layers, de-facto Internet standard.
3. **Hybrid / Five-Layer Model** — used pedagogically in Tanenbaum and KTU textbooks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Postal Service

Imagine you are sending a registered parcel from **Kerala to a friend in Germany**.

| Postal Element | Network Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The parcel contents (the actual data) | **Payload / SDU (Service Data Unit)** |
| The envelope with sender/receiver addresses | **Header (with source/destination addresses)** |
| The clerk who accepts the parcel | **Application Layer Protocol (HTTP, SMTP)** |
| The sorting office routing decisions | **Network Layer (IP routing)** |
| The delivery truck / flight | **Data Link / Physical Layer** |
| The set of rules governing "how to send" | **The Protocol** |
| Stacking (parcel → envelope → bag → container) | **Layered Encapsulation** |

Just as a parcel passes through **multiple hierarchical handling stages**, a network message passes through a **stack of protocols**, each adding/removing its own header/footer (encapsulation/decapsulation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** A *layer* is a *black box* to the layer above it. The lower layer offers a **service**; the upper layer uses that service via a well-defined **Service Access Point (SAP)** and **Service Primitive**.

## 1.3 Why Layers? — The Design Philosophy

Networks are extraordinarily complex systems. Early monolithic designs (single "megafunction") were impossible to maintain, debug, or evolve. Layering provides:

- **Modularity** — Each layer can be designed/replaced independently.
- **Abstraction** — Upper layers need not know lower-layer mechanics.
- **Reusability** — A single transport protocol serves many applications.
- **Interoperability** — Standardized interfaces allow heterogeneous vendors to interoperate.
- **Simplified Debugging** — Faults localize to a specific layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:**
> *"Each layer passes data and control information to the layer immediately below it, until the lowest layer is reached. Between peer layers (across machines), virtual communication occurs; between adjacent layers (within a machine), actual communication occurs via SAPs."*

## 1.4 Core Terminology Primer

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Protocol** | A set of rules governing data exchange between two peer entities | Fundamental to the chapter |
| **Service** | A set of operations a layer provides to the layer above | Distinguishes from protocol |
| **SAP (Service Access Point)** | The logical interface through which the *N+1* layer accesses the *N* service | ESE favorite |
| **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** | The unit of data exchanged between peer entities at layer *N* | Required for derivations |
| **SDU (Service Data Unit)** | The data passed from layer *N+1* to layer *N* for transmission | Required for derivations |
| **ICI (Interface Control Information)** | Control info passed across the SAP | Optional advanced concept |
| **Encapsulation** | Process of appending a header (and possibly trailer) at each layer | ESE mark-worthy |
| **Peer-to-Peer Communication** | Virtual, conceptual communication between layer *N* on two hosts | Conceptual exam question |

## 1.5 The Layer N → Layer N-1 Relationship

When layer *N+1* requests a service from layer *N*:

$$ \text{Layer } (N+1) \xrightarrow{\text{Service Request via SAP}} \text{Layer } N \xrightarrow{\text{Peer-to-Peer Protocol}} \text{Layer } N \text{ (remote)} $$

The data flow between adjacent layers inside a single host is **real** (actual function calls, system calls, OS buffers). The flow between *N* layers on two different hosts is **virtual** — implemented by lower layers that actually carry the bits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This distinction between *virtual* (peer) and *real* (adjacent) communication is the single most important conceptual point in Module 2. Examiners regularly award full marks to answers that crisply explain this duality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
