# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Issues for the Layers

Forouzan, Tanenbaum, and the KTU prescribed textbook *Data Communications and Networking* ( Behrouz A. Forouzan) identify the following recurring design problems that every layer must address:

### 2.1.1 Addressing
Every layer needs a mechanism to identify the sender and receiver.
- **Application Layer** — User names, URLs, email addresses.
- **Transport Layer** — Port numbers (16-bit, range 0–65535).
- **Network Layer** — IP addresses (32-bit IPv4, 128-bit IPv6).
- **Data Link Layer** — MAC addresses (48-bit).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** As we descend the protocol stack, addresses become *more localized* and *more hardware-bound*.

### 2.1.2 Error Detection and Correction
Errors occur due to noise, attenuation, and interference. Layers must:
- **Detect** errors (parity bits, checksums, CRC).
- **Correct** errors (Hamming codes, FEC, ARQ protocols).

**Bit Error Rate (BER)** is the canonical metric:

$$ \text{BER} = \frac{\text{Number of Bit Errors}}{\text{Total Number of Bits Transmitted}} $$

### 2.1.3 Flow Control
The receiver must not be overwhelmed by a faster sender. Two classic strategies:
- **Stop-and-Wait** — Sender transmits one frame, waits for ACK.
- **Sliding Window** — Sender can transmit up to *W* unacknowledged frames.

The **optimal window size** for Stop-and-Wait in a *bandwidth-delay product* scenario is bounded by:

$$ W_{\min} \geq 2a + 1, \quad \text{where } a = \frac{\text{Propagation Time}}{\text{Transmission Time}} $$

### 2.1.4 Multiplexing / Demultiplexing
Multiple upper-layer conversations must share a single lower-layer connection.
- **Upward Multiplexing** (many-to-one) — e.g., multiple HTTP sessions over one TCP connection (HTTP/2, HTTP/3).
- **Downward Multiplexing** (one-to-many) — e.g., one user over multiple underlying links.

### 2.1.5 Routing
When multiple paths exist, the network layer must decide:
- **Next-hop selection** (per-packet or per-flow).
- Algorithms: **Dijkstra (Link State)**, **Bellman-Ford (Distance Vector)**.

### 2.1.6 Connection Management
- **Connection-Oriented Service** — Establish → Data Transfer → Terminate (like a phone call).
- **Connectionless Service** — Each SDU is independent (like posting a letter).

### 2.1.7 Other Issues
- **Reliability / Sequencing** — Reassemble out-of-order PDUs.
- **Congestion Control** — Prevent network meltdown.
- **Security** — Confidentiality, integrity, authentication (covered in Module 4+).
- **Quality of Service (QoS)** — Bandwidth, delay, jitter guarantees.

## 2.2 Connection-Oriented vs. Connectionless Services

| Property | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|---|---|---|
| **Analogy** | Telephone call | Postal letter |
| **Phases** | Setup → Transfer → Teardown | Single datagram send |
| **State** | Maintained at endpoints | Stateless |
| **Ordering** | Guaranteed | Not guaranteed |
| **Example Protocols** | TCP, ATM, Frame Relay | UDP, IP, SMTP (mail transfer) |
| **Reliability** | Built-in ACK/retransmission | No guarantees |
| **Overhead** | High (headers, handshakes) | Low |
| **Use Case** | File transfer, web (HTTPS) | DNS, video streaming, VoIP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Concept Distinction:**
> - *Service* can be connection-oriented (e.g., virtual circuit) **or** connectionless (e.g., datagram) — independent of the *protocol* used to implement it.
> - The **OSI model** explicitly distinguishes between *service definition* (what the layer does) and *protocol specification* (how peer entities talk).

## 2.3 Service Primitives

A **service primitive** is the formal operation through which a layer *N+1* entity accesses the service of layer *N*. There are four canonical types:

| Primitive Type | Meaning | Example (TCP-like) |
|---|---|---|
| `LISTEN` | Block until peer attempts connection | Server waiting |
| `CONNECT` | Establish connection | Client call |
| `SEND` | Transmit data | `write()` |
| `RECEIVE` | Block until data arrives | `read()` |
| `DISCONNECT` | Terminate connection | `close()` |

These are the operations invoked at the **SAP**. They are *not* messages; they may map to system calls, library calls, or internal API calls.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *Service Primitives* (operations) with *PDUs* (data units). Primitives cause PDU transmission, but they are not the PDUs themselves.

## 2.4 Relationship of Services to Protocols — The Critical Distinction

This is the single most exam-relevant conceptual point in Module 2:

- **Service** = *what* the layer provides (semantics, semantics of operation).
- **Protocol** = *how* the peer entities implement that service (syntax, rules, formats).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule:** Services are *related to the layers below*; protocols are *related to the peer entities on another machine*. The two are orthogonal — the same service can be implemented by different protocols, and the same protocol can provide different services.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bit Error Rate | $\text{BER} = \frac{E}{B}$ | Dimensionless; $0 \leq \text{BER} \leq 1$ |
| 2 | Propagation Time | $T_p = \frac{d}{v}$ | $d$ in meters, $v$ in m/s, $T_p$ in seconds |
| 3 | Transmission Time | $T_t = \frac{L}{R}$ | $L$ in bits, $R$ in bps |
| 4 | Stop-and-Wait Window | $W \geq 2a + 1$ | $a = T_p / T_t$ (dimensionless) |
| 5 | Sliding Window Max Throughput | $\eta = \frac{W}{1 + 2a}$ | $0 < \eta \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = R \times T_p$ | bits |
| 7 | CRC Generator Degree | $G(x)$ of degree *n* ⇒ *n* CRC bits | $n \geq 1$ |
| 8 | Hamming Distance | $d(x, y) = \sum_{i} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | Integer $\geq 0$ |
| 9 | Utilization (Stop-and-Wait) | $U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | $0 < U \leq 1$ |
| 10 | Port Number Range | $0 \leq p \leq 65535$ | 16 bits |

## 2.6 Encapsulation and PDU Naming by Layer

Each layer renames the data unit as it passes through:

| Layer | PDU Name | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Application | Message / Data | HTTP request |
| Transport | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) | TCP segment |
| Network | Packet / Datagram | IP packet |
| Data Link | Frame | Ethernet frame |
| Physical | Bits / Signal | Manchester-encoded bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Expectation:** Examiners frequently expect a labeled diagram of the *encapsulation process*, showing how each layer adds its own header. This is a 5-to-7 mark item in Part B questions.

## 2.7 The OSI Reference Model — Detailed Layer Analysis

| # | Layer | Function | PDU | Example Protocols |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **Application** | Network process to user | Data | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS |
| 6 | **Presentation** | Data representation, encryption, compression | Data | SSL/TLS, JPEG, ASCII |
| 5 | **Session** | Inter-host communication, dialog control | Data | NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP |
| 4 | **Transport** | End-to-end connection, reliability, flow control | Segment/Datagram | TCP, UDP, SCTP |
| 3 | **Network** | Path determination, logical addressing | Packet | IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP |
| 2 | **Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Frame | Ethernet, PPP, HDLC |
| 1 | **Physical** | Bit transmission over medium | Bits | USB, Bluetooth, 802.11 PHY |

### 2.7.1 OSI Critique (Forouzan, Tanenbaum)
- **Why OSI failed in practice:** Bad timing, bad implementations, bad politics.
- TCP/IP was already deployed when OSI standards were finalized.
- OSI protocols were overly complex; the 7-layer split is sometimes overengineered for real-world use.

## 2.8 The TCP/IP Reference Model — Detailed Layer Analysis

| # | Layer | Function | PDU | Example Protocols |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | **Application** | Combines OSI L5–L7 functionality | Message | HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP |
| 3 | **Transport** | Host-to-host delivery | Segment/Datagram | TCP, UDP |
| 2 | **Internet** | Routing, logical addressing | Datagram | IP, ICMP, ARP, RARP |
| 1 | **Network Access / Link** | Combines OSI L1–L2 functionality | Frame / Bits | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, ATM, Frame Relay |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The TCP/IP model does **not** distinguish between *physical* and *data link* layers, nor between *session* and *presentation* layers. This is a major difference from OSI and is a frequent 3-mark short-answer question.

## 2.9 Five-Layer Hybrid Model (Pedagogical)

Often used in Forouzan's textbook and recommended for KTU Module 2:

| Layer | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Application | User-applications & high-level protocols |
| Transport | End-to-end channel |
| Network | Routing & logical addressing |
| Data Link | Framing & link access |
| Physical | Bit transmission |

This model offers a clean abstraction while preserving OSI's granularity.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stop-and-Wait Protocol Utilization

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Given a link of length $d$ meters with propagation speed $v$ m/s, frame length $L$ bits, and link bandwidth $R$ bps, derive the maximum channel utilization $U$ for a Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol. Assume the ACK transmission time and ACK propagation are negligible.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the transmission time for one frame.**

The time required to push all $L$ bits of one frame onto the link is:

$$ T_t = \frac{L}{R} $$

where $L$ is in bits and $R$ is in bits per second, giving $T_t$ in seconds.

**Step 2 — Define the propagation time.**

The time for the leading bit of the frame to travel from sender to receiver:

$$ T_p = \frac{d}{v} $$

where $d$ is the link length in meters and $v$ is the signal propagation speed (typically $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in copper, $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in vacuum).

**Step 3 — Define the parameter $a$.**

In networking literature, $a$ is the ratio of propagation to transmission time:

$$ a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L} $$

This dimensionless quantity is the canonical "link slowness" measure.

**Step 4 — Total cycle time for Stop-and-Wait.**

The sender transmits the frame ($T_t$), the last bit propagates ($T_p$), the receiver processes and sends back an instantaneous ACK (assumed negligible delay), and the ACK propagates back ($T_p$). Total time per successful cycle:

$$ T_{\text{cycle}} = T_t + T_p + T_{\text{ACK\_prop}} + T_{\text{ACK\_trans}} $$

Under the simplifying assumption that ACK time is negligible:

$$ T_{\text{cycle}} = T_t + 2T_p $$

**Step 5 — Utilization calculation.**

Utilization $U$ is the fraction of the cycle during which the sender is actually transmitting useful bits:

$$ U = \frac{T_t}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p} $$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of $a$.**

Divide numerator and denominator by $T_t$:

$$ U = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \cdot \frac{T_p}{T_t}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} $$

**Step 7 — Boundary conditions.**

- When $a \to 0$ (very high-bandwidth, very short link), $U \to 1$ — perfect utilization.
- When $a \to \infty$ (long link, low bandwidth), $U \to 0$ — the channel is essentially idle.
- For typical Ethernet LAN: $a \ll 1$, so $U$ is close to 1.
- For satellite link ($T_p \approx 270$ ms) with $T_t = 1$ ms, $a = 270$, giving $U \approx 0.0018$ — disastrous!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Stop-and-Wait is only viable when $a \ll 1$. For $a \geq 1$, the sliding window protocol is mandatory. This is the fundamental justification for using **sliding window** in TCP and HDLC.

## 3.2 Derivation — Sliding Window Maximum Throughput

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A sliding window protocol allows up to $W$ outstanding unacknowledged frames. Derive the throughput efficiency.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Window filling phase.**

The sender can transmit $W$ frames back-to-back before receiving the first ACK. The time to send $W$ frames is:

$$ T_{\text{window\_fill}} = W \cdot T_t $$

**Step 2 — Round trip wait.**

After sending the $W$-th frame, the sender must wait for the ACK of the first frame. The first ACK arrives after $2T_p$ from the moment the first frame finished transmitting. So total time for the first cycle:

$$ T_{\text{cycle}} = W \cdot T_t + 2T_p $$

**Step 3 — Throughput (efficiency).**

$$ \eta = \frac{W \cdot T_t}{W \cdot T_t + 2T_p} $$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of $a$.**

$$ \eta = \frac{W}{W + 2a} $$

**Step 5 — Special case $W = 1$ recovers Stop-and-Wait.**

$$ \eta \vert_{W=1} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} \quad \checkmark $$

**Step 6 — Asymptotic case $W \geq 2a + 1$.**

If $W \geq 2a + 1$, the entire pipe is always full, and $\eta \to 1$. This is the **bandwidth-delay product** condition:

$$ W_{\min} = 2a + 1 = \left\lceil 1 + 2 \cdot \frac{R \cdot d}{v \cdot L} \right\rceil $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For a 1 Gbps link spanning 2000 km with 1000-bit frames:
> $a = \frac{10^{9} \cdot 2 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8} \cdot 1000} = 10$. So $W_{\min} = 21$. This is why the default TCP receive window scales with the BDP!

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Layer Decision Flow

When designing or analyzing a layer, walk through this matrix:

| Step | Question | Layer-Specific Concern | Tool / Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **What data?** | Identify SDU handed from above | SDU length, format |
| 2 | **To whom?** | Identify peer entity | Addressing (port / IP / MAC) |
| 3 | **How addressed?** | Choose SAP / addressing scheme | Naming conventions |
| 4 | **Reliable?** | Connection state | ACK, SEQ, retransmission |
| 5 | **Ordered?** | Sequencing | Sequence numbers |
| 6 | **How fast?** | Flow control | Sliding window |
| 7 | **Errors?** | Error detection/correction | CRC, parity, Hamming |
| 8 | **Route?** | Path determination | Routing tables (Network L) |
| 9 | **Multiplex?** | Sharing the link | Port numbers, VPI/VCI |
| 10 | **Format?** | Framing | Header/trailer structure |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Propagation, Transmission, and Utilization

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for KTU Numericals (3–7 marks):**
>
> A satellite link has $R = 1$ Mbps, $L = 1000$ bits, and one-way propagation delay $T_p = 270$ ms. Compute: (a) the parameter $a$, (b) utilization for Stop-and-Wait, and (c) minimum window size for full utilization.

**Solution:**

(a) Transmission time:
$$ T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10^{6}} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms} $$

$$ a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{270}{1} = 270 $$

(b) Stop-and-Wait utilization:
$$ U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 540} = \frac{1}{541} \approx 0.00185 $$

This means **less than 0.2% efficiency** — the satellite link is wasted.

(c) Minimum window size:
$$ W_{\min} = 2a + 1 = 541 $$

So the sender must be allowed at least 541 outstanding frames to keep the link fully busy.

## 3.5 Conceptual Proof — Why Layering Works

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Layering Invariant):**
> Given a strictly layered protocol stack, the *N*-th layer on machine A communicates with the *N*-th layer on machine B using exactly one protocol at a time, and the headers added by layers 1 through (*N*−1) are *transparent* to the *N*-th layer.
>
> **Proof Sketch:** By construction, layer *N* treats the data handed down as an opaque blob. The lower layers add their own headers, but these are stripped off as the data ascends on the receiving side. By induction, layer *N* sees only what layer *N+1* sent, plus its own peer-protocol control fields. ∎

This invariant is the foundation of modular networking. It is the reason a web browser does not need to know whether the underlying link is fiber, copper, or radio.

## 4.6 Connection-Oriented vs. Connectionless Service State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Setup : Connection Oriented<br/>CONNECT primitive
    Idle --> Send : Connectionless<br/>SEND primitive direct
    Setup --> DataTransfer : Connection Established
    DataTransfer --> DataTransfer : Data exchange
    DataTransfer --> Teardown : DISCONNECT primitive
    Teardown --> Idle : Connection Released

    Send --> [*] : Datagram sent no state retained

    note right of Setup : Three way handshake<br/>typical in TCP
    note right of Send : Single datagram<br/>typical in UDP IP
```
