Students may only begin shouting exactly when the clock ticks. If two shout in the same tick, a collision occurs, and both wait a random number of *whole ticks* before trying again. This discrete synchronization reduces collisions and **doubles the throughput** compared to Pure ALOHA. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize:** > - **Pure ALOHA Maximum Throughput:** **18.4%** (i.e., $\frac{1}{2e}$) > - **Slotted ALOHA Maximum Throughput:** **36.8%** (i.e., $\frac{1}{e}$) > - **Number *e* (Euler's constant):** $\approx$ **2.71828** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Selective Repeat ARQ — Operational Breakdown 1. **Window Setup:** Both sender and receiver maintain a window of size *N*. The sender can transmit up to *N* unacknowledged frames. The receiver can accept up to *N* out-of-order frames and buffer them. 2. **Sequential Transmission with Sequence Numbers:** Every frame is tagged with a sequence number from 0 to $2^k - 1$, where $k$ is the number of bits in the sequence number field. 3. **Selective Retransmission:** On receiving a NAK (or after a timeout) for a *specific* sequence number, **only that frame** is retransmitted, not the entire window. 4. **Receiver Buffering:** The receiver stores correctly received frames (even out-of-order) in a buffer. When the missing frame finally arrives, all consecutive frames are flushed to the upper layer in order. 5. **Acknowledgment Mechanism:** Receiver sends a cumulative or individual ACK for the highest in-order frame received, plus selective NAKs for gaps in the sequence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Window Size Constraint for Selective Repeat:** > To avoid ambiguity between new frames and retransmitted old frames, the **sender's window size** + **receiver's window size** $\leq$ **$2^k$**. > The standard KTU constraint used is: **$N_{\text{send}} = N_{\text{receive}} \leq 2^{k-1}$**. ## 2.2 Pure ALOHA — Operational Breakdown 1. Any station with a ready frame transmits it **immediately** on the shared channel. 2. The channel is shared, so if two stations transmit overlapping frames, a **collision** destroys both frames. 3. The sender detects collision (or waits for an ACK timeout) and waits a **random back-off time** before retransmitting. 4. The vulnerable period (collision window) is **$2 \times T_{\text{frame}}$**, where $T_{\text{frame}}$ is the time to transmit one frame. ## 2.3 Slotted ALOHA — Operational Breakdown 1. Time is divided into discrete **slots**, each of duration equal to one frame transmission time $T_{\text{frame}}$. 2. A station may transmit **only at the beginning of a slot** — it must wait for the next slot boundary. 3. A collision can only occur if two (or more) stations pick the **same slot** to start transmitting. 4. The vulnerable period is reduced to **$1 \times T_{\text{frame}}$** (just the slot itself). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Notes** | |-------------|-------------|------------------------|-----------| | Pure ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $S$ = throughput (frames per frame-time), $G$ = mean offered load | S is dimensionless, $0 \le S \le 1$ | | Pure ALOHA Max Throughput | $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | Occurs at $G = 0.5$ | **18.4%** channel utilization | | Slotted ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $S$ = throughput, $G$ = offered load per slot | Discrete time version | | Slotted ALOHA Max Throughput | $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | Occurs at $G = 1.0$ | **36.8%** channel utilization | | Throughput-Load Relation | $S = G \cdot P_0$ | $P_0$ = probability of zero other transmissions in vulnerable period | Derived from Poisson process | | Selective Repeat Window Limit | $N \le 2^{k-1}$ | $N$ = window size, $k$ = sequence number bits | Avoids sequence number ambiguity | | Selective Repeat Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{W}{W + 2a}$ | $W$ = window size, $a = \frac{T_p}{T_f}$ | $T_p$ = propagation, $T_f$ = frame time | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > - **ALOHA** is the conceptual ancestor of **Ethernet's CSMA/CD** and the basis for **satellite communication** (e.g., VSAT networks), **RFID** anti-collision protocols, and the random-access channel (**RACH**) in **4G/5G LTE and 5G NR**. > - **Selective Repeat ARQ** is used in modern high-speed networks (e.g., **TCP selective acknowledgment** — SACK option, **HDLC**, **PPP**) because it provides the highest throughput among all sliding window protocols. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Pure ALOHA Throughput **Step 1 — Model the arrival of frames as a Poisson Process.** Assume the infinite number of stations collectively generate new frames according to a Poisson distribution with mean rate $\lambda$ frames per frame-time $T_f$. Let $G$ be the **mean offered load** (including both new and retransmitted frames) per frame-time. **Step 2 — Probability of zero arrivals in a given frame-time.** For a Poisson process with mean $G$, the probability of $k$ arrivals in one frame-time is: $$ P(k) = \frac{G^k \cdot e^{-G}}{k!} $$ For a successful transmission, **no other station should transmit** during the **vulnerable period** of $2 T_f$ (one frame-time before, and one frame-time after, the start of the transmission). **Step 3 — Mean number of frame arrivals in vulnerable period.** The mean number of transmission attempts in a period of $2 T_f$ is $2G$ (since $G$ is the rate per $T_f$). **Step 4 — Probability of successful transmission.** The probability that zero other frames are generated in the vulnerable period is: $$ P_0 = \frac{(2G)^0 \cdot e^{-2G}}{0!} = e^{-2G} $$ **Step 5 — Define throughput.** Throughput $S$ is the product of the offered load $G$ and the probability of a successful (collision-free) transmission: $$ S = G \cdot P_0 $$ **Step 6 — Substitute to get Pure ALOHA throughput equation:** $$ \boxed{S = G \cdot e^{-2G}} $$ **Step 7 — Find the maximum throughput by calculus.** Differentiate $S$ with respect to $G$ and set to zero: $$ \frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0 $$ Since $e^{-2G} \neq 0$, we require: $$ 1 - 2G = 0 \implies G = 0.5 $$ **Step 8 — Evaluate $S$ at $G = 0.5$:** $$ S_{\max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-2(0.5)} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{0.5}{e} = \frac{1}{2e} $$ $$ S_{\max} \approx \frac{1}{2 \times 2.71828} \approx 0.1839 \approx \mathbf{18.4\%} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Slotted ALOHA Throughput **Step 1 — Discrete time axis.** Time is divided into slots of length $T_f$. Each station can begin transmission only at slot boundaries. The **vulnerable period** shrinks to just **one slot** ($T_f$). **Step 2 — Mean arrivals in vulnerable period.** With offered load $G$ per slot, the mean number of arrivals in one slot is $G$. **Step 3 — Probability of no collision in a slot:** $$ P_0 = \frac{G^0 \cdot e^{-G}}{0!} = e^{-G} $$ **Step 4 — Throughput equation:** $$ \boxed{S = G \cdot e^{-G}} $$ **Step 5 — Maximize via differentiation:** $$ \frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 $$ Solving gives: $$ G = 1.0 $$ **Step 6 — Evaluate $S_{\max}$ at $G = 1$:** $$ S_{\max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679 \approx \mathbf{36.8\%} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Comparative Insight:** > $\frac{S_{\max}^{\text{Slotted}}}{S_{\max}^{\text{Pure}}} = \frac{1/e}{1/(2e)} = 2$. > Slotted ALOHA achieves **exactly twice** the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA — a classic KTU board exam comparison. ## 3.3 Derivation of Selective Repeat Window Constraint **Step 1 — Sequence number space.** With $k$-bit sequence numbers, the range of sequence numbers is $0, 1, 2, \dots, 2^k - 1$, giving a total of $2^k$ distinct values. **Step 2 — Consider an ambiguity scenario.** Suppose the receiver's window size is $N_r$. The receiver expects sequence numbers $\{r, r+1, \dots, r+N_r-1\} \pmod{2^k}$. If the sender's window size $N_s$ is too large, it is possible for the sender to retransmit frame $r$ (old) at the same time as it transmits a new frame whose sequence number also wraps around to $r$. **Step 3 — Ambiguity-free condition.** To prevent the receiver from confusing an old retransmission with a new frame, the union of "in-flight" frames (sender window + receiver window) must not exceed the total sequence space: $$ N_s + N_r \le 2^k $$ **Step 4 — Symmetric case.** For the standard case where sender and receiver window sizes are equal ($N_s = N_r = N$): $$ 2N \le 2^k \implies \boxed{N \le 2^{k-1}} $$ ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A Slotted ALOHA channel has a total offered load $G = 0.8$ frames per slot. Calculate the throughput and the probability that exactly 2 frames collide in a slot. **Solution — Throughput:** $$ S = G \cdot e^{-G} = 0.8 \cdot e^{-0.8} = 0.8 \cdot 0.4493 \approx 0.3595 $$ So the throughput is approximately **0.3595 frames per slot** (≈ 35.95% utilization). **Solution — Probability of exactly 2 frames in a slot (Poisson):** $$ P(k=2) = \frac{G^2 \cdot e^{-G}}{2!} = \frac{0.8^2 \cdot e^{-0.8}}{2} = \frac{0.64 \cdot 0.4493}{2} \approx 0.1438 $$ So the probability is **≈ 0.1438** (or 14.38%). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Selective Repeat ARQ — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Upper Layer Application Data"] --> B["Sender Window Manager
Window Size N"] B --> C["Frame Sequencer
Assigns Seq Num 0 to 2k-1"] C --> D["Channel Encoder CRC"] D --> E["Physical Channel Transmitter"] E --> F["Shared Transmission Medium"] F --> G["Receiver Physical Layer"] G --> H["Channel Decoder CRC Check"] H --> I{"Frame Valid?"} I -- No --> J["Send NAK
Discard or Buffer"] I -- Yes --> K["Receiver Window Manager
Buffer Out of Order Frames"] K --> L{"Frame In Order?"} L -- Yes --> M["Deliver to Upper Layer
Send ACK"] L -- No --> N["Store in Reorder Buffer
Wait for Missing Frame"] N --> O{"Missing Frame
Arrived?"} O -- Yes --> M O -- No --> P["Wait Timer Active"] P --> O J --> Q["Sender Retransmit Only
Specific NAK Frame"] M --> R["Sender Advances Window
Removes ACKed Frame"] Q --> C R --> C ``` ## 4.2 Pure ALOHA vs Slotted ALOHA — Channel Access Timeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PureALOHA["Pure ALOHA - Continuous Time Access"] P1["Station A transmits frame
at time t1"] P2["Station B transmits frame
at time t1 plus delta
COLLISION occurs"] P3["Both stations back off
random wait time"] P4["Retransmit after random delay"] end subgraph SlottedALOHA["Slotted ALOHA - Discrete Time Access"] S1["Clock defines slot boundaries
Slot 1 begins"] S2["Station A transmits
at slot start"] S3["Station B must wait
until next slot"] S4["Slot 2: Station B transmits
No collision if alone"] end ``` ## 4.3 Throughput vs Load Curve (Conceptual Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Input G Mean Offered Load"] --> B{"Channel Type"} B -- Pure ALOHA --> C["Compute S = G times e to power of -2G"] B -- Slotted ALOHA --> D["Compute S = G times e to power of -G"] C --> E["Peak at G equals 0.5
S equals 0.184"] D --> F["Peak at G equals 1.0
S equals 0.368"] E --> G["Output: 18.4 percent maximum utilization"] F --> H["Output: 36.8 percent maximum utilization"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define Pure ALOHA and write its throughput expression. State the maximum throughput value.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Pure ALOHA** is a random-access multiple access protocol developed at the University of Hawaii, where any station can transmit a frame at any instant on a shared channel without coordinating with others. If two or more frames overlap in time, a collision occurs and all colliding frames must be retransmitted after a random back-off delay. The throughput equation is: $$ S = G \cdot e^{-2G} $$ The **maximum throughput** occurs at $G = 0.5$ and is: $$ S_{\max} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184 \text{ or } 18.4\% $$ ### **Q2. Differentiate between Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | **Parameter** | **Pure ALOHA** | **Slotted ALOHA** | |---------------|----------------|-------------------| | Time | Continuous | Discrete slots of size $T_f$ | | Transmission rule | Any time | Only at slot start | | Vulnerable period | $2 T_f$ | $T_f$ | | Throughput equation | $S = G e^{-2G}$ | $S = G e^{-G}$ | | Maximum throughput | $\frac{1}{2e} \approx 18.4\%$ | $\frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ | | Synchronization needed | No | Yes (global clock) | | Complexity | Lower | Higher | ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the Selective Repeat ARQ protocol in detail with a suitable diagram. Discuss the window size constraints. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** Selective Repeat ARQ is a sliding window error control protocol in the Data Link Layer where the sender transmits multiple frames within a window of size $N$, and the receiver acknowledges each frame individually. Only the specific frame(s) that are negatively acknowledged (NAK'd) or that time out are retransmitted — not the entire window. **Working Mechanism:** 1. Both sender and receiver maintain a **window of size N** (e.g., N = 4). 2. Sender transmits all N frames with sequence numbers (e.g., 0, 1, 2, 3). 3. Suppose frame 2 is lost or corrupted. The receiver buffers frames 3, 4, 5 (if they arrive) and sends **NAK for frame 2** along with ACKs for 0, 1, 3. 4. The sender retransmits **only frame 2** (selective). 5. When frame 2 arrives, the receiver delivers frames 2, 3, 4, 5 in correct order to the upper layer. 6. The sender then advances its window. **Window Size Constraint:** With $k$-bit sequence numbers, the total sequence space is $2^k$. To prevent the receiver from confusing a new transmission with a retransmission (ambiguity), the sum of sender and receiver window sizes must not exceed $2^k$: $$ N_s + N_r \le 2^k $$ For symmetric windows ($N_s = N_r = N$): $$ N \le 2^{k-1} $$ **Example:** If $k = 3$, then $N \le 2^{2} = 4$. **Efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{N \cdot T_f}{T_f + 2 T_p} = \frac{N}{1 + 2a} $$ where $a = T_p / T_f$ is the normalized propagation delay. **(b)** A Selective Repeat ARQ system uses 4-bit sequence numbers. Determine the maximum sender and receiver window sizes. If the propagation delay is 200 μs and the frame transmission time is 800 μs, calculate the link utilization for a window size of $N = 8$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Maximum Window Size:** With $k = 4$ bits, the sequence number range is $0$ to $2^4 - 1 = 15$, giving $2^4 = 16$ sequence values. $$ N \le 2^{k-1} = 2^{4-1} = 2^3 = 8 $$ So the maximum window size is **$N_{\max} = 8$** for both sender and receiver. **Link Utilization Calculation:** $$ a = \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{200 \, \mu s}{800 \, \mu s} = 0.25 $$ $$ \eta = \frac{N}{1 + 2a} = \frac{8}{1 + 2(0.25)} = \frac{8}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{8}{1.5} \approx 5.33 $$ Since efficiency cannot exceed 1 (100%), the actual link utilization is **capped at 100%** ($1.0$ or $100\%$). The system is **bandwidth-limited, not window-limited**, because the chosen window of $N = 8$ is larger than the bandwidth-delay product. **Bandwidth-delay product check:** $$ BDP = 1 + 2a = 1.5 \text{ frames} $$ Since $N = 8 > 1.5$, the pipe is always full. The utilization is **$\eta = 1$ (100%)**. ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Derive the throughput expression for Slotted ALOHA. Find the maximum throughput and the value of $G$ at which it occurs. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Time is divided into discrete slots, each of length $T_f$ (one frame transmission time). 2. Stations transmit only at slot boundaries. 3. Frame arrivals are modeled as a Poisson process with mean offered load $G$ frames per slot (including new and retransmitted frames). **Derivation:** The probability of $k$ transmission attempts in a given slot is: $$ P(k) = \frac{G^k \cdot e^{-G}}{k!} $$ A transmission is **successful** if it is the **only** transmission in that slot, i.e., $k = 1$: $$ P(\text{success}) = P(k=1) = G \cdot e^{-G} $$ Throughput $S$ is the mean number of successful transmissions per slot: $$ \boxed{S = G \cdot e^{-G}} $$ **Maximum Throughput:** Differentiate $S$ with respect to $G$: $$ \frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-G}(1 - G) $$ Setting the derivative to zero: $$ e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \implies G = 1 $$ **Maximum value:** $$ S_{\max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368 \text{ or } 36.8\% $$ **(b)** In a Slotted ALOHA system, 100 stations share a channel. Each station generates a new frame with probability 0.01 per slot, and retransmits with probability 0.05 per slot. Find the average offered load $G$, the throughput $S$, and the probability of a successful transmission in a slot. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Average Offered Load $G$:** The total offered load per slot is the sum of contributions from all 100 stations: $$ G = N_{\text{stations}} \cdot (p_{\text{new}} + p_{\text{retransmit}}) = 100 \cdot (0.01 + 0.05) = 100 \cdot 0.06 = 6.0 $$ **Throughput $S$:** $$ S = G \cdot e^{-G} = 6.0 \cdot e^{-6.0} = 6.0 \cdot 0.002479 \approx 0.01487 $$ So the throughput is approximately **0.0149 frames per slot** (≈ 1.49% utilization). **Probability of successful transmission in a slot:** $$ P(\text{success}) = P(k=1) = G \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-6} \approx 0.002479 $$ This is approximately **0.2479%** per slot. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $G$ and $S$:** Students often swap the offered load $G$ with throughput $S$ in the formula. Remember: $G$ is the **input** (mean attempts per slot/frame-time), and $S$ is the **output** (mean successes). Always compute $G$ first, then plug into the throughput equation. > 2. **Wrong vulnerable period:** In Pure ALOHA, the vulnerable period is $2T_f$ (giving $e^{-2G}$), not $T_f$. In Slotted ALOHA, it is $T_f$ (giving $e^{-G}$). Mixing these up costs full marks. > 3. **Selective Repeat ambiguity:** Students frequently state the constraint as $N \le 2^k$ instead of $N \le 2^{k-1}$. The correct symmetric form is $\mathbf{2^{k-1}}$. Writing $2^k$ is a **frequent 1-mark deduction** trap. > 4. **Forgetting units / no decimal:** Always state final values with units (frames per slot, %) — KTU examiners allocate marks for explicit units. > 5. **Missing the throughput cap at 100%:** In Selective Repeat utilization problems, $\eta$ can exceed 1 mathematically, but physically $\eta$ is capped at $1.0$. State this explicitly. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Selective Repeat ARQ** retransmits **only the lost/damaged frame**, making it the most efficient sliding window protocol. - Window size limit: $\mathbf{N \le 2^{k-1}}$ (for symmetric sender/receiver windows). - Sender's window slides forward on receiving an **ACK**; receiver's window slides when the lowest expected frame is received **in order**. - **Pure ALOHA:** Continuous time, throughput $\mathbf{S = G \cdot e^{-2G}}$, max = $\mathbf{1/(2e) \approx 18.4\%}$ at $G = 0.5$, vulnerable period = $\mathbf{2T_f}$. - **Slotted ALOHA:** Discrete slots, throughput $\mathbf{S = G \cdot e^{-G}}$, max = $\mathbf{1/e \approx 36.8\%}$ at $G = 1$, vulnerable period = $\mathbf{T_f}$. - Slotted ALOHA is **exactly 2× more efficient** than Pure ALOHA. - ALOHA is a **random / contention-based** MAC protocol; the modern descendants are **CSMA/CD** (Ethernet) and **CSMA/CA** (Wi-Fi). - ALOHA models assume **infinite user population** and **Poisson-distributed** arrivals — be careful when applying to small fixed networks. - For Slotted ALOHA numerics, always remember to **multiply $N_{\text{stations}}$ by per-station probability** to get $G$. - The throughput of ALOHA is fundamentally limited to < 50% — this is why **controlled-access** protocols (TDMA, FDMA, CDMA) are preferred for high-utilization systems. - **Selective Repeat vs Go-Back-N:** Selective Repeat is more efficient but requires **more complex receiver logic** (reordering buffer). Go-Back-N has window $N \le 2^k - 1$ and discards all out-of-order frames. - **KTU board exam hot topics:** deriving throughput formulas, computing $G$ from station counts, comparing ARQ protocols, and drawing the sender-receiver window diagram.

topics.title: CSMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CSMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CSMA — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA)** is a probabilistic **Medium Access Control (MAC)** protocol operating at the **Data Link Layer (Sub-layer: LLC + MAC)** of the OSI/TCP-IP reference model, in which a station **senses the carrier (channel)** *before* attempting transmission, and acts on the channel state (idle/busy) according to a defined persistence strategy.

- Belongs to the family of **Contention-Based / Random Access Protocols**.
- Defined under the IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet) and IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) working groups.
- Designed to reduce (but not eliminate) **frame collisions** in a **shared broadcast medium**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Polite Telephone Party"

Imagine a conference call with **N participants** sharing **one open microphone line**:

- Every participant can **hear the line** (Carrier Sense).
- A participant wanting to speak **waits for silence**, then speaks.
- If two people start speaking simultaneously → **collision** (overlapping audio).
- The protocol differs in *how patiently* and *how aggressively* a participant waits before speaking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Carrier** = the shared communication channel's signal/medium.
> - **Sense** = listening/detecting energy on the medium.
> - **Multiple Access** = multiple stations share the same medium.
> - **Collision** = simultaneous transmission causing signal garbling.
> - **Propagation Delay (τ)** = time for a signal to travel end-to-end.
> - **Transmission Time (T)** = duration to push one full frame.

## 1.3 Why CSMA Exists — The Problem It Solves

Pure **ALOHA** allowed stations to transmit *anytime* without checking → collision rate was up to ~18.4% efficiency.

CSMA improves this by using a simple heuristic: **"Listen Before Talk (LBT)"**, which reduces the vulnerable window during which collisions can occur.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of CSMA Variants

| Variant | Persistence Strategy | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **1-persistent CSMA** | Transmits *immediately* when channel idle | Legacy Ethernet (802.3) — historical |
| **Non-persistent CSMA** | Waits a *random* time before re-sensing | Theoretical benchmark |
| **p-persistent CSMA** | Transmits with probability *p* when idle | Slotted channels (e.g., early 802.11) |
| **CSMA/CD** | Adds **Collision Detection** | Wired Ethernet (802.3) |
| **CSMA/CA** | Adds **Collision Avoidance** | Wireless LAN (802.11 Wi-Fi) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A 14-mark question often asks: *"Compare CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA. Why can't we use CSMA/CD in wireless networks?"* — This is mandatory board-prep material.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core CSMA Algorithm (Generic)

The operational steps executed by **every station** on a shared medium:

1. **Sense the carrier** — Station monitors the channel for energy/signal.
2. **Decision Branch:**
   - If channel is **IDLE** → proceed per persistence rule (1, non, or p).
   - If channel is **BUSY** → defer transmission; reschedule per rule.
3. **Transmit the entire frame** of duration *T*.
4. **Wait for ACK** (optional, application-dependent).
5. On collision (if detectable) → run **back-off algorithm** (Binary Exponential Backoff in Ethernet).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Timing Parameter:** *a* = τ / T (ratio of propagation delay to transmission time). For LANs, *a* is small (e.g., 0.01); for satellite links, *a* can exceed 1.

## 2.2 Detailed Variants — Logic Steps

### 2.2.1 1-Persistent CSMA

- **Rule:** If channel is **idle → transmit immediately**. If **busy → keep sensing continuously**, transmit the instant it becomes idle.
- **Pros:** Minimal idle time → high channel utilization under low load.
- **Cons:** If **≥2 stations** are waiting for the same idle moment → **collision guaranteed**.

### 2.2.2 Non-Persistent CSMA

- **Rule:** If channel is **idle → transmit**. If **busy → wait a *random* time**, then re-sense.
- **Pros:** Drastically reduces collisions.
- **Cons:** Random wait may cause channel to remain idle while stations wait → wasted capacity under low load.

### 2.2.3 p-Persistent CSMA

- **Rule (Slotted Time):**
  - If channel **idle** in current slot → transmit with probability *p*; defer to next slot with probability *(1−p)*.
  - If channel **busy** → wait until next slot, re-evaluate.
- **Special cases:**
  - *p = 1* → degenerates to slotted 1-persistent.
  - *p → 0* → degenerates to slotted non-persistent.
- **Tuning:** Choose *p ≈ 1/N* (N = number of contenders) for optimum.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Variant

| Variant | Best For | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| 1-persistent | Few stations, low delay links | Aggression = low wasted time |
| Non-persistent | Many stations, heavy load | Randomness breaks synchronization |
| p-persistent | Slotted/synchronized medium | Balanced trade-off via probability |

## 2.4 CSMA/CD — Collision Detection (Wired Ethernet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CSMA/CD Principle:** *Listen While Talking.* The station transmits *and simultaneously monitors* the medium; if it detects an energy level higher than its own signal → a collision is happening → abort immediately.

**Operational Steps:**

1. Sense the medium.
2. If idle → begin transmission.
3. **During** transmission, monitor for collisions.
4. On collision detection:
   - Abort transmission immediately.
   - Transmit a **Jam Signal** (32-bit pattern) to alert all stations.
   - Run **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)**: wait *k × 51.2 µs*, where *k* ∈ [0, 2ⁱ − 1] for the *i*-th collision (capped at 1023).
5. Attempt retransmission up to 16 times; drop frame after that.

**Constraint for CSMA/CD to function:**

$$T \geq 2\tau \quad \text{(frame transmission time} \geq \text{round-trip propagation delay)}$$

If *T < 2τ*, the sender finishes before a collision signal returns → collision is missed.

## 2.5 CSMA/CA — Collision Avoidance (Wireless 802.11)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CD fails in wireless:** In radio networks, a station *cannot* transmit and listen on the same frequency simultaneously (the local transmitter swamps the receiver). Hidden terminals also make detection unreliable. Hence we *avoid* collisions rather than detect them.

**Mechanisms Used:**

1. **DIFS + Backoff:** After channel becomes idle, station waits **DIFS** (DCF Inter-Frame Space), then a random backoff counter in [0, CW].
2. **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):** Collision Window *CW* doubles on each failure (CW_min = 31, CW_max = 1023 slots).
3. **RTS / CTS Handshake (Optional, 802.11g/n/ac):** Sender sends **Request-To-Send**; receiver replies **Clear-To-Send** after **SIFS**; channel is reserved.
4. **ACK frame:** Receiver sends ACK after SIFS to confirm successful receipt.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Channel parameter | $a = \tau / T$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | CSMA/CD frame time condition | $T \geq 2\tau$ | Otherwise collision missed |
| 3 | Slot time (Ethernet) | $t_{slot} = 51.2\ \mu s$ | = 512 bit times @ 10 Mbps |
| 4 | Backoff slots (i-th collision) | $k \in [0,\ 2^{i} - 1]$, capped at 1023 | Binary Exponential |
| 5 | ALOHA max throughput (pure) | $S_{max} = 0.184$ | 18.4% |
| 6 | Slotted ALOHA max throughput | $S_{max} = 0.368$ | 36.8% |
| 7 | Non-persistent CSMA throughput | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1 + 2a) + e^{-aG}}$ | *G* = offered load |
| 8 | 1-persistent CSMA throughput | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G(1+a)} \cdot [1 + G + aG(1 + G + aG/2)]}{G(1+a) + e^{-G(1+a)}}$ | Simplified form |
| 9 | Slotted non-persistent CSMA | $S = \dfrac{aG \cdot e^{-aG}}{1 - e^{-aG} + aG}$ | Slotted version |
| 10 | Max throughput non-persistent (a→0) | $S_{max} \to \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | Theoretical upper bound |
| 11 | CSMA/CA CW update | $CW_{new} = \min(2(CW + 1) - 1,\ CW_{max})$ | 802.11 DCF |
| 12 | Maximum BEB attempts (Ethernet) | 16 attempts → frame dropped | After this, error to upper layer |
| 13 | Jam signal length | 32 bits | Ensures all stations detect collision |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wired LANs (offices, data centers):** CSMA/CD formed the bedrock of classic Ethernet (10BASE5, 10BASE2, 10BASE-T). With switched full-duplex links, CSMA/CD is now largely **disabled** because each link is point-to-point — collisions are physically impossible.
- **Wireless LANs (Wi-Fi 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax):** CSMA/CA remains the **primary MAC mechanism** even in Wi-Fi 6/6E.
- **CAN Bus (Automotive):** A CSMA/CD+AMP variant where collisions are resolved by **non-destructive bitwise arbitration** using message priorities.
- **Satellite / Half-duplex Radio Links:** CSMA persists in many LEO constellations and tactical MANETs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Throughput of Slotted ALOHA → Motivation for CSMA

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation establishes **why CSMA is needed** by quantifying ALOHA's inefficiency.

**Step 1 — Define Offered Load (*G*):**
Let *G* = average number of transmission attempts per slot (Poisson distributed).

**Step 2 — Probability of Successful Transmission in a Given Slot:**
For success, exactly **one** attempt must occur in the slot.

$$
P(\text{success}) = \frac{G^{1} \cdot e^{-G}}{1!} = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

**Step 3 — Throughput (*S*) is Success Probability (since each success = 1 slot used):**

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximize *S* by Differentiating with Respect to *G*:**

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = \frac{d}{dG}\left( G \cdot e^{-G} \right)
$$

Apply the product rule: $d(uv) = u\,dv + v\,du$, with $u = G$ and $v = e^{-G}$.

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = (1) \cdot e^{-G} + G \cdot (-e^{-G}) = e^{-G}(1 - G)
$$

**Step 5 — Set Derivative to Zero for Critical Point:**

$$
e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0
$$

Since $e^{-G} \neq 0$ for all finite *G*, we require $1 - G = 0$, hence:

$$
G = 1
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate *S* at the Optimum:**

$$
S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Pure ALOHA wastes ~63% of slots. CSMA's "listen-before-talk" directly attacks this waste by *shrinking the vulnerable window* from *2T* (ALOHA) to roughly *τ + T*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Non-Persistent CSMA Throughput

**Step 1 — Define Variables:**
- *G* = mean offered load (frames per *T*).
- *a* = τ / *T* = ratio of propagation to transmission time.
- A "frame time" *T* is the unit of measurement.

**Step 2 — Identify Three Outcomes in Any Time Period of Length *T*:**
- **Outcome A:** Channel successfully carries one frame.
- **Outcome B:** Channel idle (no station transmits).
- **Outcome C:** Collision (≥2 stations transmit).

**Step 3 — Probability of an Idle Period of Length *a* in a frame-time slot:**
The channel is sensed busy for *a* × *G* attempts on average per slot. Probability of zero attempts in a unit period:

$$
P(\text{idle}) = e^{-G \cdot a}
$$

Wait — for non-persistent CSMA, the precise derivation considers the *vulnerable period* ≈ *a* slots (since a collision within *a* slots after start cannot be avoided). After the first transmission begins, other stations starting in the next *a* slots will collide.

**Step 4 — Probability Exactly One Transmission Initiates (and is successful):**
For a Poisson process with rate *G* per unit time, probability of exactly *1* event in time *a*:

$$
P(\text{one in } a) = aG \cdot e^{-aG}
$$

**Step 5 — Time Wasted on a Collision:** Each colliding transmission occupies the channel for time $a + 1$ (propagation + full frame, since the colliding station continues until it senses the collision — for non-persistent, we approximate).

**Step 6 — Standard Form (from Bertsekas & Gallager, *Data Networks*):**

$$
S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1 + 2a) + e^{-aG}}
$$

**Step 7 — Sanity Check (a → 0):**

$$
\lim_{a \to 0} S = \frac{G \cdot 1}{G + 1}
$$

Maximum of $G/(G+1)$ is at $G \to \infty$, giving $S \to 1$ as *a* vanishes — a *theoretical* benchmark.

**Step 8 — Limit as G → 0 (light load):**

$$
\lim_{G \to 0} S = \frac{G}{1} = G
$$

Throughput ≈ offered load (no collisions, no waste) — physically correct.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 1 km cable with propagation speed $2 \times 10^8$ m/s carries frames of length *L* = 1000 bits at rate *R* = 10 Mbps. Compute *a*.

**Step 1 — Propagation Delay:**

$$
\tau = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 5\ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission Time:**

$$
T = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10 \times 10^6} = 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 100\ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Parameter *a*:**

$$
a = \frac{\tau}{T} = \frac{5\ \mu s}{100\ \mu s} = 0.05
$$

**Step 4 — Throughput at G = 1 (Non-persistent):**

$$
S = \frac{1 \cdot e^{-0.05 \times 1}}{1 \cdot (1 + 2 \times 0.05) + e^{-0.05 \times 1}} = \frac{e^{-0.05}}{1.1 + e^{-0.05}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{0.9512}{1.1 + 0.9512} = \frac{0.9512}{2.0512} \approx 0.4638
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even with parameter *a* = 0.05, non-persistent CSMA delivers ~46% throughput at unit load — vastly superior to ALOHA's 18.4% ceiling.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | 1-Persistent CSMA | Non-Persistent CSMA | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vulnerable period | $2T$ | $T$ | $T + \tau$ | $T + \tau$ | $T + \tau$ | $\approx \tau$ |
| Max theoretical *S* | 0.184 | 0.368 | 0.529 (a→0) | 0.583 (a→0) | $\approx 0.9+$ (a→0) | $\approx 0.5$–$0.6$ (practical) |
| Collision handling | Retransmit | Retransmit | Retransmit | Retransmit | Detect & abort | Avoid via backoff |
| Backoff strategy | Uniform | Uniform | — | Random wait | BEB (0–2ⁱ−1) | BEB (CW doubles) |
| Channel sensing | None | None | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes + virtual (NAV) |
| Medium | Wired/Wireless | Synchronous | Wired | Wired | Wired (802.3) | Wireless (802.11) |
| Duplex requirement | Half | Half | Half | Half | Half | Half (mandatory) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CSMA Decision Flow — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Station has new frame to send] --> B{Sense Carrier}
    B -- Channel IDLE --> C{Apply Persistence Rule}
    B -- Channel BUSY --> D{Defer Transmission}
    D --> E[Wait per rule: 1-pers: keep sensing / non-pers: random delay / p-pers: wait slot with prob 1-p]
    E --> B
    C -- 1-Persistent --> F[Transmit Immediately]
    C -- Non-Persistent --> G[Transmit if random wait elapsed]
    C -- p-Persistent --> H{Transmit with prob p?}
    H -- YES --> F
    H -- NO --> I[Wait one slot, re-sense]
    I --> B
    F --> J{CD or CA active?}
    J -- CSMA/CD --> K[Monitor for collision energy]
    K -- Collision Detected --> L[Abort + Jam Signal + BEB Backoff]
    K -- No Collision --> M[Frame Sent Successfully]
    J -- CSMA/CA --> N[Wait DIFS + Random Backoff]
    N --> O{RTS Enabled?}
    O -- YES --> P[RTS - CTS Handshake]
    O -- NO --> Q[Transmit Frame]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Wait SIFS for ACK]
    R -- ACK Received --> S[Success]
    R -- ACK Timeout --> L
    L --> B
```

## 4.2 CSMA Family Tree — Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Random Access MAC Protocols] --> B[ALOHA Family]
    A --> C[CSMA Family]
    B --> B1[Pure ALOHA]
    B --> B2[Slotted ALOHA]
    C --> C1[CSMA Basic]
    C1 --> C2[1-Persistent]
    C1 --> C3[Non-Persistent]
    C1 --> C4[p-Persistent]
    C --> C5[CSMA/CD Ethernet 802.3]
    C --> C6[CSMA/CA Wi-Fi 802.11]
    C5 --> C5A[Collision Detection via energy compare]
    C5 --> C5B[Jam Signal 32 bits]
    C5 --> C5C[Binary Exponential Backoff]
    C6 --> C6A[DIFS SIFS Timing]
    C6 --> C6B[NAV Network Allocation Vector]
    C6 --> C6C[RTS CTS Handshake]
    C6 --> C6D[ACK Frame]
    C6 --> C6E[Binary Exponential Backoff CW]
```

## 4.3 Ethernet CSMA/CD — Frame Time & Collision Window

```mermaid
gantt
    title Ethernet CSMA/CD Frame and Collision Detection Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Station A
    Sense Idle and Start Transmit :a1, 0, 100
    Detect Collision and Abort :a2, 50, 60
    Send Jam Signal :a3, 60, 65
    Begin BEB Backoff :a4, 65, 116
    section Station B
    Sense Idle and Start Transmit :b1, 30, 130
    Receive Jam Signal :b2, 65, 70
    Abort Transmission :b3, 70, 75
    Begin BEB Backoff :b4, 75, 126
    section Channel Medium

    Collision Occurs at Midpoint :crit, 50, 51
    Collision Signal Returns to A :crit, 51, 55
    Jam Signal Propagation :crit, 65, 70
```

## 4.4 CSMA/CA Timing Hierarchy — 802.11 DCF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Channel Busy from Previous Frame] --> S1[Wait until Channel Idle]
    S1 --> S2[Wait DIFS Duration 34 us in 802.11b]
    S2 --> S3[Set Random Backoff Counter in 0 to CW]
    S3 --> S4{Counter Expired?}
    S4 -- NO --> S5[Defer one slot time 9 us]
    S5 --> S4
    S4 -- YES --> S6[Transmit RTS if enabled]
    S6 --> S7[Receiver waits SIFS 10 us]
    S7 --> S8[Receiver sends CTS]
    S8 --> S9[Sender waits SIFS]
    S9 --> S10[Transmit DATA Frame]
    S10 --> S11[Receiver waits SIFS]
    S11 --> S12[Receiver sends ACK]
    S12 --> S13[Frame Successfully Delivered]
```
