# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define CSMA. List and briefly explain the three persistence strategies of CSMA.**

**Model Answer:**

> **CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access)** is a contention-based MAC protocol in which a station **senses the channel** before transmitting, thereby reducing the probability of collision in a shared medium.
>
> **Three Persistence Strategies:**
>
> 1. **1-Persistent CSMA:** When the station finds the channel idle, it transmits *immediately with probability 1*. If busy, it continuously senses and transmits the moment the channel becomes idle. Aggressive → high collision risk.
> 2. **Non-Persistent CSMA:** If the channel is busy, the station waits a **random amount of time** before re-sensing. Reduces collisions at the cost of channel idle time.
> 3. **p-Persistent CSMA:** (Used in slotted channels) If idle, the station transmits with probability *p* and defers to the next slot with probability *(1−p)*. Provides a tunable trade-off.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is CSMA/CD not used in wireless networks? Mention the role of CSMA/CA.**

**Model Answer:**

> CSMA/CD requires a station to **detect collisions while transmitting** by monitoring the analog signal on the wire. In wireless networks this is infeasible for two reasons:
>
> 1. **Near-Far Problem / Self-Interference:** The local transmitter's strong signal swamps the receiver's ability to detect weaker collision signals from other stations.
> 2. **Hidden Terminal Problem:** Two stations may both be in range of the AP but **out of range of each other**; neither can detect the other's transmission, so collision detection is impossible.
>
> Hence wireless networks use **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)**, which *prevents* collisions through:
> - Carrier sensing + random backoff
> - Inter-frame spacing (DIFS, SIFS)
> - **RTS/CTS handshake** to reserve the medium
> - Explicit **ACK** to confirm receipt
>
> The IEEE 802.11 standard (Wi-Fi) mandates CSMA/CA in its Distributed Coordination Function (DCF).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — CSMA/CD Deep Dive

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **CSMA/CD** protocol. How does it detect collisions, and what is the role of the **jam signal**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A network uses CSMA/CD with a 1 km cable (propagation speed $2 \times 10^8$ m/s) transmitting frames of 1500 bytes at 100 Mbps. Compute (i) the propagation delay *τ*, (ii) the transmission time *T*, (iii) the parameter *a*, and (iv) the minimum frame size required for collision detection. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) CSMA/CD Operation [7 Marks]**

CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) is the MAC protocol used in IEEE 802.3 Ethernet. Its operation is as follows:

1. **Carrier Sense:** The station continuously monitors the medium to determine whether it is idle or busy.

2. **Frame Transmission:** If the medium is sensed idle, the station begins transmitting the frame.

3. **Collision Detection:** While transmitting, the station **simultaneously monitors the signal level** on the medium. If the observed energy exceeds the energy of the station's own transmission, a collision is inferred (since another station must be transmitting simultaneously).

4. **Collision Response:**
   - The station **aborts transmission immediately** (does not waste bandwidth sending the full frame).
   - It sends a **32-bit jam signal** to ensure that all other stations also detect the collision (collision fragments are sometimes too short to be reliably detected by all stations).
   - The station then runs the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm: it chooses a random integer $k \in [0,\ 2^{i} - 1]$ where *i* is the number of collisions experienced for this frame, and waits $k \times 51.2\ \mu s$ before re-attempting.
   - After **16 collisions**, the frame is dropped and an error is reported to the upper layer.

**Diagram (textual):**

```
Station A:    [Sense] --idle--> [Transmit] --collision!--> [Abort + Jam + BEB]
                                  |                                |
Station B:    [Sense] --busy--> [Wait]    [Transmit] --collision!--> [Abort + Jam + BEB]
                                                                  |
Medium:       [Signals overlap at point P within propagation window]|
```

**Role of Jam Signal:** The jam signal is a deliberately transmitted 32-bit pattern (e.g., alternating 1s and 0s) that guarantees all stations on the segment will recognize a collision, even if the original colliding fragments are too short or attenuated.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Cable length $d = 1\ km = 1000\ m$
- Propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^8\ m/s$
- Frame size $L = 1500\ bytes = 1500 \times 8 = 12{,}000\ bits$
- Bit rate $R = 100\ Mbps = 10^8\ bps$

**(i) Propagation Delay (τ):**

$$
\tau = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 5\ \mu s
$$

**(ii) Transmission Time (T):**

$$
T = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12{,}000}{10^{8}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 120\ \mu s
$$

**(iii) Parameter *a*:**

$$
a = \frac{\tau}{T} = \frac{5\ \mu s}{120\ \mu s} = 0.0417
$$

**(iv) Minimum Frame Size for Collision Detection:**

For CSMA/CD to function, the frame transmission time must be **at least twice the propagation delay**:

$$
T_{min} \geq 2\tau = 2 \times 5\ \mu s = 10\ \mu s
$$

At 100 Mbps, the minimum number of bits is:

$$
L_{min} = R \times 2\tau = 10^{8} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 1000\ \text{bits} = 125\ \text{bytes}
$$

> Since the actual frame (1500 bytes = 12000 bits) is much larger than 125 bytes, the condition $T \geq 2\tau$ is satisfied, and CSMA/CD will reliably detect collisions.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — CSMA/CA & Wireless Perspective

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **CSMA/CA protocol** used in IEEE 802.11 with reference to **DIFS, SIFS, contention window, and backoff procedure**. Use a timing diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **CSMA/CD** and **CSMA/CA** across 6 parameters. Why does hidden terminal problem exist in wireless networks, and how does **RTS/CTS** solve it? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) CSMA/CA Operation in 802.11 DCF [7 Marks]**

The **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** of IEEE 802.11 uses CSMA/CA with the following procedure:

1. **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space):** After the medium becomes idle, a station must wait for a **DIFS period (34 µs in 802.11b)** before attempting any transmission. This priority spacing ensures orderly access.

2. **Backoff Counter:** The station then sets a random backoff counter in the range $[0,\ CW]$ where **CW (Contention Window)** initially equals 31. The counter is decremented by 1 every slot time (9 µs) **only when the medium is idle**.

3. **Channel Sensing During Backoff:** If the medium becomes busy during the backoff countdown, the counter is **frozen** and resumes only after the medium is idle for another DIFS.

4. **Transmission:** When the counter reaches zero, the station transmits the frame.

5. **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space):** The receiver waits for a **SIFS (10 µs)** — a shorter interval than DIFS — to send back the **ACK frame**, ensuring ACK has higher priority.

6. **Collision Handling:** If no ACK is received within the timeout, the sender assumes a collision occurred and **doubles CW** (up to max 1023), selects a new backoff, and retries.

**Timing Diagram (textual):**

```
|---Frame N---|-DIFS-|--Backoff (random slots)--|--RTS--|-SIFS-|-CTS-|-SIFS-|-DATA-|-SIFS-|-ACK-|
              |<--station waits idle period-->|<--backoff countdown-->|<-- frame exchange  -->|
```

---

**(b) Comparison Table + Hidden Terminal & RTS/CTS [7 Marks]**

**Comparison Table (6 Parameters):**

| Parameter | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Detect collisions | Avoid collisions |
| Detection mechanism | Energy level on wire | Timing + backoff |
| Medium type | Wired (Ethernet) | Wireless (Wi-Fi) |
| Response to collision | Abort + jam + backoff | Backoff only; collision inferred from missing ACK |
| Hidden terminal handling | Not applicable (wired) | Solved by RTS/CTS |
| Throughput efficiency | High (~90%+) on switched LANs | Moderate (~50–60%) due to overhead |

**Hidden Terminal Problem:**

Consider three stations A, B, and C. A and C are both within range of access point B, but **A and C are out of range of each other**. When A transmits to B, C cannot sense the medium as busy and may also transmit → collision at B.

**RTS/CTS Solution:**

1. A sends a short **RTS (Request-To-Send)** frame to B.
2. B replies with **CTS (Clear-To-Send)** — this CTS is heard by **all stations in B's range**, including C.
3. C hears the CTS and sets its **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** to remain silent for the duration of the impending transmission.
4. A transmits DATA; B sends ACK.
5. Collision probability at A is virtually eliminated because C is now aware of the reservation.

> This handshake is optional in 802.11 (mandatory only for large frames) but is critical for solving the hidden terminal problem in dense wireless deployments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CSMA with CSMA/CD** — Writing "CSMA detects collisions" loses marks; only CSMA/CD does.
> 2. **Wrong jam signal size** — It is **32 bits**, not 16, not variable.
> 3. **Mis-stating the BEB range** — It is $[0,\ 2^{i} - 1]$, *not* $[1,\ 2^{i}]$. Some students forget the zero lower bound.
> 4. **Forgetting the SIFS < DIFS ordering** — The reason ACK "wins" the next transmission is that **SIFS (10 µs) is shorter than DIFS (34 µs)** in 802.11. This is examinable.
> 5. **Confusing propagation delay with transmission delay** — *τ = d/v*; *T = L/R*. These are commonly interchanged in numericals.
> 6. **Not justifying minimum frame size** — A full answer must derive $T_{min} = 2\tau$ from "collision must be detected before sender finishes transmitting."
> 7. **CSMA/CA numericals** — Board exam answers should always state that ACK timeout triggers a *doubling* of CW, not a fixed increase.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CSMA = Listen Before Talk.** Reduces collisions; never eliminates them.
- **Three persistence strategies:** 1-persistent, non-persistent, p-persistent. Choose based on number of contenders and channel synchronization.
- **CSMA/CD** is for **wired** Ethernet; uses collision *detection* + jam signal + BEB.
- **CSMA/CA** is for **wireless** (802.11); uses collision *avoidance* via DIFS/SIFS timing + backoff + optional RTS/CTS.
- **Key parameter *a* = τ / T.** Small *a* (LANs) → high throughput; large *a* (satellite) → poor throughput.
- **CSMA/CD constraint:** $T \geq 2\tau$ — frame must be long enough to detect collisions.
- **Binary Exponential Backoff:** Window $k \in [0,\ 2^{i} - 1]$, retries capped at 16 (Ethernet) or CW_max = 1023 (802.11).
- **Throughput ranking (a→0):** CSMA/CD > Non-persistent CSMA > Slotted ALOHA > Pure ALOHA.
- **Hidden terminal** is the killer problem in wireless — solved by **RTS/CTS** + **NAV** in 802.11.
- **Slot time** in classic Ethernet = **51.2 µs** = 512 bit times @ 10 Mbps.
- **Jam signal** = 32 bits transmitted after collision detection to notify all stations.
- **In modern full-duplex switched Ethernet, CSMA/CD is disabled** because point-to-point links have no collisions.
- **CSMA/CA is still active in Wi-Fi 6/6E/7** — knowledge is future-proof and exam-proof.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CSMA/CD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CSMA/CD**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** is a Media Access Control (MAC) protocol operating at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI model. It governs how multiple devices share a common, half-duplex broadcast channel — most notably classical **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)** LANs.

The protocol operates on three core principles embedded in its name:

- **Carrier Sense (CS):** A station "listens" to the shared medium and checks whether a carrier signal (i.e., ongoing transmission) is present before initiating its own transmission.
- **Multiple Access (MA):** Multiple stations compete for access to the same shared medium; no central arbiter controls who gets to transmit.
- **Collision Detection (CD):** While transmitting, the station continues to monitor the medium. If it detects voltage/power levels that are abnormal (twice the normal signal), it concludes a collision has occurred, immediately aborts transmission, and transmits a **Jam Signal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CSMA/CD belongs to the family of **Contention-Based Random Access Protocols** in the **MAC Sublayer**. It is an *unslotted, non-deterministic* protocol, meaning a station may transmit at any instant if the medium is sensed idle. Its efficiency collapses as network load approaches saturation, which is why modern switched Ethernet no longer requires CSMA/CD.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dark Dining Hall"

Imagine **six friends seated around a round table in a completely dark room** trying to have a conversation:

1. **Carrier Sense (Listen First):** Before speaking, each friend *listens* to the silence. If anyone else is already talking, they wait.
2. **Multiple Access (Shared Medium):** The "air" is the shared medium. Any of the six can start speaking the moment it goes quiet.
3. **Collision Detection (Listen While Talking):** The moment a friend starts speaking, he keeps his ears open. If he hears another voice *overlapping* with his, he immediately realizes *"Oh, we're both speaking at once — a collision!"*
4. **Jam Signal (Exclamation):** He shouts **"Collision detected!"** (the jam signal) so that *everyone* in the room stops and waits.
5. **Binary Exponential Backoff (Random Wait):** Each colliding friend mentally rolls a die and waits a *random* number of seconds (not all the same time, to avoid another collision). The longer the chaos, the longer the dice range.

This is exactly how CSMA/CD behaves on an Ethernet bus.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following IEEE 802.3 metrics are **mandatory** for KTU problem-solving:

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Slot Time ($\tau$) | **51.2 μs** | Time to traverse maximum network round-trip |
| Minimum Frame Size | **64 bytes (512 bits)** | Guarantees collision is detected by sender |
| Maximum Frame Size | **1518 bytes** | Upper bound of a valid Ethernet frame |
| Maximum Retries | **16 attempts** | Frame is dropped after 16 failed transmissions |
| Jam Signal Duration | **32 bits (3.2 μs)** | Ensures all stations recognize the collision |
| Signal Velocity in Copper | **≈ 2 × 10⁸ m/s** | Used in propagation delay calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Exam Tip:** The 51.2 μs slot time is derived as $51.2\,\mu s = 2 \times T_p$ for the maximum 10BASE5 Ethernet segment (2500 m). At 10 Mbps, $51.2\,\mu s \times 10\,\text{Mbps} = 512\,\text{bits} = 64\,\text{bytes}$ — the minimum frame size is *engineered* to equal exactly one slot time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Algorithm of CSMA/CD

CSMA/CD can be decomposed into a deterministic sequence of logical steps. Mastering these steps is the single highest-yield activity for KTU Part A and Part B questions.

### Step 1 — Carrier Sensing (Is the medium idle?)
The MAC controller samples the physical layer's **Carrier Sense** signal. If the medium is **busy**, the station defers transmission and continues sensing until the medium becomes idle.

### Step 2 — Frame Transmission Initiation
When the medium becomes idle, the station waits for the **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG)** of **9.6 μs** (for 10 Mbps Ethernet) and then begins transmitting the frame bit-by-bit.

### Step 3 — Simultaneous Collision Detection
During transmission, the station compares the **voltage on the medium** with the signal it is sending. A collision produces a DC voltage level approximately **twice the nominal signal level**, which is unambiguously detected.

### Step 4 — Jam Signal Transmission
Upon detection, the station:
1. **Immediately aborts** the rest of the frame.
2. Transmits a **32-bit jam signal** (a deliberate, recognizable pattern like `1010...10`) to ensure that *every other colliding station* also recognizes the collision and discards its partial frame.

### Step 5 — Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)
The colliding stations each wait for a **random integer** number of slot times:

$$r = \text{Random}\bigl[0,\ 2^{\min(k,\,10)} - 1\bigr] \times \tau$$

where $k$ is the current attempt number (1-indexed).

### Step 6 — Retry or Drop
After waiting, the station re-attempts Step 1. If $k = 16$ retries are exhausted, the frame is **dropped** and a higher-layer error is reported.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | One-way propagation delay | seconds |
| 2 | $\tau = 2 \cdot T_p$ | **Slot time / Contention window** (round-trip) | seconds |
| 3 | $L_{\min} = R \cdot \tau = 2 \cdot R \cdot T_p$ | Minimum frame size to detect worst-case collision | bits |
| 4 | $L_{\min} = 64$ bytes = **512 bits** | IEEE 802.3 fixed value at 10 Mbps | bits |
| 5 | $r \in [0,\ 2^{\min(k,10)} - 1]$ | Backoff slots after $k^{th}$ collision | unitless |
| 6 | Max backoff slots $= 2^{10} - 1 = 1023$ | After 10 collisions, range is frozen | slots |
| 7 | Max retries = **16** | Frame is dropped after 16 failed attempts | — |
| 8 | Throughput $S = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$ | Approx. efficiency, where $a = T_p / T_t$ | ratio |
| 9 | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L}$ | Normalized propagation parameter | ratio |
| 10 | Jam signal $= 32$ bits $= 3.2\,\mu s$ | 10 Mbps Ethernet | bits / time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule — Why the Minimum Frame is 64 Bytes:** A collision can only be detected by the sender *if the sender is still transmitting* when the collision signal propagates back. The longest possible "blind period" equals the **round-trip propagation delay** ($2T_p$). Therefore, the frame must be long enough that $T_t \geq 2T_p$, giving $L \geq R \cdot 2T_p$. For 10BASE5 ($d=2500$ m, $v=2\times10^8$ m/s, $R=10$ Mbps), this yields exactly **512 bits (64 bytes)**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

CSMA/CD remains foundational in:

- **Legacy 10BASE5 / 10BASE2 / 10BASE-T Half-Duplex Ethernet** — historical bus and hub topologies.
- **Industrial Control & Automotive Networks** — variants like **CAN (Controller Area Network)** use a closely related CSMA/CD + arbitration scheme (CSMA/CA + bit-dominant arbitration).
- **Conceptual Foundation** — every modern queueing-based MAC (Wi-Fi CSMA/CA, DOCSIS, LTE) inherits its design philosophy.
- **Exam & Interview Context** — KTU, GATE, and UGC-NET questions on Ethernet MAC almost exclusively test CSMA/CD efficiency formulas and backoff arithmetic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Frame Size from Round-Trip Delay

**Problem Statement:** A 10 Mbps CSMA/CD network uses cables of length **2500 m** with signal propagation speed **2 × 10⁸ m/s**. Derive the minimum frame size that guarantees the sender detects any collision.

### Step 1 — Compute One-Way Propagation Delay
The signal must travel from the sender to the *farthest* station to cause a worst-case collision.

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^8} = 12.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 12.5\,\mu s
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Round-Trip Delay (Slot Time)
A collision signal must propagate back from the farthest station to the sender. This is the **maximum blind period** during which the sender cannot detect a collision:

$$
\tau = 2 \cdot T_p = 2 \times 12.5\,\mu s = 25\,\mu s
$$

> For the **standard IEEE 802.3 maximum segment of 2500 m plus repeater delays**, the *official* slot time is **51.2 μs**, which is what the standard uses for minimum frame sizing. We will use the standard value.

$$
\tau_{\text{standard}} = 51.2\,\mu s
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Minimum Frame Length
For the sender to *still be transmitting* when the collision signal returns, the transmission time $T_t$ must satisfy:

$$
T_t \geq \tau
$$

Substituting $T_t = L / R$:

$$
\frac{L_{\min}}{R} \geq \tau
$$

$$
L_{\min} \geq R \cdot \tau
$$

$$
L_{\min} \geq 10 \times 10^6 \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
L_{\min} \geq 512\ \text{bits}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Bytes

$$
L_{\min} = \frac{512}{8} = 64\ \text{bytes}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{\min} = 64\ \text{bytes (512 bits)}}
$$

This is the **exact IEEE 802.3 specification** for 10 Mbps Ethernet. **Note:** For 100 Mbps (Fast Ethernet), the minimum frame is *still 64 bytes* but the maximum cable length is reduced to **250 m** to maintain the same slot-time guarantee.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Backoff Arithmetic with Worked Example

**Problem Statement:** A station attempts to transmit a frame and suffers collisions. Determine the range of slot times it must wait for attempt numbers $k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 10, 11, 16$.

### Logical Rule

$$
r \in \bigl[0,\ 2^{\min(k,\,10)} - 1\bigr]
$$

The exponent is **capped at 10**; beyond 10 collisions, the range freezes at $[0, 1023]$.

### Worked Computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
k = 1  &:\ r \in [0,\ 2^1 - 1] = [0, 1] \\
k = 2  &:\ r \in [0,\ 2^2 - 1] = [0, 3] \\
k = 3  &:\ r \in [0,\ 2^3 - 1] = [0, 7] \\
k = 4  &:\ r \in [0,\ 2^4 - 1] = [0, 15] \\
k = 5  &:\ r \in [0,\ 2^5 - 1] = [0, 31] \\
k = 6  &:\ r \in [0,\ 2^6 - 1] = [0, 63] \\
k = 10 &:\ r \in [0,\ 2^{10} - 1] = [0, 1023] \\
k = 11 &:\ r \in [0,\ 2^{\min(11,10)} - 1] = [0, 1023] \\
k = 16 &:\ \text{GIVE UP — frame dropped}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Backoff Delay in Time
If a particular attempt $k=3$ draws $r = 5$:

$$
T_{\text{wait}} = r \cdot \tau = 5 \times 51.2\,\mu s = 256\,\mu s
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CSMA/CD Throughput (Efficiency)

**Problem Statement:** Derive the maximum channel utilization (efficiency) of CSMA/CD for a 1-persistent system.

### Step 1 — Define the Time Cycle
For a single successful frame transmission:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_t + 2T_p + T_j
$$

where $T_t$ is frame transmission time, $2T_p$ is the contention/round-trip window, and $T_j$ is jam signal time (negligible in long frames).

### Step 2 — Define the "a" Parameter

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{\text{Prop. delay}}{\text{Frame transmission time}}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Classical Result (Bux-Local Model)
For 1-persistent CSMA/CD under heavy load, the approximate efficiency is:

$$
S = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example
Let $a = 0.01$ (i.e., propagation delay is 1% of frame time — typical for well-designed LANs):

$$
S = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.01} = \frac{1}{1.0644} \approx 0.9395
$$

$$
\boxed{S \approx 93.95\%}
$$

If $a = 1$ (propagation delay equals transmission time — overloaded or too-long network):

$$
S = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44} = \frac{1}{7.44} \approx 13.4\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $a \to 0$ (very long frames on short cables), efficiency approaches **100%**. As $a \to \infty$ (very short frames on long cables), efficiency collapses to **0%**. This is the central reason Ethernet frames have a 64-byte *minimum* — to bound $a$ from above and preserve efficiency.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | CSMA/CD (Ethernet) | CSMA/CA (Wi-Fi 802.11) | Token Passing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Collision handling | Detect & retransmit | **Avoid** via RTS/CTS | Impossible by design |
| Min frame size | **64 bytes** | None (variable) | None |
| Topology | Bus / Hub (legacy) | Star (Wireless) | Ring / Logical Ring |
| Determinism | Non-deterministic | Non-deterministic | **Deterministic** |
| Efficiency under heavy load | Poor | Poor | High |
| Used in | Legacy Ethernet | Wi-Fi, ZigBee | Token Ring, FDDI |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CSMA/CD State Machine — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    idleA[Station Idle] --> senseA{Sense Carrier}
    senseA -- Busy --> deferA[Defer Transmission] --> senseA
    senseA -- Idle --> waitIFG[Wait IFG 9.6 us]
    waitIFG --> txStart[Begin Frame Transmission]
    txStart --> monitor{Monitor Medium During Tx}
    monitor -- Normal Signal --> txEnd[Complete Frame Send] --> doneA[Success Exit]
    monitor -- Collision Detected --> abortTx[Abort Frame]
    abortTx --> jamSend[Transmit Jam Signal 32 bits]
    jamSend --> incK[k = k + 1]
    incK --> checkK{k less than 16}
    checkK -- Yes --> bkAlgo[Binary Exponential Backoff] --> senseA
    checkK -- No --> dropF[Drop Frame Report Error] --> doneA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows form a closed loop. A station only leaves the loop on **Success Exit** (frame transmitted cleanly) or **Drop Frame** (16 collisions). The `Binary Exponential Backoff` block is the *decision point* where the random integer $r$ is generated.

---

## 4.2 Ethernet Bus Topology with CSMA/CD

```mermaid
graph LR
    hostA[Station A] --- busA[Shared Coaxial Bus Medium]
    hostB[Station B] --- busA
    hostC[Station C] --- busA
    hostD[Station D] --- busA
    busA --- terminatorR[Terminator 50 ohm]
```

> **Visual interpretation:** All four stations tap into the **same physical medium** (a bus). Any signal injected by one station propagates in *both* directions along the bus. Because the medium is shared, the **Carrier Sense** and **Collision Detection** rules of CSMA/CD are mandatory.

---

## 4.3 Collision Detection Timing Diagram (Block Functional View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Station A
    participant Bus as Shared Medium
    participant B as Station B
    A->>A: Sense Idle
    A->>Bus: Begin Frame Transmission
    Note over A,Bus: Time t0: A starts sending
    B->>Bus: Begin Frame Transmission
    Note over B,Bus: Time t0 + dt: B starts sending
    Bus-->>A: Collision Signal Returns
    Note over A: Detects voltage > nominal
    A->>Bus: Abort Frame Transmit Jam
    B->>Bus: Abort Frame Transmit Jam
    Note over A,B: Both back off randomly
```

> **Key Observation:** Station A and Station B both sense the medium idle at *almost* the same instant because of propagation delay. They begin transmitting. The collision waveform propagates back to A after one round-trip delay. A then jams the bus, ensuring B (and everyone else) also recognizes the collision. Both then back off.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define CSMA/CD. Why is the minimum frame size in Ethernet fixed at 64 bytes?**

**Model Answer:**

CSMA/CD stands for **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection**. It is a contention-based MAC protocol in which a station senses the carrier before transmitting, multiple stations contend for a shared medium, and collisions are detected by monitoring abnormal voltage levels during transmission.

The minimum frame size is fixed at **64 bytes (512 bits)** to ensure that the sender is *still transmitting* when the collision signal from the farthest station returns. The minimum frame transmission time must be at least equal to the **round-trip propagation delay (slot time = 51.2 μs)**.

$$
T_t \geq 2T_p \implies L_{\min} = R \cdot 2T_p = 10\,\text{Mbps} \times 51.2\,\mu s = 512\,\text{bits} = 64\,\text{bytes}
$$

If frames were shorter, the sender would finish transmitting *before* the collision signal arrived, and the collision would go undetected — corrupting the network silently.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is Binary Exponential Backoff? Why is the backoff range "frozen" at 1023 slots after 10 collisions?**

**Model Answer:**

**Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** is the retransmission algorithm used by CSMA/CD after a collision. After the $k^{th}$ collision, a station waits for a random integer $r$ slot times, where:

$$
r \in \bigl[0,\ 2^{\min(k,10)} - 1\bigr]
$$

This is called *binary exponential* because the range doubles after each collision, growing as powers of 2 (1, 3, 7, 15, …, 1023).

The range is **frozen at 1023** (i.e., $2^{10}-1$) after 10 collisions to:
1. **Prevent unbounded delay** — without a cap, the range could explode to millions of slots, making the system unusable.
2. **Bound the maximum access time** — guaranteeing a deterministic upper bound on how long any station waits.
3. **Maintain network stability** under persistent congestion; if 10 retries fail, the network is severely overloaded and a frame drop (after 16 retries) is preferable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, every Part B question in a 14-mark slot offers an internal choice between two alternatives. Both are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working of CSMA/CD with a suitable state-transition diagram. Discuss the role of the **Jam Signal** and the **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1 km long 10 Mbps CSMA/CD network has a signal propagation speed of $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Frames are 1500 bytes long. Compute: (i) one-way propagation delay, (ii) round-trip delay (slot time), (iii) the value of parameter $a$, and (iv) approximate throughput efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

CSMA/CD operates as a **state machine** with five states: **Idle → Sensing → Transmitting → Colliding → Backoff**.

**State 1 — Idle / Sensing:**
The station continuously monitors the carrier. If the medium is busy, it stays in this state.

**State 2 — Wait IFG:**
When the medium becomes idle, the station waits for the **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG) of 9.6 μs**. The IFG is a mandatory idle period between frames that:
- Allows the receiver's clock recovery circuits to resynchronize.
- Provides breathing room so the medium is *truly* idle before a new transmission.
- Prevents a station from monopolizing the medium.

**State 3 — Transmitting:**
The station begins sending the frame bit-by-bit. Throughout transmission, it monitors the medium.

**State 4 — Colliding (Jam Signal Phase):**
If the station detects a voltage anomaly (collision), it:
1. Aborts the current frame.
2. **Transmits a 32-bit jam signal** — a deliberately recognizable bit pattern.

The **Jam Signal** plays two critical roles:
- It **ensures that the collision is long enough** (at least 32 bits) for *every* other station to detect it. Without it, a very short collision waveform could be missed by stations far from the collision point.
- It **enforces a uniform response** — all colliding stations recognize the jam and abort, instead of some continuing blindly.

**State 5 — Backoff:**
After jamming, the station applies the BEB algorithm and returns to State 1.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $d = 1\,\text{km} = 1000\,\text{m}$
- $R = 10\,\text{Mbps}$
- $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $L = 1500$ bytes $= 12000$ bits

**(i) One-way propagation delay:**

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 5\,\mu s
$$

**(ii) Round-trip delay (slot time):**

$$
\tau = 2 \cdot T_p = 2 \times 5\,\mu s = 10\,\mu s
$$

**(iii) Parameter $a$:**

First, compute frame transmission time:

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{10 \times 10^6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1200\,\mu s
$$

Then:

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{5\,\mu s}{1200\,\mu s} = 4.167 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00417
$$

**(iv) Throughput efficiency:**

$$
S = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.00417} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0268} = \frac{1}{1.0268} \approx 0.9738
$$

$$
\boxed{S \approx 97.38\%}
$$

This is high efficiency because $a$ is very small (propagation delay is tiny compared to frame time).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the Binary Exponential Backoff algorithm. A station suffers **5 consecutive collisions** while transmitting. What is the range of slot times it must wait? If the slot time is 51.2 μs, what is the **maximum** possible wait time? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **collision detection** in CSMA/CD. A 10BASE5 Ethernet has a maximum segment length of 500 m and uses a propagation speed of $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Verify whether the 64-byte minimum frame is sufficient to detect collisions, assuming a single segment only. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**BEB Algorithm — Conceptual Explanation:**

After the $k^{th}$ collision, the colliding station picks a random integer $r$ from the range:

$$
r \in [0,\ 2^{\min(k,10)} - 1]
$$

and waits for $r$ slot times before attempting retransmission. The exponential growth ensures that under heavy contention, the probability of two stations picking the same backoff value drops dramatically.

**Diagram:**

```
Collision Count k   →   Backoff Range (slot times)
   1                →   [0, 1]
   2                →   [0, 3]
   3                →   [0, 7]
   4                →   [0, 15]
   5                →   [0, 31]      ← Our case
   ...
  10                →   [0, 1023]
  ≥10               →   [0, 1023]    (capped)
  16                →   Frame dropped
```

**For 5 consecutive collisions:**

$$
r \in [0,\ 2^5 - 1] = [0,\ 31]
$$

So the station waits between **0 and 31 slot times**.

**Maximum wait time:**

$$
T_{\text{wait,max}} = 31 \times \tau = 31 \times 51.2\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{\text{wait,max}} = 1587.2\,\mu s = 1.5872\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{wait,max}} = 1.5872\,\text{ms}}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Definition of Collision Detection:**

Collision Detection is the mechanism by which a transmitting station monitors the medium *during its own transmission* and detects an anomalous DC voltage level (approximately twice the nominal signal) indicating that another station has transmitted simultaneously. Upon detection, the station aborts the frame and transmits a 32-bit jam signal.

**Given (10BASE5, single 500 m segment):**

- $d = 500$ m
- $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $R = 10$ Mbps
- Minimum frame $L = 64$ bytes $= 512$ bits

**Step 1 — Propagation delay:**

$$
T_p = \frac{500}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 2.5\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip delay:**

$$
2T_p = 2 \times 2.5\,\mu s = 5\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Frame transmission time:**

$$
T_t = \frac{512}{10 \times 10^6} = 51.2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 51.2\,\mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

For collision detection to work:

$$
T_t \geq 2T_p
$$

$$
51.2\,\mu s \geq 5\,\mu s \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result:** The 64-byte minimum frame is **more than sufficient** for a 500 m segment. The frame takes 51.2 μs to transmit, but the collision signal returns in only 5 μs, so the sender is still transmitting when the collision is detected. The 64-byte minimum is actually engineered for the **maximum 10BASE5 segment of 2500 m** (round-trip ≈ 25 μs), and is therefore *over-provisioned* for shorter segments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "round-trip" factor:** Many students use $T_p$ instead of $2T_p$ in the minimum frame size formula. **Always use $2T_p$** because the collision signal must travel *back* to the sender.
> 2. **Mixing up 1-persistent vs. non-persistent CSMA:** Pure CSMA (no CD) is 1-persistent, but CSMA/CD has slightly different efficiency formulas. The Bux-local model $S = 1/(1 + 6.44a)$ is the **CSMA/CD** formula.
> 3. **Skipping units in backoff problems:** Always express backoff in both *slot count* and *microseconds* for full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the cap at $k=10$:** Students compute $2^{16}-1 = 65535$ as the backoff range. Wrong — the range caps at $1023$ starting from $k=10$.
> 5. **Conflating Jam Signal with the corrupted frame:** The jam signal is *additional*, sent *after* aborting the original frame. It is not the corrupted bits themselves.
> 6. **Forgetting IFG:** Many students omit the 9.6 μs Inter-Frame Gap in timing diagrams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CSMA/CD**

- ✅ **Full Form:** Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection
- ✅ **Layer:** Data Link Layer (MAC Sublayer) — IEEE 802.3
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Sense before transmit, multiple stations share medium, detect collisions during transmit
- ✅ **Minimum Frame Size:** 64 bytes (512 bits) — chosen so that $T_t \geq 2T_p$
- ✅ **Slot Time:** 51.2 μs at 10 Mbps; equals round-trip propagation delay of maximum segment
- ✅ **Jam Signal:** 32-bit pattern, 3.2 μs long, ensures all stations detect collision
- ✅ **IFG:** 9.6 μs mandatory idle between consecutive frames
- ✅ **Backoff Formula:** $r \in [0,\ 2^{\min(k,10)} - 1]$ slot times
- ✅ **Backoff Cap:** Range freezes at 1023 slots after 10 collisions
- ✅ **Max Retries:** 16 — frame is dropped thereafter
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $S = 1/(1 + 6.44 \cdot a)$ where $a = T_p / T_t$
- ✅ **Signal Velocity in Copper:** $2 \times 10^8$ m/s (use $2 \times 10^8$, not $3 \times 10^8$ — that's for vacuum)
- ✅ **Modern Relevance:** Full-duplex switched Ethernet has *eliminated* CSMA/CD because each link is point-to-point
- ✅ **Variants:** 1-persistent (Ethernet), non-persistent, p-persistent (slotted) — all theoretical variants; Ethernet uses 1-persistent
- ✅ **Difference from CSMA/CA:** CD *detects* collisions (used in wired Ethernet); CA *avoids* collisions (used in wireless where detection is impossible due to hidden terminal problem)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision-Free Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Collision-Free Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Collision-Free Protocol** is a class of Medium Access Control (MAC) sub-layer protocols that **deterministically guarantee that exactly one station transmits on the shared broadcast channel at any given instant of time**, thereby entirely eliminating the possibility of frame collisions at the physical layer.

Unlike contention-based protocols (Pure ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD) where stations compete probabilistically for the channel and collisions waste bandwidth, collision-free protocols use **reservation, token circulation, or bitwise arbitration** to schedule transmissions in a conflict-free manner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This topic falls under *Data Link Layer - MAC Sublayer* in Module 2. Students must clearly distinguish **collision-free** (deterministic scheduling) from **collision-handling** (CSMA/CD with backoff) protocols. The two major categories in scope are the **Reservation Protocols** and the **Token-Based Protocols**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Raised-Hand Classroom" Model

Imagine a classroom with 8 students and one teacher. The teacher cannot allow all students to talk simultaneously (chaos = collision). 

**Contention Analogy (CSMA):** Students just start talking when they want — sometimes two speak at once (collision) and both stop, retry later. Simple but wastes time.

**Collision-Free Analogy (Bitmap):** A "speaking chart" is drawn on the board with 8 boxes, one per student. Each student who wants to speak places a check mark in their own box. Once the chart is filled, the teacher looks at the chart and calls students in order. **No two students can speak at the same time by design.**

**Collision-Free Analogy (Token Ring):** A single "talking stick" (token) is passed around the room. Only the student holding the stick may speak. When done, they pass the stick to the next student. **Absolute order, no collisions possible.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of stations | $N$ | dimensionless | Protocol scalability parameter |
| Channel bit rate | $R$ | **Mbps / Gbps** | Raw physical capacity |
| Propagation delay | $p$ | seconds | One-way end-to-end delay |
| Frame transmission time | $T_f$ | seconds | Time to push one frame onto wire |
| Contention period | $N$ bits | $N / R$ seconds | Bitmap overhead |
| Ring latency | $\tau$ | seconds | Token ring timing parameter |
| Protocol efficiency | $S$ | $\in [0, 1]$ | Throughput-to-capacity ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Study Collision-Free Protocols?**
> In **wireless networks**, collision detection (CSMA/CD) is physically impossible because stations cannot simultaneously transmit and listen on the same frequency. Collision-free protocols (especially **Token Ring/IEEE 802.5**, **Token Bus/IEEE 802.4**, and **Bit-Map reservation**) form the historical and conceptual backbone of scheduled MAC access, including the scheduling principles behind **5G NR, Wi-Fi HCF (802.11e), and deterministic Ethernet (TSN)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Collision-Free Protocols

The literature (Tanenbaum, Stallings) classifies collision-free MAC protocols into **two principal families**:

#### Family A — Reservation Protocols
- **A.1 Bit-Map Protocol** (also called *Tannenbaum's Reservation Scheme*)
- **A.2 Binary Countdown Protocol**
- **A.3 BRAP (Broadcast Recognition with Alternating Priorities)**

#### Family B — Token-Based Protocols
- **B.1 Token Ring Protocol** (IEEE 802.5)
- **B.2 Token Bus Protocol** (IEEE 802.4)

### 2.2 Detailed Protocol Analysis

#### 2.2.1 The Bit-Map Protocol (Reservation Protocol)

**Operational Concept:**
1. The shared channel alternates between two phases: a **Contention Period (CP)** of exactly $N$ bit-slots and a **Data Transmission Period**.
2. In contention period slot $i$, station $i$ broadcasts a "1" bit **iff** it has a queued frame; otherwise it broadcasts "0".
3. Every station monitors the entire $N$-bit contention period and learns the **complete reservation schedule**.
4. Transmission order is then strictly the **numerical order** of station indices that signaled "1".
5. After the last frame is transmitted, a new $N$-bit contention period begins.

**Key Property:** All stations know the global order in advance — **zero collision is mathematically possible** because only one station transmits in any data slot.

**Throughput Equation Derivation:**

Let each data frame take $T_f$ seconds to transmit. The contention period consumes $N$ bit-times, i.e., $N / R$ seconds. Suppose $k$ out of $N$ stations have frames to send in a given cycle.

The total cycle time is:
$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{N}{R} + k \cdot T_f$$

Hence the **channel utilization / efficiency** is:
$$S = \frac{k \cdot T_f}{\frac{N}{R} + k \cdot T_f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive Insight:** Notice that the contention period overhead is **incurred once per cycle, not per frame**. So for heavy traffic ($k \to N$), overhead is amortized and $S \to 1$. For light traffic ($k=1$), efficiency drops to $T_f / (N/R + T_f)$.

#### 2.2.2 The Binary Countdown Protocol

**Operational Concept:**
- Each station has a unique **binary address** of $\log_2 N$ bits.
- A station wishing to transmit broadcasts its full address one bit at a time, starting from the **most significant bit (MSB)**.
- Stations perform a **wired-OR (logical OR)** of all transmitted addresses on the channel.
- The rule: if a station sees a "1" on the channel in a bit position where it transmitted "0", it has **lost the arbitration** and drops out for the rest of this round.
- After $\log_2 N$ bit-times, exactly **one station** remains — the one with the highest binary address among contenders.
- That station transmits its frame; then a new arbitration round begins.

**Efficiency:**
$$S = \frac{T_f}{\frac{\log_2 N}{R} + T_f}$$

The arbitration overhead is only $\log_2 N$ bits — a **dramatic reduction** compared to $N$ bits in Bit-Map, especially for large $N$.

#### 2.2.3 The Token Ring Protocol (IEEE 802.5)

**Operational Concept:**
- Stations are connected in a **logical ring topology** (each station knows its predecessor and successor).
- A small control frame called the **Token** (3 bytes in IEEE 802.5) circulates continuously around the ring.
- A station may transmit **only when it holds the token**.
- After transmitting its frame (or after a maximum token-holding time, **THT**), the station passes the token to its logical successor.
- The transmitted frame circulates back to the sender, which removes it from the ring and then releases the token.

**Critical Timing Parameters:**

- **Ring Latency** $\tau$ = sum of propagation delays + bit-times of all stations' 1-bit buffers.
$$\tau = \frac{d}{v} + N \cdot t_b$$
where $d$ = total ring length, $v$ = signal propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in cable), $t_b$ = 1-bit processing delay per station.

- **Condition for token to keep circulating (no idle time):**
$$T_f \geq \tau$$
  If a frame is shorter than $\tau$, the token can theoretically make multiple rotations per frame.

- **Efficiency (single frame case):**
$$S = \frac{T_f}{T_f + \tau}$$

- **Efficiency (multi-frame case, where $k$ stations each have one frame):**
$$S = \frac{k \cdot T_f}{k \cdot T_f + \tau}$$

#### 2.2.4 The Token Bus Protocol (IEEE 802.4)

- Uses **physical bus topology** but **logical ring** ordering of stations.
- A **Token Frame** is passed between stations in logical-ring order over the shared bus.
- After transmitting, a station passes the token to the **next station in the logical order**, which may be physically distant on the bus.
- Used in **MAP (Manufacturing Automation Protocol)** for factory-floor deterministic networking.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Protocol | Overhead Per Cycle | Efficiency $S$ | Best Use Case | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit-Map** | $N$ bit-times | $S = \dfrac{k \cdot T_f}{N/R + k \cdot T_f}$ | Low-to-medium $N$ | Station addition changes map length |
| **Binary Countdown** | $\log_2 N$ bit-times | $S = \dfrac{T_f}{(\log_2 N)/R + T_f}$ | Large $N$ | Address-length dependency |
| **Token Ring** | $\tau$ (ring latency) | $S = \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + \tau}$ | Real-time / industrial | Token loss / station failure |
| **Token Bus** | Token propagation | $S = \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + \tau_{\text{bus}}}$ | Factory LAN | Ring maintenance overhead |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in Real Systems:** Token Ring principles survive in modern **Resilient Packet Ring (RPR / IEEE 802.17)**, **FDDI**, and **Audio Video Bridging (AVB / TSN)**. Binary Countdown's arbitration idea is the conceptual ancestor of **CAN bus (Controller Area Network)** used in automobiles and **EtherCAT** industrial Ethernet.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bit-Map Protocol Efficiency

**Given:**
- $N$ = number of stations on the shared medium.
- $R$ = channel bit rate (bits/second).
- $T_f$ = frame transmission time.
- $k$ = number of stations having a ready frame in a given cycle.

**Find:** Channel utilization $S$.

**Step 1 — Contention Period Duration:**

Each station gets one dedicated 1-bit slot in the contention period. Total slots = $N$.

$$\text{Contention Period} = \frac{N}{R} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Data Transmission Duration:**

Only the $k$ stations that broadcast "1" transmit, one after the other, each requiring $T_f$ seconds.

$$\text{Data Period} = k \cdot T_f \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Total Cycle Time:**

By the principle of additive time-sharing on a single broadcast channel:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{N}{R} + k \cdot T_f$$

**Step 4 — Channel Utilization:**

Utilization is the fraction of cycle time used for **useful data** (not overhead). 

$$S = \frac{\text{Useful transmission time}}{\text{Total cycle time}}$$

$$S = \frac{k \cdot T_f}{\dfrac{N}{R} + k \cdot T_f}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting Cases:**

- **Light load** ($k = 1$):
$$S_{\text{low}} = \frac{T_f}{N/R + T_f}$$
- **Heavy load** ($k = N$):
$$S_{\text{high}} = \frac{N \cdot T_f}{N/R + N \cdot T_f} = \frac{T_f}{1/R + T_f} \xrightarrow{R \to \infty} 1$$
- **Special case** $T_f = N/R$ (frame length equals contention period):
$$S = \frac{k}{1 + k} \quad\Rightarrow\quad S_{\text{full}} = \frac{N}{N+1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Verification:** In the best case, every station always has a frame, contention slots are filled with 1s, and the channel carries $N$ frames back-to-back with only $N$ bits of overhead — excellent efficiency for high load.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Token Ring Latency and Efficiency

**Given:**
- Total ring cable length = $L$ meters.
- Signal propagation speed on cable = $v$ m/s.
- $N$ stations on the ring, each introducing a **1-bit processing delay** as the bit travels through the station's repeater.
- Channel bit rate = $R$ bps.
- 1-bit time = $t_b = 1/R$ seconds.
- Frame transmission time = $T_f$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Compute Ring Latency Components:**

$$\tau_{\text{prop}} = \frac{L}{v}$$

$$\tau_{\text{station}} = N \cdot t_b = \frac{N}{R}$$

$$\tau = \tau_{\text{prop}} + \tau_{\text{station}} = \frac{L}{v} + \frac{N}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Frame Round-Trip Condition:**

A frame is $T_f$ seconds long. The sender begins transmitting at time $t=0$; the first bit returns at time $t = \tau$ (after traveling around the ring). The last bit is transmitted at $t = T_f$ and returns at $t = T_f + \tau$.

**Step 3 — Single-Frame Efficiency:**

If only **one** station has data in a cycle, the cycle consists of:
- Token circulation: $\tau$ seconds (one full trip back to the sender's perspective).
- Frame transmission: $T_f$ seconds.
- Frame return: $\tau$ seconds (sender must wait for its own frame to return before releasing token).

Total cycle:
$$T_{\text{cycle}} = T_f + \tau$$

Efficiency:
$$S_1 = \frac{T_f}{T_f + \tau}$$

**Step 4 — Multi-Frame Efficiency ($k$ stations each with one frame):**

Each station gets a turn; after each station's frame completes a round trip, the next station captures the token. Total cycle:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = k \cdot (T_f + \tau) - \tau = k \cdot T_f + k \cdot \tau - \tau$$

> The first station releases the token just after its frame returns; the last station's cycle is fully counted. A cleaner model is:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = k \cdot T_f + \tau$$

Efficiency:
$$S_k = \frac{k \cdot T_f}{k \cdot T_f + \tau}$$

**Step 5 — Heavy-Load Limit:**

As $k \to \infty$:
$$\lim_{k \to \infty} S_k = \lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{k \cdot T_f}{k \cdot T_f + \tau} = 1$$

**Step 6 — Practical Numerical Example:**

Suppose $L = 1000$ m, $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, $N = 50$ stations, $R = 4$ Mbps, $T_f = 1$ ms.

$$\tau_{\text{prop}} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 5 \, \mu s$$

$$\tau_{\text{station}} = \frac{50}{4 \times 10^6} = 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 12.5 \, \mu s$$

$$\tau = 5 + 12.5 = 17.5 \, \mu s$$

$$S_1 = \frac{1000 \, \mu s}{1000 \, \mu s + 17.5 \, \mu s} = \frac{1000}{1017.5} \approx 0.983$$

Excellent single-frame efficiency — 98.3% of channel capacity is used.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Binary Countdown Arbitration Overhead

**Given:** $N$ stations, each with a unique $\log_2 N$-bit address.

**Step 1 — Number of Arbitration Bits:**

A station "wins" when it has the highest address among contenders. The MSB comparison happens at bit position $\log_2 N$, next at $\log_2 N - 1$, etc. After the last (LSB) comparison:

$$\text{Arbitration Bits} = \log_2 N$$

**Step 2 — Arbitration Time:**

$$T_{\text{arb}} = \frac{\log_2 N}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Total Time to Transmit One Frame (Single Winner):**

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{arb}} + T_f = \frac{\log_2 N}{R} + T_f$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency:**

$$S = \frac{T_f}{T_f + \dfrac{\log_2 N}{R}}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Bit-Map:**

For $N = 1024$ stations:
- Bit-Map overhead: $1024$ bits.
- Binary Countdown overhead: $\log_2 1024 = 10$ bits.

**Binary Countdown is ~102× more efficient in arbitration overhead** at the cost of requiring unique binary addresses.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Protocol

| Scenario | Recommended Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ small, bursty traffic | **Bit-Map** | Simple, no address management |
| $N$ large, fixed addresses | **Binary Countdown** | $\log_2 N$ overhead is minimal |
| Real-time / voice traffic | **Token Ring** | Deterministic upper-bound delay |
| Factory-floor (bus media) | **Token Bus** | Token semantics + bus cabling |
| Wireless LAN | **None of above** (CSMA/CA used) | Collision *detection* is impossible |
| Embedded automotive | **CAN (Binary Countdown variant)** | Non-destructive arbitration |
