## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"What are collision-free protocols? Why are they preferred over contention-based protocols in real-time applications?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Collision-free protocols are a class of MAC protocols that **deterministically schedule channel access** among stations such that **only one station transmits at any given time**, eliminating the possibility of frame collisions.

In contention-based protocols like Pure ALOHA or CSMA, collisions are resolved by retransmission with backoff, introducing **unbounded random delays** that are unacceptable for real-time traffic (voice, video, industrial control). Collision-free protocols (e.g., Token Ring) provide **deterministic upper-bound delay guarantees** because the maximum wait time is bounded by the token rotation time.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — contrast with contention; 1 mark — real-time relevance.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Compare the Bit-Map Protocol and Binary Countdown Protocol with respect to arbitration overhead."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Bit-Map Protocol:** The contention period consists of exactly $N$ bit slots (one per station). Overhead per cycle = $N$ bits.
- **Binary Countdown Protocol:** Arbitration uses only $\log_2 N$ bit-times because stations compare addresses bit-by-bit and drop out as soon as they lose.

**Key Difference:** Binary Countdown has **dramatically lower overhead** for large $N$. For $N = 1024$ stations, Bit-Map needs 1024 bits vs. Binary Countdown's 10 bits — a **~100× improvement**.

*(1 mark — Bit-Map overhead; 1 mark — Binary Countdown overhead; 1 mark — quantitative comparison.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) Explain the Bit-Map Protocol in detail. Derive an expression for its channel efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Bit-Map Protocol is a **reservation-based collision-free protocol** where stations take turns announcing their intention to transmit.

**Operation:**
1. The channel time alternates between a **Contention Period (CP)** of $N$ one-bit slots and a **Data Transmission Period**.
2. In slot $i$ of CP, station $i$ transmits a "1" if it has a frame queued, else a "0".
3. All $N$ stations monitor the entire CP and thus learn the **global reservation order**.
4. Stations transmit data frames in **ascending order of their station indices** that signaled "1".
5. Once the last queued frame is sent, a new $N$-bit contention period begins.

**Mathematical Derivation:**

Let:
- $R$ = bit rate of channel
- $T_f$ = frame transmission time
- $k$ = number of stations with frames ready
- $N$ = total number of stations

The contention period occupies:
$$T_{\text{CP}} = \frac{N}{R} \text{ seconds}$$

The data transmission period occupies:
$$T_{\text{data}} = k \cdot T_f$$

Total cycle time:
$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{N}{R} + k \cdot T_f$$

Channel utilization (efficiency) — fraction of time used for actual data:
$$S = \frac{k \cdot T_f}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{k \cdot T_f}{\dfrac{N}{R} + k \cdot T_f}$$

**Limiting Behavior:**
- Light load ($k = 1$): $S = T_f / (N/R + T_f)$ — efficiency is low.
- Heavy load ($k = N$): $S = N \cdot T_f / (N/R + N \cdot T_f) \to 1$ as $R$ grows or $T_f$ grows.

**Conclusion:** Bit-Map performs poorly for light load but achieves near-perfect efficiency under saturation, making it suitable for **moderately loaded LANs with predictable traffic**.

*(1 mark — operation, 2 marks — slot sequence, 2 marks — derivation steps, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — limiting case analysis.)*

---

**(b) Explain the Token Ring protocol with a neat diagram. Discuss its efficiency under (i) light load and (ii) heavy load. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) Operation:**

In a Token Ring network, stations are connected in a **logical ring**. A small control frame called the **token** (3 bytes in IEEE 802.5) circulates continuously among stations in a fixed order.

1. A station wishing to transmit **waits until it captures the token**.
2. It transmits its data frame onto the ring.
3. The frame propagates station-by-station around the ring; each intermediate station **repeats and forwards** the bits after a 1-bit delay.
4. The destination station **copies** the frame into its buffer and sets the **frame-copied bit** in the frame trailer.
5. The frame continues to circulate and **returns to the sender**.
6. The sender **removes the frame from the ring** (verifying acknowledgment bits) and then **releases a fresh token** onto the ring.

**Ring Latency:**
$$\tau = \frac{L}{v} + \frac{N}{R}$$
where $L$ = total cable length, $v$ = propagation velocity, $N$ = number of stations, $R$ = bit rate.

**Efficiency Analysis:**

For $k$ stations each having one frame, total cycle time:
$$T_{\text{cycle}} = k \cdot T_f + \tau$$

Efficiency:
$$S = \frac{k \cdot T_f}{k \cdot T_f + \tau}$$

**(i) Light Load ($k = 1$):**
$$S_1 = \frac{T_f}{T_f + \tau}$$
The overhead $\tau$ is a large fraction of cycle time → **lower efficiency**. For example, $T_f = 1000 \, \mu s$, $\tau = 50 \, \mu s$ gives $S_1 \approx 0.95$ (still high in practice because $\tau \ll T_f$ for typical frames).

**(ii) Heavy Load ($k \to \infty$):**
$$\lim_{k \to \infty} S_k = 1$$
The ring is always busy with back-to-back frames; the per-cycle overhead $\tau$ is amortized → **near-perfect efficiency**.

**Advantages of Token Ring:**
- Deterministic, bounded delay → ideal for real-time traffic.
- Fair access — every station gets a turn.
- Inherently collision-free.

**Disadvantages:**
- Single point of failure (token loss, station failure).
- Token recovery and ring maintenance add complexity.

*(1 mark — operation, 1 mark — diagram description, 2 marks — efficiency formula, 1.5 marks — light load, 1.5 marks — heavy load.)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) Describe the Binary Countdown Protocol. Show that it requires only $\log_2 N$ bit-times for arbitration. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Binary Countdown Protocol Operation:**

1. Every station is assigned a **unique binary address** of length $\log_2 N$ bits, where $N$ is the maximum number of stations.
2. All stations wishing to transmit begin broadcasting their address **one bit at a time, starting with the Most Significant Bit (MSB)**.
3. The channel performs a **logical OR (wired-OR)** of all incoming bits in each time slot.
4. **Arbitration Rule:** If a station transmits a "0" in a given bit position but observes a "1" on the channel, it has **lost the arbitration** (because some other station has a higher-order 1) and **drops out** of contention for the remainder of this round.
5. Surviving stations proceed to the next lower bit position and repeat.
6. After all $\log_2 N$ bit positions, **exactly one station remains** — the one with the highest address.
7. That station transmits its data frame in full.
8. After frame transmission, a new arbitration round begins.

**Proof of $\log_2 N$ Arbitration Length:**

- An address has exactly $\log_2 N$ bits.
- Each bit position is examined **exactly once** during the entire arbitration.
- At each bit position, all surviving stations transmit simultaneously — so the arbitration completes in **one bit-time per address bit**.
- Total arbitration time: $\log_2 N$ bit-times.

**Channel Efficiency:**

$$S = \frac{T_f}{T_f + \dfrac{\log_2 N}{R}}$$

**Comparison with Bit-Map:**

For $N = 1024$:
- Bit-Map: $1024$ bit-times of overhead.
- Binary Countdown: $\log_2 1024 = 10$ bit-times.
- **102× reduction in arbitration overhead.**

**Drawback:**
Requires every station to have a **statically assigned unique address**, and the higher-addressed station always wins (inherent priority bias unless addresses are rotated or randomized).

*(1.5 marks — operation steps, 2 marks — arbitration rule, 1.5 marks — proof of $\log_2 N$, 1 mark — efficiency formula, 1 mark — comparison.)*

---

**(b) Compare and contrast Token Ring, Token Bus, and Bit-Map protocols. State two real-world applications of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Token Ring (802.5) | Token Bus (802.4) | Bit-Map |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical Topology** | Ring (star-wired ring using MAUs) | Linear bus | Bus (broadcast) |
| **Logical Topology** | Ring | Ring | None (centralized map) |
| **Access Method** | Token circulation | Token circulation on bus | Reservation bitmap |
| **Overhead per cycle** | Ring latency $\tau$ | Token propagation delay | $N$ bit contention |
| **Efficiency formula** | $S = T_f / (T_f + \tau)$ | $S = T_f / (T_f + \tau_{\text{bus}})$ | $S = k T_f / (N/R + k T_f)$ |
| **Worst-case delay** | $N \cdot \text{THT}$ (deterministic) | $N \cdot \text{THT}$ (deterministic) | Bounded by $N/R$ per cycle |
| **Failure mode** | Token loss, station break | Token loss, bus break | Map size change on add/remove |
| **Station addition** | Complex (reconfigure ring) | Complex (logical ring update) | Simple (extend map) |
| **Real-time suitability** | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate |

**Real-World Applications:**

**Token Ring:**
- IBM Token Ring LANs (1980s–1990s corporate networks).
- FDDI (Fiber Distributed Data Interface) for campus backbone.
- Resilient Packet Ring (IEEE 802.17) for metro Ethernet.

**Token Bus:**
- Manufacturing Automation Protocol (MAP) in factory networks.
- ARCNET (Attached Resource Computer NET) for embedded industrial control.
- IEEE 802.4 in process control and SCADA systems.

**Bit-Map:**
- Satellite channel reservation systems.
- Bluetooth piconet master-managed scheduling.
- Reservation-based wireless MACs (HIPERLAN/1, early HFC networks).

**Conclusion:** Token-based protocols excel in **deterministic real-time environments**; Bit-Map is simpler and works well for **small-to-medium broadcast networks** with predictable membership.

*(2 marks — comparison table, 1.5 marks — applications per protocol × 3 = 4.5 marks, 0.5 mark — conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "collision-free" with "collision-detection"**: Students often mix up CSMA/CD (collision *detection* + backoff) with collision-free protocols (no collision *ever*). Be explicit: in collision-free protocols, **collisions are impossible by design**, not detected and recovered.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\log_2 N$ derivation logic**: For Binary Countdown, the arbitration takes $\log_2 N$ bit-times, **not** $N$ bit-times. Many students write "$N$ bit positions examined" — wrong, it is $\log_2 N$ **address bits**.
> 3. **Token Ring efficiency formula error**: A common mistake is $S = T_f / (T_f + \tau)$ without realizing this is the **single-frame** case. Multi-frame case is $S = k T_f / (k T_f + \tau)$. Examiners reward explicit statement of which case.
> 4. **Neglecting ring latency components**: $\tau$ has **two** terms: propagation ($L/v$) and station bit-delay ($N/R$). Losing either half loses 1.5 marks.
> 5. **Bit-Map ordering**: Students often incorrectly state that the order of transmission is arbitrary or based on the order of "1"s being seen. It is strictly by **station index**, not by arrival time. This is a frequently tested point.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Collision-Free Protocol Definition:** MAC protocol that **deterministically guarantees zero collisions** through reservation, token-passing, or bit-arbitration.
- **Two Principal Families:** (1) **Reservation Protocols** (Bit-Map, Binary Countdown) and (2) **Token Protocols** (Token Ring IEEE 802.5, Token Bus IEEE 802.4).
- **Bit-Map Overhead:** $N$ bit-times per cycle. Efficiency: $S = k T_f / (N/R + k T_f)$.
- **Binary Countdown Overhead:** $\log_2 N$ bit-times per cycle. Efficiency: $S = T_f / (T_f + \log_2 N / R)$. Winner has **highest binary address**.
- **Token Ring Latency:** $\tau = L/v + N/R$ (propagation + station bit-delays).
- **Token Ring Efficiency:** $S = k T_f / (k T_f + \tau)$, tends to 1 under heavy load.
- **Token Bus:** Physical bus + logical ring; token passes in logical order regardless of physical location.
- **Real-Time Suitability:** Token protocols give **deterministic bounded delay**; Bit-Map delay is bounded only by the contention period.
- **Wireless Limitation:** Pure collision-free protocols are **rare in wireless**; modern Wi-Fi uses **hybrid** approaches (polling in HCF, TDMA in 5G NR).
- **Modern Descendants:** CAN bus (binary countdown variant), Audio Video Bridging / Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN, IEEE 802.1Qbv), Resilient Packet Ring (IEEE 802.17).
- **KTU Exam Cue:** Always mention **both** the **contention/arbitration overhead** and the resulting **efficiency formula** in any long-answer question on this topic.
- **Mnemonic for Token Ring Steps:** **W**ait → **C**apture → **T**ransmit → **D**estination copies → **F**rame returns → **R**emove → **R**elease token (**WCTDFRR**).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Layer and Transport Layer, Network Layer Design Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Layer and Transport Layer, Network Layer Design Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Network Layer — The "Postal System" of the Internet

The **Network Layer** is Layer 3 of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection)** reference model and the **TCP/IP model**. Its primary job is to deliver packets from a source host to a destination host across one or more interconnected networks. It is the layer where **routing**, **logical addressing (IP)**, **forwarding**, and **internetworking** decisions are made.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The Network Layer is responsible for host-to-host packet delivery across multiple networks. It provides best-effort, connectionless delivery as its core Internet service.

### 1.1.1 The Transport Layer — The "Sorting Office" between Processes

The **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** sits directly above the Network Layer. While the Network Layer delivers packets *between machines*, the Transport Layer delivers data *between processes (applications)* running on those machines. It offers end-to-end communication services, multiplexing/demultiplexing, flow control, and reliability (in TCP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** Network Layer = Machine-to-Machine, Transport Layer = Process-to-Process.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Courier Network

Think of the entire internet as a **national courier company**:

- **Application Layer** = The person packaging a letter (your browser).
- **Transport Layer (TCP/UDP)** = The **registered post counter** — it stamps your letter with a *destination apartment number* (port) and decides whether to send a registered, signed letter (TCP) or a simple postcard (UDP).
- **Network Layer (IP)** = The **postal truck network** — the trucks follow highways (routing algorithms) and the letter has a *city-level address* (IP address). Trucks from different states cooperate through sorting hubs (routers).
- **Data Link Layer** = The **local delivery van** that moves letters between adjacent post offices.
- **Physical Layer** = The **actual road and tires**.

So when you watch a YouTube video, the *Transport Layer* decides that the data belongs to the Chrome browser on port `443`, and the *Network Layer* ensures it travels from a server in the USA to your laptop in Kerala.

---

## 1.3 Network Layer Design Issues — The 5 Pillars

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **five key design issues** that every network layer must address:

| # | Design Issue | One-Line Meaning |
|---|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Store-and-Forward Packet Switching** | Routers must receive the full packet before forwarding it. |
| 2 | **Services Provided to the Transport Layer** | What the network promises (connectionless vs connection-oriented). |
| 3 | **Implementation of Connectionless Service** | The Datagram subnet. |
| 4 | **Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service** | The Virtual-Circuit subnet. |
| 5 | **Comparison of Virtual-Circuit and Datagram Subnets** | Trade-offs. |

### 1.3.1 Store-and-Forward Packet Switching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A packet is transmitted by a router only **after the entire packet has been received** and its checksum verified. This is called *store-and-forward switching*.

**Intuition:** Imagine a railway station. A train (packet) cannot leave until all its bogies (data bits) have arrived and the station master has verified the train's manifest (checksum). A damaged packet is simply dropped.

### 1.3.2 Services Provided to the Transport Layer

The network layer offers **two classes of service** to the transport layer:

1. **Connectionless Service (Datagram Service):** Each packet is routed independently, like sending individual letters.
2. **Connection-Oriented Service (Virtual-Circuit Service):** A logical path is set up first, like a phone call, before any data flows.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Internet (IP)** uses the **connectionless datagram** model. **ATM** and **X.25** historically used the **virtual-circuit** model.

### 1.3.3 Implementation of Connectionless Service

Each router maintains a **routing table**. Every incoming packet is examined for its destination address, and the routing table is consulted to pick the next hop. **No path is reserved in advance.**

- The Internet Protocol (IP) is the canonical example.
- Packets may arrive **out of order** or be **lost** — the upper layers (TCP) fix this.

### 1.3.4 Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service

A **virtual circuit (VC)** is established before any data transfer. Three phases:

1. **Connection Setup** — A signaling protocol reserves resources and builds a path.
2. **Data Transfer** — All packets follow the same pre-computed path; each packet carries a **VC identifier**.
3. **Connection Teardown** — Resources are released.

### 1.3.5 Comparison: Virtual-Circuit vs Datagram

| Parameter | Virtual-Circuit (VC) | Datagram |
|-----------|----------------------|----------|
| Setup phase | **Required** | None |
| Addressing | Short VC identifier per packet | Full source + destination address per packet |
| Routing | All packets take **same path** | Each packet routed **independently** |
| State in routers | Per-connection state | Per-packet state only |
| Failure effect | All VCs through a failed router break | Packets rerouted around failure |
| Congestion control | Easier (resource reservation) | Harder (best-effort) |
| Example | X.25, Frame Relay, ATM | **IPv4, IPv6** |
| Analogy | Phone call | Postal letters |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Store-and-Forward Mechanics — Delay Components

When a packet of length **L bits** travels over a link of bandwidth **R bits/sec**, the **transmission delay** is:

$$T_t = \frac{L}{R} \text{ seconds}$$

The **end-to-end delay** over **N** links (with no queuing) is:

$$T_{end-to-end} = N \cdot \left( \frac{L}{R} + T_{prop} \right)$$

Where:
- $T_{prop} = d / s$ (propagation delay) — $d$ = link length, $s$ = signal propagation speed (~ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber).
- $L / R$ = transmission (store-and-forward) delay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The transmission delay **scales with packet size and inversely with bandwidth**. The propagation delay **scales with distance, not packet size**.

### 2.1.1 Why "Store-and-Forward"?

Routers must buffer (store) the entire packet in memory because:
1. The destination address is at the packet header's front — but in some cases (lookahead processing) the full header must be parsed.
2. **Bit errors** must be detected via the trailer CRC/checksum.
3. Forwarding decisions require the full packet context for **QoS** queues.

If routers used *cut-through switching*, the latency would drop, but error detection would be deferred.

## 2.2 Connectionless Service — The Datagram Subnet (IP Model)

**Operational steps:**

1. Source host attaches **destination IP address** to each packet.
2. Each router on the path independently:
   - Inspects the destination IP.
   - Performs a **longest-prefix match** in its routing table.
   - Forwards the packet on the chosen outgoing interface.
3. Packets from the same flow may take **different paths** (e.g., due to congestion or link failure).
4. Packets may arrive **out of order**, **duplicated**, or **lost**.

**Real-world utility:** The datagram model is **robust** and **stateless in routers**, which is why the global Internet — with millions of routers and unpredictable failures — uses it. It is the foundation of **IP, UDP, and modern cloud networking (SDN)**.

## 2.3 Connection-Oriented Service — The Virtual-Circuit Subnet

**Three phases:**

### Phase 1: Connection Setup
- Source sends a **CALL REQUEST** packet.
- Each router on the path:
  - Allocates a **VC number** (local identifier) for the connection.
  - Reserves **buffer space** and **bandwidth** (for QoS).
- Destination replies with **CALL ACCEPTED**.

### Phase 2: Data Transfer
- All data packets carry the **short VC identifier** (not full addresses).
- Each router looks up the VC number in a **VC table** (faster than longest-prefix match).

### Phase 3: Tear-down
- A **CLEAR REQUEST** propagates; all routers release their state.

**Real-world utility:** Used in **ATM**, **MPLS** (modern carrier networks), and **4G/5G core data planes** where deterministic performance and traffic engineering matter more than absolute simplicity.

## 2.4 Routing Algorithms — The Heart of the Network Layer

### 2.4.1 Optimality Principle & Sink Trees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimality Principle (Gallager 1977):** If router J is on the optimal path from I to K, then the path from J to K along the same route is also optimal.

The set of optimal paths from **all sources to a given destination** forms a **sink tree** rooted at the destination. The sink tree has **no loops**.

### 2.4.2 Shortest Path Routing — Dijkstra's Algorithm

Used by **OSPF** and as a baseline. Given a graph with non-negative edge weights, computes the shortest path from a source to all other nodes.

- **Data structures:** Distance vector $D[v]$ and predecessor $\pi[v]$.
- **Time complexity:** $O(V^2)$ with array, $O((V+E)\log V)$ with min-heap.
- **Step-by-step logic:**
  1. Initialize $D[source] = 0$, all others = $\infty$.
  2. Pick the unvisited node with minimum $D$ — call it $u$.
  3. For each neighbor $v$ of $u$, **relax** the edge: $D[v] = \min(D[v], D[u] + w(u,v))$.
  4. Mark $u$ visited. Repeat until all nodes visited or target reached.

### 2.4.3 Flooding

**Procedure:** Every incoming packet is sent out on **every outgoing line** except the one it arrived on.

- **Variants:**
  - **Selective Flooding:** Send only on lines going roughly in the right direction.
  - **Hop Counter:** Discard packets whose TTL (Time-To-Live) reaches 0.
  - **Sequence Number:** Discard duplicates seen already.
- **Pros:** Maximum robustness; guaranteed shortest path (in hop count).
- **Cons:** Generates enormous traffic — used in **military networks** and **ad-hoc wireless (DSR protocol)**.

### 2.4.4 Distance Vector Routing (Bellman-Ford)

Used by the original **ARPANET** and the **RIPv2** protocol.

- Each router maintains a **vector** of distances to all destinations.
- Periodically (every ~30 s for RIP), each router sends its entire vector to **direct neighbors only**.
- On receiving a neighbor's vector, the router updates its own via the **Bellman-Ford equation**:

$$D_x(y) = \min_v \left[ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \right]$$

Where $c(x,v)$ is the cost of link from $x$ to neighbor $v$, and $D_v(y)$ is neighbor $v$'s current distance to $y$.

**The Count-to-Infinity Problem:** A bad-news (link-failure) propagates extremely slowly. Solutions:
- **Poisoned Reverse:** If $X$ routes via $Y$ to reach $Z$, $X$ advertises $D_X(Z) = \infty$ to $Y$.
- **Split Horizon:** Never advertise a route back to the neighbor from which you learned it.
- **Triggered Updates:** Send updates immediately upon change, not on timer.

### 2.4.5 Link State Routing (Dijkstra per Router)

Used by **OSPF** and **IS-IS**. Five steps:

1. **Discover neighbors** and learn their network addresses (via HELLO packets).
2. **Measure the cost** (delay, bandwidth) to each neighbor (via ECHO packets).
3. **Construct an LSP (Link State Packet)** listing all neighbors and costs.
4. **Flood the LSP** to **every router** in the subnet (reliably, with acknowledgments and checksums).
5. **Compute shortest paths** from the complete map using Dijkstra's algorithm.

**Advantages over Distance Vector:** Faster convergence, no count-to-infinity, supports multiple metrics.

### 2.4.6 Hierarchical Routing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Internet has **millions of routers** — no router can store a route to every destination. The solution: **hierarchical routing**.

- Networks are grouped into **Autonomous Systems (ASes)**.
- Within an AS: **Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP)** — e.g., OSPF, IS-IS, RIP.
- Between ASes: **Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP)** — the only one used today is **BGP-4** (Border Gateway Protocol).

A router in a 3-level hierarchy (region → cluster → router) needs only $O(K \cdot \log_K N)$ table entries, not $O(N)$.

### 2.4.7 Broadcast Routing

Methods:
- **Flooding** (expensive).
- **Multidestination Routing** (one packet per group).
- **Spanning Tree** (broadcast only on tree edges — efficient, used in Ethernet).
- **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF):** Forward a broadcast only if it arrived on the interface that leads back to the source via the shortest path; otherwise drop. Effectively prunes flood to a tree.
- **Spanning-Tree Broadcast** (used in switched Ethernet with STP).

### 2.4.8 Multicast Routing

- **Group management** via **IGMP** (IPv4) or **MLD** (IPv6).
- **Routing algorithms:**
  - **Flood-and-prune** (DVMRP — Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol).
  - **Sparse-mode PIM (Protocol-Independent Multicast):** Uses a **rendezvous point (RP)**; receivers send JOIN messages; source tree is built on demand.
  - **Core-Based Trees (CBT):** Single shared tree per group.

## 2.5 Congestion Control — The "Traffic Jam" Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Network congestion occurs when the number of packets sent into the subnet exceeds its carrying capacity.

### 2.5.1 General Principles (Yang & Reddy 1995 Taxonomy)

Two control loci:
1. **Open-loop** solutions — decide in advance, no feedback.
2. **Closed-loop** solutions — react to feedback.

### 2.5.2 Congestion Prevention Policies (4 Layers)

| Layer | Policies |
|-------|----------|
| **Network Layer** | Subnet-wide vs virtual-circuit aware; packet queuing/discard; routing algorithm choice |
| **Transport Layer** | Retransmission policy; out-of-order caching; ack policy; flow control |
| **Application Layer** | Application behavior (e.g., video bitrate adaptation) |
| **MAC Layer** | Acknowledgment, retransmission, CSMA/CD, scheduling |

### 2.5.3 Congestion Control in Virtual-Circuit Subnets

- **Admission Control:** Refuse new VCs if the subnet is congested.
- **Reservation:** VCs reserve bandwidth along the path (e.g., RSVP).
- **Bandwidth Brokering:** Negotiate QoS parameters (peak rate, burst size).

### 2.5.4 Congestion Control in Datagram Subnets

- **Congestion-Aware Routing:** Shift traffic away from congested areas.
- **Traffic-Aware Routing:** Use weighted metrics (e.g., delay, not just hop count).
- **Random Early Detection (RED):** Proactively drop packets with rising probability as the average queue grows. **AQM** (Active Queue Management).
- **Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN):** Mark packets (don't drop!) to signal senders to slow down.

### 2.5.5 Load Shedding

> [!WARNING]
> When the buffer overflows, the router must discard packets. Choosing **which** to discard is the art of **load shedding**.

- **Wine vs Milk:** Wine (email, file transfer) is fine to throw away — TCP will retransmit. Milk (real-time audio/video) is bad to throw away — by the time retransmission arrives, it's too late.
- **Priority-based shedding:** Drop the lowest-priority packets first.
- **RED/PIE/CoDel:** Modern AQM algorithms that prevent bufferbloat.

### 2.5.6 Jitter Control

**Jitter** = variation in packet arrival times. Real-time apps (VoIP, video) are highly sensitive.

- **Playback buffer** at receiver: Packets are stored and played out at a steady rate.
- **Timestamping + smoothing:** Receiver buffers for a fixed playback delay (e.g., 50 ms) before playing.

## 2.6 Internetworking — Bridging Heterogeneous Networks

**Goal:** Connect **different network technologies** (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, ATM, satellite) under a **universal protocol** (IP).

### 2.6.1 How Networks Differ

- **Service model** (connectionless vs connection-oriented).
- **Addressing** (flat vs hierarchical, variable-length vs fixed).
- **Packet size** (Ethernet 1500 B vs ATM 48 B cells).
- **QoS** (best-effort vs guaranteed bandwidth).
- **Error handling** (reliable vs unreliable links).

### 2.6.2 Concatenated Virtual Circuits

Set up a VC from source to destination, hopping through heterogeneous networks. The **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** rejected this in favor of the **datagram approach**.

### 2.6.3 Connectionless Internetworking

**Tunnels:** Encapsulate a packet of one network protocol inside another. E.g., IPv6-in-IPv4 tunneling.

### 2.6.4 Fragmentation and Reassembly

- **Path MTU Discovery** to avoid fragmentation when possible.
- **Fragmentation** occurs when a packet is too large for the next network's MTU.
- IPv4: **Fragmented at the router, reassembled only at the destination** (can be inefficient).
- IPv6: **No router fragmentation** — sender must do path MTU discovery.

### 2.6.5 Internetworking with IPv4 — Address Types

| Class | Leading Bits | Range (1st Octet) | Default Mask | Use |
|-------|--------------|-------------------|--------------|-----|
| **A** | 0 | 0–127 | /8 | Huge networks (legacy) |
| **B** | 10 | 128–191 | /16 | Medium networks |
| **C** | 110 | 192–223 | /24 | Small networks |
| **D** | 1110 | 224–239 | — | Multicast |
| **E** | 1111 | 240–255 | — | Experimental |

> [!NOTE]
> **Classful addressing is now obsolete** — replaced by **CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing)** in 1993 (RFC 1518/1519).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| Transmission delay | $T_t = L / R$ | seconds |
| Propagation delay | $T_{prop} = d / s$ | seconds |
| End-to-end delay (N hops) | $T = N \cdot (L/R + d/s)$ | seconds |
| Throughput (Stop-and-Wait) | $W = L / (L/R + 2T_{prop}) = \text{eff} \cdot R$ | bps |
| Sliding Window efficiency | $\eta = 1$ if $W \ge 2a+1$ | where $a = T_{prop}/T_t$ |
| Bellman-Ford update | $D_x(y) = \min_v [c(x,v) + D_v(y)]$ | distances |
| Dijkstra complexity | $O(V^2)$ or $O((V+E)\log V)$ | nodes/edges |
| Max RIP hops | 15 (16 = unreachable) | hops |
| IPv4 address space | $2^{32}$ = 4,294,967,296 | addresses |
| IPv6 address space | $2^{128}$ ≈ $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | addresses |
| Default TTL (IPv4) | 64 or 255 | hops |
| TCP segment header | 20–60 bytes | bytes |
| UDP header | 8 bytes | bytes |
| TCP port range | 0–65535 | ports |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Delay for a Store-and-Forward Network

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A packet of length $L = 4000$ bits is sent over a path of $N = 4$ links. Each link has bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps and length $d = 100$ km. Signal propagation speed is $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Find the end-to-end delay, ignoring queuing.

### Step 1: Identify the Per-Hop Delays
For a single hop, the total delay = transmission delay + propagation delay.

**Transmission Delay (time to push all bits onto the wire):**

$$T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{4000}{1 \times 10^6} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms}$$

**Propagation Delay (time for a bit to traverse the physical medium):**

$$T_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2: Per-Hop Total

$$T_{hop} = T_t + T_{prop} = 4 + 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3: End-to-End (N = 4 store-and-forward routers)

$$T_{end} = N \cdot T_{hop} = 4 \times 4.5 = 18 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

> The 4 ms transmission delay *per hop* dominates because the packet is large relative to the link bandwidth. Propagation (0.5 ms) is a small component for terrestrial fiber.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dijkstra's Algorithm — Worked Example

**Problem:** Compute shortest paths from source node **A** in the graph below.

| Edge | A-B | A-C | B-D | B-E | C-D | C-F | D-E | E-F | D-F |
|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Cost | 4 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 1 | 6 | 2 | 4 | 7 |

### Step 1: Initialize
- $D[A] = 0$, all others = $\infty$.
- Visited set $V = \{ \}$.

### Step 2: Pick min unvisited
Pick **A** ($D=0$). Relax neighbors:
- $D[B] = \min(\infty, 0+4) = 4$
- $D[C] = \min(\infty, 0+2) = 2$
- Predecessors: $\pi[B]=A, \pi[C]=A$

### Step 3: Pick next min unvisited
Pick **C** ($D=2$). Relax:
- $D[D] = \min(\infty, 2+1) = 3$, $\pi[D] = C$
- $D[F] = \min(\infty, 2+6) = 8$, $\pi[F] = C$

### Step 4: Next pick
Pick **D** ($D=3$). Relax:
- $D[B] = \min(4, 3+3) = 4$ (no change)
- $D[E] = \min(\infty, 3+2) = 5$, $\pi[E] = D$
- $D[F] = \min(8, 3+7) = 8$ (no change)

### Step 5: Next pick
Pick **B** ($D=4$). Relax:
- $D[E] = \min(5, 4+5) = 5$ (no change)
- $D[D] = 3$ (no change)

### Step 6: Next pick
Pick **E** ($D=5$). Relax:
- $D[F] = \min(8, 5+4) = 8$ (no change)

### Step 7: Next pick
Pick **F** ($D=8$). Done.

### Final Shortest Paths from A

| Destination | Distance | Path |
|-------------|----------|------|
| A | 0 | A |
| B | 4 | A → B |
| C | 2 | A → C |
| D | 3 | A → C → D |
| E | 5 | A → C → D → E |
| F | 8 | A → C → F (or A→C→D→E→F = 12, so 8 wins) |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Distance Vector — Count-to-Infinity

**Scenario:** Three routers A, B, C in a line. Initially, all see the link cost to X (off-screen) via the network. Then link A-B goes down.

### Initial State
- A: $D_A(X) = 1$ via B
- B: $D_B(X) = 2$ via A
- C: $D_C(X) = 3$ via B

### Step 1: A-B link fails
- A sets $D_A(X) = \infty$ (no path).
- B's table still says $D_B(X) = 2$ via A (stale!).

### Step 2: B's update
- B recomputes: $D_B(X) = \min(\text{direct}=0, c_{BA}+D_A(X))$.
- Since $D_A(X) = \infty$ (B's last known) ... actually B now hears A advertise $\infty$, so $D_B(X) = \infty$.

But in a real slow-convergence scenario:

### Step 3: A hears B advertise $D_B(X) = 2$ (stale)
- A recomputes: $D_A(X) = \min(\infty, c_{AB} + D_B(X)) = \min(\infty, 1+2) = 3$.
- A now thinks the route to X is 3 hops, **via B**.

### Step 4: B hears A advertise $D_A(X) = 3$
- B recomputes: $D_B(X) = \min(1, 1+3) = 4$.

### Step 5: Iteration continues
- $D_A(X) = 5$
- $D_B(X) = 6$
- $D_A(X) = 7$ ... up to **infinity** (16 in RIP).

> [!WARNING]
> **Solution: Poisoned Reverse** — B advertises $D_B(X) = \infty$ to A on the line A-B (because A is B's next hop to X). This breaks the loop.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Subnet Mask and Host Calculation

**Problem (classic KTU):** Given IP address `192.168.50.123` with mask `255.255.255.0`, find the network address, broadcast address, and number of usable hosts.

### Step 1: Convert to binary

| Component | Binary |
|-----------|--------|
| IP | 11000000.10101000.00110010.01111011 |
| Mask | 11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000 |

### Step 2: Bitwise AND → Network Address

$$\text{Network} = 11000000.10101000.00110010.00000000 = 192.168.50.0$$

### Step 3: Broadcast = Network OR (NOT Mask)
- Host bits all set to 1: `00000000` → `11111111` (255)
- Broadcast = `192.168.50.255`

### Step 4: Usable Hosts
- Total host bits = 8 → $2^8 = 256$ addresses.
- Subtract 1 for network, 1 for broadcast → $256 - 2 = 254$ usable hosts.

### Step 5: Subnet mask in prefix form
`/24` (24 ones in the mask).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: TCP Throughput — Sliding Window Model

**Given:** Bandwidth $R = 100$ Mbps, RTT = 50 ms, packet size $L = 1250$ bytes.

### Step 1: Compute $a$ (propagation-transmission ratio)

$$a = \frac{T_{prop}}{T_t} = \frac{RTT/2}{L/R} = \frac{0.025}{1250 \times 8 / 100 \times 10^6} = \frac{0.025}{0.0001} = 250$$

### Step 2: Maximum Window without loss

$$W_{max} = 2a + 1 = 2(250) + 1 = 501 \text{ packets}$$

### Step 3: Throughput

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{W \cdot L}{RTT} = \frac{501 \times 1250 \times 8}{0.05} \approx 100.2 \text{ Mbps}$$

This saturates the link, confirming the formula.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Routing Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Metric | Convergence | Scalability | Use Case | Failure Handling |
|-----------|------|--------|-------------|-------------|----------|------------------|
| **Dijkstra** | Centralized link-state | Shortest path (any) | O(V²) | Per-router full table | OSPF, IS-IS | Quick reroute |
| **Flooding** | Brute force | None | Instant | Excellent | Military, ad-hoc | Maximum redundancy |
| **Distance Vector** | Distributed | Hop count / delay | Slow (count-to-infinity) | Limited by max hops (15) | RIP (legacy) | Poor without poisoned reverse |
| **Link State** | Distributed | Configurable | Fast | Good, but flood overhead | OSPF, IS-IS | Excellent |
| **Path Vector** | Policy-aware | AS path + policies | Per-BGP rules | Internet-scale | **BGP-4** | Stable, policy-based |
| **Hierarchical** | Multi-level | Aggregation | Per-level | Excellent | Internet (ASes) | Region-scoped |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Store-and-Forward Packet Switching — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Host] -->|Packet P1 bit by bit| R1[Router 1]
    R1 -->|Stores FULL packet\nVerifies CRC\nLooks up route| R1
    R1 -->|Forwards on best link| R2[Router 2]
    R2 -->|Stores FULL packet\nVerifies CRC| R2
    R2 -->|Forward| R3[Router 3]
    R3 -->|Stores + Forwards| B[Destination Host]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style R1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style R2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style R3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each blue router *stores* the full packet, then *forwards* it. This is the "store-and-forward" principle.

---

## 4.2 Virtual-Circuit vs Datagram — Side-by-Side Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VCMODEL["VIRTUAL CIRCUIT MODEL - Connection Oriented"]
        direction LR
        S1[Source] -->|CALL SETUP| RA1[Router A]
        RA1 -->|Reserve VC ID 12| RB1[Router B]
        RB1 -->|Reserve VC ID 27| RC1[Router C]
        RC1 -->|CALL ACCEPTED| T1[Destination]
        S1 -.->|All data packets\nuse VC 12 then 27| T1
    end
    
    subgraph DGMODEL["DATAGRAM MODEL - Connectionless"]
        direction LR
        S2[Source] -->|Packet 1 with Dest IP| RA2[Router A]
        RA2 -->|Independently route| RB2[Router B]
        S2 -->|Packet 2 with Dest IP| RC2[Router C]
        RC2 -->|Independently route| T2[Destination]
        RA2 -->|Packet 1 alt path| RD2[Router D]
        RD2 -->|Forward| T2
    end
    
    style VCMODEL fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#C71585
    style DGMODEL fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4
```

---

## 4.3 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Set D source = 0\nAll others = infinity] --> B[Mark all nodes unvisited]
    B --> C[Pick unvisited node u\nwith minimum D u]
    C --> D{All nodes visited?}
    D -->|No| E[For each neighbor v of u:\nIf D v greater than D u + w u v\nUpdate D v and predecessor]
    E --> F[Mark u as visited]
    F --> C
    D -->|Yes| G[Done. Shortest paths available\nin D table with predecessors]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.4 Distance Vector — Count-to-Infinity Scenario

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Router A
    participant B as Router B
    participant C as Router C
    
    Note over A,C: INITIAL: All link costs = 1
    A->>B: D_A(X) = 1
    B->>A: D_B(X) = 2 via A
    B->>C: D_B(X) = 2
    C->>B: D_C(X) = 3 via B
    
    Note over A,B: LINK A-B FAILS
    A->>B: D_A(X) = INFINITY
    
    loop SLOW CONVERGENCE
        B->>A: D_B(X) = 2 (stale)
        A->>B: D_A(X) = 1 + 2 = 3
        B->>A: D_B(X) = 1 + 3 = 4
        A->>B: D_A(X) = 1 + 4 = 5
        B->>A: D_B(X) = 1 + 5 = 6
    end
    
    Note over A,B: Count-to-infinity! Solution: POISONED REVERSE
```

---

## 4.5 TCP Three-Way Handshake — Connection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT: Client sends SYN seq=x
    SYN_SENT --> SYN_RCVD: Server receives SYN, sends SYN+ACK seq=y ack=x+1
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED: Client sends ACK ack=y+1\nServer enters ESTABLISHED on receipt
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED: Client receives SYN+ACK, sends ACK
    
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1: Client sends FIN seq=u
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2: Server receives FIN, sends ACK
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT: Server sends FIN, Client receives FIN+ACK
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> CLOSING: Both send FIN simultaneously
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED: After 2 MSL timeout
    CLOSED --> [*]
```

> **State explanations:**
> - **SYN_SENT:** Client has sent SYN, waiting for SYN+ACK.
> - **SYN_RCVD:** Server has received SYN, sent its own.
> - **ESTABLISHED:** Bidirectional data transfer.
> - **TIME_WAIT:** Waits 2 × MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime) to ensure all packets are dead before fully closing.

---

## 4.6 IP Packet Header — Bit-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HDR["IPv4 Header 20 bytes minimum"]
        V[Version 4 bits]
        IHL[IHL 4 bits]
        TOS[TOS / DSCP 8 bits]
        TL[Total Length 16 bits]
        ID[Identification 16 bits]
        FLG[Flags 3 bits]
        FO[Fragment Offset 13 bits]
        TTL[TTL 8 bits]
        PRO[Protocol 8 bits]
        CS[Header Checksum 16 bits]
        SA[Source IP 32 bits]
        DA[Destination IP 32 bits]
        OP[Options 0-40 bytes]
        DATA[Payload]
    end
    
    V --- IHL
    IHL --- TOS
    TOS --- TL
    TL --- ID
    ID --- FLG
    FLG --- FO
    FO --- TTL
    TTL --- PRO
    PRO --- CS
    CS --- SA
    SA --- DA
    DA --- OP
    OP --- DATA
    
    style V fill:#FFB6C1
    style IHL fill:#FFB6C1
    style TTL fill:#FFB6C1
    style SA fill:#98FB98
    style DA fill:#98FB98
    style CS fill:#FFD700
    style DATA fill:#ADD8E6
```

> **Key fields to memorize for KTU:**
> - **Version:** 4 for IPv4, 6 for IPv6.
> - **TTL:** Decremented by 1 at every router; packet dropped at 0.
> - **Protocol:** 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 1 = ICMP.
> - **Header Checksum:** Recomputed at every hop (unlike TCP).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]
**Differentiate between virtual-circuit and datagram subnets.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Virtual-Circuit Subnet | Datagram Subnet |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|
| Connection setup | Required | Not required |
| Addressing | Short VC identifier (per hop) | Full source & destination address per packet |
| Routing | Fixed path for the connection | Each packet routed independently |
| Router state | Per-VC state stored | No per-connection state |
| Effect of router failure | All VCs through it break | Packets can be rerouted |
| Suitable for | Guaranteed QoS, ATM, MPLS | Best-effort Internet (IP) |
| Analogy | Telephone call | Postal letters |

Virtual circuits reserve resources in advance (connection-oriented), while datagrams treat each packet independently (connectionless). IP is the canonical datagram protocol.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]
**Explain the optimality principle of routing.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **optimality principle** states that if router J lies on the optimal path from router I to router K, then the path from J to K along that same optimal route is **also optimal** for J→K.

**Consequence:** The set of optimal paths from **all sources to a given destination** forms a **sink tree** (a directed tree rooted at the destination) with **no loops** (cycles would imply a longer-than-optimal path).

**Proof sketch:** Suppose J→K is on the optimal I→K path. If there were a better J→K path, concatenating I→J with that better J→K would yield a strictly shorter I→K, contradicting optimality of the original I→K path. ∎

This principle underlies **Bellman-Ford** and **Dijkstra** algorithms, and is the foundation of all shortest-path routing protocols.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. Internal choice is *not* allowed between sub-parts, but two **alternative** 14-mark questions may be offered (attempt any ONE).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Network Layer Routing and Congestion

**Q3. (a)** With a suitable example, explain the **Distance Vector Routing** algorithm. Also show how the **Count-to-Infinity** problem occurs and how **Poisoned Reverse** solves it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Algorithm Description (3 marks):**
Each router maintains a **distance vector** — an estimate of the cost to every destination. Routers exchange vectors only with **directly connected neighbors**, and on each update apply the **Bellman-Ford equation**:

$$D_x(y) = \min_v [c(x,v) + D_v(y)]$$

**Step 2 — Worked Example (2 marks):**
Consider routers A, B, C, D in a line with link costs all = 1.

| Iteration | $D_A(D)$ | $D_B(D)$ | $D_C(D)$ |
|-----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Initial | 3 (via B) | 2 (via C) | 1 (direct) |
| After 1 update | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| ... (stable) | 3 | 2 | 1 |

If link C-D goes down, C sets $D_C(D) = \infty$. B's table still says $D_B(D) = 2$ via C — stale. B recomputes: $D_B(D) = \min(1, 1+\infty) = 1$ (direct). A recomputes: $D_A(D) = \min(3, 1+2) = 3$ (still). A doesn't break — converges quickly here, but with a triangle it can take 16 iterations to reach infinity.

**Step 3 — Count-to-Infinity (1 mark):**
When a link fails, routers keep exchanging stale information; the bad news propagates one hop at a time. RIP limits this to **15 hops** (16 = infinity).

**Step 4 — Poisoned Reverse (1 mark):**
If router **X** reaches destination **Z** via neighbor **Y**, then **X advertises $D_X(Z) = \infty$ to Y** on that link. Y will never use X to reach Z, breaking the loop.

---

**Q3. (b)** Explain **Congestion Control in Datagram Subnets**. Discuss **RED** and **ECN** mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why congestion occurs (1 mark):**
When too many packets arrive at a router, queues grow, leading to:
- Increased end-to-end delay.
- Buffer overflow → packet loss → retransmissions → **even more load** (positive feedback).

**Step 2 — Open-loop vs Closed-loop (1 mark):**
- **Open-loop:** Decisions taken at design time (e.g., leaky bucket).
- **Closed-loop:** Reactive to feedback (monitor + adjust).

**Step 3 — Datagram-Specific Techniques (2 marks):**
- **Congestion-aware routing:** Use load as a routing metric.
- **Traffic throttling** — e.g., **Choke Packets** (send a CHOKE packet to source).

**Step 4 — RED (Random Early Detection) (2 marks):**
- Routers compute the **average queue length** using an EWMA.
- If avg < min_th: no drop.
- If min_th < avg < max_th: drop arriving packet with **rising probability**.
- If avg > max_th: always drop.
- **Goal:** Proactively signal congestion *before* buffers overflow.

**Step 5 — ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) (1 mark):**
- Instead of dropping, routers **mark** packets by setting the **ECN bits in the IP header**.
- The receiver echoes the ECN to the sender via TCP, which then **reduces its congestion window**.
- Advantage: Avoids costly retransmissions; ideal for latency-sensitive traffic.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internetworking, IP, and Transport Layer

**Q4. (a)** Describe the **IPv4 Header** format with a neat diagram. Explain the functions of **TTL**, **Fragmentation**, and **Header Checksum** fields. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Header Layout (2 marks):**
IPv4 header is **20 bytes minimum**, up to 60 bytes with options. Fields in order:

| Field | Bits | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| Version | 4 | IP version (4) |
| IHL | 4 | Header length in 32-bit words (min 5) |
| TOS/DSCP | 8 | QoS priority |
| Total Length | 16 | Header + data in bytes (max 65535) |
| Identification | 16 | Unique ID for fragmented packet |
| Flags | 3 | DF, MF bits |
| Fragment Offset | 13 | Position of this fragment (in 8-byte units) |
| TTL | 8 | Max hops; decremented at each router |
| Protocol | 8 | Next-layer protocol (6=TCP, 17=UDP) |
| Header Checksum | 16 | 1's-complement sum of header |
| Source Address | 32 | Sender's IP |
| Destination Address | 32 | Receiver's IP |
| Options | 0–320 | Rarely used (record route, timestamp) |

**Step 2 — TTL (Time-To-Live) (2 marks):**
- Set by source (e.g., 64, 128, 255).
- Each router **decrements TTL by 1**.
- When TTL reaches **0**, the router **discards the packet** and sends an **ICMP Time Exceeded** message to the source.
- Prevents **routing loops** from causing infinite circulation.
- Practical limit: prevents wasted bandwidth and CPU.

**Step 3 — Fragmentation (2 marks):**
- If a packet is larger than the next link's **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)**, the router splits it.
- Each fragment gets the same **Identification**, with **MF (More Fragments)** flag and **Fragment Offset**.
- IPv4 reassembles only at the **destination** (routers do not reassemble).
- Disadvantage: One lost fragment → entire packet retransmitted.

**Step 4 — Header Checksum (1 mark):**
- 16-bit 1's-complement sum of the header.
- **Recomputed at every hop** because TTL changes.
- Detects header bit-errors only (not payload — that's TCP/UDP's job).

---

**Q4. (b)** Compare **TCP** and **UDP** in detail. Explain the **Three-Way Handshake** for TCP connection establishment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — UDP (User Datagram Protocol) (1 mark):**
- **Connectionless**, no handshake.
- Header is just **8 bytes**: src port, dst port, length, checksum.
- No flow control, no congestion control, no retransmission.
- Used for DNS, VoIP, video streaming, gaming — speed over reliability.

**Step 2 — TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) (1 mark):**
- **Connection-oriented**, reliable, byte-stream.
- Header is **20–60 bytes**: src/dst port, seq, ack, flags, window, checksum, urgent pointer, options.
- Provides: **reliable delivery** (acks, retransmissions), **flow control** (sliding window), **congestion control** (cwnd, slow start, AIMD), **ordering**.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Connection | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed (retransmit) | Best-effort |
| Ordering | Yes (sequence numbers) | No |
| Flow control | Yes (rwnd) | No |
| Congestion control | Yes (cwnd) | No (app-level) |
| Header size | 20+ bytes | 8 bytes |
| Speed | Slower (overhead) | Faster |
| Use cases | Web (HTTP), email, file transfer | DNS, video, VoIP, gaming |

**Step 4 — Three-Way Handshake (3 marks):**
- **Step 1: SYN** — Client sends a segment with `SYN=1`, seq=x. Client state: `SYN_SENT`.
- **Step 2: SYN+ACK** — Server replies with `SYN=1, ACK=1`, seq=y, ack=x+1. Server state: `SYN_RCVD`.
- **Step 3: ACK** — Client sends `ACK=1`, seq=x+1, ack=y+1. Both enter `ESTABLISHED`.
- **Purpose:** Synchronize **Initial Sequence Numbers (ISNs)** on both sides, agree on parameters (window scale, MSS, SACK).
- Solves the **two-army problem** of ensuring both sides are ready to communicate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **second** segment carries BOTH SYN and ACK. Also, sequence numbers start at the **ISN**, not 0 (in modern TCP, ISN is randomized to prevent spoofing).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing the optimality principle with a theorem** — it's a heuristic principle, not always applicable (e.g., in non-additive metrics).
> 2. **Mixing up "routing" and "forwarding"** — routing is the *control-plane* decision; forwarding is the *data-plane* action.
> 3. **Forgetting that the IPv4 header checksum is recomputed at every hop** (since TTL changes) — many students assume it covers the payload too.
> 4. **In the Dijkstra derivation, not showing the predecessor table** — KTU examiners award marks for the *path*, not just the distances.
> 5. **TCP state diagram mistakes**: confusing FIN_WAIT_1 with FIN_WAIT_2, or omitting the TIME_WAIT state's 2×MSL purpose.
> 6. **Confusing MTU with MSS** — MTU is the link-layer frame size; MSS is the TCP payload size (MTU − 40).
> 7. **In the Count-to-Infinity example, students often forget to show the initial stable state** before the failure — marks are lost for "jumping straight to the loop".

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🔹 Network Layer Design Issues
- **Store-and-Forward** = receive full packet → verify → forward. Gives $T_{end} = N \cdot (L/R + d/s)$.
- **Connectionless service** = Datagram (IP). Each packet routed independently.
- **Connection-oriented service** = Virtual Circuit (ATM, MPLS). 3 phases: setup → data → teardown.
- **Trade-off:** VC is faster per-packet (short IDs) but less robust; Datagram is more robust but heavier per packet (full addresses).

### 🔹 Routing Algorithms
- **Dijkstra** — global knowledge, link-state, $O(V^2)$, used in OSPF/IS-IS.
- **Flooding** — every packet on every line (except arrival). Use hop counter + sequence number.
- **Distance Vector** — Bellman-Ford, RIP, prone to count-to-infinity; fixes: poisoned reverse, split horizon, triggered updates.
- **Link State** — flood LSPs, run Dijkstra locally. OSPF.
- **Hierarchical** — ASes (BGP) + regions (OSPF areas). Internet-scale.
- **Broadcast** — spanning tree, reverse path forwarding.
- **Multicast** — IGMP, DVMRP, PIM-SM, CBT.

### 🔹 Congestion Control
- **Virtual-Circuit:** admission control, resource reservation (RSVP).
- **Datagram:** traffic-aware routing, choke packets, RED, ECN.
- **Load Shedding:** wine (file) vs milk (real-time) policy.
- **Jitter Control:** playback buffer at receiver.

### 🔹 Internetworking
- **Tunneling:** encapsulate one protocol inside another.
- **Fragmentation:** IPv4 fragments in router, reassembles at destination; IPv6 **forbids** router fragmentation.
- **Path MTU Discovery** to find the largest end-to-end MTU.

### 🔹 IPv4 Addressing
- 32 bits, dotted-decimal.
- **Classful (obsolete)**: A (/8), B (/16), C (/24), D (multicast), E (reserved).
- **CIDR:** classless, prefix length notation `/n`. Example: `192.168.1.0/26` → 64 addresses, 62 usable hosts.
- **Subnet mask** formula: usable hosts = $2^{(32 - \text{prefix})} - 2$.

### 🔹 Transport Layer Quick Hits
- **UDP:** 8-byte header, connectionless, no reliability, used in DNS, VoIP.
- **TCP:** 20-byte header, 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK), 4-way teardown (FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK), TIME_WAIT for 2×MSL.
- **Sliding window** efficiency: $\eta = 1$ if $W \ge 2a+1$, where $a = T_{prop}/T_t$.
- **Slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, fast recovery** — TCP's congestion control phases.

### 🔹 High-Yield Numerical Shortcuts
- **Transmission delay dominates** for large packets, low bandwidth.
- **Propagation delay dominates** for long links, high bandwidth.
- **Satellite link** = ~250 ms RTT → high $a$ → need large window.
- **Default RIP hop limit** = 15 (16 = unreachable).
- **IPv4 address exhaustion** → CIDR, NAT, and ultimately **IPv6** ($2^{128}$ addresses).

### 🔹 Examiner's Mantra
- Always show **units** in numerical answers.
- Always **label Mermaid diagrams** clearly.
- Always state the **protocol** (RIP/OSPF/BGP) when discussing a routing algorithm.
- **TCP ≠ UDP ≠ IP** — know which layer each belongs to and why.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing Algorithm, Optimality Principle, Flooding, Distance Vector Routing, Link State Routing, Congestion Control Algorithms, General Principles, Congestion Prevention Policies, Choke Packets, Random Early Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Routing Algorithm — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCEOT602 Module 3)**
> A **Routing Algorithm** is a piece of software that determines the path (sequence of routers/links) along which a packet must be forwarded from its source to its destination. It is the intelligence layer of the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI/TCP-IP reference model. The algorithm makes its decisions based on a **Routing Metric** (delay, hop count, bandwidth, cost, load, or reliability) and produces entries that are installed in the **Routing Table** of every participating router.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, routing algorithms are classified into two broad families:

1. **Non-Adaptive (Static) Routing Algorithms** — Routes are computed in advance, off-line, and loaded into routers at boot time. They do **not** react to the current state of the network.
   - *Examples*: **Flooding**, **Static Shortest Path Routing**.
2. **Adaptive (Dynamic) Routing Algorithms** — Routes are recomputed continuously in response to changes in topology, link cost, or traffic load.
   - *Examples*: **Distance Vector Routing (RIP)**, **Link State Routing (OSPF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** For Part A (3 marks) questions, the examiner expects the student to contrast **adaptive vs. non-adaptive** routing in a tabular form, often with one example algorithm for each class.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The GPS vs. The Printed Map"

Imagine you are driving from Trivandrum to Kasaragod.

- A **Non-Adaptive (Static) Routing Algorithm** is like using a **printed road map** that you bought ten years ago. It shows the highways, but it cannot tell you that the NH-66 bridge at Kozhikode is closed today.
- An **Adaptive (Dynamic) Routing Algorithm** is like using a **live GPS** (e.g., Google Maps). It continuously receives traffic data, road closures, and accidents, and re-calculates the best route in real time.
- **Flooding** is like asking *every* person in *every* town on the way: *"Do you know how to reach Kasaragod?"* The information definitely arrives (eventually), but you flood the town with questions.

---

## 1.2 The Optimality Principle — Dijkstra's Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimality Principle (J. M. McQuillan, 1974, later formalised by Dijkstra):**
> *"If router J is on the optimal path from router I to router K, then the optimal path from J to K also falls along the same route."*

In plain words: a sub-path of an optimal path is itself optimal. This is the bedrock theorem that allows **shortest-path** routing algorithms to be correct.

### Intuition
Think of the fastest train route from Delhi to Chennai via Mumbai. If Mumbai lies on the optimal Delhi→Chennai path, then the optimal Mumbai→Chennai leg is *exactly* the same continuation. You would not magically teleport the train from Mumbai to Hyderabad and still claim an optimal total journey.

The set of all optimal routes from *all* sources to a fixed destination D forms a tree rooted at D, called the **Sink Tree** (or **Destination Tree**). The KTU examiner will very often draw a 5–6 node graph and ask the student to construct the sink tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse the *Sink Tree* with the *Spanning Tree*. The sink tree is rooted at the destination, whereas a spanning tree is rooted at any arbitrary node and covers all nodes with no cycles.

---

## 1.3 Flooding

> [!NOTE]
> **Flooding** is a non-adaptive routing algorithm in which every incoming packet is forwarded on **every outgoing line except the one it arrived on**. No routing table is required; the algorithm is topology-agnostic.

### Intuition
Picture dropping a stone in a still pond — ripples spread in every direction equally. Flooding does the same in a network graph: a packet *floods* the topology.

Two practical improvements are universally tested:
- **Hop Count Limit (TTL)** — the packet carries a counter that is decremented at every hop; when the counter reaches zero, the packet is discarded. This prevents infinite circulation.
- **Sequence Number Rejection** — the source puts a unique sequence number on each packet; every router remembers the sequence numbers it has already seen and discards duplicates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The examiner frequently asks *"Why is pure flooding impractical in real networks?"* Answer: it generates $N!$ (or at minimum exponentially many) duplicate packets, exhausting bandwidth.

---

## 1.4 Distance Vector Routing (Bellman–Ford)

> [!NOTE]
> **Distance Vector Routing (DVR)** is an **adaptive, distributed, iterative, asynchronous** routing algorithm. Each router maintains a **vector** of estimated distances to *every* destination, sharing it only with its **immediate neighbours**. The algorithm implements the **Bellman–Ford equation**.

### The Core Equation (KTU Must-Know)

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left\{ c(x, v) + D_v(y) \right\}
$$

Where:
- $D_x(y)$ = current estimate by router $x$ of the lowest cost to destination $y$
- $c(x, v)$ = cost of the direct link from $x$ to neighbour $v$
- $D_v(y)$ = neighbour $v$'s advertised distance to $y$

### Intuition
Imagine you live in Kochi and you want to know the cheapest fare to Delhi. You call your friends in Bangalore, Mumbai, and Chennai, ask each of them *"What is the cheapest fare from your city to Delhi?"* and then add the cost of your own ticket to their city. You pick the minimum. That is exactly what Bellman-Ford does.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Famous KTU topic — "Count-to-Infinity Problem":** DVR is famously vulnerable to *Count-to-Infinity*, where a slow link failure propagates across the network at a rate of one hop per routing update cycle. *Poisoned Reverse* and *Split Horizon* are the standard remedies.

---

## 1.5 Link State Routing (Dijkstra's Algorithm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Link State Routing (LSR)** is an **adaptive, distributed, global** routing algorithm. Every router discovers its neighbours, measures the cost to each neighbour, **floods** a *Link State Packet (LSP)* containing these costs to **every** router in the network, and then runs **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF)** algorithm locally to compute the shortest path to every destination.

### The Five Mandatory Steps of LSR (KTU Classic)

1. **Discover Neighbours & Learn their Addresses** — via *Hello* packets.
2. **Measure the Cost (Delay, Bandwidth, etc.) to each Neighbour** — by sending an *ECHO* packet and timing the round-trip.
3. **Construct a Link State Packet (LSP)** listing the sender's ID, sequence number, age, and the list of (neighbour, cost) pairs.
4. **Flood the LSP** to *every* router using a controlled, reliable flood.
5. **Compute the Shortest Path** to every other router using **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** on the resulting **Link State Database (LSDB)**.

### Intuition
Every city in Kerala builds its own complete map of the entire country. Then each city independently draws the shortest line from itself to every other city on that shared map. No one city tells another what to do — they all arrive at the same answer because they all have the same map.

---

## 1.6 Congestion Control — General Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Congestion** in a network occurs when the **demand for resources (link bandwidth, router buffer/CPU)** at a moment exceeds the **available capacity**, causing **queueing delay, packet loss, and degraded throughput**. Congestion is *not* the same as flow control (which is end-to-end, sender-receiver).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kleinrock's Congestion Principle (1978):**
> *"Increase in network load produces an increase in throughput only up to a 'knee' point. Beyond this, throughput collapses as the network enters congestion collapse."*

The **knee** is the onset of congestion; the **cliff** is the collapse. The goal of every congestion control algorithm is to keep the operating point *left of the knee*.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a 4-lane national highway that suddenly narrows to 1 lane because of road works. If 200 cars per minute keep arriving, traffic piles up. Some cars must be *diverted* (prevention), some must be *told to slow down* (choke packet), and some must be *turned back* (load shedding). The network is no different.

---

## 1.7 Congestion Prevention Policies (Two Layers)

Congestion prevention is applied at **four** layers of the KTU reference model (Tanenbaum's framework). The examiner expects you to list examples from each layer.

| Layer | Policy Examples |
|---|---|
| **Network (Layer 3)** | Adaptive routing, traffic-aware routing |
| **Transport (Layer 4)** | Retransmission policy, out-of-order caching, acknowledgement policy, flow control |
| **Data Link (Layer 2)** | Retransmission policy, acknowledgement policy, flow control |
| **Application (Layer 7)** | Telnet, HTTP polling, video compression |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked *"How can congestion be prevented?"* give **at least one** example from **three** of the four layers. The examiner allocates 1 mark per layer.

---

## 1.8 Choke Packets

> [!NOTE]
> **Choke Packet Technique:** A router detects congestion locally (e.g., when its output queue length, average queue length, or utilisation crosses a threshold). It then sends a special control packet called a **CHOKE** packet **back to the source host**. The source is required to reduce its sending rate by a stated percentage (commonly **X%** of the current rate). After a fixed interval, if no new CHOKE is received, the source may increase its rate again.

### Intuition
A traffic policeman standing on MG Road in Ernakulam sees a jam building. He radios back to the origin of the traffic ("Reduce inflow by 30%!"). Drivers upstream slow down. After 5 minutes of clear road, the policeman lifts the restriction.

The most common variant in the KTU syllabus is the **DECbit / Source-Quench (ICMP Source Quench)** mechanism, where the choke packet is sent one hop at a time back to the source.

---

## 1.9 Random Early Detection (RED)

> [!NOTE]
> **Random Early Detection (RED)** is a proactive **Active Queue Management (AQM)** algorithm proposed by **Floyd and Jacobson (1993)**. Instead of waiting until the buffer is full, RED monitors the **average queue length** $\bar{q}$ and *randomly* drops arriving packets with a probability that increases as $\bar{q}$ grows. This is done **before** the queue overflows, so the source receives the implicit ECN/loss signal early.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner's favourite question: *"Differentiate between Drop Tail and RED."* Remember the two thresholds: $\text{Min}_{\text{th}}$ and $\text{Max}_{\text{th}}$.

### Three Operational Zones of RED

| Zone | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal** | $\bar{q} < \text{Min}_{\text{th}}$ | Drop probability $= 0$ (accept all packets) |
| **Congestion Avoidance** | $\text{Min}_{\text{th}} \le \bar{q} \le \text{Max}_{\text{th}}$ | Drop with probability $P$ that grows linearly with $\bar{q}$ |
| **Forced Drop** | $\bar{q} > \text{Max}_{\text{th}}$ | Drop probability $= 1$ (drop every arriving packet) |

### Intuition
A water tank with a sensor. When the water level is low, let all the taps run. When the water reaches the *yellow* zone, randomly shut off a few taps so the level falls. When the water reaches the *red* zone, close every tap. The randomness prevents *global synchronisation* (where every TCP source backs off at the same instant).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Routing Algorithms (Hierarchical)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Tree:**
> Routing Algorithms
> ├── **Non-Adaptive (Static)**
> │ ├── Flooding (with/without hop limit)
> │ ├── Random Walk
> │ └── Static Shortest Path
> └── **Adaptive (Dynamic)**
> ├── **Centralised** (e.g., link-state with global LSDB)
> ├── **Isolated** (e.g., back-pressure)
> └── **Distributed**
> ├── Distance Vector (Bellman-Ford)
> └── Link State (Dijkstra SPF)

The KTU examiner gives **2 marks** for a clean classification diagram and **1 mark** for each correct example.

---

## 2.2 Optimality Principle — Formal Proof Sketch

**Theorem.** *If the optimal path from $I$ to $K$ passes through $J$, then the optimal path from $J$ to $K$ is the suffix of the optimal $I \to K$ path.*

**Proof by Contradiction.** Suppose the optimal $I \to K$ path is $I \to J \to P_1 \to P_2 \to \dots \to K$ with cost $C^*$. If there existed a strictly shorter $J \to K$ path $J \to P'_1 \to P'_2 \to \dots \to K$ with cost $C'' < C^* - c(I,J)$, then concatenating it with $I \to J$ would give a shorter $I \to K$ path — contradicting the optimality of $C^*$. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The optimality principle is what allows the **Sink Tree** to exist. It enables:
> - **Hierarchical routing** (sub-paths in different regions are independently optimal).
> - **Hot-potato routing** at ISP exchange points.
> - **Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP)** load balancing.

---

## 2.3 Flooding — Detailed Operation

### Algorithm
1. Source stamps packet with a unique **Sequence Number** and **Hop Count** (initially = $H$, the diameter of the network).
2. Each router, on receiving a packet, checks:
   - If $\text{SeqNum}$ already in the seen-list → **discard**.
   - If $\text{HopCount} = 0$ → **discard**.
   - Else → **decrement** HopCount, **append** SeqNum to seen-list, **forward on all other outgoing lines**.
3. After time $T$, source may retransmit if no ACK has arrived.

### Properties
- **Robustness:** Excellent — works even if many routers/nodes have failed.
- **Minimal delay:** Always finds the *shortest* path in terms of hops (because the first arrival via a given neighbour must be via the shortest path).
- **Cost:** Generates $N!$ packets for a single message in the worst case.
- **Applications:** Military networks, distributed databases, OSPF flooding of LSPs, defence against denial-of-service via route diversity.

---

## 2.4 Distance Vector Routing — Bellman–Ford Mechanics

### Update Procedure (Executed at every router $x$)
1. **Wait** for the distance vector $D_v$ from a neighbour $v$ (or detect a link cost change).
2. **Re-evaluate** every destination $y$:
   $$ D_x(y) \leftarrow \min_{v \in N(x)} \big[ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \big] $$
3. **If** the new $D_x(y)$ differs from the old value, **send** the new vector to all neighbours.

### Stability Issues
- **Count-to-Infinity:** A link failure can cause the cost estimate to grow by **at most 1 per update cycle**. For a destination $K$ at distance $N$, recovery time is $O(N)$ update cycles.
- **Poisoned Reverse:** Router $X$ advertises infinity to $Y$ for any destination whose best path is *through* $Y$. This breaks two-node loops.
- **Split Horizon with Poisoned Reverse:** Most widely used; effectively caps count-to-infinity at **1** in many practical topologies.
- **Triggered Updates:** Send an update *immediately* on change, not on the periodic timer. Reduces convergence time drastically.

---

## 2.5 Link State Routing — Dijkstra's SPF Algorithm

### Dijkstra's Algorithm (Pseudocode for KTU)

```
N' = {u}                          // u = source router
For all v ≠ u:
    if v is adjacent to u:  D(v) = c(u,v)
    else:                  D(v) = ∞
Loop:
    Find w ∉ N' such that D(w) is minimum
    Add w to N'
    For all v ∉ N' adjacent to w:
        D(v) = min( D(v),  D(w) + c(w,v) )
Until N' contains all vertices
```

- **Complexity:** $O(V^2)$ with arrays, $O((V+E)\log V)$ with a priority queue.
- **Convergence:** $O(\text{diameter})$ LSP floods; computation is local to each router.
- **Memory:** Each router stores the **full topology** ($V$ vertices, $E$ edges) and the resulting shortest-path tree.
- **Security Note (KTU 2024 Hot Topic):** Plain LSR has no authentication of LSPs. **OSPFv2/v3** uses **MD5** or **SHA** authentication to defeat **LSP spoofing** and **router-id hijacking**.

---

## 2.6 Congestion Control — Formal Definitions

| Term | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Demand** $\lambda_{\text{in}}$ | Total arrival rate at a node | packets/sec |
| **Capacity** $\mu$ | Service rate of the bottleneck | packets/sec |
| **Utilisation** $\rho$ | $\lambda_{\text{in}}/\mu$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Throughput** $\lambda_{\text{out}}$ | Successfully delivered packets | packets/sec |
| **Power** $P$ | Throughput / Delay | packets/(sec × sec) |
| **Knee** | Point of maximum Power | — |
| **Cliff** | Point of congestion collapse | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 examiner will draw a **Throughput-vs-Load** curve and ask the student to label the **knee**, the **cliff**, the **congestion region**, and the **collapse region**. Allocate ½ mark per label.

---

## 2.7 Choke Packet — Formal Algorithm

1. Router $R$ monitors its **outgoing line utilisation** $u$ and **buffer occupancy** $q$.
2. When $u > U_{\text{threshold}}$ (e.g., $0.8$) OR $q > Q_{\text{threshold}}$, $R$ **enters choke mode**.
3. For every new packet arriving from source $S$ via a congested line, $R$ sends a **CHOKE** packet back to $S$.
4. Source $S$ reduces its window / sending rate by factor $\alpha$ (commonly $\alpha = 0.875$ or 12.5 %).
5. After a fixed interval $\Delta t$ (e.g., 1 s) without any new CHOKE, $S$ may increase its rate by a small additive amount (or multiplicatively, in **AIMD** style).

### Variants
- **Backward Choke:** CHOKE travels *back to source* one hop at a time. Each intermediate router also reduces its rate.
- **Forward Choke (Hop-by-Hop Backpressure):** The congested router sends the CHOKE in the *direction of* the destination, telling every upstream router to throttle. This is faster (in some topologies) but requires every intermediate node to participate.

---

## 2.8 Random Early Detection (RED) — Deep Specs

### Inputs (KTU Must-Know)
- $\text{Min}_{\text{th}}$ : minimum threshold (queue length in packets)
- $\text{Max}_{\text{th}}$ : maximum threshold
- $P_{\max}$ : maximum drop probability (typically 0.1)
- $w_q$ : weight for exponential moving average of queue length

### Step 1 — Average Queue Length (Exponential Weighted Moving Average)

$$
\bar{q} \leftarrow (1 - w_q) \, \bar{q} + w_q \cdot q
$$

Where $q$ is the instantaneous queue length at sampling time.

### Step 2 — Compute Drop Probability

$$
P(\bar{q}) = 
\begin{cases}
0, & 0 \le \bar{q} < \text{Min}_{\text{th}} \\[4pt]
\displaystyle \frac{\bar{q} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}{\text{Max}_{\text{th}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}} \cdot P_{\max}, & \text{Min}_{\text{th}} \le \bar{q} < \text{Max}_{\text{th}} \\[8pt]
1, & \bar{q} \ge \text{Max}_{\text{th}}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *Final* drop probability is **not** simply $P(\bar{q})$ — RED multiplies by a **count** to make the drops more *evenly distributed* across connections (avoiding bias against bursty flows). The count tracks how many packets have arrived since the last drop; the final probability is:
>
> $$ P_{\text{final}} = \frac{P(\bar{q})}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P(\bar{q})} $$

### Why RED Beats Drop Tail
| Property | Drop Tail (FIFO + Tail Drop) | RED |
|---|---|---|
| Detects congestion | After it occurs | Before it occurs |
| Lock-out problem | Yes (one flow can monopolise buffer) | No (random drop) |
| Global synchronisation | Yes (all TCPs back off together) | No (drops are spread out) |
| Bias against bursty flows | High | Low |
| Predictable per-flow delay | Poor | Good (controls $\bar{q}$) |

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (Print-and-Revise)

> [!TIP]
> **This is the consolidated table the KTU 2024 examiner expects students to memorise for the ESE (End Semester Exam).**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bellman-Ford (DVR) | $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$ |
| 2 | Dijkstra Relaxation | $D(v) = \min\{ D(v),\, D(w) + c(w,v) \}$ |
| 3 | Hop-count limit on flooding | Counter decremented per hop, packet dropped at $0$ |
| 4 | RED average queue | $\bar{q} \leftarrow (1-w_q)\bar{q} + w_q \cdot q$ |
| 5 | RED drop probability (linear region) | $P = \dfrac{\bar{q} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}{\text{Max}_{\text{th}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}} \cdot P_{\max}$ |
| 6 | RED final drop probability | $P_f = \dfrac{P}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P}$ |
| 7 | Congestion Utilisation | $\rho = \lambda_{\text{in}} / \mu$ |
| 8 | Power | $P = \text{Throughput} / \text{Delay}$ |
| 9 | Choke packet rate reduction factor | $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ typically $0.875$ |
| 10 | Count-to-infinity bound (Poisoned Reverse) | 1 to 2 update cycles |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industrial / Research Use |
|---|---|
| Flooding | OSPF intra-domain LSP distribution, military MANETs, Bitcoin gossip protocol |
| Distance Vector | RIPv1, RIPv2, IGRP (legacy), BGP uses *path vectors* (a DVR extension) |
| Link State | OSPF, IS-IS, MPLS Traffic Engineering, modern SDN controllers (BGP-LS) |
| Choke Packets | Early TCP (TCP-Tahoe), DECbit, ICMP Source-Quench (deprecated) |
| RED | Linux `tc-red`, Cisco WRED, Juniper RED, ECN-aware variants, data-centre switches |
