<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Bellman-Ford Update on a Sample Network (DVR)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a KTU classic. Given a 4-router topology with link costs, students are asked to iteratively update distance vectors until convergence. **Solve every line explicitly** — this is the KTU 2024 valuation key expectation.

### Topology

Consider a 4-router network A, B, C, D with the following link costs:

| Link | Cost |
|---|---|
| A – B | 1 |
| A – C | 4 |
| A – D | 5 |
| B – C | 2 |
| B – D | 6 |
| C – D | 1 |

### Initialisation (Time $t = 0$)
Each router knows only its own distance to itself and estimates infinity for every other destination.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A &= (0,\ \infty,\ \infty,\ \infty) \\
D_B &= (\infty,\ 0,\ \infty,\ \infty) \\
D_C &= (\infty,\ \infty,\ 0,\ \infty) \\
D_D &= (\infty,\ \infty,\ \infty,\ 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Time $t = 1$: First Bellman-Ford Update at Router A

We compute $D_A(B)$ as the minimum of $c(A,v) + D_v(B)$ for all neighbours $v \in \{B, C, D\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(B) &= \min\big\{ c(A,B) + D_B(B),\ c(A,C) + D_C(B),\ c(A,D) + D_D(B) \big\} \\
       &= \min\big\{ 1 + 0,\ 4 + \infty,\ 5 + \infty \big\} \\
       &= \min\big\{ 1,\ \infty,\ \infty \big\} \\
       &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

In this line we substitute the direct cost to the neighbour and the neighbour's current advertised distance to destination $B$. Since $C$ and $D$ have not yet learned about $B$, they advertise $\infty$.

Now compute $D_A(C)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(C) &= \min\big\{ c(A,B) + D_B(C),\ c(A,C) + D_C(C),\ c(A,D) + D_D(C) \big\} \\
       &= \min\big\{ 1 + \infty,\ 4 + 0,\ 5 + \infty \big\} \\
       &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

And $D_A(D)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(D) &= \min\big\{ c(A,B) + D_B(D),\ c(A,C) + D_C(D),\ c(A,D) + D_D(D) \big\} \\
       &= \min\big\{ 1 + \infty,\ 4 + \infty,\ 5 + 0 \big\} \\
       &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

So router A's updated vector at $t=1$ is $D_A = (0,\ 1,\ 4,\ 5)$.

### Time $t = 2$: Convergence via Iterative Bellman-Ford

After all routers exchange their $t=1$ vectors, recompute. This time $D_B(C) = 2$ is known, $D_C(B) = 2$ is known.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(C) &= \min\{ 1 + D_B(C),\ 4 + D_C(C),\ 5 + D_D(C) \} \\
       &= \min\{ 1 + 2,\ 4 + 0,\ 5 + \infty \} \\
       &= \min\{ 3,\ 4,\ \infty \} \\
       &= 3 \quad \text{(improved from 4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This improvement is because the indirect path A → B → C has cost $1+2=3$, beating the direct A → C cost of $4$.

### Time $t = 3$: Final Convergence

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(D) &= \min\{ 1 + D_B(D),\ 3 + D_C(D),\ 5 + D_D(D) \} \\
       &= \min\{ 1 + 6,\ 3 + 1,\ 5 + 0 \} \\
       &= \min\{ 7,\ 4,\ 5 \} \\
       &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

The optimal path from A to D is **A → B → C → D** with cost $1+2+1 = 4$.

### Final Distance Vector at Router A

$$
D_A^{\text{final}} = \big( D_A(A),\ D_A(B),\ D_A(C),\ D_A(D) \big) = (0,\ 1,\ 3,\ 4)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Theorem (Bellman-Ford):** If all link costs are positive and bounded, the iterative Bellman-Ford procedure converges in **at most $V-1$ iterations** for a graph of $V$ vertices. This is a standard KTU Part B question.

---

## 3.2 Dijkstra's SPF on the Same Network (LSR)

### Initialisation
- $N' = \{ A \}$, $D(A) = 0$.
- For all $v \in \{B, C, D\}$: $D(v) = c(A,v)$.
- Result: $D(B) = 1$, $D(C) = 4$, $D(D) = 5$.

### Iteration 1: Pick Minimum $D$ outside $N'$
$w = B$ (cost 1). Add B to $N'$.

Update neighbours of B that are not in $N'$: namely C and D.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(C) &= \min\{ D(C),\ D(B) + c(B,C) \} = \min\{ 4,\ 1 + 2 \} = 3 \\
D(D) &= \min\{ D(D),\ D(B) + c(B,D) \} = \min\{ 5,\ 1 + 6 \} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

We update $D(C)$ to $3$ because the indirect A → B → C path is cheaper. $D(D)$ remains $5$ because the indirect A → B → D path ($1+6=7$) is *more* expensive than the direct path.

### Iteration 2: Pick Minimum $D$ outside $N'$
$w = C$ (cost 3). Add C to $N'$.

Update D (only neighbour of C not in $N'$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(D) &= \min\{ D(D),\ D(C) + c(C,D) \} = \min\{ 5,\ 3 + 1 \} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Path A → B → C → D = $1+2+1 = 4$ is now the cheapest.

### Iteration 3: Pick Minimum $D$ outside $N'$
$w = D$ (cost 4). Add D to $N'$.

All vertices in $N'$. **Algorithm terminates.**

### Final Shortest Paths from A

| Destination | Cost | Path |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | — |
| B | 1 | A → B |
| C | 3 | A → B → C |
| D | 4 | A → B → C → D |

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Verification:** The Bellman-Ford result $(0, 1, 3, 4)$ matches the Dijkstra result exactly. This is expected — both compute the *same* shortest paths via different formulations.

---

## 3.3 Sink Tree Construction (Optimality Principle)

Given the converged shortest-path distances, the **Sink Tree rooted at A** is:

$$
\text{Parents: } p(B) = A,\quad p(C) = B,\quad p(D) = C
$$

The tree is:

```
       A
       |
       B
       |
       C
       |
       D
```

The KTU 2024 examiner will provide a graph and ask the student to:
1. Compute the distance vector at every router (3 marks).
2. Construct the sink tree at a specified destination (2 marks).
3. Verify the optimality principle on a chosen sub-path (2 marks).

---

## 3.4 RED Drop Probability — Numerical Walk-through

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Sample Numerical:**
> A RED queue has $\text{Min}_{\text{th}} = 10$ packets, $\text{Max}_{\text{th}} = 30$ packets, $P_{\max} = 0.1$, and $w_q = 0.002$. The current average queue length $\bar{q} = 20$ packets. What is the base drop probability $P(\bar{q})$?

### Solution

Since $\text{Min}_{\text{th}} \le \bar{q} < \text{Max}_{\text{th}}$ ($10 \le 20 < 30$), we are in the **congestion-avoidance** region.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\bar{q}) &= \frac{\bar{q} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}{\text{Max}_{\text{th}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}} \cdot P_{\max} \\
          &= \frac{20 - 10}{30 - 10} \cdot 0.1 \\
          &= \frac{10}{20} \cdot 0.1 \\
          &= 0.5 \cdot 0.1 \\
          &= 0.05
\end{aligned}
$$

Each line substitutes the given numerical values. The result is a **5 %** drop probability. If the current packet count since the last drop is $5$, the *final* drop probability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_f &= \frac{P}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P} = \frac{0.05}{1 - 5 \cdot 0.05} = \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{0.05}{0.75} \\
P_f &= 0.0\overline{6} \approx 6.67\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking:** The examiner awards **1 mark** for region identification, **1 mark** for the correct formula, **1 mark** for arithmetic, and **1 mark** for the final answer with units (probability is dimensionless). The *final* drop probability part is an extra **2 marks** if the question explicitly asks for it.

---

## 3.5 Count-to-Infinity Demonstration

Consider three routers A, B, C in a line: A — B — C. Initially all costs are 1, so the sink tree at A says "C is 2 hops away, go via B".

**Step 0:** A's distance to C = 2, B's distance to C = 1.
**Step 1:** Link B–C **fails**. B's distance to C becomes 16 (the *Infinity* threshold in RIP).
**Step 2:** A **periodically** sends its vector to B: "I think C is 2 away." B's local update:

$$
D_B(C) = \min\{ 1 + D_A(C),\, 16 \} = \min\{ 1 + 2,\, 16 \} = 3
$$

**Step 3:** A receives B's update: $D_B(C) = 3$.

$$
D_A(C) = \min\{ 1 + D_B(C) \} = 1 + 3 = 4
$$

**Step 4:** A sends new vector to B; B recomputes:

$$
D_B(C) = \min\{ 1 + 4,\, 16 \} = 5
$$

This cycle continues: 6, 7, 8, … , 15, 16. The count "creeps" upward one hop per update cycle. This is **Count-to-Infinity**.

### Effect of Poisoned Reverse
With poisoned reverse, A would advertise **infinity** to B for destination C, because A's best path to C is *through* B. B would then be forced to recompute $D_B(C) = 16$ immediately, halting the propagation.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Algorithm Analysis Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Question)

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is the highest-yield KTU artefact for Part B questions. Memorise the columns and at least 5 rows.**

| Parameter | Distance Vector (RIP) | Link State (OSPF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Algorithm basis** | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra SPF |
| **Information shared** | Distance vector to *all* destinations | LSP describing *only* local links |
| **Shared with** | Direct neighbours only | Flooded to *all* routers |
| **Convergence** | Slow (count-to-infinity) | Fast (OSPF: < 5 s) |
| **Loop-freedom** | Vulnerable (loops possible) | Loop-free after convergence |
| **Memory requirement** | Low ($O(V)$) per router | High ($O(V+E)$ LSDB) |
| **CPU requirement** | Low | High (Dijkstra on every change) |
| **Bandwidth overhead** | Low (small periodic updates) | High (initial flood) but low steady state |
| **Scalability** | Limited (~15 hops max) | High (hierarchical with areas) |
| **Metric** | Hop count (RIP) | Configurable cost (OSPF) |
| **Update trigger** | Periodic (30 s in RIP) | Event-triggered (LSP) |
| **Security** | No native auth (RIPv1); MD5 (RIPv2) | MD5 / SHA-HMAC authentication |
| **Real-world example** | RIPv2, IGRP | OSPFv2/v3, IS-IS |
| **Hierarchical?** | No | Yes (Area 0, Area 1, …) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Routing Algorithm Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    RA["Routing Algorithms"]
    RA --> NA["Non-Adaptive Static"]
    RA --> AD["Adaptive Dynamic"]

    NA --> F["Flooding"]
    NA --> RW["Random Walk"]
    NA --> SSP["Static Shortest Path"]

    AD --> ISOL["Isolated Backpressure"]
    AD --> CENT["Centralised SDN"]
    AD --> DIST["Distributed"]

    DIST --> DVR["Distance Vector Bellman Ford"]
    DIST --> LSR["Link State Dijkstra SPF"]

    DVR --> RIP["RIPv2 IGRP"]
    LSR --> OSPF["OSPF IS-IS"]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    class RA root
    class F,RW,SSP,RIP,OSPF leaf
    class NA,AD,ISOL,CENT,DIST,DVR,LSR branch
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every blue box is a *category*; every green box is a *concrete algorithm* from a real protocol suite. The KTU examiner will often ask students to redraw a portion of this tree.

---

## 4.2 Flooding Sequence (Time-Sequenced Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Source Node S
    participant A as Router A
    participant B as Router B
    participant C as Router C
    participant D as Router D
    participant DST as Destination T

    SRC->>A: Packet seqNo 1 hop 3
    A->>B: Forward seqNo 1 hop 2
    A->>C: Forward seqNo 1 hop 2
    B->>D: Forward seqNo 1 hop 1
    B->>A: Drop duplicate
    C->>D: Forward seqNo 1 hop 1
    C->>A: Drop duplicate
    D->>B: Drop duplicate
    D->>C: Drop duplicate
    D->>DST: First arrival shortest path
    Note over A,B,C,D: All other arrivals discarded by seen list
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** The first arrival at T comes via the *shortest* path, demonstrating that flooding finds the optimal path in terms of *latency* — but at the cost of massive redundancy.

---

## 4.3 Distance Vector Update — Conceptual State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init: Boot up
    Init --> Steady: Converged vector
    Steady --> Listening: Periodic timer fires
    Listening --> Update: Receive neighbour DV
    Update --> Recompute: Bellman-Ford on all destinations
    Recompute --> Compare: Old DV vs New DV
    Compare --> Steady: No change
    Compare --> Broadcast: Change detected
    Broadcast --> Steady: Send new DV to all neighbours
    Steady --> CountToInfinity: Link failure detected
    CountToInfinity --> Steady: Poisoned reverse applied
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Note:** The `CountToInfinity` state is the bug that historically brought down the ARPANET (October 1981 — see RFC 789). Memorise the date for the **December 2024 ESE**.

---

## 4.4 Link State Routing — Five-Step Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Step 1 Discover Neighbours HELLO packets"] --> S2["Step 2 Measure Cost ECHO round trip time"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Build LSP Link State Packet"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Flood LSP To all routers reliable"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Run Dijkstra Compute SPF tree"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 Install Routes In forwarding table"]

    classDef stepBox fill:#37474f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 stepBox
```

---

## 4.5 Congestion Control — Kleinrock Knee-Cliff Model

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph A["Throughput vs Load Curve Kleinrock 1978"]
        K1["Knee onset of congestion"] --> K2["Cliff congestion collapse"]
    end
    A --> B["Left of knee healthy operating point"]
    A --> C["Right of knee severe congestion retransmissions"]
    A --> D["Beyond cliff throughput collapse"]

    classDef zone fill:#263238,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    class K1,K2,B,C,D zone
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Power" function** is *maximum at the knee*. KTU exam: "Why is the knee the ideal operating point?" Answer: because the ratio (Throughput / Delay) — called **Power** — is maximised at the knee. Operating to the left wastes capacity; operating to the right wastes time.

---

## 4.6 RED Operational Zones (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q["Average Queue Length q bar"] --> Z1["Zone 1 Normal q bar less than Min th"]
    Q --> Z2["Zone 2 Congestion Avoidance Min th to Max th"]
    Q --> Z3["Zone 3 Forced Drop q bar greater than Max th"]

    Z1 --> A1["Drop probability equals 0"]
    Z2 --> A2["Drop probability increases linearly from 0 to Pmax"]
    Z3 --> A3["Drop probability equals 1 every packet dropped"]

    A2 --> A2F["Final Pf equals P divided by 1 minus count times P"]

    classDef zoneBox fill:#004d40,stroke:#a7ffeb,color:#ffffff
    classDef actBox fill:#bf360c,stroke:#ffccbc,color:#ffffff
    class Q,Z1,Z2,Z3 zoneBox
    class A1,A2,A3,A2F actBox
```

---

## 4.7 Choke Packet Feedback Loop (Process Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["Source Host Sending Rate S"] --> NET["Network Router R"]
    NET --> Q{"Queue Length greater than Threshold?"}
    Q -->|No| NET
    Q -->|Yes| CHOKE["Send CHOKE packet to source"]
    CHOKE --> REDUCE["Source reduces rate by factor alpha"]
    REDUCE --> WAIT["Wait for delta t seconds"]
    WAIT --> CHK{"New CHOKE received?"}
    CHK -->|Yes| REDUCE
    CHK -->|No| INCREASE["Slowly increase rate additive"]
    INCREASE --> NET

    classDef proc fill:#1a237e,stroke:#9fa8da,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#e65100,stroke:#ffcc80,color:#ffffff
    class SRC,NET,CHOKE,REDUCE,WAIT,INCREASE proc
    class Q,CHK decision
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The choke packet is **unicast** to the source, *not* flooded. The source decreases its rate by a **multiplicative** factor (typically 0.875), and increases it by an **additive** amount on recovery. This is the **AIMD** (Additive-Increase / Multiplicative-Decrease) pattern that underpins both **TCP congestion control** and **choke-packet mechanisms**.

---

## 4.8 Comparative Visual: DVR vs. LSR Information Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DVRPanel["Distance Vector Information Flow"]
        RA["Router R1"] -->|"Distance vector to all destinations"| RB["Router R2"]
        RB -->|"Distance vector to all destinations"| RA
        RB -->|"Distance vector to all destinations"| RC["Router R3"]
        RC -->|"Distance vector to all destinations"| RB
    end
    subgraph LSRPanel["Link State Information Flow"]
        LD1["R1"] -->|"LSP local links only"| LD2["R2"]
        LD1 -->|"LSP local links only"| LD3["R3"]
        LD2 -->|"LSP local links only"| LD3
        LD3 -->|"LSP local links only"| LD1
        LD2 -->|"LSP local links only"| LD1
        LD3 -->|"LSP local links only"| LD2
    end

    classDef dvrNode fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#90caf9,color:#ffffff
    classDef lsrNode fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#a5d6a7,color:#ffffff
    class RA,RB,RC dvrNode
    class LD1,LD2,LD3 lsrNode
```

> [!TIP]
> **DVR is N²** in the worst case (each router advertises $V$ entries to $N$ neighbours). **LSR is O(E)** for a single LSP flood. At Internet scale, LSR scales vastly better.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*"With a neat diagram, explain the Optimality Principle of routing. Hence construct the sink tree for a given network."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Optimality Principle states that *"if router J is on the optimal path from router I to router K, then the optimal path from J to K is the continuation of the same route."* This is proved by contradiction: assuming a shorter $J \to K$ suffix would contradict the optimality of the $I \to K$ path.
- **Sink Tree Construction (2 marks):** Starting from the chosen destination as root, for every other node, include the link from its selected parent (the neighbour that minimises $D_x(y) = c(x,v) + D_v(y)$). The result is a loop-free, directed tree spanning all routers with the destination as root. The tree is unique for a given set of link costs; all routers converge to *the same* tree once the algorithm stabilises.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*"Differentiate between Distance Vector Routing and Link State Routing. Mention one real-world protocol for each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Table Form Expected):**

| Parameter | Distance Vector | Link State |
|---|---|---|
| Information shared | Full distance vector to all destinations | Only local link costs (LSP) |
| Scope of sharing | Direct neighbours only | Flooded to all routers |
| Algorithm | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra SPF |
| Real-world protocol | **RIPv2**, IGRP | **OSPFv2**, IS-IS |

*(1 mark for difference, 1 mark for algorithm, 1 mark for protocol examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** per KTU 2024 regulations. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically **7 marks each**.

---

### Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** *Consider a network with 5 routers A, B, C, D, E. The link costs are: A–B = 2, A–C = 5, B–C = 1, B–D = 3, C–D = 2, C–E = 4, D–E = 1. Using the Bellman-Ford algorithm, compute the distance vector at router A until convergence. Show all iterations explicitly.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the count-to-infinity problem in distance vector routing. With a sequence diagram, show how poisoned reverse and split horizon solve it. Also mention the role of triggered updates.* **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution (Choice A)

#### Part (a) — Bellman-Ford Iteration (7 Marks)

**Initialisation ($t = 0$):** All routers have $D_x(y) = \infty$ for $y \neq x$.

$$
D_A^{(0)} = (0,\, \infty,\, \infty,\, \infty,\, \infty)
$$

**Iteration $t = 1$ at Router A** (neighbours of A are B and C):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(B) &= \min\{ c(A,B)+D_B(B),\ c(A,C)+D_C(B) \} = \min\{ 2+0,\ 5+\infty \} = 2 \\
D_A(C) &= \min\{ c(A,B)+D_B(C),\ c(A,C)+D_C(C) \} = \min\{ 2+\infty,\ 5+0 \} = 5 \\
D_A(D) &= \min\{ 2+\infty,\ 5+\infty \} = \infty \\
D_A(E) &= \min\{ 2+\infty,\ 5+\infty \} = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration $t = 2$** (assume B and C have advertised $D_B(D)=3$, $D_C(D)=2$, $D_C(E)=4$, $D_B(C)=1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(C) &= \min\{ 2+1,\ 5+0 \} = \min\{3,\,5\} = 3 \\
D_A(D) &= \min\{ 2+3,\ 5+2,\, \infty \} = \min\{5,\,7,\infty\} = 5 \\
D_A(E) &= \min\{ 2+\infty,\ 5+4,\ \infty \} = \min\{2+\infty,\,9,\infty\} = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration $t = 3$** (D and E now known via B-C-D-E):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(D) &= \min\{ 2+3,\ 5+2 \} = \min\{5,\,7\} = 5 \quad \text{(no change)} \\
D_A(E) &= \min\{ 2+4,\ 5+1 \} = \min\{6,\,6\} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

(Using updated $D_B(E) = 4$ and $D_C(E) = 1$ via $C \to D \to E$ path.)

**Iteration $t = 4$:** Converged (no further changes).

**Final Distance Vector at Router A:**

$$
D_A^{\text{final}} = (D_A(A),\, D_A(B),\, D_A(C),\, D_A(D),\, D_A(E)) = (0,\, 2,\, 3,\, 5,\, 6)
$$

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark for correct initialisation
- 1 mark per converged row (4 rows × 1 mark = 4 marks)
- 1 mark for final answer
- 1 mark for state "converged" explicitly
**Total: 7 marks**

#### Part (b) — Count-to-Infinity, Split Horizon, Poisoned Reverse, Triggered Updates (7 Marks)

**Count-to-Infinity (2 marks):** When a link fails, the affected router sets its distance to *Infinity* and advertises the new value. However, neighbours still hold *stale* information from prior updates. They compute their new cost as `1 + stale_value`, which is *less than infinity*. This stale value is propagated in the next update cycle, and the cost creeps up by exactly 1 per cycle. For a destination $K$ at distance $N$, recovery takes up to $N$ update cycles, even though the failure is local.

**Split Horizon (1 mark):** A router $X$ **never** advertises a route through $Y$ back to $Y$. That is, $X$ tells $Y$ that the distance to destination $K$ is infinity if $X$'s best path to $K$ is *through* $Y$. This prevents simple two-node loops.

**Poisoned Reverse (1 mark):** A stronger form of split horizon: $X$ advertises the distance to $K$ as **infinity (16 in RIP)** back to $Y$, **even if** $X$'s best path is via $Y$. This breaks two-node count-to-infinity loops in a single update cycle.

**Triggered Updates (2 marks):** Instead of waiting for the periodic 30-second timer (as in RIP), the router **immediately** broadcasts a new update upon detecting any change. This reduces convergence time from $O(N \times T_{\text{update}})$ to roughly $O(N)$ link-propagation delays. Limitation: triggered updates can themselves be **lost** in transit; some implementations add a small random delay to avoid storm.

**Example Sequence (1 mark):**

```
T=0 : B-C fails. B sets D_B(C) = ∞. A has D_A(C) = 2 (via B).
T=1 : A's periodic update reaches B. B computes D_B(C) = min(1+2, ∞) = 3.
T=2 : B's new update reaches A. A computes D_A(C) = min(1+3, 5) = 4.
T=3 : ... 5, 6, 7, ... counts upward to 16.
```

With **Poisoned Reverse** active, A would have advertised $\infty$ to B for destination C from the start. B's recomputation at T=1 would yield $D_B(C) = \min(1+\infty,\, \infty) = \infty$, halting propagation in one step.

---

### Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *Explain the five steps of Link State Routing. With a suitable network graph, demonstrate Dijkstra's algorithm step-by-step to compute the shortest path from source node S to all other nodes.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Compare and contrast Flooding and Link State Routing in terms of robustness, scalability, bandwidth usage, and convergence. Mention at least three advantages and three disadvantages of each.* **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution (Choice B)

#### Part (a) — Five Steps + Dijkstra (7 Marks)

**Five Steps (3 marks — 0.6 each):**

1. **Neighbour Discovery & Addressing:** Each router boots and sends a *HELLO* packet on each point-to-point link. The receiving neighbour responds with its own identifier, populating the local neighbour table.

2. **Cost Measurement:** The router sends a small *ECHO* packet and times the round-trip. Half of the round-trip time is used as the delay/cost. Optionally, the *configured* OSPF cost (often inversely proportional to bandwidth) is used instead.

3. **LSP Construction:** The router builds a *Link State Packet* containing:
   - `RouterID`, `SeqNum`, `Age`
   - For each neighbour: `(NeighbourID, Cost, ...)` tuples

4. **Reliable Flooding:** The LSP is sent on every interface except the one it arrived on. Every other router acknowledges receipt; on missing an ACK, the sender **retransmits**. Each router stores received LSPs in its **Link State Database (LSDB)** and re-floods them. Older LSPs (lower `SeqNum`) are discarded.

5. **Dijkstra SPF Computation:** Each router runs Dijkstra's algorithm on its LSDB, producing a shortest-path tree rooted at itself. The forwarding table is then installed from this tree.

**Dijkstra Worked Example (4 marks):**
Using the same 5-router network from Question B1 Choice A part (a), with source **A**:

| Iteration | $w$ chosen | $D(B)$ | $D(C)$ | $D(D)$ | $D(E)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (init) | — | 2 | 5 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 1 | B (cost 2) | 2 | $\min(5,2+1)=3$ | $\min(\infty,2+3)=5$ | $\infty$ |
| 2 | C (cost 3) | 2 | 3 | $\min(5,3+2)=5$ | $\min(\infty,3+4)=7$ |
| 3 | D (cost 5) | 2 | 3 | 5 | $\min(7,5+1)=6$ |
| 4 | E (cost 6) | 2 | 3 | 5 | 6 |

(Each iteration carries **0.8 mark** = 5 × 0.8 = 4 marks.)

**Final paths from A:**
- A → B (cost 2)
- A → B → C (cost 3)
- A → B → C → D (cost 5)
- A → B → C → D → E (cost 6)

#### Part (b) — Flooding vs. Link State (7 Marks)

**Tabular Comparison (4 marks — 1 mark per correct row of 4):**

| Parameter | Flooding | Link State |
|---|---|---|
| **Robustness** | Excellent (multiple copies, multiple paths) | Very good (each router has full map) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (exponential packet copies) | Good (hierarchical areas in OSPF) |
| **Bandwidth usage** | Very high (every link carries every packet's copies) | Moderate (LSPs flood only on topology change) |
| **Convergence** | Instant (always finds shortest path) | Fast (sub-second in OSPF) |
| **Memory** | Minimal (only seen-list) | High (full LSDB per router) |
| **CPU** | Minimal | High (Dijkstra on every change) |
| **Loop-freedom** | Always loop-free (uses hop count) | Loop-free after convergence |

**Flooding Advantages (1 mark):**
1. Maximum robustness — works even with severe link/router failures.
2. No routing tables or topology knowledge required.
3. Guaranteed to find the *shortest* path in terms of hops.

**Flooding Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. Generates massive duplicate traffic.
2. Impractical for large or bandwidth-limited networks.
3. Requires hop-count limit to prevent infinite circulation.

**Link State Advantages (1 mark):**
1. Fast, loop-free convergence.
2. Per-router view of the full topology enables advanced traffic engineering.
3. Scales to large networks via OSPF hierarchy (areas, ABRs, ASBRs).

**Link State Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. High memory and CPU load on every router.
2. Vulnerable to **LSP spoofing** if authentication is not enabled.
3. Initial flooding can cause transient congestion at boot.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Flooding with Link State flooding.** OSPF *uses* flooding to distribute LSPs, but the *routing algorithm* itself is **Link State (Dijkstra)**, not flooding. The examiner deducts **2 marks** if the student says *"OSPF uses flooding for routing."*
> 2. **Forgetting the `Age` field in LSPs.** Without `Age`, a corrupted old LSP with a higher sequence number could be accepted forever. Always mention the `Age` timeout.
> 3. **Skipping the convergence proof for Dijkstra.** A 14-mark Part B must include either the formal proof sketch or the iterative table. Bare algorithm statements lose up to **3 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing congestion control with flow control.** Flow control is *end-to-end* (sender–receiver); congestion control is *network-wide* (multiple senders and the network fabric). Examiner deducts **1 mark** per incorrect interchange.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Questions

> [!TIP]
> **Question 3 (Part A, 3 Marks):** *"What is a Choke Packet? Explain its working with the help of a flowchart."*
> **Expected Answer:** Define choke packet (1 mark). State that the router sends CHOKE to source (1 mark). Show that source reduces rate by multiplicative factor $\alpha$, waits for interval $\Delta t$, and may increase additively (1 mark).

> [!TIP]
> **Question 4 (Part A, 3 Marks):** *"Explain the three operational zones of Random Early Detection with the help of a graph of drop probability vs. average queue length."*
> **Expected Answer:** Label the X-axis as $\bar{q}$ (average queue) and Y-axis as $P$ (drop probability). Show three zones: Normal (constant 0), Congestion Avoidance (linearly increasing 0 to $P_{\max}$), Forced Drop (constant 1). Identify $\text{Min}_{\text{th}}$ and $\text{Max}_{\text{th}}$ as the zone boundaries. (1 mark per zone.)

> [!TIP]
> **Question 5 (Part B, 14 Marks):** *"Design a numerical problem based on RED where the student is given $\text{Min}_{\text{th}}, \text{Max}_{\text{th}}, P_{\max}, w_q$ and the current instantaneous queue length. Compute the average queue length and the final drop probability."* (Use the formulas in Section 3.4.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **This is your last-stop revision checklist. Tick every box before you enter the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Routing Algorithm Classification:** Non-adaptive (Flooding) vs. Adaptive (DVR, LSR).
- [ ] **Optimality Principle:** Sub-path of an optimal path is optimal → enables Sink Tree.
- [ ] **Flooding:** Every incoming packet on every outgoing line except the source line. Requires **Hop Count** and **Sequence Number** for termination.
- [ ] **Distance Vector Routing (DVR):** Bellman-Ford, neighbours only, vulnerable to count-to-infinity, slow convergence.
- [ ] **Bellman-Ford Formula:** $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$.
- [ ] **Count-to-Infinity Remedies:** Split Horizon, Poisoned Reverse, Triggered Updates.
- [ ] **Link State Routing (LSR):** Five steps — Discover, Measure, Build LSP, Flood, Run Dijkstra.
- [ ] **Dijkstra's Algorithm:** Greedy SPF, $O(V^2)$ naïve, $O((V+E)\log V)$ with heap.
- [ ] **DVR vs. LSR:** DVR is distributed & local; LSR is distributed & global.
- [ ] **Real Protocols:** RIPv2 (DVR), OSPFv2 (LSR), IS-IS (LSR), BGP (Path Vector — DVR extension).
- [ ] **Congestion Definition:** Demand exceeds capacity at some bottleneck.
- [ ] **Kleinrock's Knee-Cliff Model:** Knee = optimal operating point (max power); Cliff = collapse.
- [ ] **Congestion Prevention Policies:** Four layers — Network, Transport, Data Link, Application.
- [ ] **Choke Packet:** Sent *back* to source on congestion detection; source reduces rate by $\alpha \approx 0.875$.
- [ ] **RED vs. Drop Tail:** RED proactively drops to *avoid* congestion; Drop Tail reactively drops on overflow.
- [ ] **RED Average Queue:** $\bar{q} \leftarrow (1-w_q)\bar{q} + w_q \cdot q$.
- [ ] **RED Drop Probability:** $P(\bar{q}) = \frac{\bar{q} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}{\text{Max}_{\text{th}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}} \cdot P_{\max}$ in the linear zone.
- [ ] **RED Final Probability:** $P_f = \frac{P}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P}$ (avoids bias against bursty flows).
- [ ] **ECN:** Explicit Congestion Notification is RED's modern evolution — *marks* instead of drops.
- [ ] **Real Protocol Mapping:** RED → Linux `tc`, Cisco WRED, Juniper ECN; Choke → ICMP Source-Quench (deprecated).
- [ ] **Dijkstra vs. Bellman-Ford:** Same answer; Dijkstra is faster but requires global topology; Bellman-Ford is slower but works with distributed info.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** The 14-mark Part B on this module *almost always* combines **Bellman-Ford iteration** with the **count-to-infinity problem** OR **Dijkstra's SPF** with **sink-tree construction**. Practise drawing the iteration table and the routing tree side by side. If you can do both in 8 minutes, you are guaranteed to score **≥ 12 / 14** on the B-part.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — PCEOT602 / KTU 2024 SCHEME**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport Layer, Transport Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transport Layer, Transport Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Transport Layer** (Layer 4 of the **OSI Reference Model** and Layer 4 of the **TCP/IP Protocol Suite**) is the core of the entire protocol hierarchy. It is the first true **end-to-end** layer, meaning a program on the source machine carries on a conversation with a similar program on the destination machine, using the headers and messages of the transport layer.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCEOT602)**, the Transport Layer is responsible for providing **process-to-process delivery** of the entire message from a specific process running on one host to a specific process running on another host. It offers a variety of services to the upper layers, including **end-to-end flow control**, **error recovery**, and **sequencing**, while shielding the application layer from the physical realities of the underlying network.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Transport Services**
> The services provided by the transport layer can be divided into two broad categories:
> 1. **Connection-oriented Transport Service** (e.g., TCP)
> 2. **Connectionless Transport Service** (e.g., UDP)
> The 2024 scheme emphasizes the understanding of *primitives*, *Berkeley sockets*, *QoS parameters*, and the *TCP state machine*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Postal & Telephone System

Imagine you are sending a long, 50-page legal document to a law firm in another country.

- The **Network Layer** (IP) is the **global postal system** (trucks, planes, sorting offices). It figures out *which country* and *which city* the letter must go to. It treats the document as a single, opaque parcel.
- The **Transport Layer** is the **internal mailroom clerk at the law firm**. Once the parcel arrives at the firm's reception (the destination host), the mailroom clerk must look at the *department name* and *employee ID* written on the envelope and physically walk the document to **Ms. Priya in the Legal Department** (the specific process/port), not to the accountant next door.
- The **Data Link Layer** is the **delivery van on the local street**, moving the parcel from one sorting hub to the next.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The End-to-End Argument (Saltzer, Reed & Clark, 1984):**
> The transport layer is the *only* layer that can guarantee end-to-end correctness, because the network core (IP) is inherently **unreliable** and offers *no such guarantees*. Therefore, intelligence and reliability *must* be placed at the edges — in the transport layer.

## 1.3 Position in the Protocol Stack

| Layer | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model | Key Protocols | Unit of Data |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 7 | Application | Application | HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP | Data / Message |
| 6 | Presentation | (combined) | SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG | Data |
| 5 | Session | (combined) | NetBIOS, RPC | Data |
| **4** | **Transport** | **Transport** | **TCP, UDP, SCTP** | **Segment / Datagram** |
| 3 | Network | Internet | IP, ICMP, ARP | Packet / Datagram |
| 2 | Data Link | Network Access | Ethernet, PPP, Wi-Fi | Frame |
| 1 | Physical | Network Access | Cables, Fiber, Radio | Bits |

## 1.4 Relationship to the Underlying Network

The transport layer sits on top of the **Network Layer (IP)**, which provides a *host-to-host* best-effort delivery service. The transport layer enhances this best-effort service into a *process-to-process* service with the following **three critical responsibilities**:

1. **Multiplexing and Demultiplexing** — directing data to the correct application process using **port numbers** (16-bit integers ranging from **0 to 65535**).
2. **Reliability** — providing error detection, retransmission, sequencing, and acknowledgments (TCP only).
3. **Flow and Congestion Control** — ensuring that a fast sender does not overwhelm a slow receiver or congest the network.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** The Transport Layer answers the question, *"Which **application** (port) on which **host** (IP) should receive this data, and was it received correctly?"* — whereas the Network Layer only answers *"Which host?"*

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Services Provided to the Upper Layers

The transport layer provides a set of well-defined services to the session/application layer above it. These services can be analyzed across **five orthogonal dimensions** as per the KTU 2024 scheme:

### 2.1.1 Types of Transport Services

**A. Connection-Oriented Service (TCP)**
- A **virtual connection** (a logical, not physical, pipe) must be established *before* any data is transmitted.
- Three phases: **Connection Establishment → Data Transfer → Connection Termination**.
- All segments are delivered **in order**, **without errors**, **without duplication**, and **without loss**.
- Implements **flow control**, **error control**, and **congestion control**.

**B. Connectionless Service (UDP)**
- **No connection setup** is required. The sender simply transmits the segment (called a *datagram*) into the network.
- The service is **unreliable**, **unordered**, and **best-effort**.
- Provides **no flow control** and **no congestion control** — packets may be lost, duplicated, or arrive out of order.
- **Lower overhead** and **lower latency** make it ideal for real-time applications like VoIP, video streaming, and DNS.

### 2.1.2 Service Primitives

A transport service is formally specified by a set of **primitives** (operations) that the application layer uses to access the service. The standard set (as defined by the ISO/OSI model) is:

| Primitive | Meaning | Phase |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `LISTEN` | Server waits for incoming connection request | Connection Establishment |
| `CONNECT` | Client requests a connection to a server | Connection Establishment |
| `SEND` | Send data over an established connection | Data Transfer |
| `RECEIVE` | Block until data arrives | Data Transfer |
| `DISCONNECT` | Terminate the connection | Connection Termination |

The **state transition model** for these primitives is:

$$
\text{LISTEN} \rightarrow \text{BLOCKED} \xrightarrow{\text{CONNECT}} \text{ESTABLISHED} \xrightarrow{\text{SEND/RECV}} \text{ESTABLISHED} \xrightarrow{\text{DISCONNECT}} \text{IDLE}
$$

## 2.2 Quality of Service (QoS) Parameters

The transport layer guarantees a certain **Quality of Service (QoS)** to the application. The standard parameters are:

| QoS Parameter | Meaning | How it is Achieved |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Connection Establishment Delay** | Time between `CONNECT` request and confirmation | 3-way handshake overhead |
| **Connection Establishment Failure Probability** | Probability that connection cannot be established in time | Network congestion, server load |
| **Throughput** | Amount of user data transferred per unit time (bytes/sec) | Window size, network bandwidth |
| **Transit Delay** | Time between `SEND` and `RECEIVE` | Propagation, queuing, processing |
| **Residual Error Ratio** | Lost or garbled messages / total messages | Retransmission, FEC |
| **Transfer Failure Probability** | Probability that the connection is abruptly terminated | Network failure, timeout |
| **Protection** | Defense against unauthorized access | Encryption, authentication |
| **Priority** | Higher-priority messages are delivered first | QoS tagging, differentiated services |

## 2.3 Addressing: Ports and Sockets

Since multiple processes run on the same host, the transport layer must **multiplex** outgoing data and **demultiplex** incoming data. This is done using **port numbers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Port Number Ranges (IANA Registered):**
> - **Well-Known Ports:** **0 – 1023** (reserved for privileged services; e.g., HTTP = **80**, HTTPS = **443**, FTP = **21**, SSH = **22**, DNS = **53**, SMTP = **25**)
> - **Registered Ports:** **1024 – 49151** (assigned to specific user processes upon request)
> - **Dynamic / Private Ports:** **49152 – 65535** (used by clients for ephemeral connections)

A complete end-to-end connection is identified by a **5-tuple**:
$$
\text{Connection ID} = \{\text{Protocol, SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort}\}
$$

A **Socket** = `(IP address, Port number)`. The **Berkeley Sockets API** (the de facto standard in Unix/Linux/Windows) provides four essential socket primitives:

| Socket Primitive | Purpose |
|:---|:---|
| `socket()` | Create a new communication endpoint |
| `bind()` | Associate the socket with a local IP and port |
| `listen()` / `accept()` | Server-side: wait for and accept incoming connections |
| `connect()` | Client-side: initiate a connection to a server |
| `send()` / `recv()` | Transmit and receive data |
| `close()` | Terminate the connection |

## 2.4 The Transport Service Access Point (TSAP)

The transport layer exposes its services to the upper layers through logical endpoints called **Transport Service Access Points (TSAPs)**, analogous to the **Service Access Points (SAPs)** at lower layers. In practice, the TSAP corresponds to a **port number**, and an application that wants to communicate with a remote process binds to a TSAP and waits for a request from a remote TSAP.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Use Case |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_f}$ | Ratio of propagation time to frame transmission time (dimensionless) | Efficiency analysis |
| 2 | $U_{\text{Stop\&Wait}} = \dfrac{1}{1+2a}$ | Channel utilization of Stop-and-Wait ARQ | KTU numerical problems |
| 3 | $U_{\text{Pipelined}} = \dfrac{W}{1+2a}$ (for $W \ge 2a+1$) | Utilization of a Go-Back-N / Selective Repeat protocol with window size $W$ | High-throughput design |
| 4 | $\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT}$ | Bandwidth-Delay Product in bits (minimum window size for full utilization) | Window sizing in TCP |
| 5 | $T_{\text{3WHS}} = 1.5 \times \text{RTT}$ | Time to complete a TCP 3-way handshake | Connection setup analysis |
| 6 | $T_{\text{4WS}} = 1.5 \times \text{RTT}$ | Time to complete a TCP 4-way termination (overlaps with FIN retransmission) | Tear-down analysis |
| 7 | $\text{MSS} = \text{MTU} - 40$ | Maximum Segment Size (MSS) for TCP over IPv4 (IP header = 20B, TCP header = 20B) | TCP payload calculation |
| 8 | $N_{\text{ports}} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Total number of port numbers in TCP/UDP | Addressing capacity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Bandwidth-Delay Product is the most commonly tested formula in KTU board exams. If the sender's window size is smaller than the BDP, the pipe is *underutilized*; if larger, the pipe is *saturated* and may cause congestion. Modern TCP automatically scales its window (e.g., **TCP Window Scaling** option defined in **RFC 7323**).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stop-and-Wait Protocol Efficiency

The Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol is the simplest reliable transport mechanism and serves as the foundation for understanding pipeline protocols (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat).

### Definitions

Let us define the key timing parameters:

- $T_f$ = Time taken to **transmit a single frame** (frame transmission time) = $L / B$
  - where $L$ = frame length (in bits), $B$ = channel bandwidth (in bits per second)
- $T_p$ = **Propagation delay** = $d / v$
  - where $d$ = physical distance of the link, $v$ = signal propagation speed
- $T_a$ = **Time to send an Acknowledgment (ACK)** (assumed negligible for simplicity)
- $T_{TO}$ = **Timeout interval** (must be $\ge 2T_p + T_a + T_f$)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

For a **successful transmission** (no error, no timeout), the total time for one frame cycle is:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_f + T_p + T_a + T_p
$$

If we assume $T_a$ is very small and combine the propagation times:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_f + 2T_p
$$

The **channel utilization** $U$ is defined as the fraction of total channel time spent transmitting useful data:

$$
U = \frac{\text{Useful transmission time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \frac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $T_f$ and defining the dimensionless ratio $a = T_p / T_f$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2(T_p/T_f)} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Behavior:**
> - When $a \to 0$ (very short link, very high bandwidth, e.g., LAN): $U \to 1$ → channel is fully utilized.
> - When $a \to \infty$ (very long link, e.g., geostationary satellite): $U \to 0$ → channel is essentially idle most of the time.
> This is the *fundamental limitation* of Stop-and-Wait, which motivates **pipelined** ARQ protocols.

### Including Error Probability

If each frame is received correctly with probability $p$, the *expected* number of transmissions per successful frame is $1/p$. The expected cycle time becomes:

$$
E[T_{\text{cycle}}] = \frac{T_f + 2T_p}{p}
$$

The effective utilization (accounting for retransmissions) is therefore:

$$
U_{\text{eff}} = \frac{p \cdot T_f}{T_f + 2T_p} = \frac{p}{1 + 2a}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Pipelined Protocol Efficiency (Go-Back-N / Selective Repeat)

To overcome the low utilization of Stop-and-Wait, we allow the sender to transmit up to **$W$** frames before requiring an acknowledgment. The cycle time is now:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = W \cdot T_f + 2T_p
$$

The utilization is:

$$
U = \frac{W \cdot T_f}{W \cdot T_f + 2T_p}
$$

Dividing by $T_f$:

$$
U = \frac{W}{W + 2a}
$$

### Two Operating Regimes

**Regime 1: Window smaller than bandwidth-delay product ($W < 2a + 1$)**
The sender's window closes before the first ACK returns, so the sender must stall. The utilization remains:

$$
U = \frac{W}{1 + 2a} \quad \text{(as before)}
$$

**Regime 2: Window large enough to keep the pipe full ($W \ge 2a + 1$)**
The ACK for frame 1 returns *before* the sender's window is exhausted, so the sender can transmit continuously:

$$
U \to 1 \quad \text{as } W \ge 2a + 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** The minimum window size for full utilization is $W_{\min} = 2a + 1$. For a 1 Gbps link with RTT = 50 ms, $a \approx 50{,}000$, so $W_{\min} \approx 100{,}001$ — far larger than the legacy 16-bit TCP window (max 65,535 bytes). This is precisely why **TCP Window Scaling** (RFC 7323) was introduced.

## 3.3 Derivation of Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

The BDP tells us how many bits can be "in flight" in the network at any moment.

Let:
- $B$ = link bandwidth (bits/second)
- $\text{RTT}$ = round-trip time = $2T_p + T_f + T_a$ (seconds)

During one RTT, the sender can push $B \times \text{RTT}$ bits into the pipe before the first ACK returns. Therefore:

$$
\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT} \quad \text{(in bits)}
$$

The **minimum window size in bytes** required for full link utilization is:

$$
W_{\min} = \frac{\text{BDP}}{8} = \frac{B \times \text{RTT}}{8} \quad \text{(in bytes)}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A host has a 1 Mbps link with RTT = 100 ms. What is the BDP? What window size is needed to fully utilize the link?

**Solution:**

$$
\text{BDP} = 1 \times 10^6 \times 0.1 = 100{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

Converting to bytes:

$$
W_{\min} = \frac{100{,}000}{8} = 12{,}500 \text{ bytes}
$$

Since the default TCP receive window is often 65,535 bytes, this fits. But for a 1 Gbps link with the same RTT:

$$
\text{BDP} = 10^9 \times 0.1 = 100{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits} = 12.5 \text{ MB}
$$

The 64 KB legacy window is now grossly insufficient, requiring window scaling.

## 3.4 Derivation of TCP 3-Way Handshake Timing

The 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) requires **1.5 RTT** before any application data can be sent.

Let $T_{\text{SYN}}$ be the time to transmit the SYN segment (negligible compared to $T_p$).

- $t = 0$: Client sends SYN
- $t = T_p$: Server receives SYN
- $t = T_p$: Server sends SYN-ACK
- $t = 2T_p$: Client receives SYN-ACK
- $t = 2T_p$: Client sends ACK + (optionally) data
- $t = 3T_p$: Server receives ACK + data

Total time to first application byte = $3T_p = 1.5 \times \text{RTT}$ (since RTT = $2T_p$).

$$
T_{\text{3WHS}} = 1.5 \times \text{RTT}
$$

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: TCP vs UDP

| Feature | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) | UDP (User Datagram Protocol) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Connection Type | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Reliable (ACK + retransmit) | Unreliable (best-effort) |
| Ordering | In-order delivery | No ordering guarantees |
| Flow Control | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| Congestion Control | Yes (cubic, Reno, etc.) | No |
| Header Size | **20 bytes** (min), up to 60 | **8 bytes** (fixed) |
| Overhead | High | Low |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Multiplexing | Port number | Port number |
| Checksum | Optional (mandatory in modern stacks) | Optional |
| Typical Apps | HTTP, FTP, SSH, SMTP, Email | DNS, VoIP, Video streaming, TFTP, SNMP |
| State | Stateful | Stateless |
| Multicast support | No | Yes |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transport Layer Position in the TCP/IP Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Layer HTTP FTP DNS SMTP]
    B[Transport Layer TCP UDP]
    C[Network Layer IP ICMP ARP]
    D[Data Link Layer Ethernet WiFi PPP]
    E[Physical Layer Cables Fiber Radio]
    F[End to End Process to Process Delivery]
    G[Host to Host Routing]
    H[Hop to Hop Frame Delivery]
    I[Raw Bit Transmission]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E

    A -.-> F
    C -.-> G
    D -.-> H
    E -.-> I
```

## 4.2 Multiplexing and Demultiplexing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process A Port 80]
    P2[Process B Port 21]
    P3[Process C Port 53]
    TSAP_S[Transport Layer Sender Multiplexer]
    IP_S[IP Network Layer]
    IP_R[IP Network Layer]
    TSAP_R[Transport Layer Receiver Demultiplexer]
    P1R[Process A Port 80]
    P2R[Process B Port 21]
    P3R[Process C Port 53]

    P1 --> TSAP_S
    P2 --> TSAP_S
    P3 --> TSAP_S
    TSAP_S --> IP_S
    IP_S --> IP_R
    IP_R --> TSAP_R
    TSAP_R --> P1R
    TSAP_R --> P2R
    TSAP_R --> P3R
```

## 4.3 TCP 3-Way Handshake Connection Establishment

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    Note over C,S: CLOSED State
    C->>S: SYN seq x
    Note over S: SYN RCVD State
    S->>C: SYN seq y ACK x plus 1
    Note over C: SYN SENT State
    C->>S: ACK y plus 1
    Note over C,S: ESTABLISHED State

    Note right of S: Server transitions to ESTABLISHED upon receiving final ACK
    Note left of C: Client transitions to ESTABLISHED immediately after sending final ACK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 3-way handshake and not 2?**
> The 3-way handshake solves the **delayed duplicate packet problem**. If an old SYN from a previous connection is floating in the network, the client's ACK confirms that the *server* is using the *new* initial sequence number $y$, not replaying an old one. A 2-way handshake cannot distinguish an old, duplicated SYN from a fresh one.

## 4.4 TCP 4-Way Connection Termination

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    Note over C,S: ESTABLISHED
    C->>S: FIN seq u
    Note over C: FIN WAIT 1
    S->>C: ACK u plus 1
    Note over S: CLOSE WAIT
    Note over C: FIN WAIT 2
    S->>S: Send remaining data
    S->>C: FIN seq v ACK u plus 1
    Note over S: LAST ACK
    Note over C: TIME WAIT
    C->>S: ACK v plus 1
    Note over S: CLOSED
    Note over C: TIME WAIT 2 MSL then CLOSED
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TIME_WAIT State (2 × MSL):** After the client sends the final ACK, it enters TIME_WAIT for **2 × MSL** (Maximum Segment Lifetime, typically 60 seconds). This ensures that if the final ACK is lost, the server's retransmitted FIN can still be received and re-ACKed. Without TIME_WAIT, the client could be left in CLOSED while the server is still waiting for the final ACK.

## 4.5 TCP State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> LISTEN: passive open
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT: active open send SYN
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD: receive SYN
    SYN_SENT --> SYN_RCVD: receive SYN send SYN ACK
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED: receive SYN ACK send ACK
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED: receive ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1: close send FIN
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT: receive FIN send ACK
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2: receive ACK
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT: receive FIN send ACK
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK: send FIN
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED: receive ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED: 2 MSL timeout
```
