If the final result equals `0xFFFF` (all ones), the segment is deemed error-free. $$ \text{Result} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i' \quad \text{where} \quad \text{Result} = \text{0xFFFF} \Rightarrow \text{No Error} $$ #### Worked Numerical Example Suppose a TCP segment has: - TCP header = 20 bytes (10 × 16-bit words) - Data = 40 bytes (20 × 16-bit words) - Pseudo-header = 12 bytes (6 × 16-bit words) - Total words to sum: $N = 10 + 20 + 6 = 36$ words The sender computes $S$ from the first 35 words, then the **checksum is set** to whatever value makes the **36th "word"** (the checksum field itself) produce a final sum of `0xFFFF` when computed at the receiver. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The TCP pseudo-header is a defensive engineering choice:** it ensures that any IP-layer address corruption (e.g., a routing loop delivering the segment to the wrong host) is caught by the checksum, even though the IP header itself has its own (weaker) header checksum that doesn't cover the payload. ### 3.3 Derivation: Why "Data Offset × 4" Gives Header Length in Bytes The TCP header length must be expressed in **32-bit words** because the options field requires 32-bit alignment, and IPv4 historically aligned headers to 32-bit boundaries. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Header Length (bytes)} &= \text{Data Offset (in 32-bit words)} \times 4 \text{ bytes/word} \\ &= \text{Data Offset} \times 32 \text{ bits/word} \div 8 \text{ bits/byte} \\ &= \text{Data Offset} \times 4 \end{aligned} $$ For a 20-byte fixed header: $$ \text{Data Offset} = \frac{20 \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes/word}} = 5 $$ For a 60-byte maximum header: $$ \text{Data Offset} = \frac{60 \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes/word}} = 15 $$ This 4-bit field can encode $2^4 = 16$ values (0–15), and 5–15 are the valid TCP header lengths. ### 3.4 Analytical Insight: Sequence Number Byte-Stream Semantics Consider a connection where the ISN = 1000 and the sender transmits two segments back-to-back: - **Segment 1**: 500 bytes of data - **Segment 2**: 300 bytes of data | Segment | SeqNum Field | Data Length | Next Expected SeqNum | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1000 | 500 | 1500 | | 2 | 1500 | 300 | 1800 | The receiver's ACK for Segment 1 (if it received Segment 1 fully) would carry **ACK = 1500**, meaning "I have received bytes 1000 through 1499; send 1500 next." If the receiver received only the first 200 bytes of Segment 1 (cumulative ACK semantics), it would still send **ACK = 1200** — this is why **selective ACK (SACK)** was invented (RFC 2018) to allow receivers to explicitly report non-contiguous received blocks. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 TCP Segment Header — Visual Bit Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HEADER[TCP SEGMENT HEADER - 20 to 60 bytes] direction TB SP[Source Port\n16 bits] DP[Destination Port\n16 bits] SQN[Sequence Number\n32 bits] ACKN[Acknowledgment Number\n32 bits] DOFF[Data Offset\n4 bits] RES[Reserved\n3 bits] FLAGS[Control Flags\n9 bits] WIN[Window Size\n16 bits] CHK[Checksum\n16 bits] URP[Urgent Pointer\n16 bits] OPT[Options + Padding\n0 to 40 bytes] end PAYLOAD[Application Data\n0 to 65495 bytes] SP --> DP --> SQN --> ACKN --> DOFF --> RES --> FLAGS --> WIN --> CHK --> URP --> OPT --> PAYLOAD classDef fixed fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef options fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef payload fill:#064e3b,stroke:#022c22,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px class SP,DP,SQN,ACKN,DOFF,RES,FLAGS,WIN,CHK,URP fixed class OPT options class PAYLOAD payload ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TCP Header Responsibilities ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IDENT[IDENTIFICATION LAYER - 32 bits] SP_A[Source Port] DP_A[Destination Port] end subgraph RELIAB[RELIABILITY LAYER - 64 bits] SQN_A[Sequence Number] ACKN_A[Acknowledgment Number] end subgraph METADATA[STRUCTURAL METADATA - 16 bits] DOFF_A[Data Offset] RES_A[Reserved Bits] FLAGS_A[Control Flags] end subgraph CONTROL[FLOW & ERROR CONTROL - 48 bits] WIN_A[Window Size] CHK_A[Checksum] URP_A[Urgent Pointer] end subgraph EXTEND[EXTENSION LAYER - 0 to 40 bytes] OPT_A[Options] PAD_A[Padding] end IDENT --> RELIAB --> METADATA --> CONTROL --> EXTEND SP_A -.identifies.-> APP_SRC[Source App Socket] DP_A -.routes to.-> APP_DEST[Destination App Socket] SQN_A -.orders.-> BYTESTREAM[Byte Stream Ordering] ACKN_A -.confirms.-> DELIVERY[Delivery Confirmation] WIN_A -.throttles.-> FLOWCTL[Flow Control] CHK_A -.validates.-> INTEGRITY[End-to-End Integrity] FLAGS_A -.controls.-> LIFECYCLE[Connection Lifecycle] URP_A -.flags.-> URGENT[Urgent Data] classDef layer fill:#312e81,stroke:#1e1b4b,color:#ffffff classDef meta fill:#831843,stroke:#4c0519,color:#ffffff classDef target fill:#065f46,stroke:#022c22,color:#ffffff class SP_A,DP_A,SQN_A,ACKN_A,DOFF_A,RES_A,FLAGS_A,WIN_A,CHK_A,URP_A,OPT_A,PAD_A layer class APP_SRC,APP_DEST,BYTESTREAM,DELIVERY,FLOWCTL,INTEGRITY,LIFECYCLE,URGENT target ``` ### 4.3 TCP Three-Way Handshake — Header Field State Transitions ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant S as Server Note over C,S: Three-Way Handshake (SYN, SYN+ACK, ACK) C->>S: Segment 1
SYN=1, SeqNum=x (ISN)
ACK=0, Window=Wc Note right of S: Server allocates state,
SYN-Received S->>C: Segment 2
SYN=1, ACK=1
SeqNum=y (server ISN)
AckNum=x+1, Window=Ws Note left of C: SYN-ACK received,
connection half-open C->>S: Segment 3
SYN=0, ACK=1
SeqNum=x+1
AckNum=y+1, Window=Wc Note over C,S: ESTABLISHED state
Data transfer begins Note over C,S: Note: SeqNum x, y are 32-bit values
AckNum is cumulative (next expected byte) ``` ### 4.4 Pseudo-Header Construction for Checksum — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PSH[Pseudo-Header - 96 bits - NOT transmitted] SIP[Source IP\n32 bits] DIP[Destination IP\n32 bits] ZERO[Zero\n8 bits] PROTO[Protocol = 6\n8 bits] LEN[TCP Length\n16 bits] end subgraph TCPH[TCP Header - at least 160 bits] THDR[TCP Header Fields\n160 bits] end subgraph TDAT[TCP Data - variable] DATA[Application Payload\n0 to 65495 bytes] end SIP --> DIP --> ZERO --> PROTO --> LEN --> THDR --> DATA CHKSUM[Checksum = NOT sum of all 16-bit words]:::hl DATA -.computed over.-> CHKSUM classDef hl fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef ph fill:#1e40af,stroke:#0c1f4a,color:#ffffff classDef th fill:#5b21b6,stroke:#2e1065,color:#ffffff classDef td fill:#047857,stroke:#022c22,color:#ffffff class SIP,DIP,ZERO,PROTO,LEN ph class THDR th class DATA td ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **List any six fields of the TCP segment header and explain the role of the Sequence Number and Acknowledgment Number fields.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The TCP segment header contains the following key fields: 1. **Source Port (16 bits)** — Identifies the sending application. 2. **Destination Port (16 bits)** — Identifies the receiving application. 3. **Sequence Number (32 bits)** — Indicates the byte-stream offset of the first data byte in the segment. 4. **Acknowledgment Number (32 bits)** — Specifies the next expected byte (cumulative ACK); valid when ACK=1. 5. **Data Offset / Header Length (4 bits)** — Number of 32-bit words in the header. 6. **Window Size (16 bits)** — Flow-control credit advertised by the receiver. 7. **Checksum (16 bits)** — 16-bit one's-complement error-detection over header, data, and pseudo-header. 8. **Control Flags (URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN)** — Control connection lifecycle and special functions. **Role of Sequence and Acknowledgment Numbers (1 mark dedicated):** The **Sequence Number** is a **32-bit counter** that assigns a unique number to every byte in the byte stream. The initial value (ISN) is chosen randomly during connection setup. Each subsequent byte increments the counter by 1, allowing the receiver to: - Reorder out-of-order segments - Detect lost segments - Reassemble the byte stream correctly The **Acknowledgment Number** is sent by the receiver to indicate the **next byte it expects to receive**. It is *cumulative* — an ACK of 1500 means "I have successfully received all bytes up to 1499." This piggybacks flow information on data segments, reducing overhead. The ACK field is meaningful only when the **ACK flag = 1**. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **What is the purpose of the pseudo-header in TCP checksum computation? Why is it used even though it is not transmitted?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **pseudo-header** is a 12-byte (96-bit) construct **prepended to the TCP segment (header + data) only for the purpose of checksum computation**. It contains: - Source IP address (32 bits) - Destination IP address (32 bits) - Zero byte (8 bits) - Protocol number = 6 (8 bits, indicating TCP) - TCP segment length (16 bits) **Purpose (1.5 marks):** The pseudo-header allows TCP to **verify the correct end-to-end delivery context** even though these fields are not part of the TCP header itself. Specifically, it: 1. **Detects misdelivered segments** — if a segment is delivered to the wrong IP address (e.g., due to a routing error or IP header corruption), the recomputed checksum at the receiver will fail. 2. **Validates the protocol type** — ensures the segment is actually TCP, not a corrupted UDP/ICMP packet misinterpreted at the receiver. 3. **Confirms segment length** — the receiver can validate that no bytes were lost or appended during transit. **Why it is not transmitted (1.5 marks):** The pseudo-header is **not transmitted** to avoid duplication and to maintain **layering independence** — the IP layer has its own header with source/destination addresses and protocol fields. Including these in the TCP header would violate the **protocol layering principle** and cause duplication of information. However, the checksum *must* cover them to ensure end-to-end integrity across layers, hence the pseudo-header is reconstructed identically at both sender and receiver using fields from the IP header and TCP length. --- ### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **(a)** Draw the TCP segment header format and explain any **ten fields** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **mechanism of TCP checksum calculation** using the pseudo-header. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks **TCP Segment Header Diagram (1 mark):** The TCP header has a fixed 20-byte structure (160 bits) with an optional options field of up to 40 bytes, making the maximum size 60 bytes (480 bits). **Bit Layout (table or diagram, 1 mark):** | Bits 0–15 | 16–31 | 32–63 | 64–95 | 96–99 | 100–105 | 106–111 | 112–127 | 128–143 | 144–159 | 160+ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Source Port | Dest Port | Seq Num | Ack Num | Data Offset | Reserved | Flags | Window | Checksum | Urgent Ptr | Options+Padding | **Explanation of Ten Fields (5 marks — 0.5 mark each):** 1. **Source Port (16 bits):** Identifies the sending application process. Range 0–65,535. Combined with the source IP forms the source socket. 2. **Destination Port (16 bits):** Identifies the receiving application process. Examples: 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS), 22 (SSH), 53 (DNS). 3. **Sequence Number (32 bits):** The byte-stream offset of the first data byte in this segment. The ISN is randomly chosen during the 3-way handshake. Every byte transmitted increments the counter by 1. 4. **Acknowledgment Number (32 bits):** Specifies the next byte the sender of this segment expects to receive. It is cumulative and valid only when ACK flag = 1. Enables piggybacking. 5. **Data Offset (4 bits):** Indicates the header length in 32-bit words. Range 5–15. Used to locate the start of the data payload. 6. **Control Flags (6 bits):** Six 1-bit flags: - **URG** — Urgent pointer field is valid. - **ACK** — Acknowledgment field is valid. - **PSH** — Push function; data should be delivered to the application immediately. - **RST** — Reset the connection due to error. - **SYN** — Synchronize sequence numbers to initiate connection. - **FIN** — No more data from sender; used for graceful close. 7. **Window Size (16 bits):** Flow-control mechanism. Specifies the number of bytes the sender of this segment is willing to receive. Default range 0–65,535; with window scaling up to ~1 GB. 8. **Checksum (16 bits):** 16-bit one's-complement checksum over TCP header + data + pseudo-header. Mandatory (unlike UDP). Used for end-to-end error detection. 9. **Urgent Pointer (16 bits):** Meaningful only when URG = 1. Points to the byte following the urgent data, allowing out-of-band signaling. 10. **Options (0–40 bytes):** Variable-length field used for: - **MSS (Maximum Segment Size):** 4 bytes — negotiated during SYN. - **Window Scale:** 3 bytes (RFC 1323) — supports large windows. - **SACK (Selective ACK):** reports non-contiguous received blocks. - **Timestamps:** 10 bytes — RTT measurement and PAWS. **Padding:** appended so total header length is a multiple of 32 bits. --- ##### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Conceptual Setup (1 mark):** The TCP checksum uses a **16-bit one's complement** algorithm and covers **three components**: the TCP pseudo-header, the TCP header, and the TCP data. Unlike UDP, TCP's checksum is **mandatory**. **Step 2 — Pseudo-Header Structure (1.5 marks):** The pseudo-header has 96 bits, structured as: $$ \text{Pseudo-Header} = \{ \underbrace{\text{Source IP}}_{32}, \underbrace{\text{Destination IP}}_{32}, \underbrace{00000000}_{8 \text{ zero bits}}, \underbrace{00000110}_{8 \text{ bits, Protocol = 6}}, \underbrace{\text{TCP Length}}_{16} \} $$ **Step 3 — Concatenation and Padding (1 mark):** The message over which the checksum is computed is: $$ M = \text{Pseudo-Header} \,\|\, \text{TCP Header} \,\|\, \text{TCP Data} \,\|\, \text{Pad (if needed)} $$ If the total length of $M$ is not a multiple of 16 bits, a single 0 bit is appended as padding. **Step 4 — Word Sum (1.5 marks):** Break $M$ into 16-bit words $W_1, W_2, \dots, W_N$. Compute the one's-complement sum: $$ S = W_1 \oplus_c W_2 \oplus_c W_3 \oplus_c \dots \oplus_c W_N $$ where $\oplus_c$ denotes one's-complement addition (carry wrap-around). **Step 5 — Final Checksum (1 mark):** $$ \text{Checksum} = \overline{S} \quad \text{(one's complement of S)} $$ This value is placed in the Checksum field of the TCP header before transmission. **Step 6 — Receiver Verification (1 mark):** At the receiver, the **same pseudo-header** is reconstructed using the IP-layer fields. The receiver sums **all** 16-bit words of (pseudo-header ‖ TCP header ‖ TCP data), **including the received checksum field**. If the result is `1111 1111 1111 1111` (`0xFFFF`), the segment is error-free; otherwise it is discarded. **Worked Numerical Example (optional, 0 marks but adds clarity):** Suppose the message (excluding checksum field) sums to `0x4A3F`. Then: - $\text{Checksum} = \overline{0x4A3F} = 0xB5C0$ - Receiver sum of *all* words (including 0xB5C0) = `0x4A3F + 0xB5C0 = 0xFFFF` ✓ (no error) If a single bit flips in transit, say the word becomes `0x4A3E`, the receiver sum becomes `0xB5C1 ≠ 0xFFFF`, and the segment is discarded. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** Explain the **TCP connection establishment and termination procedures** with diagrams. Describe the role of the **SYN, ACK, and FIN flags** in the segment header. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a clear example, describe the **concept of window size and window scaling in TCP**. How does the effective window size formula help in long-fat networks (LFN)? **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks **1. Three-Way Handshake — Connection Establishment (3 marks):** TCP uses a **three-way handshake** to establish a connection. Each step carries specific header flag combinations. **Step 1 — SYN (Client → Server):** - Flags: **SYN = 1**, ACK = 0 - SeqNum = $x$ (client's randomly chosen ISN) - Window = $W_c$ (client's initial receive window) - State transition: Client moves to **SYN-SENT**; Server enters **LISTEN**. **Step 2 — SYN + ACK (Server → Client):** - Flags: **SYN = 1, ACK = 1** - SeqNum = $y$ (server's ISN) - AckNum = $x + 1$ (acknowledging client's SYN) - Window = $W_s$ - State: Server enters **SYN-RECEIVED**. **Step 3 — ACK (Client → Server):** - Flags: **ACK = 1**, SYN = 0 - SeqNum = $x + 1$ - AckNum = $y + 1$ (acknowledging server's SYN) - State: Both sides enter **ESTABLISHED**. Data transfer can now begin. **Diagram (1 mark):** ``` Client Server |--- SYN, Seq=x, Ack=0 ------>| (SYN-SENT) |<-- SYN,ACK, Seq=y, Ack=x+1 --| (SYN-RECEIVED) |--- ACK, Seq=x+1, Ack=y+1 --->| (ESTABLISHED) ``` **2. Four-Way Handshake — Connection Termination (2 marks):** TCP's graceful close uses a **four-way handshake** because TCP is **full-duplex** — each side must independently close its send channel. **Step 1 — FIN (Active Closer → Passive Closer):** - Flags: **FIN = 1, ACK = 1** - SeqNum = last byte sent + 1 - State: Active closer → **FIN-WAIT-1** **Step 2 — ACK (Passive Closer → Active Closer):** - Flags: **ACK = 1** - AckNum = FIN_SeqNum + 1 - State: Passive → **CLOSE-WAIT**; Active → **FIN-WAIT-2** **Step 3 — FIN (Passive Closer → Active Closer):** - Flags: **FIN = 1, ACK = 1** - State: Passive → **LAST-ACK** **Step 4 — ACK (Active Closer → Passive Closer):** - Flags: **ACK = 1** - State: Active → **TIME-WAIT** (2 × MSL); Passive → **CLOSED** **3. Role of Flags (1 mark):** - **SYN** — Initiates synchronization of sequence numbers; sent only during connection establishment. - **ACK** — Validates the Acknowledgment Number field; present in nearly every segment after handshake. - **FIN** — Signals graceful termination from the sender's side; sent by the side that has finished transmitting. --- ##### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks **1. Window Size Concept (2 marks):** The **Window Size field (16 bits)** in the TCP header carries the **receive window (rwnd)** in bytes. It tells the sender: *"I can currently accept up to $W$ bytes of data beyond the last acknowledged byte."* This implements **sliding-window flow control**: - The sender maintains a buffer of unacknowledged bytes within the receiver's advertised window. - When the receiver's application consumes data from its buffer, it sends an ACK with an updated (typically larger) window. - If the receiver's buffer fills, it advertises window = 0, causing the sender to stop. **2. The Bandwidth-Delay Product Problem (1.5 marks):** The **bandwidth-delay product (BDP)** measures the maximum amount of data that can be "in flight" on a network: $$ \text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth (bps)} \times \text{RTT (seconds)} \div 8 \quad \text{(bytes)} $$ **Long-Fat Networks (LFNs)** — networks with high bandwidth and high RTT — have a huge BDP. For example: - 1 Gbps link × 100 ms RTT = 100 Mb / 8 = **12.5 MB** of in-flight data needed. - But the legacy 16-bit Window field caps rwnd at **65,535 bytes (~64 KB)**. This means the sender is **idle most of the time**, waiting for ACKs. The connection is **window-limited**, not network-limited. **3. Window Scaling (RFC 1323) — 2 marks:** To solve this, the **Window Scale option** (3 bytes: kind=3, length=3, shift count = $S$, where $0 \le S \le 14$) is negotiated during the 3-way handshake. The effective window is computed by **left-shifting** the 16-bit Window field by $S$ bits: $$ \text{Effective Window} = \text{Window Field} \times 2^{S} $$ **Effective Window Formula (1 mark):** $$ W_{\text{eff}} = W_{\text{field}} \cdot 2^{S}, \quad S \in [0, 14] $$ For $S = 14$ (maximum), the effective window is: $$ W_{\text{eff}} = 2^{16-1} \times 2^{14} = 2^{29} = 1 \text{ GB} $$ **4. Worked Example (1.5 marks):** Suppose a server advertises Window = 65,535 with Shift = 8. Then: $$ W_{\text{eff}} = 65{,}535 \times 2^8 = 65{,}535 \times 256 = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ MB} $$ On a 1 Gbps link with 100 ms RTT (BDP = 12.5 MB), this 16 MB effective window is **more than sufficient** to keep the pipe full. Without scaling, only 64 KB could be in flight, severely underutilizing the network. > [!NOTE] > **Why max shift = 14?** Shifts of 0–14 cover $2^{16}$ to $2^{30}$ effective values, more than enough for any practical BDP. A 16-bit TCP sequence number space (4 GB) would otherwise wrap before a delayed ACK arrived on extreme high-BDP links — **timestamps + window scaling work together to prevent this** (PAWS — Protection Against Wrapped Sequences). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing sequence numbers with segment numbers** — sequence numbers refer to *bytes*, not segments. Always say "byte-stream offset." > 2. **Forgetting the pseudo-header** — when asked about TCP checksum, the pseudo-header is a *must-mention*. Students often describe UDP-style checksum and lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Off-by-one error on ACK** — an ACK value of $N$ means *"next expected byte is $N$"*, **not** *"I have received $N$ bytes."* A common losing-spot. > 4. **Window scaling is negotiated, not used immediately** — students often forget that the scale factor is exchanged in the SYN/SYN-ACK. > 5. **TCP checksum is mandatory** — never say "optional" or "can be disabled" in TCP (unlike UDP). > 6. **FIN does not close the connection alone** — both sides must send FIN, and TIME-WAIT state holds the connection for 2 × MSL after the final ACK to absorb stray segments. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **TCP Header = 20 bytes fixed + 0–40 bytes options**; data offset field encodes header length in 32-bit words (5 to 15). - **Sequence Number (32 bits)** is a **byte counter**, not a segment counter; ISN is randomly chosen. - **Acknowledgment Number (32 bits)** indicates the **next expected byte** (cumulative ACK); valid only when ACK=1. - **Six standard flags:** URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN — each controls a specific aspect of the TCP state machine. - **Window Size (16 bits)** = receive window in bytes; combined with Window Scale option (0–14) → effective window up to **1 GB**. - **Checksum is mandatory**, uses 16-bit one's complement, and covers **pseudo-header + TCP header + data**. - **Pseudo-header** contains Source IP, Dest IP, Zero, Protocol (=6), TCP length — reconstructed at receiver from IP layer. - **Urgent Pointer (16 bits)** is meaningful only with URG=1; offsets the urgent data location from the sequence number. - **Common options:** MSS (4 bytes), Window Scale (3 bytes), SACK (variable), Timestamps (10 bytes). - **Three-way handshake:** SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK. **Four-way close:** FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK. - **TIME-WAIT state** holds the connection for **2 × MSL** (typically 2 × 60 s = 120 s) to absorb late-arriving segments. - **Window scaling must be negotiated** during connection establishment — it cannot be changed mid-connection. - **Effective window formula:** $W_{\text{eff}} = W_{\text{field}} \times 2^{S}$, where $S$ is the negotiated shift count. - **PAWS (Protection Against Wrapped Sequences)** uses timestamps to distinguish between fresh and delayed segments when sequence numbers wrap around. - **TCP is full-duplex and connection-oriented** — every segment header must carry accurate source/destination ports, sequence numbers, and (usually) ACKs to maintain the byte-stream abstraction.

topics.title: TCP Connection Establishment and Release
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TCP Connection Establishment and Release

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — TCP Connection Establishment & Release

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** is a connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream transport layer protocol standardized by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** in **RFC 793** (with updates in **RFC 9293**, 2022). Before any user data can be exchanged between a client and a server, TCP mandates a formal *connection establishment* procedure known as the **Three-Way Handshake**, and a structured *connection release* procedure referred to as the **Four-Way Termination (or graceful close)** sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** Students must understand the TCP segment header fields used during setup/teardown, the state-transition diagram, and the rationale behind design choices like the Three-Way Handshake and the **2 Maximum Segment Lifetime (2MSL)** TIME_WAIT timer.

The two procedures are:

| Procedure | Purpose | Segments Exchanged |
|---|---|---|
| **Three-Way Handshake** | Synchronize sequence numbers and establish bidirectional connectivity | **3 segments** (SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK) |
| **Four-Way Termination** | Gracefully release the full-duplex connection in both directions | **4 segments** (FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK) |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Telephone Call"

Imagine you want to have a phone conversation with a friend:

1. **Establishment:** You **dial** the number (analogous to sending a **SYN** segment). Your friend **picks up and says "Hello, this is me"** (analogous to **SYN + ACK**). You reply **"Hi, I can hear you, let's talk"** (analogous to **ACK**). Now both parties are synchronized and ready.
2. **Data Transfer:** You speak and listen simultaneously (full-duplex communication, just like TCP).
3. **Release:** When the conversation ends, you say **"Goodbye"** (analogous to **FIN**). Your friend says **"Goodbye"** back (analogous to **ACK**). Your friend then says **"Okay, take care"** (analogous to a second **FIN**). You finally say **"Bye"** (analogous to a final **ACK**). Both sides formally end the call.

> [!NOTE]
> The asymmetry — where either side can independently decide to close — is what makes TCP a true *full-duplex* protocol and requires the *four* segments for graceful release (unlike establishment, which uses only *three*).

---

### 1.3 TCP Header Fields Critical to Setup & Teardown

The relevant fields in the **20-byte minimum TCP header** are:

| Field | Size (bits) | Role in Handshake |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequence Number (SEQ)** | **32** | Identifies the byte position of the first byte in the segment's payload. Initialized to **ISN (Initial Sequence Number)**. |
| **Acknowledgment Number (ACK)** | **32** | Next expected byte from the peer. `ACK = received SEQ + 1` during connection setup. |
| **SYN flag** | 1 | Synchronize sequence numbers — used in establishment. |
| **ACK flag** | 1 | Indicates the Acknowledgment field is valid. |
| **FIN flag** | 1 | No more data from sender — used in termination. |
| **Window Size** | 16 | Flow control — **not zero** during handshake. |
| **Checksum** | 16 | Mandatory error detection on header + data. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Initial Sequence Numbers (ISNs) are generated by a secure **32-bit counter** that increments approximately every **4 microseconds**, wrapping every **4.55 hours**. Modern TCP (RFC 6528) uses cryptographic ISN generation to defeat **TCP spoofing attacks**.

---

### 1.4 Why Connection-Oriented?

TCP is termed *connection-oriented* because the endpoints maintain **state information** for the duration of the conversation, including:

- The peer's **IP address and port number**.
- The current **sequence** and **acknowledgment numbers**.
- **Buffer** pointers, **window size**, **RTT estimates**, and **retransmission timers**.

This state ensures *reliable*, *in-order*, *duplicate-free* delivery — guarantees that **User Datagram Protocol (UDP)** does not provide.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Way Handshake — Step-by-Step Logic

### Segment 1: Client → Server (SYN)

- The **client** (typically the active opener) chooses an **Initial Sequence Number** $ISN_c$ and sends a segment with:
  - `SYN = 1`
  - `ACK = 0`
  - `SEQ = ISN_c$
  - No payload (length = 0)
- The client transitions from `CLOSED → SYN_SENT`.
- The segment consumes **one sequence number** even though it carries no bytes.

### Segment 2: Server → Client (SYN + ACK)

- The **server** allocates resources, chooses its own $ISN_s$, and replies with:
  - `SYN = 1`
  - `ACK = 1`
  - `SEQ = ISN_s$
  - `ACK = ISN_c + 1$  *(acknowledges the client's SYN)*
- The server transitions from `LISTEN → SYN_RECEIVED`.

### Segment 3: Client → Server (ACK)

- The **client** confirms the server's SYN with:
  - `SYN = 0`
  - `ACK = 1`
  - `SEQ = ISN_c + 1$  *(consumed by the SYN)*
  - `ACK = ISN_s + 1$  *(acknowledges the server's SYN)*
- Both sides transition to `ESTABLISHED`. Data transfer can now begin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three segments and not two?** A two-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK) would fail to handle the case of *duplicate* old SYN segments arriving from the network (e.g., from a previous connection attempt). The third ACK synchronizes both sides and lets the server confirm the client's ability to receive. This is called the **delayed-duplicate problem** or the **SYN collision** problem.

---

## 2.2 The Four-Way Termination Sequence

Because TCP is **full-duplex**, each direction must be closed *independently*. Either side (client or server) can initiate termination.

### Segment 1: Active Closer → Passive Closer (FIN)

- The **active closer** sends `FIN = 1` with current SEQ number. This signals "I have no more data to send."
- The active closer enters `FIN_WAIT_1`.

### Segment 2: Passive Closer → Active Closer (ACK)

- The **passive closer** ACKs the FIN with `ACK = received SEQ + 1$.
- The active closer moves to `FIN_WAIT_2`. The passive closer enters `CLOSE_WAIT` — it can still send data upstream.
- At this point the connection is **half-closed**.

### Segment 3: Passive Closer → Active Closer (FIN)

- When the passive closer finishes sending its remaining data, it sends its own `FIN = 1$.
- It enters `LAST_ACK`.

### Segment 4: Active Closer → Passive Closer (ACK)

- The active closer ACKs the second FIN and enters `TIME_WAIT`.
- The passive closer receives the ACK and transitions to `CLOSED`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TIME_WAIT Duration = 2 × MSL**, where MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime) is the maximum time any segment can remain alive in the network. The IETF-recommended value is **MSL = 120 seconds**, so **TIME_WAIT = 240 seconds** (or 4 minutes). This wait ensures:
> 1. The final ACK reaches the peer (retransmission safety).
> 2. Any *old* duplicate segments from this connection expire before the same `(client_IP, client_port, server_IP, server_port)` 4-tuple is reused.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential**. Memorize both forms (algebraic and verbal).

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Verbal Statement | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SYN acknowledgement | $ACK_{recv} = SEQ_{peer} + 1$ | ACK number equals peer's SEQ plus 1, since SYN consumes one sequence number. | bytes |
| 2 | Data acknowledgement | $ACK_{recv} = SEQ_{peer} + L$ | ACK number equals peer's SEQ plus length of payload $L$. | bytes |
| 3 | Next SEQ after segment | $SEQ_{next} = SEQ_{sent} + L_{payload} + S_{ctrl}$ | Add payload bytes plus sequence-number consumption by control flags. SYN/FIN each consume 1. | bytes |
| 4 | TIME_WAIT duration | $T_{TW} = 2 \cdot MSL$ | Default MSL = 120 s ⇒ TIME_WAIT = 240 s. | seconds |
| 5 | ISN wrap time | $T_{wrap} = \dfrac{2^{32} \cdot 4\,\mu s}{3600} \approx 4.55\,h$ | ISN counter wraps every ~4.55 hours at 4 µs tick. | hours |
| 6 | Half-close payload limit | $L \leq W - S_{ctrl}$ | Last data segment must respect receiver's window. | bytes |
| 7 | Simultaneous open | Allowed but rare | Both sides send SYN to each other; only one connection is established. | — |
| 8 | Simultaneous close | Allowed | Both sides send FIN; each side independently traverses FIN_WAIT_1 → CLOSING → TIME_WAIT. | — |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Browsing (HTTP/HTTPS over TCP Port 80/443):** Every page load begins with a three-way handshake. Browsers often use **TCP Fast Open (TFO, RFC 7413)** to send data in the SYN segment itself, saving one RTT.
- **Database Replication:** Stateful connections (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) use persistent TCP. The connection release guarantees all writes are flushed.
- **Financial Trading Systems:** TIME_WAIT reuse is tuned via `SO_LINGER` and `tcp_tw_reuse` sysctl parameters to reduce latency.
- **Security:** The handshake is the foundation of defenses like **SYN cookies** and the vector of attacks like **SYN flooding**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Sequence & Acknowledgment Number Evolution

> [!NOTE]
> The example below assumes an **Initial Sequence Number $ISN_c = 100$** for the client and **$ISN_s = 300$** for the server. We trace every sequence number transition to ensure no ambiguity.

### Stage 1: SYN (Client → Server)

The client sends:

$$SEQ_{client} = ISN_c = 100$$

Since the SYN flag is set, the segment **consumes one sequence number** even though it carries no payload. Therefore:

$$SEQ_{next,\ client} = 100 + 1 = 101$$

No ACK is sent, so `ACK = 0`.

### Stage 2: SYN + ACK (Server → Client)

The server replies acknowledging the client's SYN by setting:

$$ACK_{server} = SEQ_{client} + 1 = 100 + 1 = 101$$

The server's own sequence number is initialized:

$$SEQ_{server} = ISN_s = 300$$

Again, the SYN flag consumes one sequence number on the server's side:

$$SEQ_{next,\ server} = 300 + 1 = 301$$

### Stage 3: ACK (Client → Server)

The client ACKs the server's SYN:

$$ACK_{client} = SEQ_{server} + 1 = 300 + 1 = 301$$

The client's sequence number, after consuming its earlier SYN, is:

$$SEQ_{client} = 101$$

After sending this ACK, both sides are in `ESTABLISHED` state, and `SEQ_{client} = 101$, `SEQ_{server} = 301`.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Half-Close with 500 Bytes of Data

Assume after connection establishment, the **client sends 500 bytes** of data, and then **half-closes**.

### Step 1: Client sends 500 bytes

$$SEQ_{client} = 101 \quad \text{(continues from after SYN)}$$

Payload length $L = 500$. The next expected sequence number from the server's perspective:

$$ACK_{server} = 101 + 500 = 601$$

The client's new sequence number:

$$SEQ_{next,\ client} = 101 + 500 = 601$$

### Step 2: Client sends FIN (half-close)

The client sets `FIN = 1` with $SEQ = 601$. FIN consumes one sequence number:

$$SEQ_{next,\ client} = 601 + 1 = 602$$

The server ACKs the FIN:

$$ACK_{server} = 601 + 1 = 602$$

Now the client is in `FIN_WAIT_2` (it can still receive data, but cannot send).

### Step 3: Server sends remaining 200 bytes + FIN

Server payload length $L = 200$:

$$SEQ_{server} = 301 + 200 = 501$$

$$ACK_{server} = 601 + 1 + 0 = 602 \quad \text{(unchanged, FIN not yet acked from server's view)}$$

After the 200 bytes:

$$SEQ_{next,\ server} = 301 + 200 = 501$$

The server then sends FIN:

$$SEQ_{server} = 501 \quad (\text{FIN consumes 1}) \Rightarrow SEQ_{next,\ server} = 502$$

### Step 4: Client sends final ACK

$$ACK_{client} = SEQ_{server} + 1 = 501 + 1 = 502$$

$$SEQ_{client} = 602 \quad (\text{after FIN})$$

Client enters `TIME_WAIT`. Server enters `CLOSED`.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — State Transitions

| Current State | Event | Action | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CLOSED** | Application calls `connect()` | Send SYN | **SYN_SENT** |
| **LISTEN** | Receive SYN | Send SYN + ACK | **SYN_RECEIVED** |
| **SYN_SENT** | Receive SYN + ACK | Send ACK | **ESTABLISHED** |
| **SYN_RECEIVED** | Receive ACK | — | **ESTABLISHED** |
| **ESTABLISHED** | Application calls `close()` | Send FIN | **FIN_WAIT_1** |
| **FIN_WAIT_1** | Receive ACK | — | **FIN_WAIT_2** |
| **FIN_WAIT_2** | Receive FIN | Send ACK | **TIME_WAIT** |
| **ESTABLISHED** (peer) | Receive FIN | Send ACK | **CLOSE_WAIT** |
| **CLOSE_WAIT** | Application calls `close()` | Send FIN | **LAST_ACK** |
| **LAST_ACK** | Receive ACK | — | **CLOSED** |
| **TIME_WAIT** | 2 × MSL timer expires | — | **CLOSED** |

---

## 3.4 Why TIME_WAIT Must Be Exactly 2 × MSL — A Formal Argument

Let:
- $T_{tx}$ = time when the active closer sends the final ACK.
- $T_{ack\_arrive}$ = time when the ACK reaches the passive closer.
- $R$ = worst-case one-way network delay.

$$T_{ack\_arrive} \leq T_{tx} + R$$

The passive closer waits one MSL for a retransmitted FIN. If it does not arrive within MSL after receiving the ACK, it retransmits. The worst-case retransmission:

$$T_{retransmit} = T_{ack\_arrive} + MSL \leq T_{tx} + R + MSL$$

The active closer must remain in TIME_WAIT until this retransmission (if any) could arrive:

$$T_{close} \geq T_{tx} + R + MSL$$

Since the *worst-case* for the original FIN's own propagation is also bounded by MSL, we sum:

$$T_{close} - T_{tx} \geq MSL + MSL = 2 \cdot MSL$$

$$\boxed{T_{TIME\_WAIT} = 2 \cdot MSL}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This guarantees that no segment from the old connection can be mistaken for a new one once the 4-tuple `(src_ip, src_port, dst_ip, dst_port)` is reused. Without this, stale segments could corrupt a brand-new connection.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Three-Way Handshake + Data + Graceful Close

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant ClientApp as Client Application
    participant ClientTCP as Client TCP
    participant ServerTCP as Server TCP
    participant ServerApp as Server Application

    Note over ClientTCP,ServerTCP: CONNECTION ESTABLISHMENT (Three-Way Handshake)
    ClientApp->>ClientTCP: connect()
    ClientTCP->>ServerTCP: SYN (SEQ = x, ACK = 0)
    ServerTCP->>ClientTCP: SYN+ACK (SEQ = y, ACK = x+1)
    ClientTCP->>ServerTCP: ACK (SEQ = x+1, ACK = y+1)
    Note over ClientTCP,ServerTCP: ESTABLISHED

    Note over ClientTCP,ServerTCP: DATA TRANSFER
    ClientTCP->>ServerTCP: DATA (SEQ = x+1, ACK = y+1, 500 bytes)
    ServerTCP->>ClientTCP: ACK (SEQ = y+1, ACK = x+502)
    ServerTCP->>ClientTCP: DATA (SEQ = y+1, ACK = x+502, 200 bytes)
    ClientTCP->>ServerTCP: ACK (SEQ = x+502, ACK = y+201)

    Note over ClientTCP,ServerTCP: CONNECTION RELEASE (Four-Way Termination)
    ClientApp->>ClientTCP: close()
    ClientTCP->>ServerTCP: FIN (SEQ = x+502)
    ServerTCP->>ClientTCP: ACK (ACK = x+503)
    Note over ClientTCP: FIN_WAIT_2
    ServerApp->>ServerTCP: close()
    ServerTCP->>ClientTCP: FIN (SEQ = y+201)
    ClientTCP->>ServerTCP: ACK (ACK = y+202)
    Note over ClientTCP: TIME_WAIT (2 x MSL)
    Note over ServerTCP: CLOSED
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Full TCP Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> LISTEN: passive open (server)
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT: active open (client connect)

    LISTEN --> SYN_RECEIVED: receive SYN, send SYN+ACK
    SYN_SENT --> SYN_RECEIVED: simultaneous open, receive SYN
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED: receive SYN+ACK, send ACK
    SYN_RECEIVED --> ESTABLISHED: receive ACK
    SYN_RECEIVED --> FIN_WAIT_1: simultaneous close, send FIN

    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1: close, send FIN
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT: receive FIN, send ACK

    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2: receive ACK
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> CLOSING: receive FIN (no ACK yet)
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT: receive FIN, send ACK

    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK: close, send FIN
    CLOSING --> TIME_WAIT: receive ACK

    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED: receive ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED: 2 x MSL timer expires

    CLOSED --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Segment Field Structure (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        A1["User Data Payload"]
    end

    subgraph TCP["TRANSPORT LAYER - TCP SEGMENT"]
        B1["Source Port"]
        B2["Destination Port"]
        B3["Sequence Number"]
        B4["Acknowledgment Number"]
        B5["Flags: SYN / ACK / FIN / RST"]
        B6["Window Size"]
        B7["Checksum"]
        B8["Urgent Pointer"]
    end

    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER - IP DATAGRAM"]
        C1["Source IP"]
        C2["Destination IP"]
        C3["Protocol = 6 TCP"]
    end

    A1 --> B3
    A1 --> B8
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B5 --> C3
    B7 --> B3
    B7 --> B4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block-level architecture shows how the application data is encapsulated by TCP header fields (especially SEQ, ACK, and control flags), which are themselves wrapped inside an IP datagram. During handshake/teardown, the *flags* field is what distinguishes control segments from data segments.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Explain the three-way handshake used by TCP to establish a connection. List the flags set in each of the three segments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The TCP three-way handshake synchronizes sequence numbers and establishes a full-duplex connection between a client and server in **three segments**:

1. **Segment 1 (Client → Server):** The client sends a segment with `SYN = 1` and `ACK = 0`, carrying its Initial Sequence Number $ISN_c$. The client enters `SYN_SENT`.
2. **Segment 2 (Server → Client):** The server replies with `SYN = 1` and `ACK = 1`. The sequence number is $ISN_s$, and the acknowledgment number is $ISN_c + 1$. The server enters `SYN_RECEIVED`.
3. **Segment 3 (Client → Server):** The client sends `SYN = 0` and `ACK = 1` with `ACK = ISN_s + 1$. Both sides enter `ESTABLISHED`.

**(2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for listing flags.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is TIME_WAIT state in TCP? Why is the duration fixed at 2 × MSL?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**TIME_WAIT** (also called 2MSL_WAIT) is a state the *active closer* enters after sending the final ACK during connection termination. It lasts **2 × MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime)** — typically **240 seconds** (with MSL = 120 s).

The duration is fixed at **2 × MSL** for two reasons:

1. **ACK Reliability:** The final ACK could be lost; the passive closer retransmits its FIN. Holding the active closer in TIME_WAIT ensures it can re-send the ACK if needed.
2. **Old Segment Discard:** It guarantees that any *old duplicate segments* from the previous connection are purged from the network before the same 4-tuple `(src_ip, src_port, dst_ip, dst_port)` is reused, preventing stale data from corrupting a new connection.

**(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for the two reasons.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly *lose marks* by (a) confusing the SYN-flag sequence-number consumption rule, (b) forgetting that FIN also consumes one sequence number, and (c) omitting the state transitions alongside the segment diagram. Always pair the segment exchange with the corresponding state-transition table for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat timing diagram, illustrate the TCP three-way handshake. Explain the role of each segment and show the sequence and acknowledgment number evolution if the client's ISN is 200 and server's ISN is 500.**

**Model Solution:**

```
Client                          Server
------                          ------
CLOSED                          LISTEN

  |---[SYN, SEQ=200, ACK=0]-------->|     Client: CLOSED → SYN_SENT
  |                                  |     Server: LISTEN → SYN_RECEIVED

  |<--[SYN, SEQ=500, ACK=201]--------|     ACK = 200+1 = 201

  |---[ACK, SEQ=201, ACK=501]------>|     ACK = 500+1 = 501
  |                                  |     Both: ESTABLISHED
```

**Sequence Number Evolution:**

| Step | Segment | SEQ Sent | SEQ Consumes | Next SEQ | ACK Field |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SYN (Client) | 200 | 1 (SYN flag) | 201 | — |
| 2 | SYN+ACK (Server) | 500 | 1 (SYN flag) | 501 | 200 + 1 = **201** |
| 3 | ACK (Client) | 201 | 0 (no flag) | 201 | 500 + 1 = **501** |

**Role of each segment:**
- **SYN:** Synchronizes the client's initial sequence number.
- **SYN + ACK:** Server agrees, sends its own ISN, and acknowledges client's SYN.
- **ACK:** Client confirms; both sides have synchronized ISNs.

**(4 marks for diagram, 2 marks for SEQ/ACK evolution, 1 mark for role explanation.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the four-way handshake used by TCP for connection termination. Use a state transition diagram in your explanation. What is half-close and when does it occur?**

**Model Solution:**

The TCP connection termination requires four segments because TCP is *full-duplex* — each direction must be closed independently.

**Four-Way Termination Sequence:**

```
Active Closer (Client)              Passive Closer (Server)
------------------------            ------------------------
ESTABLISHED                         ESTABLISHED
                                   
  |---[FIN, SEQ=x, ACK=0]---------->|     Client: FIN_WAIT_1
  |                                  |     Server: ESTABLISHED → CLOSE_WAIT
  |<--[ACK, SEQ=y, ACK=x+1]----------|     Client: FIN_WAIT_1 → FIN_WAIT_2
                                   
  |     (half-close: client cannot  |
  |      send, but server can)      |
  |                                  |
  |<--[FIN, SEQ=y, ACK=x+1]---------|     Server: CLOSE_WAIT → LAST_ACK
  |                                  |
  |---[ACK, SEQ=x+1, ACK=y+1]------>|     Client: FIN_WAIT_2 → TIME_WAIT
  |                                  |     Server: LAST_ACK → CLOSED
```

**State Transitions:**

| Event | Active Closer State | Passive Closer State |
|---|---|---|
| Send FIN | FIN_WAIT_1 | ESTABLISHED |
| Receive ACK | FIN_WAIT_2 | CLOSE_WAIT |
| Receive FIN | TIME_WAIT (after ACK) | LAST_ACK |
| Receive final ACK | TIME_WAIT → CLOSED (after 2 MSL) | CLOSED |

**Half-Close:** A half-close occurs between the **first FIN** and the **second FIN**. The active closer has signaled "no more data to send," but the passive closer can still send data upstream. The connection is unidirectional at this point. This is exposed via the socket API as `shutdown(SHUT_WR)`.

**(3 marks for segment exchange, 2 marks for state diagram, 2 marks for half-close explanation.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain why TCP uses a three-way handshake instead of a two-way handshake. How does it solve the delayed-duplicate problem?**

**Model Solution:**

A two-way handshake would consist of only:
1. Client → Server: **SYN**
2. Server → Client: **SYN + ACK**

The third segment (client → server ACK) is **essential** to solve the **delayed-duplicate problem**.

**The Delayed-Duplicate Problem:**

Consider an old, retransmitted `SYN` from a previous connection arriving at the server:

- The server replies with `SYN + ACK` and allocates buffers (state resources).
- The original client is unaware of this old duplicate and **never sends the third ACK**.
- The server wastes resources waiting for an ACK that will never arrive.

**Solution — Three-Way Handshake:**

The mandatory third ACK from the client forces the *client* to confirm the connection. Only when the server receives this ACK does it commit resources. Any old duplicate `SYN` will not be confirmed by a currently active client, and the server's half-open state will time out and be cleaned up.

Additionally, the three-way handshake ensures that both sides have **synchronized sequence numbers** correctly — the client's ACK to the server confirms that the client received the server's `ISN_s`, and vice-versa.

**Mathematical Justification:**

$$P_{collision} = \frac{1}{2^{32}} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-10}$$

With a 32-bit ISN space, the probability of an old `SYN` colliding with a new connection is negligible, but the third ACK still validates uniqueness in real time.

**(3 marks for delayed-duplicate explanation, 2 marks for three-way solution, 2 marks for sequence-number synchronization.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A TCP connection is established between host A (client) and host B (server). After the handshake, A sends 800 bytes of data and then initiates a graceful close. Assuming $ISN_A = 1000$ and $ISN_B = 3000$, compute:**
**(i) The sequence and acknowledgment numbers in the third segment of the handshake.**
**(ii) The acknowledgment number sent by B after receiving A's 800-byte data segment.**
**(iii) The sequence number in A's FIN segment.**
**(iv) The acknowledgment number sent by A in response to B's FIN.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Third handshake segment (Client → Server):**

$$SEQ_A = ISN_A + 1 = 1000 + 1 = 1001$$

$$ACK_A = ISN_B + 1 = 3000 + 1 = 3001$$

**(ii) B's ACK after receiving 800 bytes:**

$$ACK_B = SEQ_A + L = 1001 + 800 = 1801$$

**(iii) A's FIN sequence number:**

After sending 800 bytes, A's next SEQ = 1801. The FIN consumes one sequence number, so:

$$SEQ_{A,FIN} = 1801$$

**(iv) A's ACK in response to B's FIN:**

B's FIN has `SEQ = 3001` (after its own SYN consumed one byte). A acknowledges:

$$ACK_A = SEQ_B + 1 = 3001 + 1 = 3002$$

**Summary Table:**

| Step | Sender | SEQ | ACK |
|---|---|---|---|
| Handshake ACK | A → B | 1001 | 3001 |
| Data ACK | B → A | 3001 | 1801 |
| A's FIN | A → B | 1801 | 3001 |
| A's Final ACK | A → B | 1802 | 3002 |

**(2 marks per sub-question, 7 marks total.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting that SYN and FIN consume sequence numbers.** Many students compute `ACK = SEQ + payload` only, missing the control flag's consumption.
> 2. **Conflating half-close with full close.** Half-close ends only *one direction* of data flow; full close ends both.
> 3. **Skipping the state diagram.** KTU examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for the state transition diagram alongside the segment exchange.
> 4. **Wrong TIME_WAIT value.** TIME_WAIT is held by the *active closer*, not the passive closer (which moves straight to CLOSED).
> 5. **Mixing up simultaneous open vs. simultaneous close.** Both are valid but rare edge cases; know the difference.
> 6. **Not mentioning RFC 9293.** In 2024, RFC 793 has been updated — citing the modern standard earns bonus credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TCP is connection-oriented** — it sets up state before sending data and tears it down gracefully after.
- **Three-Way Handshake** uses 3 segments: SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK, synchronizing both ISNs.
- **Four-Way Termination** uses 4 segments: FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK, because each direction is closed independently.
- **SYN and FIN flags each consume one sequence number**, even when no payload is carried.
- **Sequence Number Formula:** $SEQ_{next} = SEQ_{sent} + L_{payload} + (\text{1 if SYN or FIN else 0})$.
- **Acknowledgment Number Formula:** $ACK = SEQ_{peer} + 1$ for control segments; $ACK = SEQ_{peer} + L$ for data segments.
- **TIME_WAIT = 2 × MSL** (≈ 240 s with MSL = 120 s), held by the **active closer** only.
- **Two reasons for TIME_WAIT:** ensure final ACK delivery; purge stale segments before 4-tuple reuse.
- **Half-close** = one direction finished (after first FIN); **Full close** = both directions finished (after final ACK).
- **Simultaneous Open / Close** are valid but extremely rare in practice.
- **ISN is a 32-bit counter** incremented every 4 µs; modern systems use cryptographically random ISNs (RFC 6528).
- **RFC 9293 (2022)** is the current authoritative TCP specification, superseding RFC 793.
- **TCP Fast Open (RFC 7413)** allows data in the SYN segment — reduces handshake latency by **1 RTT**.
- **SYN cookies** are a defense against SYN flood DoS attacks by encoding state in the ISN itself.
- **Default MSL** per RFC is **2 minutes** historically; modern Linux uses **60 s** (TIME_WAIT = 120 s).
- **State path of an active closer:** `CLOSED → SYN_SENT → ESTABLISHED → FIN_WAIT_1 → FIN_WAIT_2 → TIME_WAIT → CLOSED`.
- **State path of a passive closer:** `CLOSED → LISTEN → SYN_RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED → CLOSE_WAIT → LAST_ACK → CLOSED`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a question gives numerical ISNs and asks for SEQ/ACK values, always draw a small segment diagram *and* a state table side-by-side. This is the formula for full marks in any TCP question under KTU 2024 evaluation patterns.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Security, Introduction to Network Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Security, Introduction to Network Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Module 4 — Introduction to Network Security

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (RFC 4949 / Stallings Terminology)**
> **Network Security** is the collection of policies, procedures, hardware, software, and cryptographic mechanisms designed to **protect the integrity, confidentiality, and availability** of computer networks, the data traversing them, and the resources accessible through them, against unauthorized access, modification, disruption, or destruction.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this concept is tightly bound to the **OSI Security Architecture (X.800)** defined by the ITU-T, which classifies the universe of network security into three orthogonal dimensions:

| Dimension | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| **Security Attack** | Any action that compromises the security of information owned by an organization. |
| **Security Mechanism** | A process (or device) designed to detect, prevent, or recover from a security attack. |
| **Security Service** | A processing or communication service that enhances the security of the data processing systems and the information transfers. |

### 🌟 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Model

Imagine a bank's **physical security** system:
- **Confidentiality** = The vault's thick steel door — only authorized people see the money.
- **Integrity** = The CCTV cameras — no one can tamper with cash without being noticed.
- **Availability** = Power backup generators — tellers and ATMs work even during a power cut or a riot.
- **Authentication** = The signature verification at the door.
- **Non-repudiation** = The receipt printed after a withdrawal — the customer cannot later deny they made the transaction.

Network Security applies the **exact same set of principles**, but to bits and bytes flowing over Ethernet cables, Wi-Fi, and the public Internet.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Highlight:** Almost every KTU question begins with the phrase *"Explain the various security services/mechanisms/attacks."* Memorize the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** plus **Authentication and Non-Repudiation** — these five together cover ~70% of the marks in this module.

---

## 1.2 The CIA Triad — Foundation of All Network Security

| Pillar | Meaning | Threat Example | Countermeasure Example |
|--------|---------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Confidentiality** | Data is disclosed only to authorized parties. | Eavesdropping, packet sniffing | **AES-256 encryption**, TLS tunnels |
| **Integrity** | Data is not altered or destroyed in an unauthorized manner. | Man-in-the-middle, bit-flipping | **SHA-256 hashing**, digital signatures |
| **Availability** | Systems and data are accessible when needed. | DoS / DDoS attacks | Redundancy, rate limiting, firewalls |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auxiliary Services (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **Authentication** — The assurance that the communicating entity is the one it claims to be.
> - **Access Control** — Prevention of unauthorized use of a resource.
> - **Data Integrity** — Assurance that data received is exactly as sent.
> - **Non-Repudiation** — Protection against false denial of having performed an action (e.g., sending a message).
> - **Privacy** — Control over what information is collected and how it is used.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The OSI Security Framework (X.800)

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Security Attacks

> [!NOTE]
> **William Stallings Classification:** The textbook reference for KTU Module 4 splits all attacks into two top-level families. This is a guaranteed **Part-A (3 mark)** question.

### A. Passive Attacks (Silent — Hard to Detect)

Passive attacks attempt to **learn or make use of information** from the system but **do not modify the resources**. They are extremely difficult to detect because they do not involve any alteration of the data.

| Subtype | Operation | Goal |
|---------|-----------|------|
| **Release of Message Contents** | Attacker reads the plaintext of a conversation (e.g., a confidential email). | Confidentiality breach |
| **Traffic Analysis** | Even if content is encrypted, attacker observes *who* is talking to *whom*, the volume, timing, and frequency of messages. | Inference of relationships and patterns |

### B. Active Attacks (Disruptive — Detectable but Damaging)

Active attacks involve **modification of the data stream** or the **creation of a false stream**. They are difficult to prevent entirely (so the focus is on detection and recovery).

| Subtype | Operation | Threat to |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **Masquerade** | One entity pretends to be a different entity. | Authentication |
| **Replay** | A captured valid transmission is maliciously retransmitted. | Integrity, Authenticity |
| **Modification of Messages** | Portion of a legitimate message is altered or delayed. | Integrity |
| **Denial of Service (DoS)** | Prevents or inhibits the normal use of communications. | Availability |

---

## 2.2 Security Services (X.800 — 5 Categories)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** "List and explain the security services" is a **14-mark** model question. The mapping from *Service → Attack Mitigated* is the most frequently tested skill.

### 2.2.1 Data Confidentiality Services
- **Connection Confidentiality** — Protects all user data on a single connection.
- **Connectionless Confidentiality** — Protects a single message block.
- **Selective-Field Confidentiality** — Protects specific fields within a message.

### 2.2.2 Data Integrity Services
- **Connection Integrity with Recovery** — Detects and recovers from modification.
- **Connection Integrity without Recovery** — Detects modification only.
- **Selective-Field Connection Integrity** — Integrity of specific fields.
- **Connectionless Integrity** — Single block integrity.
- **Selective-Field Connectionless Integrity** — Field-level integrity for a single block.

### 2.2.3 Authentication Services
- **Peer-Entity Authentication** — Confirms the identity of a peer in a connection.
- **Data-Origin Authentication** — Confirms the source of a connectionless message.

### 2.2.4 Non-Repudiation Services
- **Non-Repudiation of Origin** — Proof that a message was sent.
- **Non-Repudiation of Destination** — Proof that a message was received.

### 2.2.5 Access Control Service
- Limits and controls access to host systems and applications via communication links.

---

## 2.3 Security Mechanisms (X.800 — 10 Categories)

| # | Mechanism | Security Service Supported |
|---|-----------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Encipherment (Encryption)** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication |
| 2 | **Digital Signature** | Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation |
| 3 | **Access Control** | Access Control |
| 4 | **Data Integrity** | Integrity |
| 5 | **Authentication Exchange** | Authentication |
| 6 | **Traffic Padding** | Confidentiality (against Traffic Analysis) |
| 7 | **Routing Control** | Confidentiality, Availability |
| 8 | **Notarization** | Non-Repudiation, Integrity |
| 9 | **Padded Cell / Pseudo-Host** | Availability (against DoS) |
| 10 | **Honeypot / Trapping** | Detection of intruders |

---

## 2.4 The Fundamental Relationship

> **X.800 Golden Triangle:**
> A *security service* is **realized** by using one or more *security mechanisms*. A *security mechanism* is **deployed** to counter one or more *security attacks*.

$$ \text{Threat} \;\xrightarrow{\text{manifests as}}\; \text{Attack} \;\xrightarrow{\text{countered by}}\; \text{Mechanism} \;\xrightarrow{\text{realizes}}\; \text{Service} $$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on formulas:** Network Security is largely conceptual; the "formulas" below are **definitions, security-level expressions, and Shannon-style metrics** that frequently appear in numerical questions.

| Concept | Expression / Definition | KTU Use Case |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------|
| **Information Theoretic Security** | $I(M;C) = 0$ | Perfect secrecy — ciphertext reveals zero information about plaintext. |
| **Unicity Distance** (Shannon) | $U = \frac{H(K)}{D}$ | Minimum ciphertext bytes needed to uniquely break the key. |
| **Hamming Distance** | $d(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \oplus y_i$ | Measure of code strength in error-correcting ciphers. |
| **Confusion** | $C = f(P, K)$ | Each bit of ciphertext should depend on multiple key bits. |
| **Diffusion** | $\Delta P \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta C_{\text{full}}$ | A single plaintext bit change should affect many ciphertext bits. |
| **Birthday Attack Probability** | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot 2^m)}$ | Probability of finding a hash collision after $n$ trials over an $m$-bit hash. |
| **Work Factor** | $W = 2^k$ for $k$-bit key | Average brute-force operations to recover a key. |
| **Entropy of a Key** | $H(K) = \log_2 \vert K \vert$ bits | Strength of a perfectly random $k$-bit key is $k$ bits. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $\vert K \vert$ inside markdown tables which breaks the formatting. **Always use `\vert K \vert` or `\mid K \mid` inside table cells.** I have already applied this rule above.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking:** TLS 1.3 + AES-256-GCM provides **confidentiality + integrity** for every HTTP/S request.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** Digital signatures (RSA-2048 / ECDSA P-256) provide **non-repudiation** of prescriptions.
- **Cloud (AWS, Azure):** IAM policies realize **access control** across thousands of virtual machines.
- **IoT / 5G:** Lightweight ciphers (PRESENT, ASCON) provide **confidentiality** for sensor packets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Mapping Attacks to Services to Mechanisms

## 3.1 The X.800 Mapping Matrix (Exhaustive Derivation)

The X.800 framework is built by answering a sequence of logical questions. We derive the complete mapping **step-by-step** as the KTU examiner expects.

### Step 1 — Identify the Threat
Given a real-world problem (e.g., *"An attacker is reading credit-card numbers as they travel over a public Wi-Fi network"*), first classify it.

$$\text{Threat} = \underbrace{\text{Eavesdropping}}_{\text{Reads data}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Attack Type: Passive, Release of Message Contents}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Compromised Security Goal
The attacker is *learning* the contents without modifying them. This violates **Confidentiality**.

$$\text{Compromised Property} = C \;\in\; \{C, I, A\}$$

### Step 3 — Select the Required Security Service
To restore $C$, we need a **Data Confidentiality Service**.

$$\text{Service} = \text{Connection Confidentiality} \;\; (\text{because it's a continuous stream})$$

### Step 4 — Select the Security Mechanism(s)
According to X.800, the only mechanism that directly provides confidentiality is **Encipherment (Encryption)**.

$$\text{Mechanism} = \text{Encipherment}, \quad \text{e.g., AES-256 in GCM mode}$$

### Step 5 — Verify the Coverage
AES-256-GCM simultaneously gives us:
- **Confidentiality** ✓ (via the AES block cipher)
- **Integrity** ✓ (via the Galois Message Authentication Code)
- **Authentication** ✓ (via the GMAC tag)

> [!NOTE]
> This is why AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) ciphers are the **gold standard** in modern protocols (TLS 1.3, IPsec, WPA3).

---

## 3.2 The Universal Mapping Table (All Possible X.800 Mappings)

The following analytical matrix is the **single most important deliverable** for KTU Module 4. It is fully derived from the X.800 specification.

| Security Attack | Category | Service Required | Primary Mechanism(s) |
|-----------------|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Release of Message Contents | Passive | Data Confidentiality | Encipherment, Routing Control |
| Traffic Analysis | Passive | Confidentiality (limited) | Traffic Padding, Encipherment |
| Masquerade | Active | Authentication, Access Control | Authentication Exchange, Encipherment |
| Replay | Active | Data Integrity, Authentication | Digital Signature, Data Integrity |
| Modification of Messages | Active | Data Integrity | Encipherment, Digital Signature |
| Denial of Service | Active | Availability | Traffic Padding, Padded Cell, Routing Control |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — The Unicity Distance

> **Problem:** A classical substitution cipher has a key space of size $\vert K \vert = 26!$ (≈ $4.03 \times 10^{26}$) and the average redundancy of English is $D = 1.5$ bits/character. Estimate the number of ciphertext characters needed to uniquely determine the key.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the entropy of the key.

$$
H(K) = \log_2 \vert K \vert = \log_2 (26!)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Stirling's approximation $\log_2(n!) \approx n \log_2 n - 1.44 \, n$.

$$
H(K) \approx 26 \log_2 26 - 1.44 \times 26
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the numerical value.

$$
26 \log_2 26 = 26 \times 4.7004 \approx 122.21
$$

$$
1.44 \times 26 = 37.44
$$

$$
H(K) \approx 122.21 - 37.44 = 84.77 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the unicity-distance formula.

$$
U = \frac{H(K)}{D} = \frac{84.77 \text{ bits}}{1.5 \text{ bits/char}} \approx 56.5 \text{ characters}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.

> **Therefore**, on average, about **57 characters** of ciphertext are sufficient to break a simple monoalphabetic substitution cipher. This is why the Caesar cipher is the first one students break in any introductory cryptography lab — it has *very low* unicity distance.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Birthday Attack on a Hash Function

> **Problem:** A 64-bit hash function (e.g., MD5 truncated) is used for integrity. How many random messages must an attacker generate to have a **50% probability** of finding a collision?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** State the birthday-bound formula.

$$
P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot 2^m)}
$$

**Step 2:** Set $P = 0.5$ and solve for $n$.

$$
0.5 = 1 - e^{-n^2 / 2^{m+1}}
$$

$$
e^{-n^2 / 2^{m+1}} = 0.5
$$

$$
-\frac{n^2}{2^{m+1}} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $n^2$.

$$
n^2 = 2^{m+1} \cdot \ln 2 \approx 1.386 \cdot 2^m
$$

**Step 4:** Plug in $m = 64$.

$$
n \approx \sqrt{1.386 \times 2^{64}} \approx \sqrt{1.386} \times 2^{32}
$$

$$
n \approx 1.177 \times 4.295 \times 10^9 \approx 5.06 \times 10^9
$$

> **Conclusion:** An attacker only needs **≈ 5 billion** random hashes to find a collision in a 64-bit digest with 50% probability. This is why modern hash functions require **at least 256 bits** (SHA-256) for security.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The X.800 Architectural Triangle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> The X.800 framework is best visualized as a triangle where **Attacks** drive **Mechanisms** which realize **Services**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1(["Passive: Release of Contents"]) --> AttMasterAttacks
    A2(["Passive: Traffic Analysis"]) --> AttMasterAttacks
    A3(["Active: Masquerade"]) --> AttMasterAttacks
    A4(["Active: Replay"]) --> AttMasterAttacks
    A5(["Active: Modification"]) --> AttMasterAttacks
    A6(["Active: Denial of Service"]) --> AttMasterAttacks
    AttMasterAttacks["Security Attacks"] --> MechMaster
    M1["Encipherment"] --> MechMaster
    M2["Digital Signature"] --> MechMaster
    M3["Access Control"] --> MechMaster
    M4["Data Integrity"] --> MechMaster
    M5["Authentication Exchange"] --> MechMaster
    M6["Traffic Padding"] --> MechMaster
    MechMaster["Security Mechanisms"] --> ServMaster
    S1["Confidentiality"] --> ServMaster
    S2["Integrity"] --> ServMaster
    S3["Authentication"] --> ServMaster
    S4["Non Repudiation"] --> ServMaster
    S5["Access Control Service"] --> ServMaster
    ServMaster["Security Services"] --> Goal1["Confidentiality"]
    ServMaster --> Goal2["Integrity"]
    ServMaster --> Goal3["Availability"]
    Goal1 --> CIA["CIA Triad"]
    Goal2 --> CIA
    Goal3 --> CIA
```

---

## 4.2 The OSI Security Layer Mapping (Mermaid Schematic)

Different security services operate at different layers of the OSI model. This is a **highly-mark-rewarding diagram** to draw in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L7["Application Layer\nSSL, TLS, PGP, S/MIME"] --> L6["Presentation Layer\nEncryption, Compression"]
    L6 --> L5["Session Layer\nAuthentication Exchange"]
    L5 --> L4["Transport Layer\nTLS 1.3, DTLS, SSH"]
    L4 --> L3["Network Layer\nIPsec, AH, ESP, OSPF Auth"]
    L3 --> L2["Data Link Layer\nWPA2, WPA3, MACsec, 802.1X"]
    L2 --> L1["Physical Layer\nTEMPEST shielding, Fiber tapping"]
    L1 --> NoteSec(["Layer-by-Layer\nDefense in Depth"])
    L7 --> NoteSec
    L6 --> NoteSec
    L5 --> NoteSec
    L4 --> NoteSec
    L3 --> NoteSec
    L2 --> NoteSec
```

---

## 4.3 Passive vs Active Attack Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Network Traffic Intercepted"]) --> Q1{Does the attacker\nalter the data?}
    Q1 -- "NO" --> Passive["PASSIVE ATTACK"]
    Q1 -- "YES" --> Active["ACTIVE ATTACK"]
    Passive --> P1["Release of Contents"]
    Passive --> P2["Traffic Analysis"]
    Active --> Ac1["Masquerade"]
    Active --> Ac2["Replay"]
    Active --> Ac3["Modification of Messages"]
    Active --> Ac4["Denial of Service"]
    P1 --> Def1["Counter: Encipherment"]
    P2 --> Def2["Counter: Traffic Padding"]
    Ac1 --> Def3["Counter: Authentication Exchange"]
    Ac2 --> Def4["Counter: Timestamps + Nonces"]
    Ac3 --> Def5["Counter: MAC / Digital Signature"]
    Ac4 --> Def6["Counter: Redundancy + Rate Limiting"]
```

---

## 4.4 Defense-in-Depth Layered Model (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> **Defense in Depth** is the principle that *no single security mechanism is sufficient*; instead, multiple layers of protection are stacked. The KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks for a diagram of this kind.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Outer["1. Policies, Procedures, Awareness\n(Administrative Controls)"] --> Phys
    Phys["2. Physical Security\n(Locks, CCTV, Faraday Cages)"] --> Perim
    Perim["3. Perimeter Security\n(Firewalls, IDS, IPS, WAF)"] --> Net
    Net["4. Network Security\n(IPsec, VPN, 802.1X, NAC)"] --> Host
    Host["5. Host Security\n(Antivirus, HIDS, OS Hardening)"] --> App
    App["6. Application Security\n(SDLC, Code Review, Input Validation)"] --> Data
    Data["7. Data Security\n(AES, RSA, SHA, DLP, Tokenization)"] --> Audit
    Audit["8. Audit, Monitoring, SIEM\n(Continuous Validation)"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]**
**Define the term "Network Security". List the three primary goals of network security.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** Network Security is the collection of measures, policies, protocols, and cryptographic tools used to protect the **confidentiality, integrity, and availability** of data and resources in a computer network against unauthorized access, misuse, malfunction, modification, destruction, or improper disclosure.
>
> **Three primary goals (CIA Triad):**
> 1. **Confidentiality** — Ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it.
> 2. **Integrity** — Ensuring that information is not modified or destroyed in an unauthorized manner.
> 3. **Availability** — Ensuring that authorized users have reliable, timely access to information and resources.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar]**
**Differentiate between active and passive security attacks with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
> |--------|----------------|---------------|
> | Nature | Monitors or listens to the traffic silently. | Modifies data or disrupts the service. |
> | Detectability | Very hard to detect. | Hard to prevent, but easier to detect. |
> | Effect | Compromise **confidentiality**. | Compromise **integrity, availability, or authenticity**. |
>
> **Examples of Passive:** (i) Eavesdropping / packet sniffing, (ii) Traffic analysis.
> **Examples of Active:** (i) Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack, (ii) Replay attack or message modification.

---

## 📝 PART B — Essay Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two Part-B questions are asked from each module. Each question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### 🔷 Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] **Explain the X.800 security architecture. List and briefly describe the five categories of security services defined by ITU-T X.800.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **ITU-T Recommendation X.800 (Security Architecture for OSI)**, also adopted as **ISO 7498-2**, provides a systematic framework for defining security requirements and characterizing the approaches to achieve them. The architecture rests on three pillars:
> 1. **Security Attack** — Any action that compromises security.
> 2. **Security Mechanism** — A process designed to detect, prevent, or recover from an attack.
> 3. **Security Service** — A service that enhances security of data processing and information transfer; it makes use of one or more mechanisms.
>
> **The Five Categories of Security Services:**
>
> 1. **Authentication Services** — Provide assurance about the identity of an entity.
>    - *Peer-Entity Authentication:* Used in connection-oriented sessions; confirms the identity of the peer.
>    - *Data-Origin Authentication:* Used in connectionless transfers; confirms the source of a single message.
>
> 2. **Access Control Service** — Prevents the unauthorized use of a resource. It governs which entities may access what resources under what conditions (read-only, read-write, etc.).
>
> 3. **Data Confidentiality Services** — Protect data from unauthorized disclosure.
>    - *Connection Confidentiality* protects all user data on a connection.
>    - *Connectionless Confidentiality* protects a single message.
>    - *Selective-Field Confidentiality* protects specific chosen fields.
>    - *Traffic-Flow Confidentiality* protects the metadata of traffic flow.
>
> 4. **Data Integrity Services** — Assure that data has not been altered or destroyed.
>    - *With Recovery* vs *Without Recovery.*
>    - *Connection vs Connectionless* and *Selective-Field* variants.
>
> 5. **Non-Repudiation Services** — Prevent an entity from denying previous commitments or actions.
>    - *Non-Repudiation of Origin:* Proof of sending.
>    - *Non-Repudiation of Destination:* Proof of receipt.

#### (b) [7 Marks] **List and explain any four security mechanisms defined by X.800. How do mechanisms relate to services?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Mechanism 1 — Encipherment (Encryption):**
> The mathematical transformation of plaintext into ciphertext using an algorithm and a key. Provides **confidentiality**, and when combined with hashing, also provides **integrity** and **authentication**. Examples: AES, ChaCha20.
>
> **Mechanism 2 — Digital Signature:**
> A cryptographic transformation that allows the receiver to verify the origin and integrity of a message and protects against forgery. Provides **data integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation.** Examples: RSA-PSS, ECDSA, Ed25519.
>
> **Mechanism 3 — Access Control Mechanisms:**
> A set of procedures that enforce the access rights of entities to resources. Includes **Access Control Lists (ACLs), Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), and capability-based systems.** Provides the **access control service.**
>
> **Mechanism 4 — Data Integrity Mechanism:**
> Uses checksums, hash functions, or Message Authentication Codes (MACs) to detect modification of a data unit. Provides the **data integrity service.** Examples: SHA-256, HMAC-SHA256.
>
> **Mechanism 5 (Bonus) — Traffic Padding:**
> Insertion of random bits into gaps between data units to thwart traffic analysis. Provides **confidentiality against traffic analysis.**
>
> **Relationship between Mechanisms and Services:**
> A *security service* is implemented (realized) by one or more *security mechanisms*. Conversely, a *security mechanism* may be used to support multiple *security services*. The mapping is **many-to-many**:
>
> $$ \text{Service}_i = f\big(\text{Mechanism}_1, \text{Mechanism}_2, \ldots, \text{Mechanism}_k\big) $$
>
> Example: The **Authentication Service** may use *Authentication Exchange* (passwords/challenge-response) plus *Encipherment* (to encrypt the password).

---

### 🔷 Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] **Explain the OSI security architecture's classification of attacks. With neat diagrams, describe the four major types of active attacks.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Classification of Attacks:**
> The OSI security architecture classifies attacks into two broad categories:
> - **Passive Attacks:** Attempts to learn or make use of information from the system but **does not modify resources**. They are hard to detect but easy to prevent (via encryption).
> - **Active Attacks:** Involve **modification of the data stream** or the **creation of a false stream**. Hard to prevent absolutely, so the focus is on **detection and recovery**.
>
> **Four Major Types of Active Attacks:**
>
> 1. **Masquerade:**
>    - An entity pretends to be a different entity.
>    - Example: An attacker captures an IP address and sends packets pretending to be the legitimate host. Often a *precursor* to other attacks.
>    - **Counter:** Authentication exchange (e.g., Kerberos, certificates).
>
> 2. **Replay:**
>    - An attacker captures a valid message and later retransmits it to produce an unauthorized effect.
>    - Example: Replaying an authenticated bank transfer to drain the account again.
>    - **Counter:** Sequence numbers, timestamps, and nonces.
>
> 3. **Modification of Messages:**
>    - A portion of a legitimate message is altered, delayed, or reordered.
>    - Example: An attacker changes the destination account number in a wire transfer packet.
>    - **Counter:** MACs, digital signatures, integrity checksums.
>
> 4. **Denial of Service (DoS):**
>    - Prevents or inhibits the normal use or management of communications facilities.
>    - Example: SYN flood, UDP flood, ICMP smurf, or distributed DDoS via a botnet.
>    - **Counter:** Rate limiting, traffic filtering, redundancy, scrubbing centers.
>
> *(Note: A clearly labelled diagram showing the four attacks — Masquerade, Replay, Modification, DoS — should be drawn, with sender, attacker, and receiver positions, as per the Mermaid schematics in Section 4.)*
