#### (b) [7 Marks] **Discuss the various types of security services as per X.800. Map each service to the specific threat it mitigates.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Detailed Security Services in X.800:**
>
> | Service | Description | Threat Mitigated |
> |---------|-------------|------------------|
> | **Peer-Entity Authentication** | Confirms the identity of the peer in a connection. | Masquerade, IP spoofing |
> | **Data-Origin Authentication** | Confirms the source of a connectionless message. | Forged source address |
> | **Access Control** | Restricts who can do what on the system. | Unauthorized access, privilege escalation |
> | **Connection Confidentiality** | Encrypts all data on a connection. | Release of message contents (passive) |
> | **Connectionless Confidentiality** | Encrypts a single message. | Sniffing UDP/datagram traffic |
> | **Selective-Field Confidentiality** | Encrypts only specific fields. | Leaking PII from a partially-encrypted form |
> | **Traffic-Flow Confidentiality** | Hides source-destination patterns. | Traffic analysis |
> | **Connection Integrity (with/without recovery)** | Detects/repairs modification in a stream. | Message modification, bit-flipping |
> | **Connectionless Integrity** | Verifies integrity of a single datagram. | Modification of DNS replies |
> | **Selective-Field Integrity** | Verifies integrity of chosen fields. | Tampered payment amount in a structured form |
> | **Non-Repudiation, Origin** | Sender cannot deny having sent. | Repudiation of an electronic contract |
> | **Non-Repudiation, Destination** | Receiver cannot deny having received. | Repudiation of receipt of a legal notice |
>
> **Synthesis — Why Five Categories?**
> Each category is **mutually exclusive** in purpose but they **overlap in mechanism**. Authentication is the foundation on which access control is built; confidentiality and integrity are implemented by the same primitives (AEAD ciphers). Non-repudiation is unique because it requires **asymmetric cryptography** (digital signatures) to bind an identity to a message in a way that a third party can verify.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing "Attack" with "Threat" or "Vulnerability".** A *threat* is the potential; an *attack* is the realized attempt; a *vulnerability* is the weakness. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for mixing these.
> 2. **Writing "cryptography" for "encryption".** Cryptography is the broader field; encryption, hashing, signatures, and key exchange are its sub-disciplines.
> 3. **Forgetting the X.800 standard number.** Always cite *"X.800 / ISO 7498-2"* at least once in your answer — it earns you an **easy 1 mark**.
> 4. **Mapping Replay → Encryption only.** Encryption does **not** stop replay. You **must** mention **nonces, sequence numbers, or timestamps** to score full marks.
> 5. **Saying "firewall solves DoS".** A stateless firewall cannot stop a volumetric DDoS. Mention **rate limiting, scrubbing, anycast, or CDNs** for full credit.
> 6. **Forgetting Non-Repudiation.** It is a *separate* service from integrity. You need **digital signatures**, not just MACs.
> 7. **Not labelling the Mermaid/diagram.** A diagram without a caption and labeled axes/arrows loses 1–2 marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Network Security** = the protection of confidentiality, integrity, and availability of networked data and resources.
- [x] **CIA Triad** is the foundation; **Authentication** and **Non-Repudiation** are the two additional services.
- [x] **X.800 / ISO 7498-2** is the standard OSI security architecture that defines **Attacks, Mechanisms, and Services**.
- [x] **Attacks** = Passive (Release of Contents, Traffic Analysis) + Active (Masquerade, Replay, Modification, DoS).
- [x] **Passive attacks are hard to detect but easy to prevent**; **Active attacks are easy to detect but hard to prevent**.
- [x] **5 Security Services**: Authentication, Access Control, Data Confidentiality, Data Integrity, Non-Repudiation.
- [x] **10 Security Mechanisms**: Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization, plus detection mechanisms (Padded Cell, Honeypot).
- [x] **Service ↔ Mechanism** is a **many-to-many** relationship.
- [x] **Encipherment** is the *primary* mechanism for **confidentiality**.
- [x] **Digital Signature** is the *primary* mechanism for **non-repudiation**.
- [x] **Defense in Depth** uses layered security across administrative, physical, perimeter, network, host, application, and data layers.
- [x] **Unicity Distance** $U = H(K) / D$ — fewer characters needed for low-redundancy / high-key-space ciphers indicates *stronger*? No — it indicates *weaker*, because less ciphertext breaks the key. **The LOWER the unicity distance, the weaker the cipher.**
- [x] **Birthday attack on $m$-bit hash:** $n \approx 1.177 \times 2^{m/2}$ hashes for 50% collision probability.
- [x] **Replay** is mitigated by **nonces / timestamps / sequence numbers**, not by encryption alone.
- [x] **DoS** is mitigated by **redundancy, rate limiting, and scrubbing centers**, not by firewalls alone.

> **Final Tip:** Before submitting the exam paper, glance at your diagram and your X.800 mapping table — these two together typically carry **6–8 marks out of 14** in a Module-4 Part-B question. ✍️
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Cryptography, Symmetric Key Cryptography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Cryptography, Symmetric Key Cryptography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cryptography?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Term):**
> *Cryptography* is the science and art of transforming meaningful information (plaintext) into an unintelligible form (ciphertext) and back again, using mathematical algorithms and keys, to provide security across insecure communication channels.

The word **cryptography** originates from the Greek words *kryptos* (hidden) and *graphein* (to write). Modern cryptography is built upon four foundational pillars:

| Pillar | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensure only authorized parties can read the data |
| **Integrity** | Detect any unauthorized modification of data |
| **Authentication** | Verify the identity of sender and receiver |
| **Non-repudiation** | Prevent sender from denying they sent the message |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Locked Diary

Imagine two friends, **Alice** and **Bob**, who want to exchange secret notes during class. They both own a small *identical* padlock, but only they have the matching keys.

1. Alice writes her secret in a diary and locks it with her padlock.
2. She sends the locked diary to Bob during lunch break.
3. Bob uses his identical key to unlock the diary and read it.
4. If an eavesdropper (**Eve**) intercepts the diary, she cannot read it because she has no key.

> [!NOTE]
> In this analogy, the *padlock and key together* represent the **shared secret key** used in **Symmetric Key Cryptography**. The diary represents the plaintext, and the locked diary is the ciphertext. Notice that *both parties share the same key* — this is the defining feature of symmetric cryptography.

### 1.3 Symmetric Key Cryptography — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetric Key Cryptography (Private-Key Cryptography):**
> A cryptographic system in which the *same secret key* is used for both **encryption** (plaintext → ciphertext) and **decryption** (ciphertext → plaintext), and that key must be shared between sender and receiver over a secure channel *before* communication begins.

The general mathematical model is:

$$C = E(K, P)$$

$$P = D(K, C)$$

where $E$ is the encryption function, $D$ is the decryption function, $K$ is the shared secret key, $P$ is plaintext, and $C$ is ciphertext. By definition, $D = E^{-1}$ and the same $K$ is used for both.

### 1.4 Cryptanalysis vs Cryptography

> [!NOTE]
> *Cryptanalysis* is the art of breaking ciphers without knowledge of the key. *Cryptology* is the umbrella term covering both cryptography and cryptanalysis. The strength of a modern cipher is measured by the *computational infeasibility* of mounting a successful cryptanalytic attack, not by keeping the algorithm secret (**Kerckhoffs's Principle**, 1883).

**Kerckhoffs's Principle** states that *the security of a cryptosystem must rest entirely on the secrecy of the key, not on the secrecy of the algorithm*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 OSI Security Architecture (X.800 Framework)

The **ITU-T X.800** standard defines a layered security model that the KTU syllabus expects you to memorize:

### Security Attacks
Any action that compromises the security of information flowing across a system.

| Category | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| **Passive** | *Release of message contents* | Eavesdropper reads the content |
| **Passive** | *Traffic analysis* | Eavesdropper studies message patterns/lengths |
| **Active** | *Masquerade* | One entity pretends to be another |
| **Active** | *Replay* | Captured valid data is maliciously retransmitted |
| **Active** | *Modification* | Altering message contents in transit |
| **Active** | *Denial of Service (DoS)* | Disrupting normal service availability |

> [!NOTE]
> Passive attacks are hard to detect (prevention is the only defense). Active attacks are hard to prevent (detection and recovery are the defenses).

### Security Services (X.800)

| Service | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Data Confidentiality** | Protects against unauthorized disclosure |
| **Data Integrity** | Detects insertion, deletion, modification, replay |
| **Authentication** | Confirms identity of communicating peer |
| **Non-repudiation** | Prevents sender/receiver from denying the action |
| **Access Control** | Restricts resource access to authorized users |
| **Availability** | Ensures systems remain usable (DoS resistance) |

### Security Mechanisms (X.800)

| Mechanism | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Encipherment** | Hiding data via mathematical transformation |
| **Digital Signature** | Binds sender identity to message |
| **Access Control** | Enforces authorization rules |
| **Data Integrity** | Uses hash values and sequence numbers |
| **Authentication Exchange** | Two-party identity verification via protocols |
| **Traffic Padding** | Inserts dummy traffic to frustrate analysis |
| **Routing Control** | Uses secure alternate paths |
| **Notarization** | Trusted third party validates communication |

## 2.2 The Five Pillars — Cryptographic Goals Explained

For a KTU answer worth full marks, every pillar should be defined with a *mechanism that achieves it*:

| Goal | Cryptographic Mechanism |
|------|------------------------|
| Confidentiality | Symmetric/Asymmetric encryption |
| Integrity | Hash functions (MD5, SHA-256) |
| Authentication | MAC, Digital Signatures |
| Non-repudiation | Asymmetric digital signatures |
| Access Control | ACLs, Capabilities, Firewalls |

## 2.3 Classical Symmetric Encryption Techniques

### 2.3.1 Caesar Cipher (Shift Cipher)

The simplest monoalphabetic substitution. Each letter is shifted by a fixed amount $k$ (the key) modulo 26:

$$C = E_k(P) = (P + k) \bmod 26$$

$$P = D_k(C) = (C - k) \bmod 26$$

- Key space: only **26** possible keys (trivially broken by brute force).
- Variants: ROT-13 is the Caesar cipher with $k = 13$.

### 2.3.2 Monoalphabetic Substitution Cipher

A random one-to-one mapping of the 26-letter alphabet to itself.

- Key space: $26! \approx 4.03 \times 10^{26}$ keys.
- **Weakness**: Frequency analysis — in English, the letters *e, t, a, o, i, n* have predictable frequencies. By matching ciphertext letter frequencies to expected English letter frequencies, an attacker can recover the mapping.

### 2.3.3 Playfair Cipher (Digraph Cipher)

Invented by Charles Wheatstone (1854), promoted by Lord Playfair. Encrypts *pairs* of letters (digraphs) using a 5×5 matrix constructed from a keyword.

- Key space: effectively $\approx 26!$ but constrained by keyword.
- **Weakness**: vulnerable to digraph frequency analysis but much harder than Caesar.

### 2.3.4 Hill Cipher (1929)

A polygraphic substitution cipher based on linear algebra. Each block of $m$ letters is treated as an $m$-component vector and multiplied by an $m \times m$ invertible key matrix modulo 26:

$$\vec{C} = (K \cdot \vec{P}) \bmod 26$$

$$\vec{P} = (K^{-1} \cdot \vec{C}) \bmod 26$$

where $K^{-1}$ is the matrix inverse modulo 26.

### 2.3.5 Vigenère Cipher (Polyalphabetic)

A polyalphabetic substitution cipher using a *repeating keyword*. Encryption:

$$C_i = (P_i + K_{i \bmod m}) \bmod 26$$

Decryption:

$$P_i = (C_i - K_{i \bmod m}) \bmod 26$$

- Was called *le chiffre indéchiffrable* ("the indecipherable cipher") for 300 years.
- **Weakness**: broken by the **Kasiski examination** (repeated digram distances reveal key length) and **Index of Coincidence** by Friedman (1920s).

### 2.3.6 Rail Fence Cipher (Transposition)

A columnar transposition cipher that writes plaintext in a zigzag on $r$ rails (the key) and reads row by row.

### 2.3.7 Route / Columnar Transposition

Plaintext is written into rows under a keyword, then columns are read out in *alphabetical order of the keyword letters*.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Caesar | $C = (P + k) \bmod 26$ | $k \in \{0, \dots, 25\}$ |
| 2 | Caesar decrypt | $P = (C - k) \bmod 26$ | Same key $k$ used |
| 3 | Multiplicative cipher | $C = (P \cdot k) \bmod 26$ | $k$ must be coprime to 26 |
| 4 | Affine cipher | $C = (aP + b) \bmod 26$ | $a$ coprime to 26 |
| 5 | Vigenère | $C_i = (P_i + K_{i \bmod m}) \bmod 26$ | $m$ = keyword length |
| 6 | Hill cipher | $\vec{C} = K \cdot \vec{P} \bmod 26$ | $\det(K)$ invertible mod 26 |
| 7 | Playfair | Digraph substitution, I/J merged | 5×5 matrix |
| 8 | Mod-26 inverse | Find $a^{-1}$ such that $a \cdot a^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$ | Used in affine/Hill |
| 9 | Key space Caesar | $26$ keys | Brute-force trivial |
| 10 | Key space monoalpha | $26!$ | Defeated by frequency analysis |
| 11 | DES key length | $56$ effective bits ($64$ with parity) | $2^{56}$ key space |
| 12 | AES key length | $128, 192, \text{ or } 256$ bits | NIST FIPS 197 |
| 13 | DES rounds | $16$ Feistel rounds | Block size 64 bits |
| 14 | AES rounds | $10, 12, \text{ or } 14$ | Block size always 128 bits |
| 15 | XOR property | $A \oplus B \oplus B = A$ | Used in stream ciphers and Feistel |
| 16 | Number of rounds (Hill) | $\det(K) \bmod 26$ must be invertible | Tested against $\{2, 13\}$ |

## 2.5 Modern Symmetric Cipher Architecture

Modern symmetric ciphers are broadly classified as **block ciphers** or **stream ciphers**:

| Property | Block Cipher | Stream Cipher |
|----------|--------------|---------------|
| Unit of processing | Fixed-size block (64/128 bits) | 1 bit or 1 byte at a time |
| Operation | Substitution + Permutation | XOR with keystream |
| Examples | DES, AES, 3DES, Blowfish | RC4, Salsa20, ChaCha20 |
| Memory | Required to store whole block | Minimal — operates in real time |
| Speed | Slower for short data | Fast for continuous streams |
| Diffusion | High (one bit changes propagates) | Depends on keystream quality |

### 2.5.1 Feistel Cipher Structure (Horst Feistel, 1973)

The foundational design used in **DES**. Splits a $2w$-bit block into two halves $L_0$ and $R_0$, then applies $n$ rounds:

$$L_i = R_{i-1}$$

$$R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$$

Decryption uses the same structure with **subkeys applied in reverse order** — this elegant property makes hardware implementation trivial.

### 2.5.2 Data Encryption Standard (DES)

- Adopted by **NIST (then NBS)** in **1977** as FIPS 46.
- Block size: **64 bits**, Key size: **56 bits** (64 bits with 8 parity bits), Rounds: **16 Feistel rounds**.
- S-boxes (substitution boxes) provide the non-linearity; P-box (permutation) provides diffusion.
- **Differential cryptanalysis** (Biham-Shamir, 1990) and **linear cryptanalysis** (Matsui, 1993) reduced effective security. Brute force became feasible in the 1990s ($2^{56}$ ≈ 7.2×10¹⁶).

### 2.5.3 Triple DES (3DES / TDEA)

Applies DES three times with two or three distinct keys:

$$C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$$

- Effective key strength: **112 bits** (with two-key 3DES) or **168 bits** (with three-key 3DES).
- Slow: roughly 3× slower than DES. Deprecated by NIST in 2023 for sensitive use.

### 2.5.4 Advanced Encryption Standard (AES / Rijndael)

Selected by NIST in **2001** (FIPS 197) after an open competition (Vincent Rijmen & Joan Daemen, Belgium).

- Block size: **128 bits**
- Key sizes: **128, 192, or 256 bits** → 10, 12, or 14 rounds
- **Not** a Feistel cipher — uses a substitution-permutation network (SPN).
- Round operations: **SubBytes (S-box), ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey**.
- Final round skips MixColumns.
- Practically unbroken: best known attack requires $2^{126.1}$ operations on AES-128 (Biclique, 2011) — still computationally infeasible.

## 2.6 Block Cipher Modes of Operation

The same block cipher used in different ways yields very different security properties:

| Mode | Full Name | Operates On | Parallelizable? | IV Needed? | Error Propagation |
|------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|------------|-------------------|
| **ECB** | Electronic Codebook | Single block | Yes | No | One block only |
| **CBC** | Cipher Block Chaining | Block chain | No (encryption) | Yes | Whole block + next |
| **CFB** | Cipher Feedback | Stream | No | Yes | Affects subsequent blocks |
| **OFB** | Output Feedback | Stream | Yes (keystream) | Yes | No propagation |
| **CTR** | Counter | Stream | Yes | Yes (nonce) | No propagation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECB mode reveals patterns** because identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. CBC, CTR, and other modes are overwhelmingly preferred in practice. The famous *ECB Penguin* image (2007) demonstrated this by encrypting a Linux Tux logo in ECB mode — the Tux silhouette remained visible.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Symmetric cryptography is the workhorse of modern secure communications:

- **TLS/HTTPS** uses AES (often in GCM mode) for bulk data encryption, with asymmetric crypto (RSA/ECC) only to exchange the symmetric key.
- **Wi-Fi security** (WPA2/WPA3) uses AES-CCMP.
- **Disk encryption** (BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS) uses AES-XTS.
- **Mobile messaging** (Signal, WhatsApp) uses the Double Ratchet Algorithm built on AES-256 and ChaCha20.
- **Banking** (EMV chip cards) uses 3DES and AES for transaction authentication.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through the most commonly tested numerical/derivations in KTU exams.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example: Caesar Cipher Encryption/Decryption

**Problem:** Encrypt the plaintext `NETWORK` using the Caesar cipher with key $k = 7$. Then decrypt the ciphertext.

**Step 1 — Assign numerical values.**
Using the convention $A=0, B=1, \dots, Z=25$:

$$P_{NETWORK} = [N, E, T, W, O, R, K] = [13, 4, 19, 22, 14, 17, 10]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the encryption formula $C = (P + 7) \bmod 26$.**

$$C_1 = (13 + 7) \bmod 26 = 20 \rightarrow U$$

$$C_2 = (4 + 7) \bmod 26 = 11 \rightarrow L$$

$$C_3 = (19 + 7) \bmod 26 = 0 \rightarrow A$$

$$C_4 = (22 + 7) \bmod 26 = 3 \rightarrow D$$

$$C_5 = (14 + 7) \bmod 26 = 21 \rightarrow V$$

$$C_6 = (17 + 7) \bmod 26 = 24 \rightarrow Y$$

$$C_7 = (10 + 7) \bmod 26 = 17 \rightarrow R$$

**Step 3 — Result.**
The ciphertext is `ULADVYR`.

**Step 4 — Decrypt using $P = (C - 7) \bmod 26$.**

$$P_i = (C_i - 7 + 26) \bmod 26 \quad \text{(adding 26 keeps the value non-negative)}$$

$$P = [20, 11, 0, 3, 21, 24, 17]$$

$$P_1 = (20 - 7) \bmod 26 = 13 \rightarrow N$$

$$P_2 = (11 - 7) \bmod 26 = 4 \rightarrow E$$

$$P_3 = (0 - 7 + 26) \bmod 26 = 19 \rightarrow T$$

$$P_4 = (3 - 7 + 26) \bmod 26 = 22 \rightarrow W$$

$$P_5 = (21 - 7) \bmod 26 = 14 \rightarrow O$$

$$P_6 = (24 - 7) \bmod 26 = 17 \rightarrow R$$

$$P_7 = (17 - 7) \bmod 26 = 10 \rightarrow K$$

**Recovered plaintext:** `NETWORK` ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Multiplicative Cipher & Modular Inverse

**Problem:** You receive ciphertext `WHQJ` produced by a multiplicative cipher with key $k = 5$. Decrypt it.

**Step 1 — Find the modular inverse $k^{-1} \pmod{26}$.**
We need an integer $a$ such that $5 \cdot a \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$. Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$26 = 5 \cdot 5 + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 = 26 - 5 \cdot 5$$

So $5 \cdot (-5) \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$, and $-5 \bmod 26 = 21$. Thus $5^{-1} \equiv 21 \pmod{26}$.

**Step 2 — Convert ciphertext to numbers.**
$$C_{WHQJ} = [22, 7, 9, 16]$$

**Step 3 — Decrypt $P = (k^{-1} \cdot C) \bmod 26 = (21 \cdot C) \bmod 26$.**

$$P_1 = (21 \cdot 22) \bmod 26 = 462 \bmod 26 = 462 - 17 \cdot 26 = 462 - 442 = 20 \rightarrow U$$

$$P_2 = (21 \cdot 7) \bmod 26 = 147 \bmod 26 = 147 - 5 \cdot 26 = 147 - 130 = 17 \rightarrow R$$

$$P_3 = (21 \cdot 9) \bmod 26 = 189 \bmod 26 = 189 - 7 \cdot 26 = 189 - 182 = 7 \rightarrow H$$

$$P_4 = (21 \cdot 16) \bmod 26 = 336 \bmod 26 = 336 - 12 \cdot 26 = 336 - 312 = 24 \rightarrow Y$$

**Recovered plaintext:** `URHY` ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Affine Cipher

**Problem:** Using the affine cipher with $a = 5, b = 8$, encrypt `DATA`.

**Formula:** $C = (5P + 8) \bmod 26$

**Step 1 — Convert plaintext to numbers.**
$$P_{DATA} = [D, A, T, A] = [3, 0, 19, 0]$$

**Step 2 — Encrypt.**
$$C_1 = (5 \cdot 3 + 8) \bmod 26 = 23 \bmod 26 = 23 \rightarrow X$$

$$C_2 = (5 \cdot 0 + 8) \bmod 26 = 8 \bmod 26 = 8 \rightarrow I$$

$$C_3 = (5 \cdot 19 + 8) \bmod 26 = 103 \bmod 26 = 103 - 3 \cdot 26 = 103 - 78 = 25 \rightarrow Z$$

$$C_4 = (5 \cdot 0 + 8) \bmod 26 = 8 \bmod 26 = 8 \rightarrow I$$

**Result:** Ciphertext is `XIZI`.

**Decryption formula:** $P = a^{-1}(C - b) \bmod 26$ where $a^{-1} = 21 \pmod{26}$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Vigenère Cipher

**Problem:** Encrypt `ATTACKATDAWN` with keyword `LEMON` using the Vigenère cipher.

**Step 1 — Repeat the keyword to match plaintext length.**
Plaintext:  `A  T  T  A  C  K  A  T  D  A  W  N`
Keyword:   `L  E  M  O  N  L  E  M  O  N  L  E`
Numeric:   `11 4 12 14 13 11 4  12 14 13 11 4`

**Step 2 — Apply $C_i = (P_i + K_i) \bmod 26$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
A + L &: (0 + 11) \bmod 26 = 11 \rightarrow L \\
T + E &: (19 + 4) \bmod 26 = 23 \rightarrow X \\
T + M &: (19 + 12) \bmod 26 = 5 \rightarrow F \\
A + O &: (0 + 14) \bmod 26 = 14 \rightarrow O \\
C + N &: (2 + 13) \bmod 26 = 15 \rightarrow P \\
K + L &: (10 + 11) \bmod 26 = 21 \rightarrow V \\
A + E &: (0 + 4) \bmod 26 = 4 \rightarrow E \\
T + M &: (19 + 12) \bmod 26 = 5 \rightarrow F \\
D + O &: (3 + 14) \bmod 26 = 17 \rightarrow R \\
A + N &: (0 + 13) \bmod 26 = 13 \rightarrow N \\
W + L &: (22 + 11) \bmod 26 = 7 \rightarrow H \\
N + E &: (13 + 4) \bmod 26 = 17 \rightarrow R
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Ciphertext is `LXFOPVEFRNHR`.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the two `A`s at positions 1 and 4 produced different ciphertexts (`L` and `O`) — this is the strength of polyalphabetic ciphers. Frequency analysis on individual letters now fails.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Hill Cipher (m = 2)

**Problem:** Encrypt the plaintext `HI` using the Hill cipher with key matrix

$$K = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$$

**Step 1 — Convert to numbers.**
$$P = \begin{pmatrix} H \\ I \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Verify $K$ is invertible mod 26.**
$$\det(K) = (3 \cdot 5 - 3 \cdot 2) = 15 - 6 = 9$$

$\gcd(9, 26) = 1$, so $K$ is invertible mod 26. ✓

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= K \cdot P \pmod{26} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} (3 \cdot 7 + 3 \cdot 8) \\ (2 \cdot 7 + 5 \cdot 8) \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} (21 + 24) \\ (14 + 40) \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} 45 \\ 54 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to letters.**
$$C = (19, 2) \rightarrow (T, C)$$

**Ciphertext:** `TC`

**Decryption:** Compute $K^{-1}$:

$$K^{-1} \equiv (\det K)^{-1} \cdot \text{adj}(K) \pmod{26}$$

$\text{adj}(K) = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 23 \\ 24 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$

$(9)^{-1} \pmod{26}$: We need $9x \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$. By inspection $9 \cdot 3 = 27 \equiv 1$, so $9^{-1} \equiv 3$.

$$K^{-1} = 3 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 23 \\ 24 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 69 \\ 72 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$$

Verify: $K^{-1} \cdot K \equiv I \pmod{26}$.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Playfair Cipher

**Problem:** Encrypt the digraph `HE` using Playfair with keyword `MONARCHY`.

**Step 1 — Build the 5×5 matrix.** Write the keyword (dropping duplicates) then remaining alphabet letters (combine I/J):

```
M O N A R
C H Y B D
E F G I/J K
L P Q S T
U V W X Z
```

**Step 2 — Locate each letter.**
- `H` is at row 2, column 2.
- `E` is at row 3, column 1.

**Step 3 — Apply Playfair rules (rectangle rule: letters are in different rows AND different columns → take the corners of the rectangle).**
- `H` → row 2, column 1 → `C`
- `E` → row 3, column 2 → `F`

**Ciphertext:** `CF`

**Rules summary:**

| Case | Rule |
|------|------|
| Same row | Replace each letter with the one to its right (wrap) |
| Same column | Replace each letter with the one below (wrap) |
| Rectangle | Each letter is replaced by the one in its row but the other letter's column |

**Same-letter digraph handling:** Insert an `X` between repeated letters (e.g., `BALLOON` → `BA LX LO ON`). Append an `X` if the plaintext length is odd.

---

## 3.7 Feistel Cipher Round Derivation

**Given:** $L_{i-1}, R_{i-1}$, round function $F$, round key $K_i$.

**Forward round:**
$$L_i = R_{i-1}$$

$$R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$$

**Proof that decryption uses the same structure with reversed subkeys:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_i &= R_{i-1} \\
R_i &= L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)
\end{aligned}$$

From these:
$$R_{i-1} = L_i$$

$$L_{i-1} = R_i \oplus F(L_i, K_i)$$

So given $(L_i, R_i)$ we can recover $(L_{i-1}, R_{i-1})$ using:
$$R_{i-1} = L_i$$

$$L_{i-1} = R_i \oplus F(L_i, K_i)$$

This is the **exact same form** as the encryption rule, with $K_i$ in the same position. Therefore decryption applies the round function $n$ times with the subkeys in *reverse order*: $K_n, K_{n-1}, \dots, K_1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This self-inverting property is why Feistel ciphers like DES are so popular — the same hardware or code decrypts and encrypts with only a subkey schedule change.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Classical Ciphers

| Cipher | Type | Key Space | Cryptanalysis Method |
|--------|------|-----------|----------------------|
| Caesar | Substitution (mono) | $26$ | Brute force |
| Monoalphabetic | Substitution (mono) | $26!$ | Letter frequency analysis |
| Playfair | Substitution (digraph) | $\approx 26!$ | Digraph frequency analysis |
| Hill | Substitution (poly) | Invertible matrices mod 26 | Known-plaintext attack |
| Vigenère | Substitution (poly) | $26^m$ | Kasiski / Index of Coincidence |
| Rail Fence | Transposition | $r$ | Pattern recognition |
| DES | Block (Feistel) | $2^{56}$ | Differential/Linear (theoretical), brute force (practical) |
| 3DES | Block (Feistel) | $2^{168}$ or $2^{112}$ | Meet-in-the-middle reduces 2-key to 112 |
| AES | Block (SPN) | $2^{128}, 2^{192}, 2^{256}$ | Biclique (theoretical only) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Symmetric Cipher Model (End-to-End Communication)

This diagram captures the full symmetric encryption pipeline used in every block cipher mode:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext P] --> B[Encryption Algorithm E]
    K1[Secret Key K] --> B
    B --> C[Ciphertext C]
    C --> D[Secure Channel]
    D --> E[Decryption Algorithm D]
    K2[Secret Key K same as K1] --> E
    E --> F[Plaintext P recovered]
    Eve((Eve Eavesdropper)) -.->|Intercepts C but no K| C
```

**Reading guide:** The same key $K$ is generated once and shared over a secure channel (e.g., Diffie–Hellman in TLS). Eve sees the ciphertext but lacks $K$, so she cannot invert $E$.

---

## 4.2 OSI Security Architecture — Attack / Service / Mechanism Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Goals[Security Goals]
        G1[Confidentiality]
        G2[Integrity]
        G3[Authentication]
        G4[Non Repudiation]
        G5[Access Control]
    end

    subgraph Threats[Security Attacks]
        T1[Passive Interception]
        T2[Active Modification]
        T3[Masquerade]
        T4[Replay]
        T5[Denial of Service]
    end

    subgraph Services[Security Services X800]
        S1[Data Confidentiality]
        S2[Data Integrity]
        S3[Authentication]
        S4[Non Repudiation]
        S5[Access Control]
    end

    subgraph Mechanisms[Security Mechanisms X800]
        M1[Encipherment]
        M2[Digital Signature]
        M3[Access Control]
        M4[Data Integrity Check]
        M5[Authentication Exchange]
        M6[Traffic Padding]
        M7[Routing Control]
        M8[Notarization]
    end

    Threats -->|Defended by| Services
    Services -->|Implemented by| Mechanisms
    Goals -.->|Achieved via| Services
```

---

## 4.3 Feistel Cipher Structure (One Round)

This is the cornerstone diagram every KTU student must reproduce for the DES question:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plaintext Block 2w bits] --> SPLIT{Split into two halves}
    SPLIT --> L0[L sub i minus 1 w bits]
    SPLIT --> R0[R sub i minus 1 w bits]
    R0 --> F[Round Function F]
    Ksub[Subkey K sub i] --> F
    F --> XOR1{XOR}
    L0 --> XOR1
    R0 --> NEWL[New L sub i]
    XOR1 --> NEWR[New R sub i]
    NEWL --> R1[R sub i]
    NEWR --> L1[L sub i]
    R1 --> NEXT[Go to next round]
    L1 --> NEXT
```

**Key formulas encoded in the diagram:**

$$L_i = R_{i-1}$$

$$R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$$

---

## 4.4 DES Block Diagram (16-Round Feistel)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PT[Plaintext 64 bits] --> IP[Initial Permutation IP]
    IP --> L0[L0 32 bits]
    IP --> R0[R0 32 bits]

    subgraph Rounds[Sixteen Feistel Rounds]
        direction LR
        R1[Round 1 F K1]
        R2[Round 2 F K2]
        R3[Round 3 F K3]
        R4[Round 4 F K4]
        R5[Round 5 F K5]
        R6[Round 6 F K6]
        R7[Round 7 F K7]
        R8[Round 8 F K8]
        R9[Round 9 F K9]
        R10[Round 10 F K10]
        R11[Round 11 F K11]
        R12[Round 12 F K12]
        R13[Round 13 F K13]
        R14[Round 14 F K14]
        R15[Round 15 F K15]
        R16[Round 16 F K16]
    end

    L0 --> R1
    R0 --> R1
    R1 --> L16[L16]
    R16 --> R16b[R16]
    L16 --> SWP{Swap L16 and R16}
    R16b --> SWP
    SWP --> FP[Inverse Initial Permutation IP inverse]
    FP --> CT[Ciphertext 64 bits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note that after round 16, the two halves are swapped *before* the final inverse permutation. This ensures that the encryption and decryption processes are identical in form, with only the subkey schedule reversed.

---

## 4.5 AES (Rijndael) Round Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[State 128 bits] --> SB[SubBytes S box substitution]
    SB --> SR[ShiftRows row shift]
    SR --> MC[MixColumns matrix multiply]
    MC --> AK[AddRoundKey XOR with round key]
    AK --> S1[Next Round State]

    subgraph Skip[Final Round omits MixColumns]
        F1[SubBytes] --> F2[ShiftRows] --> F3[AddRoundKey] --> OUT[AES Ciphertext]
    end
```

---

## 4.6 Block Cipher Modes of Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Plaintext Blocks] --> Q{Mode Selection}

    Q --> ECB[ECB Mode]
    ECB --> E1[Encrypt each block independently]
    E1 --> C1[Ciphertext]

    Q --> CBC[CBC Mode]
    CBC --> XOR1{XOR previous ciphertext block}
    IV[Initialization Vector IV] --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> E2[Encrypt]
    E2 --> C2[Ciphertext Block]
    C2 --> NEXT[Feedback to next block]

    Q --> CTR[CTR Mode]
    CTR --> NONCE[Counter Nonce]
    NONCE --> E3[Encrypt counter]
    E3 --> KS[Keystream]
    P --> XOR2{XOR with keystream}
    KS --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> C3[Ciphertext]
```

**Properties:**

| Mode | IV/Nonce | Error Effect |
|------|----------|--------------|
| ECB | None | One block corrupted → only that block garbled |
| CBC | Random IV needed | Bit flip in $C_i$ garbles $P_i$ and flips one bit in $P_{i+1}$ |
| CFB | Random IV needed | Bit flip in $C_i$ garbles $P_i$ and a few bits in $P_{i+1}$ |
| OFB | Random IV needed | Bit flip in $C_i$ flips only the corresponding bit in $P_i$ |
| CTR | Unique nonce | Bit flip in $C_i$ flips only corresponding bit in $P_i$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the four security goals of cryptography. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The four fundamental security goals are:

1. **Confidentiality** — Ensuring that information is accessible *only* to parties authorized to view it. Achieved via encryption (AES, RSA).

2. **Integrity** — Guaranteeing that data has not been altered or tampered with during transit or storage. Achieved via hash functions (SHA-256) and Message Authentication Codes (MAC).

3. **Authentication** — Verifying the claimed identity of a user, system, or message origin. Achieved via passwords, certificates, digital signatures, or challenge–response protocols.

4. **Non-Repudiation** — Preventing the sender (or receiver) from later denying that a message was sent/received. Achieved uniquely via *digital signatures*.

A common fifth goal, **Availability**, ensures systems remain operational and resistant to Denial-of-Service attacks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric Key |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Number of keys | One shared secret | Pair: public + private |
| Key distribution | Hard problem | Public key is freely shared |
| Speed | Fast (1000× faster) | Slower due to modular exponentiation |
| Algorithms | DES, AES, 3DES, Blowfish | RSA, ECC, ElGamal, Diffie–Hellman |
| Key length | 128–256 bits sufficient | 2048+ bits (RSA) for similar strength |
| Use case | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |
| Confidentiality | Yes (encryption) | Yes (encryption) |
| Non-repudiation | Not directly | Yes (digital signature) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "symmetric is faster than asymmetric" but forget to mention that asymmetric cryptography solves the *key distribution problem*. Always state both **speed** and **key exchange** in your answer for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Classical Ciphers & DES Analysis

**Part (a)** Explain the Feistel cipher structure in detail with a neat diagram. Show that the encryption and decryption operations are structurally identical. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

A **Feistel cipher** is a symmetric block cipher design proposed by Horst Feistel (IBM, 1973). It is the foundation of DES.

**Structure of one round:**

Given a plaintext block of $2w$ bits split into left half $L_{i-1}$ and right half $R_{i-1}$, and a round subkey $K_i$:

$$L_i = R_{i-1} \quad \text{and} \quad R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$$

where $F$ is the *round function* (any function — does not need to be invertible).

**Properties:**
- After $n$ rounds, the ciphertext is $(L_n, R_n)$.
- The function $F$ provides confusion; the swap-and-XOR provides diffusion.

**Proof of structural equivalence of encryption and decryption:**

For encryption (forward direction):

$$L_i = R_{i-1} \quad \text{(1)}$$

$$R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i) \quad \text{(2)}$$

From equation (1) we get $R_{i-1} = L_i$.

Substituting into equation (2):

$$R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(L_i, K_i)$$

Solving for $L_{i-1}$:

$$L_{i-1} = R_i \oplus F(L_i, K_i)$$

So decryption computes:

$$R_{i-1} = L_i$$

$$L_{i-1} = R_i \oplus F(L_i, K_i)$$

This has the *exact same form* as encryption. The only difference is that the subkeys are applied in **reverse order** ($K_n, K_{n-1}, \dots, K_1$).

Hence, the same hardware/software module can perform both encryption and decryption by merely reversing the key schedule. This is the chief advantage of the Feistel design.

*Diagram: refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.*

---

**Part (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the Data Encryption Standard (DES). Mention its key size, block size, number of rounds, and the role of S-boxes. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Overview:**

The **Data Encryption Standard (DES)** is a symmetric block cipher adopted by the U.S. National Bureau of Standards (now NIST) in 1977 as FIPS Publication 46. It is a direct application of the Feistel structure.

**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Block size | 64 bits |
| Key size | 64 bits (only 56 bits effective — 8 are parity bits) |
| Number of rounds | 16 Feistel rounds |
| Structure | Feistel network |
| S-boxes | 8 different 6×4 S-boxes providing non-linearity |
| P-box | 32-bit permutation providing diffusion |

**Algorithm steps:**

1. **Initial Permutation (IP):** A fixed permutation applied to the 64-bit plaintext block.

2. **Splitting:** The 64-bit block is split into two 32-bit halves: $L_0$ and $R_0$.

3. **Sixteen rounds:** For each round $i = 1, 2, \dots, 16$:
   - **Expansion (E-box):** Expand $R_{i-1}$ from 32 bits to 48 bits.
   - **Key mixing:** XOR the expanded $R_{i-1}$ with the 48-bit round subkey $K_i$.
   - **Substitution (S-boxes):** Pass the 48-bit result through 8 S-boxes, each taking 6 input bits and producing 4 output bits, yielding 32 bits.
   - **Permutation (P-box):** Apply a fixed bit permutation to spread the influence of each S-box output bit.
   - **Final XOR:** Compute $L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$ and assign it to $R_i$; set $L_i = R_{i-1}$.

4. **Swap:** After round 16, swap the two halves $(L_{16}, R_{16}) \rightarrow (R_{16}, L_{16})$.

5. **Inverse Initial Permutation (IP⁻¹):** Apply the inverse of the initial permutation to produce the 64-bit ciphertext.

**Role of S-boxes:**

S-boxes (Substitution boxes) are the *only non-linear* component of DES. Without them, DES would be entirely linear and trivially breakable by Gaussian elimination. Each of the 8 S-boxes maps 6 input bits to 4 output bits, creating the **confusion** property described by Shannon. They also provide the resistance against differential and linear cryptanalysis.

**Weaknesses:**

- The 56-bit key is now considered insecure; brute-force attack using specialized hardware (EFF's "Deep Crack" in 1998) cracked DES in under 24 hours.
- Differential cryptanalysis (Biham–Shamir, 1990) and linear cryptanalysis (Matsui, 1993) further reduced effective security.
- Replaced by **Triple DES** (3DES) and ultimately by **AES** as the modern standard.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — AES & Block Cipher Modes

**Part (a)** Explain the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) algorithm with its structure, parameters, and round transformations. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Background:**

The **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is a symmetric block cipher selected by NIST in 2001 after a public competition (1997–2000). The winning algorithm, **Rijndael**, was designed by Vincent Rijmen and Joan Daemen (Belgium) and was standardized as **FIPS 197**.

**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Block size | 128 bits (fixed) |
| Key sizes | 128 / 192 / 256 bits |
| Rounds | 10 (AES-128), 12 (AES-192), 14 (AES-256) |
| Structure | Substitution–Permutation Network (SPN), *not* Feistel |
| Byte-oriented | Operates on a 4×4 matrix of bytes called the *State* |

**Structure:**

The 128-bit input is arranged into a 4×4 matrix of bytes (called the *State*). AES treats each byte as an element of the finite field $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$ with the irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$.

**Round Transformations (for rounds 1 to N−1):**

1. **SubBytes:** A nonlinear byte-wise substitution using a single 16×16 lookup table called the *S-box*. This provides **confusion**.

2. **ShiftRows:** Cyclically left-shifts the rows of the State matrix:
   - Row 0: no shift
   - Row 1: shift left by 1 byte
   - Row 2: shift left by 2 bytes
   - Row 3: shift left by 3 bytes
   
   This provides inter-column diffusion.

3. **MixColumns:** Each column of the State is multiplied by a fixed 4×4 matrix over $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$. This provides intra-column diffusion.

4. **AddRoundKey:** Each byte of the State is XORed with the corresponding byte of the round key derived from the cipher key via the *key expansion* routine.

**Final round** (round N) skips MixColumns and consists only of SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey.

**Key Expansion:**

The AES key schedule generates $N + 1$ round keys (each 128 bits) from the original cipher key using a combination of byte substitution, rotation, and XOR with *round constants* (RCON).

**Why AES is secure:**

- Effective key size (128–256 bits) makes brute force computationally infeasible.
- Best known attack (Biclique, 2011) reduces effective security of AES-128 to about 126 bits — still $\approx 2^{126}$ operations, far beyond practical capability.
- All operations are efficiently implementable in both hardware and software.
- Side-channel resistance (cache-timing, fault injection) can be added via constant-time implementations.

---

**Part (b)** Explain the five standard block cipher modes of operation. Why is ECB mode considered insecure for messages longer than one block? **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

Block ciphers process fixed-size blocks (e.g., 128 bits for AES). To encrypt a multi-block message securely, we need a **mode of operation**. NIST SP 800-38A defines five standard modes:

**1. Electronic Codebook (ECB) Mode:**

$$C_i = E_K(P_i)$$

$$P_i = D_K(C_i)$$

Each block is encrypted independently with the same key. **Parallelizable** (both encryption and decryption).

**2. Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) Mode:**

$$C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$$

with $C_0 = IV$ (a random initialization vector sent alongside the ciphertext).

Decryption: $P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$.

- Encryption is *sequential*; decryption is parallelizable.
- A one-bit error in $C_i$ garbles $P_i$ completely and flips exactly one bit in $P_{i+1}$.

**3. Cipher Feedback (CFB) Mode:**

$$C_i = P_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(C_{i-1}))$$

where $C_0 = IV$.

- Turns a block cipher into a self-synchronizing stream cipher.
- Operates on $s$-bit segments (commonly $s = 8$ or $s = 128$).

**4. Output Feedback (OFB) Mode:**

$$O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$$

$$C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$$

with $O_0 = IV$.

- Produces a keystream $O_i$ independent of plaintext and ciphertext.
- No error propagation — bit flip in $C_i$ flips only that bit in $P_i$.

**5. Counter (CTR) Mode:**

$$C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(\text{Nonce} \,\Vert\, \text{Counter}_i)$$

- Fully parallelizable for both encryption and decryption.
- Random access: any block can be decrypted without processing predecessors.
- Used in modern protocols like AES-GCM (CTR + GHASH for authentication).

**Comparison Table:**

| Mode | Parallel Encryption | Parallel Decryption | Random Access | IV/Nonce | Error Propagation |
|------|--------------------:|--------------------:|--------------:|----------|-------------------|
| ECB  | Yes | Yes | Yes | None | One block |
| CBC  | No | Yes | No | Required | Whole block + 1 bit |
| CFB  | No | Yes | No | Required | A few blocks |
| OFB  | Yes (keystream) | Yes (keystream) | No | Required | One bit |
| CTR  | Yes | Yes | Yes | Required (unique) | One bit |

**Why ECB is Insecure for Long Messages:**

ECB encrypts identical plaintext blocks into identical ciphertext blocks. This has two critical consequences:

1. **Pattern leakage:** An attacker who knows the structure of the plaintext can detect repetitions. The famous "ECB penguin" demonstration showed that encrypting a bitmap image of a penguin in ECB mode produced a ciphertext that still visibly revealed the penguin's outline.

2. **Determinism:** Identical messages under the same key always produce the same ciphertext, enabling replay attacks, traffic analysis, and chosen-plaintext attacks.

In contrast, CBC, CTR, and other modes randomize the encryption via an IV or counter, so identical plaintext blocks yield different ciphertext blocks across different messages and even within the same message.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students describe the modes but forget to mention that **ECB has no IV** and that **CTR mode is the modern default** (used in AES-GCM). Always state at least one practical application per mode to earn full marks. A common mistake is to claim ECB is "secure if you have only one block" — true, but a single block has no confidentiality in most real-world contexts. Mention *pattern leakage* explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> Use this as a rapid-revision checklist before walking into the KTU exam hall.

- [ ] **Cryptography ≠ Encryption.** Cryptography is the broader field; encryption is one technique within it.
- [ ] **Kerckhoffs's Principle:** Security rests on the key, *not* on algorithm secrecy.
- [ ] **Symmetric key = same key for E and D.** Asymmetric uses a public–private pair.
- [ ] **X.800 distinguishes attacks (passive/active), services (5+1), and mechanisms (8).** Memorize all three lists.
- [ ] **Caesar key space = 26; monoalphabetic = $26!$; Vigenère = $26^m$.**
- [ ] **Caesar formula:** $C = (P + k) \bmod 26$.
- [ ] **Multiplicative inverse** needed for multiplicative, affine, and Hill ciphers.
- [ ] **Hill cipher matrix must have $\det(K)$ coprime with 26** to be invertible mod 26.
- [ ] **Playfair uses digraphs, 5×5 matrix, merges I/J.**
- [ ] **Vigenère key is repeated** to plaintext length; same keyword gives polyalphabetic output.
- [ ] **Feistel cipher is self-inverting** when subkeys are applied in reverse order.
- [ ] **DES:** 64-bit block, 56-bit effective key, **16 Feistel rounds**, 8 S-boxes. Now broken by brute force.
- [ ] **3DES** applies DES three times; effective key **112 bits** (two-key) or **168 bits** (three-key); deprecated by NIST in 2023.
- [ ] **AES (Rijndael):** 128-bit block, **128/192/256-bit keys**, 10/12/14 rounds, **SPN** structure.
- [ ] **AES round:** SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey. **Final round skips MixColumns.**
- [ ] **ECB mode is insecure** for any non-trivial message due to pattern leakage.
- [ ] **CTR mode is parallelizable** and used in modern authenticated encryption (AES-GCM).
- [ ] **Modes of operation:** ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR. Know the IV requirement, parallelizability, and error propagation for each.
- [ ] **Modern TLS uses symmetric crypto (AES) for bulk data** and asymmetric crypto (RSA/ECC) only for key exchange.
- [ ] **Cipher feedback in stream ciphers** uses XOR with a keystream.
- [ ] **Frequency analysis defeats monoalphabetic ciphers**, not polyalphabetic ones.
- [ ] **Kasiski examination** breaks Vigenère by detecting repeated ciphertext digrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Key Cryptography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Public Key Cryptography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Public Key Cryptography (PKC)**, also known as **Asymmetric Cryptography**, is a cryptographic system that uses a mathematically related pair of keys — a **Public Key** (freely distributed for encryption) and a **Private Key** (kept secret by the owner for decryption) — to perform secure data exchange, digital signing, and key establishment over an insecure channel.

The formal KTU 2024 Scheme definition emphasizes that PKC satisfies three foundational principles:
1. **Confidentiality** via encryption using the recipient's public key.
2. **Authenticity & Non-Repudiation** via digital signing using the sender's private key.
3. **Secure Key Exchange** via protocols such as **Diffie-Hellman**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 strictly expects conceptual clarity on **RSA**, **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (DHKE)**, **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)**, **Man-in-the-Middle Attack (MITM)**, and **Digital Signatures**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Public Mailbox" Model

Imagine every citizen in a city has a **transparent mailbox with an open slot (Public Key)** and a **personal unique key (Private Key)**.
- Anyone in the city can **drop a sealed letter** into your open slot (encrypt with your public key).
- **Only you** possess the unique physical key to open and read the letter (decrypt with your private key).
- Conversely, if you wish to **prove a letter came from you**, you can stamp it with a unique personal seal only you own (sign with your private key), and anyone can verify the seal's authenticity using the city's public registry (verify with your public key).

This is the philosophical core of asymmetric cryptography — **two mathematically entangled keys** that perform inverse operations on shared plaintext/ciphertext.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Plaintext ($M$)** | Original readable message |
| **Ciphertext ($C$)** | Encrypted unreadable output |
| **Public Key ($K_{pub}$)** | Key distributed openly for encryption |
| **Private Key ($K_{pr}$)** | Secret key held by owner for decryption |
| **Trapdoor Function** | One-way function easy to compute, hard to invert without key |
| **Key Pair** | $(e, n)$ public and $(d, n)$ private for RSA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The security of PKC rests on **trapdoor one-way functions** — computations that are computationally infeasible to reverse without holding the secret trapdoor (the private key).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles of Public Key Cryptography

Public Key Cryptography operates on six mandatory design principles. A KTU examiner will look for these:

1. **Computationally Easy:** Generating key pairs, encryption, and decryption must be polynomial-time operations.
2. **Computationally Infeasible to Break:** An attacker holding $K_{pub}$ must not be able to recover plaintext or derive $K_{pr}$.
3. **Trapdoor Property:** Knowing $K_{pub}$ + plaintext is useless for decryption — the **trapdoor** ($K_{pr}$) is required.
4. **Key Pair Uniqueness:** Every user possesses a unique $(K_{pub}, K_{pr})$ pair.
5. **Wide Applicability:** Supports encryption, digital signatures, and key exchange.
6. **Mathematical Foundation:** Built on **Number Theory** — modular exponentiation, prime factorization, discrete logarithms.

### 2.2 The RSA Algorithm (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman, 1977)

RSA is the **de facto standard** public key cryptosystem and is the highest-weightage topic in KTU Module 4.

#### 2.2.1 Key Generation Procedure
1. Select two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$.
2. Compute modulus $n = p \times q$.
3. Compute **Euler's Totient** $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
4. Choose public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
5. Compute private exponent $d$ such that $e \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ (modular inverse).

#### 2.2.2 Encryption & Decryption
- **Encryption (by Sender using Receiver's Public Key):** $C = M^e \bmod n$
- **Decryption (by Receiver using Private Key):** $M = C^d \bmod n$

#### 2.2.3 Why RSA Works — Euler's Theorem
The cryptographic correctness of RSA is founded on:

$$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

Applying this, decryption recovers the original message:

$$C^d \bmod n = (M^e)^d \bmod n = M^{ed} \bmod n = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \bmod n = M$$

### 2.3 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (DHKE) — 1976

DHKE is **not** an encryption algorithm; it is a **key agreement protocol** that allows two parties to establish a **shared symmetric secret** over an insecure channel.

- Both parties agree on a large prime $p$ and a primitive root generator $g$ (public parameters).
- **Alice** picks secret $a$, computes $A = g^a \bmod p$, sends $A$.
- **Bob** picks secret $b$, computes $B = g^b \bmod p$, sends $B$.
- **Shared Secret:** $K = B^a \bmod p = A^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.

> [!NOTE]
> The security of DHKE relies on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**: given $g, p,$ and $g^a \bmod p$, it is computationally infeasible to recover $a$.

### 2.4 Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)

ECC provides equivalent security to RSA with **much smaller key sizes**, making it the preferred choice for **mobile, IoT, and constrained environments**.

- Operates on elliptic curves over finite fields: $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod{p}$
- **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**: Given points $P$ and $kP$, finding $k$ is computationally hard.
- A 256-bit ECC key ≈ 3072-bit RSA key in security strength.

### 2.5 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack on DHKE

DHKE is **vulnerable to MITM** without authentication. An attacker (Eve) can intercept $A$ and $B$, substitute her own values $A'$ and $B'$, and establish two separate shared secrets with Alice and Bob.

> [!WARNING]
> **Mitigation:** Use **authenticated DHKE** variants like **Station-to-Station (STS)** protocol with digital signatures, or rely on **RSA-based key transport** for authentication.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RSA Modulus** | $n = p \times q$ | Product of two distinct primes |
| **Euler's Totient** | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Used for key generation |
| **Modular Inverse** | $e \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Extended Euclidean Algorithm |
| **RSA Encryption** | $C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}$ | $0 \le M < n$ |
| **RSA Decryption** | $M \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$ | Private key $d$ |
| **Euler's Theorem** | $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | Foundation of RSA |
| **Fermat's Little Theorem** | $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | For prime $p$ |
| **DH Public Value** | $A = g^a \bmod p$ | Alice transmits |
| **DH Shared Secret** | $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Computed by both parties |
| **ECC Equation** | $y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod{p}$ | Over finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$ |
| **ECC Point Multiplication** | $Q = kP$ | ECDLP is hard |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HTTPS/TLS 1.3** uses **RSA** for digital signatures and **X25519 (ECC-based DH)** for key exchange.
- **PGP / GPG email encryption** uses RSA + AES hybrid.
- **Bitcoin & Blockchain** uses **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)** with the secp256k1 curve.
- **IoT devices** prefer **ECC** for low power consumption and small key footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme evaluations, students **must** explicitly state the mathematical relationship between keys and the underlying hard problem (Integer Factorization for RSA, DLP for DH, ECDLP for ECC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive RSA Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Format):**
> In a public key system based on RSA, suppose $p = 17$ and $q = 11$. 
> (a) Find $n$ and $\phi(n)$. (b) Choose $e = 7$ and find $d$. (c) Encrypt $M = 88$ and verify by decryption.

#### Step (a): Compute Modulus and Totient

Given: $p = 17$, $q = 11$.

$$n = p \times q = 17 \times 11 = 187$$

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 16 \times 10 = 160$$

**Explanation:** $n$ is the public modulus; $\phi(n)$ counts integers less than $n$ that are coprime to $n$.

#### Step (b): Find the Private Exponent $d$

We must solve:

$$e \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

$$7 \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$$

Using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

$$\begin{aligned}
160 &= 22 \times 7 + 6 \\
7 &= 1 \times 6 + 1 \\
6 &= 6 \times 1 + 0
\end{aligned}$$

Back-substitute to find the inverse:

$$1 = 7 - 1 \times 6$$

$$1 = 7 - 1 \times (160 - 22 \times 7)$$

$$1 = 23 \times 7 - 1 \times 160$$

Therefore:

$$d = 23$$

**Verification:** $7 \times 23 = 161 = 160 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always perform this verification check in your exam answer — examiners explicitly look for the confirmation that $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

#### Step (c): Encrypt $M = 88$ and Decrypt

**Encryption** using public key $(e=7, n=187)$:

$$C = M^e \bmod n = 88^7 \bmod 187$$

We compute this using **successive squaring (modular exponentiation)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
88^1 \bmod 187 &= 88 \\
88^2 \bmod 187 &= 7744 \bmod 187 = 7744 - 41 \times 187 = 7744 - 7667 = 77 \\
88^4 \bmod 187 &= 77^2 \bmod 187 = 5929 \bmod 187 = 5929 - 31 \times 187 = 5929 - 5797 = 132 \\
88^7 &= 88^4 \times 88^2 \times 88^1 \bmod 187 \\
88^7 \bmod 187 &= 132 \times 77 \times 88 \bmod 187 \\
132 \times 77 \bmod 187 &= 10164 \bmod 187 = 10164 - 54 \times 187 = 10164 - 10098 = 66 \\
66 \times 88 \bmod 187 &= 5808 \bmod 187 = 5808 - 31 \times 187 = 5808 - 5797 = 11
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore: $C = 11$.

**Decryption** using private key $(d=23, n=187)$:

$$M = C^d \bmod n = 11^{23} \bmod 187$$

Using successive squaring:

$$\begin{aligned}
11^1 \bmod 187 &= 11 \\
11^2 \bmod 187 &= 121 \\
11^4 \bmod 187 &= 121^2 \bmod 187 = 14641 \bmod 187 = 14641 - 78 \times 187 = 14641 - 14586 = 55 \\
11^8 \bmod 187 &= 55^2 \bmod 187 = 3025 \bmod 187 = 3025 - 16 \times 187 = 3025 - 2992 = 33 \\
11^{16} \bmod 187 &= 33^2 \bmod 187 = 1089 \bmod 187 = 1089 - 5 \times 187 = 1089 - 935 = 154 \\
11^{23} &= 11^{16} \times 11^4 \times 11^2 \times 11^1 \bmod 187 \\
&= 154 \times 55 \times 121 \times 11 \bmod 187 \\
154 \times 55 \bmod 187 &= 8470 \bmod 187 = 8470 - 45 \times 187 = 8470 - 8415 = 55 \\
55 \times 121 \bmod 187 &= 6655 \bmod 187 = 6655 - 35 \times 187 = 6655 - 6545 = 110 \\
110 \times 11 \bmod 187 &= 1210 \bmod 187 = 1210 - 6 \times 187 = 1210 - 1122 = 88
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore: $M = 88$ ✓ (Original message recovered).

### 3.2 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Detailed Derivation

> **Problem Statement:** Let $p = 23$, $g = 5$. Alice chooses $a = 6$, Bob chooses $b = 15$. Compute the shared secret.

**Step 1: Public parameters:** $p = 23$, $g = 5$ (publicly known).

**Step 2: Alice's Public Value:**

$$A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23$$

Computing:

$$5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^3 = 5 \times 2 = 10 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^6 = (5^3)^2 = 10^2 = 100 \equiv 100 - 4 \times 23 = 8 \pmod{23}$$

So $A = 8$.

**Step 3: Bob's Public Value:**

$$B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23$$

We can write $5^{15} = 5^8 \times 5^4 \times 5^2 \times 5^1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
5^4 &= (5^2)^2 = 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23} \\
5^8 &= 4^2 = 16 \pmod{23} \\
5^{15} &= 5^8 \times 5^4 \times 5^2 \times 5^1 = 16 \times 4 \times 2 \times 5 = 640 \\
640 \bmod 23 &= 640 - 27 \times 23 = 640 - 621 = 19
\end{aligned}$$

So $B = 19$.

**Step 4: Shared Secret Computation (Both Parties):**

Alice computes:

$$K = B^a \bmod p = 19^6 \bmod 23$$

$$\begin{aligned}
19^2 &= 361 = 15 \times 23 + 16 \equiv 16 \pmod{23} \\
19^3 &= 19 \times 16 = 304 = 13 \times 23 + 5 \equiv 5 \pmod{23} \\
19^6 &= (19^3)^2 = 5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
\end{aligned}$$

So $K = 2$.

Bob computes:

$$K = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23$$

$$\begin{aligned}
8^2 &= 64 \equiv 64 - 2 \times 23 = 18 \pmod{23} \\
8^4 &= 18^2 = 324 \equiv 324 - 14 \times 23 = 2 \pmod{23} \\
8^8 &= 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23} \\
8^{15} &= 8^8 \times 8^4 \times 8^2 \times 8^1 = 4 \times 2 \times 18 \times 8 = 1152 \\
1152 \bmod 23 &= 1152 - 50 \times 23 = 1152 - 1150 = 2
\end{aligned}$$

So $K = 2$ ✓ (Both parties derive the same shared secret).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of PKC Algorithms

| Property | RSA | Diffie-Hellman | ECC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Underlying Hard Problem** | Integer Factorization | Discrete Logarithm (DLP) | Elliptic Curve DLP (ECDLP) |
| **Primary Use** | Encryption, Signatures | Key Exchange | Encryption, Signatures, Key Exchange |
| **Key Size (Equivalent Security)** | 2048+ bits | 2048+ bits | 256 bits |
| **Speed** | Slow (large modular exponentiation) | Moderate | Fast |
| **Vulnerability** | Quantum (Shor's algorithm) | Quantum + MITM | Quantum |
| **Signature Standard** | RSA-PSS, PKCS#1 | N/A (not a signing scheme) | ECDSA, EdDSA |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RSA Cryptosystem — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Selects Primes p and q] --> B[Compute n = p * q]
    B --> C[Compute phi n = p-1 times q-1]
    C --> D[Choose Public Exponent e]
    D --> E[Compute Private Exponent d = e inv mod phi n]
    E --> F[Publish Public Key e and n]
    E --> G[Keep Private Key d Secret]
    F --> H[Sender Encrypts M to C = M to power e mod n]
    H --> I[Transmit Ciphertext C over Insecure Channel]
    I --> J[Receiver Decrypts M = C to power d mod n]
    G --> J
    J --> K[Original Plaintext M Recovered]
```

### 4.2 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Protocol Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Alice
    participant B as Bob
    participant E as Eve (Passive Attacker)
    A->>A: Pick secret a
    B->>B: Pick secret b
    A->>B: Send A = g^a mod p
    B->>A: Send B = g^b mod p
    E->>E: Intercepts A and B (cannot solve DLP)
    A->>A: Compute K = B^a mod p = g^ab mod p
    B->>B: Compute K = A^b mod p = g^ab mod p
    Note over A,B: Shared Secret K = g^ab mod p established
    Note over E: Eve cannot compute K without solving DLP
```

### 4.3 Man-in-the-Middle Attack on DHKE (Vulnerability Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Alice
        A1[Alice Secret a]
        A2[Compute A = g^a mod p]
    end
    subgraph Eve
        E1[Eve Secret e]
        E2[Send A prime = g^e mod p to Alice]
        E3[Send B prime = g^e mod p to Bob]
    end
    subgraph Bob
        B1[Bob Secret b]
        B2[Compute B = g^b mod p]
    end
    A2 -->|Intercepted| E2
    E2 -->|A prime| A1
    B2 -->|Intercepted| E3
    E3 -->|B prime| B1
    A1 -->|K1 = g^ae mod p| E1
    B1 -->|K2 = g^be mod p| E1
    Note1[Alice and Bob think they share K, but Eve holds two separate secrets]
```

### 4.4 ECC Point Multiplication — High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select Elliptic Curve y^2 = x^3 + ax + b mod p] --> B[Choose Generator Point G on Curve]
    B --> C[Choose Private Key k Random Integer]
    C --> D[Compute Public Key Q = kG via Point Addition and Doubling]
    D --> E[Publish Q Keep k Secret]
    E --> F[Security Based on ECDLP Hardness]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Public Key Cryptography. List any three requirements that a public key cryptosystem must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

Public Key Cryptography is an asymmetric cryptographic system that uses a pair of mathematically related keys — a **public key** for encryption and a **private key** for decryption — to provide secure communication.

**Three Requirements:**
1. It should be computationally easy to encrypt and decrypt messages using the respective keys.
2. It should be computationally infeasible for an adversary to determine the private key from the public key.
3. Encryption and decryption should be efficiently reversible operations in either order (commutativity property: $M = E_{K_{pub}}(D_{K_{pr}}(M))$).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)? Why is it important for the security of Diffie-Hellman key exchange?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** states that given a prime $p$, a generator $g$, and a value $Y = g^x \bmod p$, it is computationally infeasible to determine $x$ for large $p$.

**Importance for DHKE Security:**
In Diffie-Hellman, an eavesdropper observes $A = g^a \bmod p$ and $B = g^b \bmod p$. To compute the shared secret $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$, the attacker would need to solve $a = \log_g A \bmod p$, which is the DLP. For sufficiently large primes (≥ 2048 bits), this is computationally intractable, thus securing the exchange.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the RSA algorithm in detail. Generate the public and private keys for $p = 7$ and $q = 17$. Take $e = 5$. Perform encryption and decryption on the message $M = 5$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm. What is the Man-in-the-Middle attack on DHKE? How can it be prevented?

---

**Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Key Generation:**
- $p = 7$, $q = 17$
- $n = p \times q = 7 \times 17 = 119$
- $\phi(n) = (7-1)(17-1) = 6 \times 16 = 96$
- $e = 5$ (given, and $\gcd(5, 96) = 1$ ✓)
- Find $d$ such that $5d \equiv 1 \pmod{96}$

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$\begin{aligned}
96 &= 19 \times 5 + 1 \\
5 &= 5 \times 1 + 0
\end{aligned}$$

Back-substitute: $1 = 96 - 19 \times 5$, so $d = -19 \equiv 77 \pmod{96}$.

**Verification:** $5 \times 77 = 385 = 4 \times 96 + 1 = 385$ ✓

- **Public Key:** $(e=5, n=119)$
- **Private Key:** $(d=77, n=119)$

**Step 2 — Encryption:**

$$C = M^e \bmod n = 5^5 \bmod 119 = 3125 \bmod 119$$

$$3125 \div 119 = 26.26 \Rightarrow 26 \times 119 = 3094$$

$$C = 3125 - 3094 = 31$$

**Step 3 — Decryption:**

$$M = C^d \bmod n = 31^{77} \bmod 119$$

Using successive squaring (truncated for brevity; final result):

$$M = 5 \text{ (original message recovered ✓)}$$

---

**Solution (b):**

**Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Algorithm:**

1. Alice and Bob publicly agree on a large prime $p$ and primitive root $g$.
2. Alice picks secret $a$, sends $A = g^a \bmod p$ to Bob.
3. Bob picks secret $b$, sends $B = g^b \bmod p$ to Alice.
4. Alice computes $K = B^a \bmod p$.
5. Bob computes $K = A^b \bmod p$.
6. Both share $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$.

**Man-in-the-Middle Attack:**

Eve intercepts $A$ and $B$, generates her own secret $e$, sends $g^e \bmod p$ to both parties. She now holds two separate shared secrets — one with Alice and one with Bob — and can read/modify all messages.

**Prevention:**
- Use **digital signatures** to authenticate the exchanged values.
- Employ **authenticated key agreement** protocols like **STS (Station-to-Station)**.
- Use **public key certificates** issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)? Explain point addition and scalar multiplication on the curve $y^2 = x^3 + 2x + 3$ over $\mathbb{F}_5$. Given $P = (1, 1)$ on this curve, compute $2P$ and $3P$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare RSA and ECC in terms of key size, security, computational efficiency, and quantum vulnerability. State any two real-world applications of ECC.

---

**Solution (a):**

**Definition:** ECC is a public key cryptography system based on the algebraic structure of elliptic curves over finite fields. Its security relies on the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**.

**Verification that $P = (1, 1)$ lies on the curve over $\mathbb{F}_5$:**

$$y^2 = 1^2 = 1$$

$$x^3 + 2x + 3 = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6 \equiv 1 \pmod{5}$$

Thus $1 = 1$ ✓ — the point is on the curve.

**Point Doubling ($2P = P + P$):**

The slope $\lambda$ for point doubling (when $y_P \neq 0$):

$$\lambda = \frac{3x_P^2 + a}{2y_P} = \frac{3(1)^2 + 2}{2(1)} = \frac{5}{2} \equiv \frac{0}{2} = 0 \pmod{5}$$

So $\lambda = 0$.

$$x_{2P} = \lambda^2 - 2x_P = 0 - 2 = -2 \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$$

$$y_{2P} = \lambda(x_P - x_{2P}) - y_P = 0 \times (1 - 3) - 1 = 0 - 1 = -1 \equiv 4 \pmod{5}$$

So $2P = (3, 4)$.

**Point Addition ($3P = 2P + P$):**

Slope $\lambda$ between $P = (1, 1)$ and $2P = (3, 4)$:

$$\lambda = \frac{y_{2P} - y_P}{x_{2P} - x_P} = \frac{4 - 1}{3 - 1} = \frac{3}{2} \equiv 3 \times 3 = 9 \equiv 4 \pmod{5}$$

(Using the modular inverse $2^{-1} \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$ since $2 \times 3 = 6 \equiv 1$.)

$$x_{3P} = \lambda^2 - x_P - x_{2P} = 16 - 1 - 3 = 12 \equiv 2 \pmod{5}$$

$$y_{3P} = \lambda(x_P - x_{3P}) - y_P = 4(1 - 2) - 1 = -4 - 1 = -5 \equiv 0 \pmod{5}$$

So $3P = (2, 0)$.

---

**Solution (b):**

| Property | RSA | ECC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Key Size for 128-bit security** | 3072 bits | 256 bits |
| **Underlying Hard Problem** | Integer Factorization | ECDLP |
| **Computational Efficiency** | Slower (large modular exponentiation) | Faster (point multiplication) |
| **Memory/Power Footprint** | High | Low (ideal for IoT/mobile) |
| **Quantum Vulnerability** | Vulnerable to Shor's algorithm | Also vulnerable to Shor's algorithm |

**Two Real-World Applications of ECC:**
1. **Bitcoin & Blockchain:** Uses **ECDSA (secp256k1 curve)** for transaction signing.
2. **TLS 1.3 & HTTPS:** Uses **X25519 (Curve25519)** for ephemeral key exchange.
3. **Apple iMessage & Signal Protocol** use Curve25519 for end-to-end encryption.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams (Module 4 — Public Key Cryptography):**
> 1. **Forgetting Modular Arithmetic Parentheses:** Writing $M^e \bmod n$ as $M^{e \bmod n}$ — a critical sign error worth 2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping Extended Euclidean Algorithm Steps:** Examiners allocate 2-3 marks explicitly for showing the steps of computing $d$. Writing just "$d = 23$" without justification loses full marks.
> 3. **Confusing Encryption/Decryption Keys:** Sending $M^e \bmod n$ with $d$ instead of $e$. Always verify the recipient uses the public key for encryption.
> 4. **No Verification in RSA:** Always close your RSA solution with "**Hence original message recovered**" after decryption — examiners reward this closing statement.
> 5. **MITM Without Mitigation:** Simply describing the MITM attack without stating **prevention (authentication/digital signatures)** is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Confusing DLP with ECDLP:** State the precise hard problem in each question. Mixing them is a common error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Public Key Cryptography (KTU Module 4):**

- ✅ PKC uses **two keys** (public + private); symmetric uses **one shared key**.
- ✅ RSA is based on **Integer Factorization**; security $\propto$ difficulty of factoring $n = pq$.
- ✅ **Key generation:** Compute $n$, $\phi(n)$, choose $e$, derive $d$ via **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.
- ✅ **Encryption:** $C = M^e \bmod n$ ; **Decryption:** $M = C^d \bmod n$.
- ✅ **Verification of RSA:** Always end with $M = C^d \bmod n = M$ ✓.
- ✅ **DHKE** is a **key agreement**, NOT encryption — both derive same $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
- ✅ **DHKE vulnerability:** MITM attack → mitigated via **authentication/digital signatures**.
- ✅ **ECC** offers equivalent security with **smaller keys** (256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA).
- ✅ **Hard problems** map to: RSA → IFP, DHKE → DLP, ECC → ECDLP.
- ✅ **Real-world protocols:** TLS 1.3, PGP, Bitcoin (ECDSA), Signal (X25519).
- ✅ All three (RSA, DHKE, ECC) are vulnerable to **quantum attacks (Shor's algorithm)**.
- ✅ **Trapdoor function** is the cornerstone of asymmetric cryptography.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Integrity and Digital Signatures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Message Integrity and Digital Signatures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Message Integrity — Formal Definition

**Message Integrity** is the assurance that a message, data packet, or digital document has *not been altered or tampered with* during transit from the sender to the receiver. In KTU 2024 terminology, it is one of the three pillars of Information Security (alongside *Confidentiality* and *Authentication*), and is achieved cryptographically through **Hash Functions** and **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU Exams):**
> - **Message Integrity** = Has the message been *modified*?
> - **Message Authentication** = Is the sender *who they claim* to be AND is the message *intact*?
> - **Digital Signature** = Integrity + Authentication + **Non-Repudiation** (the strongest property).

## 1.2 Digital Signature — Formal Definition

A **Digital Signature** is a cryptographic mechanism that mimics the functionality of a handwritten signature, providing:
1. **Authentication** — verifying the signer's identity
2. **Integrity** — detecting any alteration of the signed data
3. **Non-Repudiation** — the signer *cannot later deny* having signed

The mathematical foundation is **asymmetric (public-key) cryptography**, where the signer uses a **private key** to sign, and any verifier uses the signer's **public key** to verify.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sealed Wax Letter Analogy**
>
> Imagine you write a letter to a bank manager. To prove *you* wrote it, you drip **hot wax** stamped with your unique signet ring seal.
> - The **wax seal** = your **Digital Signature**
> - The **unique signet pattern** = your **Private Key** (only you possess it)
> - The bank's ability to recognize your seal = the **Public Key** (available to anyone)
> - If the letter is opened and resealed, the wax cracks → **Integrity is broken**
> - The bank cannot forge your seal because they don't own your signet → **Non-Repudiation**
> - Anyone holding your published seal imprint can verify it → **Verifiability**

## 1.4 Message Authentication Code (MAC) — Intuition

A **MAC** is like a *tamper-evident seal computed using a secret shared key*. Both sender and receiver hold the same secret key `K`. The sender computes `MAC = C(K, M)`, and the receiver recomputes it on receipt. If they match, the message is authentic *and* intact. Unlike digital signatures, MACs **do not provide non-repudiation** because either party could have generated the MAC.

## 1.5 Hash Function — Intuition

A **Cryptographic Hash Function** `H(M)` is a mathematical one-way function that:
- Takes an input of *any length*
- Produces a fixed-size output (e.g., **128 bits for MD5**, **160 bits for SHA-1**, **256 bits for SHA-256**)
- Is *deterministic* (same input → same output)
- Is *collision-resistant* (infeasible to find two inputs with the same hash)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU One-Liner to Memorize:**
> "A hash provides *integrity check*; a MAC provides *integrity + authentication*; a digital signature provides *integrity + authentication + non-repudiation*."

## 1.6 Standard Cryptographic Hash Metrics (KTU 2024 Mandate)

| Algorithm | Output Size (bits) | Status | Block Size |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|------------|
| MD5 | **128** | Broken (collisions found) | 512 bits |
| SHA-1 | **160** | Deprecated | 512 bits |
| SHA-256 | **256** | Secure (current standard) | 512 bits |
| SHA-512 | **512** | Secure (high-security) | 1024 bits |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of a Cryptographic Hash Function `H(M)`

For a hash function to be cryptographically secure, it must satisfy **five** properties:

1. **Pre-image Resistance (One-Wayness):** Given a hash value `h`, it is computationally *infeasible* to find any message `M` such that `H(M) = h`.
2. **Second Pre-image Resistance:** Given a message `M₁`, it is infeasible to find a *different* message `M₂` such that `H(M₁) = H(M₂)`.
3. **Collision Resistance:** It is infeasible to find *any two distinct* messages `M₁ ≠ M₂` such that `H(M₁) = H(M₂)`.
4. **Determinism:** The same input always produces the same output.
5. **Avalanche Effect:** A single bit change in the input must change approximately **50%** of the output bits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Birthday Paradox — Why Collision Resistance Matters Mathematically**
> To find *any* collision, an attacker needs approximately `2^(n/2)` operations for an n-bit hash. For MD5 (128 bits), this is only `2^64` operations — which is why MD5 is considered **cryptographically broken** for digital signatures.

## 2.2 The Three-Step Digital Signature Process

### Step 1 — Key Generation
- The signer generates a **key pair**: `{PU, PR}` (Public key, Private key) using a trapdoor one-way function.

### Step 2 — Signing
- Compute a hash of the message: `h = H(M)`
- Encrypt the hash with the private key: `S = E(PR, h)` (or `S = E(PR, H(M))`)
- Send `{M, S}` to the receiver

### Step 3 — Verification
- Receive `{M, S}`
- Decrypt the signature using the sender's public key: `h' = D(PU, S)`
- Independently hash the message: `h = H(M)`
- **Accept the signature if and only if `h == h'`**

## 2.3 Message Authentication Code (MAC) — Operational Logic

A **MAC** is computed as `MAC = C(K, M)` where:
- `K` = shared secret key
- `M` = message
- `C` = MAC algorithm (e.g., HMAC, CMAC)

The receiver, who also knows `K`, recomputes the MAC and compares. **Key principle:** MACs are symmetric — they require key distribution and **do not scale** to large networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **HMAC (Hash-based MAC) — KTU Frequently Asked**
>
> $$HMAC(K, M) = H\Big((K^+ \oplus opad) \;\Vert\; H((K^+ \oplus ipad) \;\Vert\; M)\Big)$$
>
> Where `ipad` = inner padding (`0x36` repeated) and `opad` = outer padding (`0x5C` repeated).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol/Formula | Meaning | Domain |
|----------------|---------|--------|
| `H(M)` | Cryptographic hash of message M | Integrity |
| `MAC = C(K, M)` | Message Authentication Code | Integrity + Authentication |
| `S = E(PR_A, H(M))` | Digital signature generation | All three security services |
| `D(PU_A, S) == H(M)` | Digital signature verification | All three security services |
| `n` (RSA) | Modulus `n = p × q` | Key size |
| `λ(n) = lcm(p-1, q-1)` | Carmichael function | RSA efficiency |
| `φ(n) = (p-1)(q-1)` | Euler totient | RSA key generation |
| `e · d ≡ 1 mod φ(n)` | Public/Private key relation | RSA |
| `S₁ ≡ g^k mod p` | ElGamal signature component | ElGamal |
| `S₂ ≡ (H(M) - x·S₁) · k^{-1} mod (p-1)` | ElGamal signature component | ElGamal |
| Collision search cost | `≈ 2^{n/2}` | Birthday attack |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **SSL/TLS Handshakes** — Server signs certificates for trust establishment
- **Email Security (PGP, S/MIME)** — Signed emails prove sender identity
