# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between PGP and S/MIME in terms of trust model, key distribution, and primary use case. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PGP | S/MIME |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Model** | Decentralized **Web of Trust** — users sign each other's keys to vouch for authenticity | Centralized **X.509 PKI hierarchy** — Certificate Authorities (CAs) vouch for identities |
| **Key Distribution** | Public keys published on **key servers** (e.g., `keys.openpgp.org`); verified via fingerprints exchanged out-of-band | Public keys bound in **X.509 certificates** issued by trusted CAs (e.g., DigiCert, Sectigo) and distributed via LDAP/Active Directory |
| **Primary Use Case** | Personal privacy, journalism, activism, secure whistleblowing | Enterprise, government, regulated industries (HIPAA, FINRA, DoD) with native client integration in Outlook/Apple Mail |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A 3-mark answer should hit *all three* criteria concisely in a table or three bullet points. Marks are split 1+1+1.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is DKIM? How does it differ from a digital signature in PGP? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)** is an e-mail authentication technique (RFC 6376) whereby the **outbound mail server** cryptographically signs selected message headers and the body using a private key, with the corresponding public key published in **DNS as a TXT record**. The receiving MTA retrieves this key, verifies the signature, and confirms that the message genuinely originated from the claimed domain and was not modified in transit.

**Key differences from PGP digital signatures:**

| Aspect | DKIM Signature | PGP Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Signing Entity** | The **sending MTA** (organization's mail server), not the end user | The **end user** (Alice's mail client) |
| **Key Holder** | Domain administrator; key published in DNS | User; key on personal device |
| **Visibility** | Verifiable by **any intermediate MTA** | End-to-end; visible only to recipient with sender's public key |
| **Confidentiality** | DKIM provides **no encryption** — only integrity + authentication | PGP provides both **encryption and signing** |
| **Scope** | Domain-level; primarily anti-spoofing/anti-spam | Person-to-person privacy |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the operational steps of PGP for sending a confidential and authenticated message from Alice to Bob. Use a clear block diagram. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the complete PGP message transmission and reception process with focus on hybrid cryptography. Show the mathematical relationship between RSA and AES in this context. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) PGP Operational Steps (7 marks):**

1. **Key Pair Generation (preliminary):** Alice and Bob each generate their own RSA/ECC key pair. Alice has $(K_{pub, A}, K_{priv, A})$; Bob has $(K_{pub, B}, K_{priv, B})$.

2. **Signing — Alice's Side:**
   - Alice composes plaintext message $M$.
   - Compute hash: $H_M = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ — produces a 256-bit digest.
   - Sign: $\sigma = \text{RSA-Sign}(K_{priv, A}, H_M)$ — encrypt the hash with Alice's private key.
   - The signature $\sigma$ is appended to the message.

3. **Encryption — Alice's Side:**
   - Generate a **random 256-bit symmetric session key** $K_s$.
   - Encrypt message: $C_1 = \text{AES-256-Encrypt}(K_s, M)$.
   - Encrypt session key: $C_2 = \text{RSA-Encrypt}(K_{pub, B}, K_s)$ — only Bob can recover $K_s$ using his private key.

4. **Transmission:** Alice sends the packet $\{C_1, C_2, \sigma\}$ over SMTP to Bob.

5. **Decryption & Verification — Bob's Side:**
   - $K_s = \text{RSA-Decrypt}(K_{priv, B}, C_2)$.
   - $M = \text{AES-256-Decrypt}(K_s, C_1)$.
   - $H_M' = \text{SHA-256}(M)$.
   - Verify: $\text{RSA-Verify}(K_{pub, A}, H_M', \sigma) = \text{true}$ (i.e., $\sigma$ decrypts to the same hash).
   - If verification passes, Bob has authenticated Alice and confirmed integrity.

**Block Diagram (worth 2-3 marks):**

```
 Alice                                          Bob
 ┌──────────────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────────┐
 │  M  ──►  SHA-256  ──► H  │            │                          │
 │              │            │            │                          │
 │              ▼            │            │                          │
 │  RSA-Sign(KprivA, H)  ──►σ ──────┐    │                          │
 │                                 │    │                          │
 │  M  ──►  AES-256(Ks, M)  ──► C1 │    │                          │
 │                                 │    │                          │
 │  Ks ──► RSA-Encrypt(KpubB, Ks)►C2│   │                          │
 │                                 │    │                          │
 │         C1, C2, σ              │    │                          │
 └─────────────────────────────────┼────┘                          │
                                   │                                │
                                   ▼                                │
                         ════════ SMTP ════════►                     │
                                                                    │
                          C2 ──► RSA-Decrypt(KprivB, C2) ──► Ks   │
                          C1 ──► AES-Decrypt(Ks, C1) ──────► M    │
                          M  ──► SHA-256 ──► H'                  │
                          σ  ──► RSA-Verify(KpubA, σ) ──► H     │
                          Compare H == H' ✓                       │
 └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**(b) Hybrid Cryptography — RSA and AES Relationship (7 marks):**

**Why hybrid?** RSA (asymmetric) is **~1000× slower** than AES (symmetric) and has a payload limit (~245 bytes for RSA-2048 with OAEP). AES can encrypt gigabytes efficiently. PGP uses **both**: AES for the bulk message, RSA to securely deliver the AES session key.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

Let $M$ be the plaintext message (size up to gigabytes). PGP performs:

$$
C_1 = E_{AES}(K_s, M), \quad C_2 = E_{RSA}(K_{pub, B}, K_s)
$$

where $K_s$ is a **one-time 256-bit random session key**. The receiver computes:

$$
K_s = D_{RSA}(K_{priv, B}, C_2), \quad M = D_{AES}(K_s, C_1)
$$

**Correctness verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{AES}(K_s, C_1) &= D_{AES}(K_s, E_{AES}(K_s, M)) = M \\
D_{RSA}(K_{priv, B}, C_2) &= D_{RSA}(K_{priv, B}, E_{RSA}(K_{pub, B}, K_s)) = K_s
\end{aligned}
$$

The **forward secrecy** of PGP (in OpenPGP v6) is enhanced by using **ephemeral** $K_s$ per message — even if Bob's long-term private key is later compromised, past messages remain secure (assuming $K_s$ is not recoverable from the ciphertext).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that **signing and encryption are independent operations** in PGP. The signature is over the *plaintext hash*, not the ciphertext. Also, do not confuse the public/private key — the sender signs with their **own private key** and encrypts with the **recipient's public key**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the working of DKIM with a neat diagram. How does DKIM differ from SPF and DMARC? (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the complete architecture of S/MIME. List the key cryptographic operations involved in sending and receiving an S/MIME-signed and encrypted message. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) DKIM Working (7 marks):**

**Step-by-step DKIM operation:**

1. **Domain Setup:** The sending organization (e.g., `example.com`) generates an RSA key pair and publishes the public key as a DNS TXT record at `selector1._domainkey.example.com`.

2. **Signing at Outbound MTA:**
   - The MTA canonicalizes the body and selected headers (relaxed mode by default).
   - Computes `bh = base64(SHA-256(canonicalized body))`.
   - Constructs the `DKIM-Signature:` header containing: version (`v=1`), signing algorithm (`a=rsa-sha256`), canonicalization (`c=relaxed/relaxed`), domain (`d=example.com`), selector (`s=selector1`), signed headers list (`h=from:to:subject:date`), body hash (`bh=`), and the actual signature (`b=`).

3. **Verification at Receiver:**
   - The receiver's MTA extracts `d=`, `s=`, and `b=` from the DKIM-Signature header.
   - Queries DNS: `TXT selector1._domainkey.example.com` → retrieves the public key.
   - Recomputes the body hash and signed-headers hash.
   - Verifies the signature using RSA public key. If valid → `dkim=pass`.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | SPF | DKIM | DMARC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What it verifies** | Envelope sender IP | Message content & headers signature | Alignment of SPF/DKIM with `From:` header |
| **Cryptography** | None (plaintext DNS) | RSA signature | None (policy framework) |
| **Granularity** | Per-MTA IP | Per-message signature | Per-domain policy |
| **What it prevents** | Direct domain spoofing | Header tampering, content modification | Header From spoofing, gives reporting |
| **Policy Action** | Hint only (`-all`, `~all`) | Hint only | **Enforced** (`p=reject/quarantine/none`) |
| **Reporting** | None | None | Aggregate (RUA) + Forensic (RUF) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students often say *"DKIM encrypts the email"* — this is **wrong**. DKIM only *signs* — it provides authentication and integrity, **not confidentiality**. Marks are routinely lost here.

**(b) S/MIME Architecture (7 marks):**

**S/MIME (Secure/MIME) v3.1+** (RFC 8551) layers end-to-end cryptographic protection on top of the standard MIME message format. It is built on **CMS (Cryptographic Message Syntax, RFC 5652)** which itself is derived from PKCS#7.

**Key Cryptographic Operations:**

**SENDING — Signed + Encrypted Message:**

1. **Hashing:** Compute $H = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ over the MIME entity.
2. **Signing:** Generate signature $\sigma = \text{RSA-Sign}(K_{priv, A}, H)$.
3. **ContentInfo Construction:** Package $M \parallel \sigma$ into a `SignedData` CMS structure (ASN.1 DER-encoded).
4. **MIME Wrapping:** The signed data is wrapped in a `multipart/signed` MIME part (for clear-signed) OR embedded in `application/pkcs7-mime; smime-type=signed-data` (for opaque-signed).
5. **Encryption (optional, for confidentiality):**
   - Generate random AES-256 session key $K_s$.
   - Encrypt: $C = \text{AES-256-GCM}(K_s, \text{SignedData})$.
   - Wrap: $C_2 = \text{RSA-OAEP-Encrypt}(K_{pub, B}, K_s)$.
   - Package as `EnvelopedData` CMS structure.
6. **MIME Wrapping:** Final MIME entity becomes `application/pkcs7-mime; smime-type=enveloped-data`.

**RECEIVING — Verification + Decryption:**

1. **Parse MIME** → extract CMS structure.
2. **Identify recipient** by `To:` or by extracting the Subject Key Identifier from the CMS.
3. **Decrypt session key** $K_s = \text{RSA-OAEP-Decrypt}(K_{priv, B}, C_2)$.
4. **Decrypt content** $S = \text{AES-256-GCM-Decrypt}(K_s, C)$.
5. **Extract signed data** and verify signature: $\text{RSA-Verify}(K_{pub, A}, H_{recomputed}, \sigma)$.
6. **Check certificate chain** against trusted CA store (including revocation via CRL/OCSP).

**CMS Content Types in S/MIME:**

| `smime-type` | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `signed-data` | Message + signature (opaque — body hidden) |
| `enveloped-data` | Encrypted message (confidentiality) |
| `compressed-data` | Compressed before encryption (efficiency) |

**Difference from PGP — Final Comparison:**

| | S/MIME | PGP |
|---|---|---|
| Certificate Format | **X.509** | Custom OpenPGP packets |
| Encoding | **ASN.1 DER** | Binary + ASCII armor |
| Integration | Native in **Outlook, Apple Mail, iOS, Android** | Requires **GPG plugin** in most clients |
| Key Revocation | **CRL / OCSP** | Manual fingerprint revocation |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Master Pitfall List

> [!WARNING]
> **1. "PGP encrypts with the recipient's private key"** — WRONG. RSA encryption always uses the **public key** of the recipient. Private keys are *never* transmitted and are *only* used for decryption or signing.
>
> **2. "DKIM/S/MIME/PGP provide confidentiality"** — Only partially true. **PGP and S/MIME** provide confidentiality *when used in encryption mode*. **DKIM provides NO confidentiality** — it only signs.
>
> **3. "S/MIME is the same as PGP"** — They use the same underlying primitives (RSA, AES, SHA-256) but differ in **trust model** (X.509 vs. Web of Trust), **encoding** (ASN.1 vs. binary), and **certificate format**.
>
> **4. Confusing sender and recipient keys** — In a 14-mark question, students mix up whose public/private key does what. Use a **clear labeled block diagram** to avoid this.
>
> **5. Forgetting hybrid reasoning** — When asked "why use both RSA and AES?", many students say "for security" instead of "RSA is too slow for bulk data and has a payload limit; AES handles bulk efficiently while RSA securely delivers the session key."
>
> **6. Treating SPF/DKIM/DMARC as the same thing** — SPF authenticates the **envelope sender IP**; DKIM authenticates the **message content**; DMARC **enforces a policy** based on alignment with the visible `From:` header. They are *complementary*, not interchangeable.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Securing E-mail (Module 4)**

### PGP (Pretty Good Privacy)
- ⭐ **Hybrid cryptosystem**: AES for data, RSA for key delivery.
- ⭐ **Web of Trust** trust model — decentralized.
- ⭐ Five services: **Authentication, Confidentiality, Compression, E-mail compatibility, Segmentation**.
- ⭐ Default ciphers: **RSA-2048+**, **AES-256**, **SHA-256**.
- ⭐ Signing uses sender's **private key**; encryption uses recipient's **public key**.
- ⭐ Standardized as **OpenPGP** (RFC 4880, RFC 9580).

### S/MIME
- ⭐ **X.509 PKI** trust model — centralized CA hierarchy.
- ⭐ Built on **CMS / PKCS#7** using **ASN.1 DER** encoding.
- ⭐ Native in **Outlook, Apple Mail, iOS, Android** — no plugin needed.
- ⭐ MIME types: `multipart/signed` and `application/pkcs7-mime`.
- ⭐ Standard: **RFC 8551** (S/MIME v3.2) and **RFC 5751**.

### DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)
- ⭐ **RFC 6376** — domain-level message signing.
- ⭐ Public key in **DNS TXT** at `selector._domainkey.domain.com`.
- ⭐ Signs **headers and body hash** (SHA-256, base64-encoded `bh=`).
- ⭐ Provides **integrity + authentication**, **NOT confidentiality**.
- ⭐ Canonicalization modes: `simple` and `relaxed`.

### SPF (Sender Policy Framework)
- ⭐ **RFC 7208** — DNS-based IP allowlist.
- ⭐ Published as `TXT` record at the **apex domain** (e.g., `example.com`).
- ⭐ Qualifiers: `+` (pass), `-` (fail), `~` (softfail), `?` (neutral).
- ⭐ Limits: max **10 DNS lookups**, max **255-char string**.

### DMARC
- ⭐ **RFC 7489** — unifies SPF + DKIM with enforced policy + reporting.
- ⭐ Published at `_dmarc.domain.com`.
- ⭐ Policies: `p=none` (monitor), `p=quarantine`, `p=reject`.
- ⭐ **Alignment** required: DKIM domain or SPF domain must match the `From:` domain (relaxed = organizational; strict = exact).
- ⭐ Reports: `rua` (aggregate XML) + `ruf` (forensic, deprecated in favor of TLS-encrypted reporting).

### Transport Security
- ⭐ **SMTPS** (port 465), **SMTP Submission with STARTTLS** (port 587), **POP3S** (995), **IMAPS** (993).
- ⭐ Opportunistic TLS vs. **DANE/MTA-STS** for enforced TLS.

### Threats & Mitigations
- ⭐ **Phishing** → DMARC + user training + anti-phishing gateway.
- ⭐ **Spam** → Bayesian filters + DNSBL + Greylisting + DMARC.
- ⭐ **BEC (Business Email Compromise)** → DMARC `p=reject` + DKIM + out-of-band payment verification.
- ⭐ **Malware** → Sandboxing + AV + attachment filtering (e.g., `.exe`, `.scr`, `.js`).

### KTU 2024 Module 4 — Top 5 Exam Tips
- ⭐ Always draw a **labeled block diagram** for any 7+ mark question on PGP/S/MIME.
- ⭐ Explicitly state the **order of operations**: hash → sign → encrypt.
- ⭐ For DKIM/SPF/DMARC questions, mention the **specific RFC number**.
- ⭐ For comparison questions, use a **comparison table** (the examiner awards marks for each correctly contrasted row).
- ⭐ Always end with the **practical outcome**: "PGP provides end-to-end confidentiality, while DMARC protects the domain from spoofing — they are complementary, not competing."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Securing TCP Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Securing TCP Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Securing TCP Connections** refers to the set of cryptographic protocols, mechanisms, and engineering practices layered on top of the standard Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) to provide **confidentiality**, **data integrity**, **authentication**, and **replay protection** for application data exchanged between two communicating endpoints. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the canonical implementation of this concept is the **Transport Layer Security (TLS)** protocol (and its predecessor, SSL), which operates directly above TCP in the protocol stack and transforms an otherwise plaintext byte-stream into an authenticated, encrypted tunnel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEOT602 – Module 4):**
> *Securing TCP Connections focuses on the SSL/TLS architecture, the TLS handshake protocol, the TLS Record protocol, cryptographic computations (HMAC, PRF, key derivation), and the practical engineering trade-offs between security strength and latency.*

A "secured TCP connection" is therefore a **TCP connection that has been augmented with a session-state machine** running TLS, where:
- **Plaintext TCP segments** are replaced with **TLS Records** carrying encrypted Application Data.
- The TCP **three-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)** is *still required* to establish the underlying transport, but it is **immediately followed by a TLS handshake** that negotiates cryptographic parameters and authenticates the endpoints before any application payload is exchanged.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Diplomatic Pouch" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine TCP alone as a **public postal service** — anyone along the route (a router, an ISP, a Wi-Fi sniffer) can read your letter, copy it, or even swap the pages. Now picture **TLS** as wrapping that letter in a **sealed diplomatic pouch** with three extra features:
>
> 1. **Confidentiality (the lock):** The letter is written in a code only the recipient knows how to decode — even if a postal worker opens the pouch, they see only gibberish.
> 2. **Integrity (the tamper-evident seal):** The pouch is stamped with a unique wax seal whose pattern is mathematically tied to the contents — if a forger tries to alter a single word, the seal breaks and the recipient knows instantly.
> 3. **Authentication (the notary's signature):** Before sealing, both parties exchange **government-issued cryptographic passports** signed by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA), proving "I am who I claim to be" before the conversation begins.
>
> The TCP handshake is the equivalent of *agreeing on a meeting at a café*; the TLS handshake is the equivalent of *exchanging verified identity cards and agreeing on a secret codebook* before whispering secrets.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **SSL (Secure Sockets Layer)** | The original (now deprecated) protocol developed by Netscape in 1994–1996 for securing TCP. | Frequently appears in exam questions comparing SSL vs. TLS. |
| **TLS (Transport Layer Security)** | The IETF-standardized successor to SSL, currently at **TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018)**. | Primary focus of the syllabus. |
| **MAC (Message Authentication Code)** | A short cryptographic tag, computed using a shared secret key, that proves both integrity and authenticity of a message. | Foundation of TLS record integrity. |
| **HMAC (Hashed MAC)** | A specific MAC construction using a cryptographic hash (e.g., SHA-256) and a secret key. | Required in TLS 1.2 cipher suites. |
| **PRF (Pseudo-Random Function)** | A deterministic function that expands a secret seed into a stream of pseudo-random bytes. | Used in TLS key derivation. |
| **Cipher Suite** | A named bundle of algorithms, e.g., `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256`. | TLS handshake negotiates exactly one. |
| **Session vs. Connection** | A TLS *session* is a long-lived security association; a TLS *connection* is one instantiation of it. | Common KTU short-answer topic. |
| **X.509 Certificate** | A digital identity document binding a public key to an identity (e.g., a domain name), signed by a CA. | The basis of server authentication. |

## 1.4 Why TCP Itself Is *Not* Secure

Plain TCP guarantees only reliable, ordered byte delivery. It provides **no confidentiality** (payload is plaintext), **no integrity** beyond a 16-bit checksum (which is easily forged), and **no peer authentication**. Common attacks against unauthenticated TCP include:

- **TCP Session Hijacking** — an attacker injects packets with a guessed/floated sequence number.
- **SYN Flooding / DoS** — half-opening many connections to exhaust resources.
- **Data Tampering** — modifying payloads mid-stream without detection.
- **Eavesdropping** — passive capture on any unencrypted hop.

> [!NOTE]
> TLS is **not** a replacement for TCP — it is an **application-layer shim** that sits between TCP and the user application (e.g., HTTPS = HTTP over TLS over TCP). TCP's reliability features (retransmission, flow control, congestion control) remain fully active underneath.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Stack — Where TLS Lives

In the standard TCP/IP model, TLS occupies a unique position historically called the **"between-layer"** or *application sub-layer*:

| OSI Layer | Protocol(s) | TLS's Relation |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| 7 — Application | HTTP, SMTP, IMAP | Application data passed **into** TLS |
| 6 — Presentation | **TLS Record Protocol** | **← TLS lives here** |
| 5 — Session | **TLS Handshake / Alert / ChangeCipherSpec** | **← TLS lives here** |
| 4 — Transport | TCP | TCP delivers TLS records |
| 3 — Network | IP | Unaware of TLS |
| 2 — Data Link | Ethernet / Wi-Fi | Unaware of TLS |
| 1 — Physical | Copper / Fiber / Radio | Unaware of TLS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> TLS itself is **sub-divided** into four sub-protocols: **Handshake**, **ChangeCipherSpec**, **Alert**, and **Record**. The Handshake, ChangeCipherSpec, and Alert protocols are themselves carried *inside* Record Protocol messages during the negotiation phase — a self-bootstrapping design.

## 2.2 TLS Operational Phases — The Big Picture

Every TLS connection moves through **four sequential phases**:

### Phase 1 — **TCP Establishment**
Standard three-way handshake: `SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`. No TLS data is exchanged yet.

### Phase 2 — **TLS Handshake (Negotiation & Authentication)**
- Client and server exchange `ClientHello` and `ServerHello`.
- Server presents its **X.509 certificate**.
- Key exchange occurs (RSA, Diffie-Hellman, or ECDHE).
- Both sides derive the **Master Secret** and a set of session keys.
- Both sides send `Finished` messages to verify the handshake integrity.

### Phase 3 — **Cipher Switch**
Both sides send `ChangeCipherSpec` to declare: *"All subsequent messages I send will be encrypted and authenticated with the negotiated algorithms."*

### Phase 4 — **Secure Data Transfer**
Application data (e.g., HTTP requests) is fragmented, compressed (optionally), MAC'd, encrypted, and sent as **TLS Records**. Each record is authenticated and integrity-protected.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Cryptographic Operations Inside TLS

### 2.3.1 Master Secret Derivation (TLS 1.2)

The **Master Secret (MS)** is the root key from which all session keys are derived. It is computed using the TLS **PRF** seeded with the **Pre-Master Secret (PMS)** and known labels:

$$
\text{Master\_Secret} = \text{PRF}\bigl(\text{PMS},\ \text{"master secret"},\ \text{ClientRandom} \Vert \text{ServerRandom}\bigr)
$$

Where:
- **PMS** = either an RSA-encrypted 48-byte random value, or the result of a Diffie-Hellman exchange.
- **ClientRandom** = 32-byte nonce sent in `ClientHello`.
- **ServerRandom** = 32-byte nonce sent in `ServerHello`.

### 2.3.2 Key Block Expansion

The Master Secret is then expanded into a **key block** of length sufficient for all required keys and IVs:

$$
\text{Key\_Block} = \text{PRF}\bigl(\text{MS},\ \text{"key expansion"},\ \text{ServerRandom} \Vert \text{ClientRandom}\bigr)
$$

From the key block, the following six values are extracted (in this fixed order for cipher suites using CBC + MAC):

$$
\text{client\_write\_MAC\_key} \Vert \text{server\_write\_MAC\_key} \Vert
$$
$$
\text{client\_write\_encryption\_key} \Vert \text{server\_write\_encryption\_key} \Vert
$$
$$
\text{client\_write\_IV} \Vert \text{server\_write\_IV}
$$

### 2.3.3 HMAC — The Integrity Engine

The integrity tag for each TLS record is computed using **HMAC**:

$$
\text{HMAC}(K, m) = H\!\Bigl(\bigl(K \oplus opad\bigr) \;\Vert\; H\!\bigl(\bigl(K \oplus ipad\bigr) \;\Vert\; m\bigr)\Bigr)
$$

Where:
- $H$ = the negotiated hash function (SHA-256, SHA-384, etc.)
- $K$ = the MAC key from the key block
- $m$ = the message (sequence number ‖ record-type ‖ protocol-version ‖ length ‖ fragment)
- $ipad$ = the byte `0x36` repeated to the hash block size
- $opad$ = the byte `0x5C` repeated to the hash block size
- $\oplus$ = bitwise XOR

### 2.3.4 Bulk Encryption (AES Example)

For cipher suite `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256`, encryption uses **AES-128 in CBC mode**:

$$
C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1} \oplus \text{IV}_{\text{next}})
$$

Decryption is correspondingly:

$$
P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1} \oplus \text{IV}_{\text{next}}
$$

For TLS 1.2, an **explicit random IV** is prepended to each record to prevent deterministic ciphertexts.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula you may be asked to reproduce** in a KTU exam on this topic. Memorize the symbols and the order of operations.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Marks |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Master Secret** | $MS = PRF(PMS,\ \text{"master secret"},\ CR \Vert SR)$ | 2 |
| 2 | **Key Block** | $KB = PRF(MS,\ \text{"key expansion"},\ SR \Vert CR)$ | 2 |
| 3 | **HMAC** | $HMAC(K,m) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert m))$ | 3 |
| 4 | **PRF (TLS 1.2)** | $PRF(secret, label, seed) = P\_\text{HMAC}(secret,\ label \Vert seed)$ | 2 |
| 5 | **CBC Encryption** | $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$ | 1 |
| 6 | **TLS Record MAC Input** | $m = seq\_num \Vert type \Vert version \Vert length \Vert fragment$ | 2 |
| 7 | **Finished MAC (TLS 1.2)** | $verify\_data = PRF(MS,\ \text{"client finished"},\ MD5(h.c.) \Vert SHA-1(h.c.))$ | 2 |
| 8 | **DHE Public Key** | $Y = g^x \bmod p$ (client), $Y = g^y \bmod p$ (server) | 1 |
| 9 | **DHE Shared Secret** | $Z = g^{xy} \bmod p$ | 1 |
| 10 | **RSA Pre-Master Encryption** | $PMS_{\text{enc}} = RSA\_Encrypt(PK_{server}, PMS)$ | 1 |
| 11 | **Record Sequence Number** | 64-bit counter, resets at `ChangeCipherSpec`; **never transmitted**. | 2 |
| 12 | **TLS Port** | HTTPS = **TCP 443**; SMTPS = 465; IMAPS = 993 | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember:**
> - Pre-Master Secret length = **48 bytes** (TLS 1.2).
> - Master Secret length = **48 bytes**.
> - Sequence number is incremented *before* each record and is the **first** input to the MAC.
> - `Finished` message is the **only** message that uses the `MD5 ‖ SHA-1` concatenation in TLS 1.2 PRF.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Securing TCP connections is the **backbone of all e-commerce, banking, and authenticated web traffic** today. Every time a user sees a 🔒 padlock in a browser, a TLS handshake has just occurred. Beyond HTTPS, the same machinery secures:
- **Email** (SMTPS, IMAPS, POP3S)
- **VPN** (OpenVPN, WireGuard)
- **Database connections** (TLS-enabled PostgreSQL, MySQL)
- **IoT/OT** (MQTT over TLS, CoAPs)
- **API communications** (gRPC, REST over HTTPS)

In a KTU placement interview, expect questions such as: *"Why does TLS use both asymmetric and symmetric cryptography?"* — the answer hinges on the **performance asymmetry**: RSA is ~1000× slower than AES, so asymmetric crypto is used only to **bootstrap** a shared symmetric key.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through every mathematical and protocol-level derivation you are likely to encounter in a KTU examination. **No step is skipped; every transition is explicit.**

## 3.1 Detailed TLS 1.2 Handshake — Line-by-Line Walkthrough

Below is the **canonical RSA-based TLS 1.2 handshake**, the version most commonly tested. We will annotate each message with what the sender is computing and what the receiver verifies.

### Step 0 — TCP Three-Way Handshake (Pre-conditions)

Before any TLS byte is exchanged, the transport layer must complete:

1. **Client → Server:** `SYN, seq=x`
2. **Server → Client:** `SYN-ACK, seq=y, ack=x+1`
3. **Client → Server:** `ACK, seq=x+1, ack=y+1`

After Step 3, the TCP connection is **OPEN**, and the TLS layer can begin.

### Step 1 — `ClientHello`

The client sends a `ClientHello` record carrying:

$$
\text{ClientHello} = \langle \text{client\_version},\ CR,\ S_ID,\ CS_{\text{list}},\ CM_{\text{list}} \rangle
$$

Where:
- $\text{client\_version}$ = highest TLS version supported (e.g., TLS 1.2 = `0x0303`).
- $CR$ = **ClientRandom**, a 32-byte fresh nonce.
- $S_ID$ = **Session ID** (used for session resumption).
- $CS_{\text{list}}$ = **list of acceptable cipher suites** (e.g., `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256`).
- $CM_{\text{list}}$ = list of acceptable compression methods (usually `null` only).

### Step 2 — `ServerHello`

The server replies with a single cipher suite choice and its own nonce:

$$
\text{ServerHello} = \langle \text{server\_version},\ SR,\ S_ID',\ CS_{\text{chosen}} \rangle
$$

Where $SR$ = **ServerRandom**, also a 32-byte fresh nonce.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The reason both random nonces are included is to ensure that even if the **Pre-Master Secret is ever leaked later**, the resulting keys will differ in every session — providing **forward secrecy only when paired with (EC)DHE**.

### Step 3 — `Certificate`

The server sends its **X.509 certificate chain**:

$$
\text{Certificate} = \langle \text{server\_cert},\ \text{intermediate\_CA\_certs} \rangle
$$

The client **verifies** the chain by:
1. Checking the signature of each cert with the public key of the issuer.
2. Validating that no cert in the chain is expired or revoked (CRL/OCSP).
3. Confirming that the **Subject Alternative Name (SAN)** or **Common Name (CN)** matches the hostname being contacted.

### Step 4 — `ServerHelloDone`

A trivial 0-byte message indicating the server has no further handshake parameters to send.

### Step 5 — Client Generates the Pre-Master Secret

The client generates a fresh 48-byte random number:

$$
PMS \xleftarrow{R} \{0,1\}^{48}
$$

### Step 6 — `ClientKeyExchange`

For an RSA cipher suite, the client encrypts the PMS with the server's public key:

$$
PMS_{\text{enc}} = \text{RSA\_Encrypt}\bigl(PK_{\text{server}},\ PMS\bigr)
$$

Only the server, who holds the matching private key, can recover the PMS.

### Step 7 — Client Computes the Master Secret

Both sides independently compute:

$$
MS = \text{PRF}\bigl(PMS,\ \text{"master secret"},\ CR \Vert SR\bigr)
$$

The `PRF` (in TLS 1.2) is defined as:

$$
PRF(\text{secret},\ \text{label},\ \text{seed}) = P_{\text{HMAC}}(\text{secret},\ \text{label} \Vert \text{seed})
$$

Where $P_{\text{HMAC}}$ is an expansion function based on HMAC-SHA-256 (or SHA-384 for stronger suites). The expansion iterates HMAC with an incrementing counter until enough bytes are produced.

### Step 8 — Client Computes the Key Block

$$
KB = PRF\bigl(MS,\ \text{"key expansion"},\ SR \Vert CR\bigr)
$$

Both sides parse $KB$ into the six cryptographic parameters:

$$
KB = \underbrace{K_{c\text{MAC}}}_{16} \Vert \underbrace{K_{s\text{MAC}}}_{16} \Vert \underbrace{K_{c\text{enc}}}_{16} \Vert \underbrace{K_{s\text{enc}}}_{16} \Vert \underbrace{IV_c}_{16} \Vert \underbrace{IV_s}_{16}
$$

(Lengths vary with the cipher suite; the example uses AES-128 + HMAC-SHA-256.)

### Step 9 — `ChangeCipherSpec` (Client → Server)

A 1-byte message that **activates** the pending write state for the client. From this point, all client messages are encrypted and authenticated.

### Step 10 — `Finished` (Client → Server)

The `Finished` message is **the first message protected with the newly negotiated algorithms**. It contains a **verify_data** value:

$$
\text{verify\_data}_{\text{client}} = PRF\bigl(MS,\ \text{"client finished"},\ H_{\text{all\_handshake}}\bigr)
$$

Where $H_{\text{all\_handshake}}$ is the **MD5 and SHA-1 hash of every handshake message exchanged so far**:

$$
H_{\text{all}} = \text{MD5}(\text{all handshake}) \Vert \text{SHA-1}(\text{all handshake})
$$

This message **proves the client possesses the correct Master Secret** and that none of the handshake messages were tampered with.

### Step 11 — Server Re-computes and Verifies

The server independently recomputes $MS$, $KB$, and the expected `verify_data`. If its recomputation matches the received value, the server sends its own `ChangeCipherSpec` and `Finished`.

### Step 12 — Secure Application Data Exchange

Application data now flows as **TLS Records**:

$$
\text{TLS Record} = \langle \text{type},\ \text{version},\ \text{length},\ \text{fragment} \rangle
$$

Where the fragment = `[explicit IV] ‖ ciphertext ‖ MAC`. The MAC is computed over:

$$
m = \text{seq\_num}_{64} \Vert \text{type}_8 \Vert \text{version}_{16} \Vert \text{length}_{16} \Vert \text{plaintext}_*
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — HMAC Computation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A TLS record uses HMAC-SHA-1 (block size = **64 bytes**, output = **20 bytes**) with key $K$ = `"KTU"` (4 bytes). The plaintext message is `"EXAM"` (4 bytes). Compute `HMAC(K, m)` and show every step.

> [!NOTE]
> Hash block size for SHA-1 = **64 bytes**.
> $ipad = 0x36$ repeated 64 times.
> $opad = 0x5C$ repeated 64 times.

**Step 1 — Pad the key to the block size.**
$K$ is 4 bytes, block is 64 bytes → pad with **60 zero bytes**:

$$
K_{\text{pad}} = K \Vert 0x00^{60}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inner XOR.**

$$
K_{\text{ipad}} = K_{\text{pad}} \oplus \text{ipad}_{64}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inner hash.**

$$
H_{\text{inner}} = \text{SHA-1}\bigl(K_{\text{ipad}} \Vert m\bigr) = \text{SHA-1}\bigl(K_{\text{ipad}} \Vert \text{"EXAM"}\bigr)
$$

Let us denote this 20-byte output symbolically as $H_{\text{inner}}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the outer XOR.**

$$
K_{\text{opad}} = K_{\text{pad}} \oplus \text{opad}_{64}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the outer hash.**

$$
\text{HMAC}(K, m) = \text{SHA-1}\bigl(K_{\text{opad}} \Vert H_{\text{inner}}\bigr)
$$

This produces the final **20-byte authentication tag** that is appended to the ciphertext in the TLS record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two hashes?** The nested structure guarantees that even if an attacker finds a collision in the inner hash, they cannot forge a valid HMAC without the secret key $K$, because $K_{\text{opad}}$ is also unknown to them.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SSL vs. TLS vs. Plain TCP

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU exams. Use this template when answering.

| Property | Plain TCP | SSL 3.0 | TLS 1.2 | TLS 1.3 |
|----------|-----------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Year** | 1974 (RFC 675) | 1996 | 2008 (RFC 5246) | 2018 (RFC 8446) |
| **Authentication** | ❌ None | ✅ One-way (server) | ✅ One-way / mutual (optional) | ✅ Mutual recommended |
| **Cipher Negotiation** | ❌ N/A | ⚠️ Weak | ✅ Strong | ✅ Mandatory strong |
| **PRF Algorithm** | N/A | MD5 + SHA-1 combo | HMAC-SHA-256/384 | HKDF (HMAC-based Extract-and-Expand KDF) |
| **Master Secret Derivation** | N/A | MD5 + SHA-1 combo | Single PRF call | HKDF-Extract + HKDF-Expand |
| **Cipher Suites Allowed** | N/A | RC4, DES, 3DES, AES | AES, ChaCha20 (no RC4/DES) | AEAD only (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) |
| **Forward Secrecy** | ❌ | ⚠️ Optional | ⚠️ Optional (ECDHE preferred) | ✅ Mandatory |
| **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | 1 RTT | 2 RTT | 2 RTT | **1 RTT** (0-RTT optional) |
| **Certificate Encryption** | N/A | Optional | Optional (only PMS) | Server cert always encrypted |
| **Compression** | N/A | Allowed | Allowed (CRIME attack risk) | **Removed** |
| **Status** | Production | **Deprecated** | Allowed | **Current standard** |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | Background | Mentioned in comparisons | **Primary focus** | Mentioned as modern best practice |

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification — Why AES Beats RSA for Bulk Data

A KTU favourite is asking why TLS uses **hybrid cryptography**. The justification is a numerical comparison:

For a typical 1 MB HTTP response:

- **RSA-2048** decryption: ~$5 \times 10^{-3}$ seconds per operation.
- **AES-128-CBC**: ~$5 \times 10^{-9}$ seconds per byte → ~$5 \times 10^{-3}$ seconds per MB.

So RSA and AES take **similar total times** for 1 MB — but RSA scales **catastrophically**: at 10 MB it would take 50 ms versus ~50 ms for AES, and at 1 GB RSA would take **5 seconds** versus ~5 ms for AES. RSA's per-operation overhead is fixed regardless of data size; AES's overhead grows linearly with much smaller slope.

**Conclusion:** RSA's role in TLS is strictly **bootstrap** — it is used once at the start to deliver the symmetric key, after which AES does the bulk lifting.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Complete TLS 1.2 Handshake (RSA Variant)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client Browser
    participant S as Web Server
    participant CA as Certificate Authority

    Note over C,S: PHASE 0 - TCP Three-Way Handshake
    C->>S: SYN (seq=x)
    S->>C: SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
    C->>S: ACK (ack=y+1)
    Note over C,S: TCP connection OPEN

    Note over C,S: PHASE 1 - TLS Handshake Negotiation
    C->>S: ClientHello (version, ClientRandom, CipherSuites, SessionID)
    S->>C: ServerHello (version, ServerRandom, chosen CipherSuite)
    S->>C: Certificate (X.509 chain signed by CA)
    CA-->>S: (out-of-band root signing)
    S->>C: ServerHelloDone

    Note over C,S: PHASE 2 - Key Exchange and Authentication
    C->>C: Generate Pre-Master Secret (PMS) - 48 random bytes
    C->>S: ClientKeyExchange (RSA-encrypted PMS using server's public key)
    C->>C: Compute Master Secret = PRF(PMS, "master secret", CR || SR)
    C->>C: Derive Key Block = PRF(MS, "key expansion", SR || CR)
    C->>C: Extract MAC keys, encryption keys, IVs

    Note over C,S: PHASE 3 - Cipher Switch
    C->>S: ChangeCipherSpec (activate pending write state)
    C->>S: Finished (verify_data = PRF(MS, "client finished", hash of all handshake))
    S->>S: Verify verify_data matches expectation
    S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec (activate pending write state)
    S->>C: Finished (verify_data = PRF(MS, "server finished", hash of all handshake))
    C->>C: Verify server Finished

    Note over C,S: PHASE 4 - Secure Application Data Transfer
    C->>S: Application Data (HTTP Request) as TLS Record - encrypted + MAC
    S->>C: Application Data (HTTP Response) as TLS Record - encrypted + MAC
    S-->>C: Alert (close_notify) when terminating
    Note over C,S: TCP FIN handshake closes transport
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — TLS Protocol Stack and Record Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph APP_LAYER [Application Layer]
        HTTP[HTTP Request or SMTP or IMAP payload]
    end

    subgraph TLS_LAYER [TLS Protocol Suite]
        direction TB
        HS[Handshake Protocol]
        CCS[Change Cipher Spec Protocol]
        ALT[Alert Protocol]
        REC[Record Protocol - fragmentation, compression, MAC, encryption]
    end

    subgraph CRYPTO [Cryptographic Engine]
        direction LR
        HMAC_BOX[HMAC SHA 256]
        AES_BOX[AES 128 CBC or AES 256 GCM]
        PRF_BOX[PRF - HMAC based expansion]
        RSA_BOX[RSA Public Key of Server]
    end

    subgraph TCP_LAYER [TCP Transport Layer]
        TCP[TCP Segment with seq ack flow control]
    end

    subgraph NET_LAYER [Network Layer]
        IP[IP Packet routing]
    end

    HTTP -->|plaintext fragment| REC
    HS -->|negotiated parameters| REC
    CCS -->|signal cipher activation| REC
    ALT -->|warnings and fatal errors| REC

    REC -->|compute tag| HMAC_BOX
    REC -->|encrypt plaintext| AES_BOX
    HS -->|derive Master Secret| PRF_BOX
    HS -->|protect PMS| RSA_BOX

    REC -->|TLS Record segment| TCP
    TCP -->|TCP segment| IP
    IP -->|IP packet| NET_DST[Destination Host]

    style REC fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PRF_BOX fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style AES_BOX fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style HMAC_BOX fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style RSA_BOX fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A "Locked Pouch" Data Path

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** This block diagram maps the **data path** of a single application message as it travels through TLS and emerges at the destination. Each block represents a transformation; arrows show data direction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP[Application Data<br/>e.g. HTTP GET] --> FRAG[Fragmentation<br/>into 16 KB blocks]
    FRAG --> COMP[Optional Compression<br/>null in TLS 1.3]
    COMP --> MAC[Append HMAC Tag<br/>using write MAC key]
    MAC --> PAD[Padding and IV prepend<br/>for CBC mode]
    PAD --> ENC[AES Encryption<br/>using write encryption key]
    ENC --> REC_HDR[Add TLS Record Header<br/>type version length]
    REC_HDR --> TCP_TX[Transmit as TCP payload]

    TCP_TX -.stream.-> TCP_RX[Receive from TCP]
    TCP_RX --> REC_PARSE[Parse TLS Record Header]
    REC_PARSE --> DEC[AES Decryption]
    DEC --> UNPAD[Strip padding and IV]
    UNPAD --> VERIFY[Verify HMAC Tag<br/>compare with computed]
    VERIFY -->|valid| DECOMP[Optional Decompression]
    VERIFY -->|invalid| ALERT_FATAL[Send fatal alert<br/>bad_record_mac]
    DECOMP --> REASS[Reassemble fragments]
    REASS --> APP_DEST[Deliver to Application]

    style ENC fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style DEC fill:#B6E0FF,stroke:#06C,stroke-width:2px
    style MAC fill:#D5A6FF,stroke:#609,stroke-width:2px
    style VERIFY fill:#D5A6FF,stroke:#609,stroke-width:2px
    style ALERT_FATAL fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#C00,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.4 Attack-Resilience Map — How TLS Defeats Each TCP Attack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ATTACKS[TCP Layer Attacks] --> A1[Eavesdropping<br/>passive sniff]
    ATTACKS --> A2[Tampering<br/>modify payload mid stream]
    ATTACKS --> A3[Session Hijacking<br/>inject with guessed seq]
    ATTACKS --> A4[Impersonation<br/>fake server]

    A1 -->|defeated by| D1[Symmetric Encryption<br/>AES 128 or 256]
    A2 -->|defeated by| D2[HMAC Integrity Tag]
    A3 -->|defeated by| D3[Sequence Number in MAC<br/>and 64 bit replay defense]
    A4 -->|defeated by| D4[X.509 Certificate<br/>and CA chain validation]

    D1 --> RES[Secure TCP Connection]
    D2 --> RES
    D3 --> RES
    D4 --> RES

    style A1 fill:#FFB6B6
    style A2 fill:#FFB6B6
    style A3 fill:#FFB6B6
    style A4 fill:#FFB6B6
    style D1 fill:#B5EAD7
    style D2 fill:#B5EAD7
    style D3 fill:#B5EAD7
    style D4 fill:#B5EAD7
    style RES fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Explain the role of the TLS Handshake Protocol in securing a TCP connection. Why is it necessary even though TCP already provides reliable transport?*

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark depth):**

The TCP transport guarantees only **reliable, ordered byte delivery** — it has no notion of *who* is at the other end, *whether* the data was seen by anyone else, or *whether* the data was altered. The TLS Handshake Protocol fills these gaps by performing three security functions before any application data is exchanged:

1. **Authentication** — The server (and optionally the client) presents an X.509 certificate, allowing the peer to verify the endpoint's identity via a trusted Certificate Authority chain.
2. **Key Negotiation** — Both sides exchange nonces and use either RSA encryption or (EC)Diffie-Hellman to agree on a shared **Pre-Master Secret**, from which the **Master Secret** and session keys are derived using the PRF.
3. **Algorithm Agreement** — Both sides negotiate a **cipher suite** (e.g., `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`) defining the encryption algorithm, MAC algorithm, and key-exchange method.

Without the TLS handshake, TCP alone is vulnerable to eavesdropping, tampering, and impersonation. Hence the handshake is **mandatory** to transform an unreliable-in-security TCP byte stream into a **confidentiality-, integrity-, and authenticity-protected** channel.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*What is an HMAC? Write the expression for HMAC and explain each term.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark depth):**

**HMAC (Hashed Message Authentication Code)** is a cryptographic construction that uses a secret key in combination with a cryptographic hash function to produce a fixed-size tag authenticating a message.

$$
\text{HMAC}(K, m) = H\!\Bigl(\bigl(K \oplus opad\bigr) \;\Vert\; H\!\bigl(\bigl(K \oplus ipad\bigr) \;\Vert\; m\bigr)\Bigr)
$$

Explanation of terms:
- $K$ — the **secret key** shared between sender and receiver; padded with zeros if shorter than the hash block size $B$.
- $m$ — the **message** to be authenticated (e.g., the TLS record plaintext).
- $H$ — the **cryptographic hash function** (SHA-256 in modern TLS).
- $ipad$ — inner pad: the byte `0x36` repeated $B$ times.
- $opad$ — outer pad: the byte `0x5C` repeated $B$ times.
- $\oplus$ — bitwise XOR.
- $\Vert$ — concatenation.

HMAC provides both **data integrity** (any change in $m$ changes the tag) and **authenticity** (only a holder of $K$ can compute a valid tag).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions provide an **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PCEOT602 Module 4]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*With a neat diagram, explain the TLS handshake protocol in detail. List the messages exchanged between the client and server and explain how the Master Secret is derived.*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Explain the TLS Record Protocol. How does it provide confidentiality and integrity? Write the expression for HMAC and show how the MAC is computed for a TLS record.*

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — TLS Handshake Protocol (7 Marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):** Use the sequence diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes. It carries full marks for the diagram portion.

**Message sequence (2 marks):**

1. **ClientHello** — Client sends TLS version, `ClientRandom` (32-byte nonce), session ID, and supported cipher suites.
2. **ServerHello** — Server replies with chosen TLS version, `ServerRandom`, session ID, and chosen cipher suite.
3. **Certificate** — Server sends its X.509 certificate chain.
4. **ServerHelloDone** — Server signals end of its hello parameters.
5. **ClientKeyExchange** — Client generates a 48-byte Pre-Master Secret (PMS), encrypts it with the server's RSA public key, and sends the ciphertext.
6. **ChangeCipherSpec (Client → Server)** — Client activates its pending write state.
7. **Finished (Client → Server)** — Client sends `verify_data` = `PRF(MS, "client finished", MD5(handshake) ‖ SHA-1(handshake))`. This is the **first encrypted message**.
8. **ChangeCipherSpec (Server → Client)** — Server activates its pending write state.
9. **Finished (Server → Client)** — Server sends its own `verify_data`. Upon successful verification, the handshake is complete.

**Master Secret derivation (2 marks):**

After the client sends the RSA-encrypted PMS and both sides have exchanged nonces, both endpoints independently compute:

$$
MS = PRF\bigl(PMS,\ \text{"master secret"},\ CR \Vert SR\bigr)
$$

The PRF in TLS 1.2 expands its output using HMAC-SHA-256 with incrementing counters until 48 bytes are produced. The MS is then expanded into a key block:

$$
KB = PRF\bigl(MS,\ \text{"key expansion"},\ SR \Vert CR\bigr)
$$

Six cryptographic parameters (client/server write MAC keys, encryption keys, and IVs) are parsed from $KB$.

#### Part (b) — TLS Record Protocol (7 Marks)

**Purpose (1 mark):** The TLS Record Protocol is the **lowest layer** of TLS. It takes application data from the upper layer (HTTP, SMTP, etc.), fragments it, optionally compresses it, appends a MAC for integrity, encrypts it for confidentiality, and transmits the result as a normal TCP segment.

**Operations (2 marks):** For each record, the sender performs:
1. **Fragmentation** — split the message into blocks ≤ $2^{14}$ bytes (16 KB).
2. **Compression** — apply the negotiated compression (or `null`).
3. **MAC** — compute the authentication tag.
4. **Padding** — add PKCS#7-style padding for CBC mode.
5. **Encryption** — encrypt with AES, 3DES, or ChaCha20.

**Confidentiality & Integrity (2 marks):**
- **Confidentiality** is provided by the symmetric encryption (e.g., AES-128-CBC or AES-256-GCM) using the negotiated `client_write_encryption_key` / `server_write_encryption_key`.
- **Integrity** is provided by the MAC computed using the negotiated `client_write_MAC_key` / `server_write_MAC_key`. The MAC input includes the **64-bit sequence number** to prevent replay and reordering.

**HMAC expression (1 mark):**

$$
\text{HMAC}(K, m) = H\!\Bigl(\bigl(K \oplus opad\bigr) \;\Vert\; H\!\bigl(\bigl(K \oplus ipad\bigr) \;\Vert\; m\bigr)\Bigr)
$$

**MAC computation for a record (1 mark):**

$$
\text{MAC}_{\text{record}} = \text{HMAC}\bigl(K_{\text{write}},\ seq\_num_{64} \Vert type_8 \Vert version_{16} \Vert length_{16} \Vert plaintext_*\bigr)
$$

Where `seq_num` is the implicit 64-bit counter that is incremented for every record (and never transmitted on the wire).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PCEOT602 Module 4 (Alternative)]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Compare SSL and TLS protocols in detail. Explain why TLS 1.3 is considered more secure than TLS 1.2.*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Explain the various attacks possible on a plain TCP connection. Describe how TLS mitigates each of these attacks.*

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — SSL vs. TLS (7 Marks)

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | SSL 3.0 | TLS 1.0 | TLS 1.2 | TLS 1.3 |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| Standardization | Netscape proprietary | IETF RFC 2246 | IETF RFC 5246 | IETF RFC 8446 |
| PRF | MD5 + SHA-1 combined | MD5 + SHA-1 combined | Single hash (SHA-256/384) | HKDF |
| Cipher suite negotiation | Weak | Weak | Strong | **Mandatory strong** |
| Allowed ciphers | RC4, DES, 3DES | RC4, 3DES, AES | AES, ChaCha20 | **AEAD only** (GCM, Poly1305) |
| Authentication | Server only | Server + optional client | Server + optional client | Mutual recommended |
| Forward secrecy | Optional | Optional | Optional (ECDHE preferred) | **Mandatory** |
| Handshake RTT | 2 | 2 | 2 | **1 (0-RTT optional)** |
| Compression | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed | **Removed** |

**Why TLS 1.3 is more secure (3 marks):**

1. **Mandatory Forward Secrecy** — TLS 1.3 drops all static RSA and DH key exchanges; only (EC)DHE is allowed, ensuring each session has unique ephemeral keys.
2. **AEAD-only ciphers** — AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 provide both confidentiality and integrity in a single construction, eliminating padding-oracle attacks.
3. **Reduced attack surface** — Removed legacy features like compression (CRIME attack), renegotiation, and custom DH groups.
4. **Encrypted handshake** — In TLS 1.3, even the server's certificate is encrypted, hiding identity from passive eavesdroppers.
5. **Faster, cleaner handshake** — 1-RTT (or 0-RTT) reduces exposure to DoS and reduces the window of negotiation attacks.

#### Part (b) — Attacks on Plain TCP & TLS Mitigations (7 Marks)

**Attack 1 — Eavesdropping (1 mark):**
- *Description:* Passive attacker on the same network reads unencrypted TCP segments containing sensitive data.
- *TLS Mitigation:* **Encryption** with AES-128/256 (or ChaCha20) using session keys derived from the negotiated Pre-Master Secret.

**Attack 2 — Data Tampering (1 mark):**
- *Description:* Active man-in-the-middle modifies payload mid-stream and recomputes the TCP checksum.
- *TLS Mitigation:* **HMAC integrity tag** — any modification causes the receiver's recomputed HMAC to mismatch, triggering a `bad_record_mac` fatal alert.

**Attack 3 — TCP Session Hijacking (2 marks):**
- *Description:* Attacker guesses or observes sequence numbers and injects forged packets into the established connection, taking over the session.
- *TLS Mitigation:* Even if sequence numbers are guessed, the attacker cannot forge valid HMAC tags without the session keys, nor can they authenticate as the legitimate peer because the certificate chain binds the identity to a trusted CA. Additionally, TLS keys are re-derived per session.

**Attack 4 — Server Impersonation / MITM (2 marks):**
- *Description:* Attacker poses as the legitimate server and establishes a TCP connection with the client.
- *TLS Mitigation:* **X.509 certificate validation** — the client checks the certificate chain against trusted root CAs, validates the hostname in the SAN/CN field, and checks revocation status (CRL/OCSP). A self-signed or mismatched certificate produces a browser warning.

**Attack 5 — Replay Attack (1 mark):**
- *Description:* Attacker captures an encrypted record and replays it later.
- *TLS Mitigation:* The implicit **64-bit sequence number** is included in the HMAC input; replays are detected because the receiver's sequence counter has advanced.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students frequently **omit the sequence number** from the HMAC input — this loses 2 marks and shows incomplete understanding of replay protection.
> - Many answers confuse **MAC** with **digital signatures** — MACs use **symmetric** keys, signatures use **asymmetric** keys.
> - Students often forget that TLS **does not protect the TCP headers** — IP-level attacks (e.g., replay at the IP layer) require IPsec, not TLS.
> - On the question "Why not use RSA for everything?", students often say "RSA is slow" without giving a numerical comparison — always cite a concrete figure (e.g., RSA is ~1000× slower than AES per byte).
> - **Diagrams carry marks.** A labeled handshake sequence diagram can earn 3–4 marks by itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] TCP alone is **not secure** — TLS is layered on top to add confidentiality, integrity, and authentication.
- [ ] TLS consists of **four sub-protocols**: Handshake, ChangeCipherSpec, Alert, Record.
- [ ] The **TLS Record Protocol** is the carrier; the **Handshake** negotiates parameters; **ChangeCipherSpec** activates them; **Alert** handles errors.
- [ ] TLS uses **hybrid cryptography**: asymmetric (RSA / ECDHE) for key exchange + symmetric (AES / ChaCha20) for bulk encryption.

### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize
- [ ] Pre-Master Secret length = **48 bytes**.
- [ ] Master Secret length = **48 bytes**.
- [ ] `ClientRandom` and `ServerRandom` length = **32 bytes each**.
- [ ] Max TLS record payload = **$2^{14}$ = 16,384 bytes**.
- [ ] Sequence number = **64-bit**, never transmitted.
- [ ] HTTPS default port = **TCP 443**.

### ✅ Key Formulas
- [ ] $MS = PRF(PMS,\ \text{"master secret"},\ CR \Vert SR)$
- [ ] $KB = PRF(MS,\ \text{"key expansion"},\ SR \Vert CR)$
- [ ] $HMAC(K,m) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert m))$
- [ ] MAC input: $seq\_num_{64} \Vert type_8 \Vert version_{16} \Vert length_{16} \Vert plaintext$
- [ ] DHE shared: $Z = g^{xy} \bmod p$

### ✅ Critical Distinctions
- [ ] **MAC** (symmetric, fast) ≠ **Digital Signature** (asymmetric, slow).
- [ ] **TLS Record** ≠ **TCP Segment** — TLS records are the *payload* of TCP segments.
- [ ] **Session** (long-lived) ≠ **Connection** (one instantiation).
- [ ] **Forward secrecy** requires (EC)DHE — RSA key exchange alone does not provide it.
- [ ] **TLS 1.3** mandates forward secrecy, AEAD only, 1-RTT, no compression.

### ✅ Attack → Defense Map
- [ ] Eavesdropping → **Encryption** (AES / ChaCha20)
- [ ] Tampering → **HMAC integrity tag**
- [ ] Hijacking → **Session keys + certificate authentication**
- [ ] Impersonation → **X.509 certificate + CA chain validation**
- [ ] Replay → **Sequence number in MAC**

### ✅ Exam-Style Mnemonic — **"CHARM-S"**
- **C**ertificate exchange
- **H**ello messages (ClientHello / ServerHello)
- **A**uthentication via X.509
- **R**ecord Protocol (encryption + MAC)
- **M**aster Secret derivation
- **S**ymmetric + Asymmetric hybrid cryptography

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IPSec
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IPSec

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **IPSec (Internet Protocol Security)** is a suite of protocols standardized by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** under **RFC 4301–RFC 4309** that operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and anti-replay protection** for IP datagrams. It secures communications over unprotected networks such as the public Internet by authenticating and encrypting each IP packet of a communication session.

IPSec is a **framework**, not a single protocol. It defines the architecture, key management, and the actual security protocols. The two primary security protocols in the IPSec suite are:

1. **Authentication Header (AH)** — Provides integrity, authentication, and anti-replay, but **no confidentiality (no encryption)**.
2. **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** — Provides integrity, authentication, anti-replay, and **confidentiality (encryption)**.

These protocols operate in conjunction with supporting mechanisms:
- **Security Association (SA)** — A logical, simplex connection between two peers.
- **Security Association Database (SAD)** — Stores the active SAs.
- **Security Policy Database (SPD)** — Stores the security policies.
- **Internet Key Exchange (IKE / IKEv2)** — Automates the negotiation and management of keys and SAs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy — "The Armored Diplomatic Pouch"**
> Imagine two embassies (Host A and Host B) needing to send sensitive letters across a hostile border (the Internet). An IPSec connection works like an **armored diplomatic pouch**:
> - **The Pouch (ESP/AH)** wraps every letter with a tamper-proof seal and a heavy lock, ensuring nobody can read, modify, or duplicate the message in transit.
> - **The Courier's Handshake (IKE)** is the initial meeting where the two embassies exchange secret codes, agree on which lock type to use, and confirm each other's identity before the first letter is ever sealed.
> - **The Courier's Rulebook (SPD)** dictates *which* letters must be armored, *which* can travel normally, and *which* must be rejected outright.
> - **The Courier's Memory (SAD)** records the exact lock configuration currently in use for the active conversation.

Without IPSec, your IP packets are like **postcards** — anyone along the route can read them, alter them, or copy them. With IPSec, every packet becomes a **sealed, signed, encrypted capsule**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE] **Key IPSec Parameters to Memorize**
> - **IP Protocol Number for AH:** **51**
> - **IP Protocol Number for ESP:** **50**
> - **Default IP Protocol Number for IKE (UDP):** **500**
> - **IPSec Header Insertion Point:** Between the IP header and the Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) payload
> - **Anti-Replay Window Size:** Typically **32 or 64 bits** (sequence number counter)
> - **Recommended Hash for HMAC:** **SHA-256 / SHA-384 / SHA-512** (MD5 and SHA-1 are deprecated)
> - **Recommended Encryption:** **AES-128 / AES-256** (DES and 3DES are deprecated)
> - **Diffie-Hellman Groups:** Group **14 (2048-bit)** or higher (Group 1, 2 deprecated)

## 1.4 Why IPSec? (The KTU Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why does KTU emphasize IPSec?**
> 1. **Layer 3 Operation:** Secures ALL applications without modifying them (unlike TLS which is application-layer).
> 2. **End-to-End Security:** Can protect traffic between two hosts (host-to-host), two gateways (gateway-to-gateway), or a host and gateway.
> 3. **Mandatory in IPv6:** IPSec is a **mandatory component** of the IPv6 protocol suite.
> 4. **Foundation for VPNs:** IPSec is the backbone of nearly all site-to-site and remote-access Virtual Private Networks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IPSec Architecture — The Three Pillars

The IPSec framework, as defined in **RFC 4301**, is built on three architectural pillars:

### Pillar 1: Security Protocols (AH & ESP)
These are the actual algorithms applied to each packet.

- **AH (Protocol 51):** Protects the **entire IP packet** (header + payload) using an Integrity Check Value (ICV). It does **NOT encrypt** the payload.
- **ESP (Protocol 50):** Protects the **payload** (and parts of the ESP header/trailer) using encryption + ICV. The original IP header is **not** protected by ESP's authentication.

### Pillar 2: Security Association (SA)
> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition of SA**
> A **Security Association (SA)** is a **simplex (one-way) logical connection** between two IPSec peers that defines the security parameters (algorithms, keys, lifetimes, modes) to be used for protecting traffic. Since it is simplex, **two SAs are required for bidirectional communication** — one for each direction.

An SA is uniquely identified by a triple known as the **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** tuple:

$$ \text{SA Identifier} = \langle \text{Destination IP},\ \text{IPSec Protocol (AH/ESP)},\ \text{SPI} \rangle $$

Where **SPI** is a **32-bit arbitrary value** chosen by the receiving end to identify the SA.

### Pillar 3: Key Management (IKE / IKEv2)
**Internet Key Exchange (IKE)** is the protocol that automates:
- Peer authentication (via pre-shared keys, certificates, or EAP)
- Key generation and exchange (via **Diffie-Hellman** algorithm)
- SA negotiation and establishment
- Periodic key refresh (anti-replay & forward secrecy)

## 2.2 IPSec Modes of Operation

| Mode | Scope | Use Case | Protection Coverage |
|------|-------|----------|---------------------|
| **Transport Mode** | Host-to-Host | End-to-end security between two end systems | Protects payload of original IP packet; original IP header is preserved |
| **Tunnel Mode** | Gateway-to-Gateway or Host-to-Gateway | Site-to-site VPN, Remote-access VPN | Protects the **entire original IP packet** by encapsulating it in a new IP packet |

> [!NOTE] **Quick Recall Trick**
> - **Transport Mode** = "**T**he original **T**op is **T**here" (original IP header remains visible)
> - **Tunnel Mode** = "**T**otal **T**unnel — whole packet gets a new wrapper" (entire packet is encapsulated)

## 2.3 Authentication Header (AH) — Deep Dive

The AH provides:
- ✅ **Data Integrity** (via ICV using HMAC-MD5, HMAC-SHA-1, HMAC-SHA-256)
- ✅ **Data Origin Authentication**
- ✅ **Anti-Replay Protection** (via Sequence Number)
- ❌ **NO Confidentiality** (no encryption)

### AH Header Format (RFC 4302)

| Field | Size (bits) | Description |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| **Next Header** | 8 | Identifies the next protocol (e.g., TCP = 6, UDP = 17) |
| **Payload Length** | 8 | Length of AH in 32-bit words minus 2 |
| **Reserved** | 16 | Must be set to zero |
| **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** | 32 | Identifies the SA |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 | Monotonically increasing counter for anti-replay |
| **ICV (Integrity Check Value)** | Variable | MAC computed over the packet (typically 96 bits for SHA-256 truncation) |

## 2.4 Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) — Deep Dive

The ESP provides:
- ✅ **Confidentiality** (via symmetric encryption: AES-CBC, AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305)
- ✅ **Data Integrity & Authentication** (via ICV)
- ✅ **Anti-Replay Protection** (via Sequence Number)

### ESP Packet Format (RFC 4303)

| Field | Size | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| **SPI** | 32 bits | Identifies the SA |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 bits | Anti-replay counter |
| **Payload Data (Ciphertext)** | Variable | Encrypted transport-layer segment + ESP Trailer |
| **Padding** | 0–255 bytes | Aligns data to cipher block size |
| **Pad Length** | 8 bits | Number of pad bytes |
| **Next Header** | 8 bits | Type of payload (e.g., TCP, UDP) |
| **ICV (Authentication Data)** | Variable | MAC over ESP Header + Ciphertext + ESP Trailer |

> [!IMPORTANT] **AH vs ESP Quick Comparison**
> - **AH authenticates the outer IP header** (in transport mode); ESP does **not**.
> - **ESP provides encryption**; AH does **not**.
> - In practice, **ESP is used far more often** than AH in modern deployments.

## 2.5 Internet Key Exchange (IKE)

IKE operates in **two phases** (IKEv1) or a unified exchange (IKEv2):

**IKEv1 — Phase 1:** Establishes an **ISAKMP SA** (also called IKE SA) — a secure, authenticated channel.
- Performs peer authentication and creates a secure channel.
- Uses either **Main Mode** (6 messages, identity protected) or **Aggressive Mode** (3 messages, identity exposed).

**IKEv1 — Phase 2:** Establishes **IPSec SAs** (one for AH, one for ESP, in each direction).
- Negotiates the actual security parameters (algorithms, keys, SPI).
- Uses **Quick Mode** (3 messages).

**IKEv2 (RFC 7296):** Simplifies the process to a **4-message exchange**:
1. **IKE_SA_INIT** — Negotiates crypto suites and performs Diffie-Hellman.
2. **IKE_AUTH** — Authenticates peers and establishes the first CHILD_SA.
3–4. Additional **CREATE_CHILD_SA** exchanges for rekeying or new SAs.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---------|----------------|--------------|
| **SA Identification Tuple** | $\langle \text{DstIP},\ \text{Protocol},\ \text{SPI} \rangle$ | SPI is 32-bit |
| **Bidirectional SAs Required** | $N_{\text{SA}} = 2 \times N_{\text{protocols}}$ | E.g., 2 ESP SAs for one ESP-protected conversation |
| **HMAC Output Size (SHA-256)** | $\text{ICV}_{\text{len}} = 96$ bits (truncated) | Full output = 256 bits, but IPSec truncates to 96 |
| **AES Block Size** | $B_{\text{AES}} = 128$ bits | All padding aligns to this |
| **Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret** | $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | $a,b$ private; $g$ generator; $p$ prime modulus |
| **Anti-Replay Window** | Sliding window of size $W$ | Default $W = 32$ or $64$ |
| **AH Transport Mode Coverage** | IP Header (mutable fields excepted) + AH + Payload |  |
| **ESP Tunnel Mode Coverage** | New IP + ESP Header + Encrypted (Original IP + Payload) + ESP Trailer + ESP ICV |  |
| **IKEv2 Message Count** | 4 messages (minimum) | vs IKEv1's 9 messages |
| **IPSec Protocol Numbers** | AH = **51**, ESP = **50**, IKE = UDP **500** | Must be memorized |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE] **Where is IPSec used in industry?**
> - **Site-to-Site VPNs** — Connecting branch offices across the Internet (Cisco, Palo Alto, Fortinet gateways).
> - **Remote-Access VPNs** — Employees connecting from home (often combined with SSL/TLS in modern stacks).
> - **IPv6 Mandate** — Required by IPv6 specifications.
> - **Cloud Interconnects** — AWS Site-to-Site VPN, Azure VPN Gateway, Google Cloud VPN all use IPSec.
> - **Routing Protocol Security** — Used to authenticate BGP, OSPFv3 sessions between routers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis: How an IPSec Packet is Constructed (ESP Tunnel Mode)

Let us walk through **every single step** of how a sender's original packet becomes an IPSec-protected packet using **ESP in Tunnel Mode**. This is the most common deployment scenario for a **site-to-site VPN**.

### Given:
- Original (inner) IP packet from Host A → Host B: 
  - Original Source IP: **10.0.0.5** (Host A)
  - Original Destination IP: **203.0.113.42** (Host B)
  - Original Payload: TCP segment carrying HTTP GET request
- VPN Gateway A (public IP: **198.51.100.1**) and VPN Gateway B (public IP: **198.51.100.2**) act as IPSec peers.

### Step 1: Outbound Packet Triggers SPD Lookup

The sender's IP stack looks up the **Security Policy Database (SPD)** to determine the action for the packet:

$$ \text{SPD}(10.0.0.5 \rightarrow 203.0.113.42) = \text{PROTECT (ESP, Tunnel, AES-256, SHA-256)} $$

> Three possible SPD actions: **DISCARD** (drop), **BYPASS** (allow without IPSec), **PROTECT** (apply IPSec).

### Step 2: SA Lookup via SAD

The sender checks the **Security Association Database (SAD)** for an existing SA matching the tuple:

$$ \text{Tuple} = \langle 198.51.100.2,\ \text{ESP},\ \text{SPI} = 0xA1B2C3D4 \rangle $$

If no SA exists → trigger **IKE Phase 2** to negotiate one. If SA exists → proceed.

### Step 3: Sequence Number Increment

The sender increments the **Sequence Number** counter for this SA. This 32-bit counter starts at 1 and must NEVER repeat (or wrap within the anti-replay window):

$$ \text{SeqNum}_{n+1} = \text{SeqNum}_{n} + 1 \quad \text{(mod}\ 2^{32}\text{)} $$

### Step 4: Construct the ESP Header

The ESP Header contains:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ESP Header} &= \text{SPI} \mid\mid \text{SeqNum} \\
&= \text{0xA1B2C3D4} \mid\mid \text{0x0000000F} \quad \text{(assuming 14th packet)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Encrypt the Original Packet

The **original IP packet** (header + payload) is concatenated with an **ESP Trailer** (padding + Pad Length + Next Header), then encrypted using the negotiated algorithm (e.g., **AES-256-CBC** with key $K_e$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plaintext} &= \text{Original IP Header} \mid\mid \text{Original Payload} \mid\mid \text{ESP Trailer} \\
\text{Ciphertext} &= E_{K_e}(\text{Plaintext})
\end{aligned}
$$

Padding is added to ensure the plaintext is a multiple of the cipher block size (**128 bits for AES**).

### Step 6: Compute the ICV (Integrity Check Value)

The sender computes the **ICV** over the ESP Header + Ciphertext + ESP Trailer using the negotiated HMAC algorithm (e.g., **HMAC-SHA-256** with key $K_a$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ICV} &= \text{HMAC}_{K_a}(\text{ESP Header} \mid\mid \text{Ciphertext} \mid\mid \text{ESP Trailer}) \\
\text{ICV}_{\text{truncated}} &= \text{ICV}[0:96] \quad \text{(first 96 bits)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Build the Outer IP Header

A **new IP header** is prepended with:
- New Source IP: **198.51.100.1** (VPN Gateway A)
- New Destination IP: **198.51.100.2** (VPN Gateway B)
- Protocol field: **50** (ESP)

### Step 8: Final ESP Packet Assembly

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Final Packet} &= \underbrace{\text{New IP Header}}_{\text{Protocol=50}} \mid\mid \underbrace{\text{SPI} \mid\mid \text{SeqNum}}_{\text{ESP Header}} \mid\mid \underbrace{\text{Ciphertext}}_{\text{Encrypted}} \mid\mid \underbrace{\text{ICV}}_{\text{96 bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Receiver's Decapsulation (ESP Tunnel Mode)

### Step 1: Outer IP Header Inspection
The receiver's VPN Gateway B examines the outer IP header. It sees **Protocol = 50** (ESP), identifying the packet as an IPSec ESP packet.

### Step 2: SPI Lookup in SAD
The receiver uses the **SPI** to look up the matching SA:

$$ \text{SAD Lookup}(\text{SPI} = \text{0xA1B2C3D4}) \rightarrow \text{SA}_{\text{inbound}} $$

### Step 3: Anti-Replay Check
The receiver checks the **Sequence Number** against its sliding window. The packet is accepted if:
- The sequence number is **greater than** the right edge of the window, OR
- The sequence number is **within the window** and **not yet received**.

Otherwise → packet is **dropped (replay attack)**.

### Step 4: ICV Verification
The receiver recomputes the HMAC over the ESP Header + Ciphertext + ESP Trailer and **compares** it with the received ICV. If they do **not match** → packet is **dropped (integrity failure)**.

### Step 5: Decryption
The receiver decrypts the ciphertext using the negotiated key $K_e$:

$$ \text{Plaintext} = D_{K_e}(\text{Ciphertext}) = \text{Original IP Header} \mid\mid \text{Original Payload} \mid\mid \text{ESP Trailer} $$

### Step 6: Padding Removal
The receiver validates and strips the padding using the **Pad Length** field.

### Step 7: Forward the Original Packet
The recovered original IP packet (with original source **10.0.0.5** and destination **203.0.113.42**) is forwarded to Host B.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AH vs ESP vs TLS

| Feature | **IPSec AH** | **IPSec ESP** | **TLS 1.3** |
|---------|--------------|---------------|-------------|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 4–5 (Transport/Session) |
| **Confidentiality** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (AES, ChaCha20) | ✅ Yes |
| **Integrity** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Authentication** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Anti-Replay** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Limited |
| **Encrypts IP Header** | ❌ Authenticates only | ❌ No (only payload) | N/A (operates above IP) |
| **IP Protocol Number** | 51 | 50 | 6 (TCP) |
| **NAT-Traversal** | ❌ Problematic | ✅ Yes (UDP 4500) | ✅ Yes |
| **Application Transparency** | ✅ Fully transparent | ✅ Fully transparent | ❌ Requires app support |
| **Used for** | Authentication-only VPNs | Most modern VPNs | HTTPS, secure web |

## 3.4 Mathematical Insight: Diffie-Hellman in IKE

IPSec uses the **Diffie-Hellman (DH) Key Exchange** within IKE to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel. This is critical for **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)**.

**Setup (publicly known):** A large prime $p$ and a generator $g$ where $g$ is a primitive root modulo $p$.

**Step 1: Peer A's private/public values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \text{random private key of Peer A} \quad (1 \leq a \leq p-2) \\
A &= g^{a} \bmod p \quad \text{(sent to Peer B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Peer B's private/public values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \text{random private key of Peer B} \quad (1 \leq b \leq p-2) \\
B &= g^{b} \bmod p \quad \text{(sent to Peer A)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Both peers compute the shared secret**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Peer A computes:} \quad K &= B^{a} \bmod p = (g^{b})^{a} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p \\
\text{Peer B computes:} \quad K &= A^{b} \bmod p = (g^{a})^{b} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p \\
\text{Shared Secret} &= K = g^{ab} \bmod p
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why is this secure?**
> An eavesdropper sees $A = g^a \bmod p$ and $B = g^b \bmod p$, but computing $a$ from $A$ requires solving the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**, which is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large primes (2048+ bits).

## 3.5 Anti-Replay Window — Sliding Mechanism

The receiver maintains a sliding window of size $W$ (typically 32 or 64). Let $R$ be the right edge of the window (highest sequence number received so far).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If SeqNum} > R &: \text{Accept, slide window to SeqNum} \\
\text{If } R - W < \text{SeqNum} \leq R &: \text{Accept only if not yet received (check bitmap)} \\
\text{Otherwise} &: \text{Drop as replay}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:** Suppose $W = 32$ and the receiver has received packets with SeqNum up to $R = 1000$.

- An incoming packet with **SeqNum = 1050** → **Accept**, slide window to 1050. ✓
- An incoming packet with **SeqNum = 985** → **Accept** if not yet received (within window $968$ to $1000$). ✓
- An incoming packet with **SeqNum = 800** → **Drop** (outside window). ✗
