# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IPSec Architecture — High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer Data] --> B[Transport Layer TCP/UDP Header]
    B --> C[Original IP Header]
    C --> D{IPSec Policy Lookup}
    D -->|PROTECT ESP Tunnel| E[Encrypt Payload with AES-256]
    D -->|PROTECT AH Transport| F[Compute HMAC-SHA-256]
    D -->|BYPASS| G[Send Packet Unprotected]
    D -->|DISCARD| H[Drop Packet]
    E --> I[Build New Outer IP Header Protocol 50]
    F --> J[Insert AH Header Protocol 51]
    I --> K[Add ESP Trailer and ICV]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Transmit Over Untrusted Network]
    L --> M[Receiver IPSec Endpoint]
    M --> N{SPI Lookup in SAD}
    N -->|Match Found| O[Verify ICV and Decrypt]
    N -->|No Match| P[Drop Packet]
    O --> Q[Anti Replay Window Check]
    Q -->|Valid| R[Forward Original Packet to Destination]
    Q -->|Replay Detected| S[Drop Replay Packet]
```

## 4.2 IPSec Protocol Suite — Component Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IPSec Framework RFC 4301] --> B[Security Protocols]
    A --> C[Key Management]
    A --> D[Databases]
    B --> B1[AH Authentication Header RFC 4302]
    B --> B2[ESP Encapsulating Security Payload RFC 4303]
    C --> C1[IKEv1 RFC 2409]
    C --> C2[IKEv2 RFC 7296]
    D --> D1[SPD Security Policy Database]
    D --> D2[SAD Security Association Database]
    D --> D3[PAD Peer Authentication Database]
    C1 --> E1[Phase 1 Main Aggressive Mode]
    C1 --> E2[Phase 2 Quick Mode]
    C2 --> F1[IKE SA INIT]
    C2 --> F2[IKE AUTH]
    C2 --> F3[CREATE CHILD SA]
    B1 --> G1[Integrity Only No Encryption]
    B2 --> G2[Integrity Plus Encryption]
    B1 --> H1[Transport Mode]
    B1 --> H2[Tunnel Mode]
    B2 --> H1
    B2 --> H2
```

## 4.3 ESP Packet Construction Flow (Tunnel Mode)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Original IP Packet 10.0.0.5 to 203.0.113.42] --> B[Append ESP Trailer Padding PadLen NextHdr]
    B --> C[Encrypt with AES 256 CBC Key Ke]
    C --> D[Prepend ESP Header SPI Sequence Number]
    D --> E[Compute ICV HMAC SHA 256 Key Ka]
    E --> F[Append ICV 96 bits]
    F --> G[Prepend New Outer IP Header 198.51.100.1 to 198.51.100.2 Protocol 50]
    G --> H[Final IPSec ESP Tunnel Packet]
```

## 4.4 IKEv2 4-Message Exchange Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Init as Initiator Host A
    participant Resp as Responder Host B
    Init->>Resp: Message 1 IKE SA INIT with SA proposals, KE, Nonces
    Resp->>Init: Message 2 IKE SA INIT with chosen SA, KE, Nonces
    Note over Init,Resp: Both peers compute Diffie-Hellman shared secret
    Init->>Resp: Message 3 IKE AUTH with AUTH payload and first CHILD SA
    Resp->>Init: Message 4 IKE AUTH with AUTH payload and first CHILD SA
    Note over Init,Resp: IKE SA and IPSec CHILD SA are now established
    Init->>Resp: Optional Message 5 CREATE CHILD SA for rekeying
    Resp->>Init: Optional Message 6 CREATE CHILD SA reply
```

## 4.5 AH vs ESP Header Position — Transport vs Tunnel Mode

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Transport Mode AH] --> A2[Original IP Header]
    A2 --> A3[AH Header]
    A3 --> A4[TCP/UDP Payload]
    A4 --> A5[Authenticated Entire Packet Except Mutable Fields]

    B1[Transport Mode ESP] --> B2[Original IP Header]
    B2 --> B3[ESP Header]
    B3 --> B4[Encrypted TCP/UDP Payload]
    B4 --> B5[ESP Trailer]
    B5 --> B6[ESP ICV]

    C1[Tunnel Mode AH] --> C2[New Outer IP Header]
    C2 --> C3[AH Header]
    C3 --> C4[Original IP Header]
    C4 --> C5[TCP/UDP Payload]

    D1[Tunnel Mode ESP] --> D2[New Outer IP Header]
    D2 --> D3[ESP Header]
    D3 --> D4[Encrypted Original IP Header]
    D4 --> D5[Encrypted TCP/UDP Payload]
    D5 --> D6[ESP Trailer]
    D6 --> D7[ESP ICV]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Oldest Received SeqNum 968] --> B[Window Start]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the three main components of the IPSec architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The IPSec architecture, defined in **RFC 4301**, consists of three main components:

1. **Security Protocols:** These are **AH (Authentication Header)** and **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)**. AH provides integrity, authentication, and anti-replay but no confidentiality. ESP additionally provides confidentiality through encryption. Both can operate in transport or tunnel mode.

2. **Security Association (SA):** A **simplex logical connection** between two IPSec peers that defines the security parameters such as encryption algorithm, authentication algorithm, keys, lifetime, and mode. Each SA is identified by a triple: $\langle \text{Destination IP},\ \text{Protocol},\ \text{SPI} \rangle$. Since SAs are one-way, two SAs are required for bidirectional communication.

3. **Key Management (IKE — Internet Key Exchange):** IKE automates peer authentication, key generation (via Diffie-Hellman), SA negotiation, and key refresh. IKEv1 operates in two phases (Phase 1 establishes IKE SA, Phase 2 establishes IPSec SAs), while **IKEv2** simplifies this into a 4-message exchange.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between IPSec Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode |
|--------|----------------|-------------|
| **IP Header** | Original IP header is preserved | Entire original packet is encapsulated with a NEW outer IP header |
| **Coverage** | Protects only the payload (TCP/UDP) | Protects the ENTIRE original IP packet |
| **Use Case** | Host-to-host communication on a private network | Site-to-site VPN, remote-access VPN, gateway-to-gateway |
| **Overhead** | Lower (no extra IP header) | Higher (adds new IP header) |
| **AH Auth Coverage** | Authenticates original IP header (except mutable fields) + payload | Authenticates new outer IP header (except mutable) + inner IP header + payload |

**ASCII Diagram:**

```
TRANSPORT MODE (ESP):
[Orig IP | ESP Header | Encrypted Payload | ESP Trailer | ESP ICV]

TUNNEL MODE (ESP):
[New IP | ESP Header | Encrypted (Orig IP | Payload) | ESP Trailer | ESP ICV]
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With neat diagrams, explain the operation of the IPSec Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) protocol in both Transport and Tunnel modes. List the fields of the ESP header.**

**Model Solution:**

**ESP Operation Overview:**
ESP (defined in **RFC 4303**, IP Protocol Number **50**) provides four security services: **confidentiality** (via encryption), **data integrity** (via ICV), **data origin authentication**, and **anti-replay protection** (via Sequence Number).

**ESP Header Fields (4 marks):**
1. **SPI (Security Parameters Index) — 32 bits:** Identifies the SA to which this packet belongs.
2. **Sequence Number — 32 bits:** A monotonically increasing counter used for anti-replay.
3. **Payload Data — Variable:** The encrypted transport-layer segment.
4. **Padding (0–255 bytes):** Used to align plaintext to cipher block boundary.
5. **Pad Length — 8 bits:** Number of padding bytes appended.
6. **Next Header — 8 bits:** Identifies the protocol (e.g., TCP = 6) inside the encrypted payload.
7. **ICV / Authentication Data — Variable:** MAC (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256 truncated to 96 bits) over the ESP Header + Ciphertext + ESP Trailer.

**Transport Mode Operation (2 marks):**
In transport mode, ESP encrypts and authenticates **only the payload** of the original IP packet. The original IP header remains visible (and unprotected by ESP's authentication). Used for host-to-host communication.

```
| Original IP | ESP Hdr | Encrypted (TCP/UDP Payload) | ESP Trailer | ESP ICV |
   Visible       SPI,Seq   Confidentiality applied         PadLen,NH    MAC
```

**Tunnel Mode Operation (2 marks):**
In tunnel mode, the **entire original IP packet** (header + payload) is encrypted, and a **new outer IP header** is prepended. This hides the original source/destination IPs and is used for VPNs.

```
| NEW Outer IP | ESP Hdr | Encrypted (Orig IP | TCP/UDP) | ESP Trailer | ESP ICV |
   Src/Dst=VPN GW         Full original packet is encrypted                 MAC
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of IKEv2 with its message exchange. How does it differ from IKEv1?**

**Model Solution:**

**IKEv2 Working (5 marks):**
IKEv2 (defined in **RFC 7296**) simplifies the key exchange to a minimum of **4 messages** between an Initiator and a Responder:

1. **Message 1 (IKE_SA_INIT):** Initiator sends SA proposals (crypto suites), its Diffie-Hellman public value, and a nonce.
2. **Message 2 (IKE_SA_INIT):** Responder selects the crypto suite, sends its DH value, and its nonce. Both peers can now compute the **Diffie-Hellman shared secret** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
3. **Message 3 (IKE_AUTH):** Initiator authenticates itself (via PSK, certificates, or EAP) using an AUTH payload and requests the first CHILD_SA (IPSec SA).
4. **Message 4 (IKE_AUTH):** Responder authenticates itself and confirms the CHILD_SA. The IPSec SA is now established and protected traffic can flow.

**Optional Messages 5–6 (CREATE_CHILD_SA):** Used for rekeying or establishing additional SAs.

**Differences from IKEv1 (2 marks):**

| Feature | IKEv1 | IKEv2 |
|---------|-------|-------|
| **Number of Messages** | 9 (Main Mode) or 6 (Aggressive) minimum | 4 minimum |
| **Phases** | 2 phases (Phase 1 + Phase 2) | Single unified exchange |
| **Modes** | Main Mode and Aggressive Mode | Single mode (built-in NAT-T, mobility support) |
| **Reliability** | No built-in reliability | Built-in retransmission with sequence numbers |
| **Authentication Methods** | Limited (PSK, certificates) | PSK, certificates, EAP |
| **DoS Protection** | Vulnerable to half-open SA DoS | Stateless cookie-based DoS protection |
| **Efficiency** | Slower (more round-trips) | Faster (fewer round-trips) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**What is a Security Association (SA) in IPSec? Explain the components of the Security Association Database (SAD) and Security Policy Database (SPD).**

**Model Solution:**

**Security Association (SA) (2 marks):**
A **Security Association (SA)** is a **simplex (one-way) logical connection** between an IPSec source and destination that defines the security parameters to be applied to the traffic flowing on that connection. SAs are stateful — both peers must maintain SA state. Since an SA is one-way, **two SAs are required for a full bidirectional IPSec session**.

Each SA is uniquely identified by the tuple:
$$ \text{SA} = \langle \text{Destination IP Address},\ \text{IPSec Protocol (AH/ESP)},\ \text{SPI} \rangle $$

The **SPI (Security Parameters Index)** is a 32-bit value selected by the destination to identify the SA.

**Security Association Database (SAD) (2.5 marks):**
The SAD stores all active and dormant SAs. For each SA, it contains:

- **Sequence Number Counter** — 32-bit counter for anti-replay.
- **Sequence Number Overflow Flag** — Indicates whether overflow has occurred.
- **Anti-Replay Window** — Sliding window for detecting replays.
- **AH/ESP Algorithm Information** — Encryption algorithm, key, HMAC algorithm, key, IV.
- **Lifetime** — Time or byte-count after which the SA must be replaced.
- **Path MTU** — Maximum transmission unit for the path.
- **Mode** — Transport or Tunnel.
- **Tunnel Source/Destination** — For tunnel mode SAs only.

**Security Policy Database (SPD) (2.5 marks):**
The SPD stores the rules that determine **which traffic gets protected, which is bypassed, and which is discarded**. Each entry contains:

- **Selectors:** Source IP, Destination IP, Port numbers, Protocol, User ID.
- **Action:** One of three values:
  - **DISCARD** — Drop the packet.
  - **BYPASS** — Allow without IPSec.
  - **PROTECT** — Apply IPSec (specifies which protocol, mode, and SA to use).
- **SA Pointer** — Links to the SAD entry.

**Example SPD entry:**

| Src IP | Dst IP | Protocol | Action | IPSec Spec |
|--------|--------|----------|--------|------------|
| 10.0.0.0/8 | 192.168.0.0/16 | Any | PROTECT | ESP, Tunnel, AES-256, SHA-256 |
| Any | Any | HTTP | BYPASS | — |
| 10.0.0.5 | 203.0.113.42 | Any | DISCARD | — |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the IPSec Authentication Header (AH) protocol. Why is ESP preferred over AH in modern deployments?**

**Model Solution:**

**Authentication Header (AH) Overview (4 marks):**
AH, defined in **RFC 4302** and identified by **IP Protocol Number 51**, provides:
- ✅ **Data integrity** (via ICV computed using HMAC-MD5, HMAC-SHA-1, or HMAC-SHA-256)
- ✅ **Data origin authentication** (verifies the sender's identity)
- ✅ **Anti-replay protection** (via 32-bit Sequence Number)
- ❌ **NO confidentiality** (no payload encryption)

**AH Header Fields:**
1. **Next Header (8 bits):** Type of payload immediately following AH.
2. **Payload Length (8 bits):** Length of AH in 32-bit words, minus 2.
3. **Reserved (16 bits):** Must be zero.
4. **SPI (32 bits):** Identifies the SA.
5. **Sequence Number (32 bits):** Anti-replay counter.
6. **ICV (Variable, typically 96 bits):** MAC computed over **mutable fields treated as zero, all immutable fields of the outer IP header in tunnel mode, AH header, and upper-layer data**.

**Modes of AH:**

- **AH Transport Mode:** `Original IP | AH Header | TCP/UDP Payload` — Authenticates the entire original IP header (except mutable fields like TTL) + payload.
- **AH Tunnel Mode:** `New IP | AH | Original IP | TCP/UDP Payload` — Authenticates the new outer IP header + entire inner packet.

**Why ESP is Preferred over AH (3 marks):**

1. **No Encryption in AH:** AH does not provide confidentiality. Most modern security requirements demand both encryption and authentication, which ESP provides in a single protocol.

2. **NAT Incompatibility:** AH authenticates the **source and destination IP addresses** of the IP header. When a NAT device rewrites these addresses, the AH ICV becomes invalid. ESP, by contrast, has NAT-Traversal (NAT-T) that encapsulates ESP in UDP port 4500, making it NAT-compatible.

3. **Encapsulation Overhead Similar:** ESP with NULL encryption (authentication only) provides essentially the same security as AH while being NAT-friendly. There is little reason to choose AH.

4. **Industry Trend:** Modern vendors (Cisco, Palo Alto, Fortinet) ship ESP-only or ESP+IPsec-default configurations. **AH is rarely deployed in production.**

5. **IPv6 Compatibility Issues:** In IPv6, extension headers can mutate in transit, which complicates AH's authentication. ESP's authentication scope is more predictable.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse IP Protocol Numbers:** AH = **51**, ESP = **50**, IKE = UDP **500**. Mixing these up is an instant 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Do NOT claim AH provides encryption** — it does not. This is the single most common KTU mistake.
> 3. **Remember SA is SIMPLEX** — if a question asks "how many SAs for a bidirectional ESP session?", the correct answer is **2 (one inbound, one outbound)**.
> 4. **Transport Mode preserves the original IP header; Tunnel Mode adds a NEW outer IP header.** Students often forget to mention the new outer IP header in tunnel mode.
> 5. **IKEv2 has 4 messages minimum**, not 3 or 6. Students confuse this with IKEv1 Aggressive Mode (3 messages).
> 6. **Anti-replay uses Sequence Number + Sliding Window**, not timestamps. Do not confuse with Kerberos or NTP timestamp anti-replay.
> 7. **The ICV authenticates ESP Header + Ciphertext + ESP Trailer**, NOT the outer IP header. This is a frequent point of confusion with AH.
> 8. **SPD has three actions: DISCARD, BYPASS, PROTECT.** Students often miss DISCARD.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for IPSec**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- ✅ IPSec is a **Layer 3** security framework, not a single protocol.
- ✅ IPSec is **mandatory in IPv6**, optional in IPv4.
- ✅ Two main security protocols: **AH (Protocol 51)** and **ESP (Protocol 50)**.
- ✅ **AH = Authentication Only** (no encryption).
- ✅ **ESP = Authentication + Encryption** (preferred in modern deployments).

### 🔑 Key Identifiers (Memorize!)
- ✅ AH Protocol Number = **51**
- ✅ ESP Protocol Number = **50**
- ✅ IKE = UDP Port **500**; NAT-T = UDP Port **4500**
- ✅ SPI = **32-bit** SA identifier
- ✅ Sequence Number = **32-bit** anti-replay counter

### 🔑 Modes of Operation
- ✅ **Transport Mode** = Original IP header preserved; protects payload only.
- ✅ **Tunnel Mode** = New outer IP header; protects ENTIRE original packet.
- ✅ Tunnel Mode is used for **VPNs**; Transport Mode for host-to-host.

### 🔑 Security Association (SA)
- ✅ SA = **Simplex (one-way)** logical connection.
- ✅ SA Identifier = $\langle \text{DstIP},\ \text{Protocol},\ \text{SPI} \rangle$
- ✅ **Two SAs** required for one bidirectional IPSec session.
- ✅ SAs are stored in **SAD**; rules are in **SPD**.

### 🔑 Databases
- ✅ **SPD** has three actions: **DISCARD, BYPASS, PROTECT**.
- ✅ **SAD** stores active SAs with sequence numbers, anti-replay windows, keys, lifetimes.
- ✅ **PAD** stores peer authentication data (PSKs, certificates).

### 🔑 IKE (Key Management)
- ✅ **IKEv1** = 2 phases (Phase 1: Main/Aggressive Mode; Phase 2: Quick Mode).
- ✅ **IKEv2** = 4 messages minimum (IKE_SA_INIT × 2, IKE_AUTH × 2).
- ✅ IKE uses **Diffie-Hellman** for key exchange.
- ✅ Provides **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** when rekeyed with new DH exchange.

### 🔑 Algorithms (Know the Modern Suite)
- ✅ **Encryption:** AES-128/256 (CBC or GCM), ChaCha20-Poly1305.
- ✅ **Integrity:** HMAC-SHA-256 (ICV truncated to 96 bits).
- ✅ **DH Groups:** Group 14 (2048-bit) or higher.
- ❌ **Deprecated:** MD5, SHA-1, DES, 3DES, DH Group 1/2.

### 🔑 Anti-Replay Mechanism
- ✅ Uses a **sliding window** of size W (typically 32 or 64).
- ✅ Sequence number must NEVER repeat within the window.
- ✅ Packets outside the window are dropped.

### 🔑 Exam-Favorite Comparison Tables
- ✅ AH vs ESP (services, coverage, encryption, NAT-T support).
- ✅ Transport vs Tunnel Mode (header preservation, use case).
- ✅ IKEv1 vs IKEv2 (message count, phases, efficiency).
- ✅ IPSec vs TLS (OSI layer, app awareness, encryption scope).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Firewalls and Intrusion Detection Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT602 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION & NETWORK SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Firewalls and Intrusion Detection Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Firewall: Sentinel of the Network

### Formal Definition
A **Firewall** is a network security device (hardware, software, or a hybrid of both) that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on an organization's previously established security policies. It acts as a **perimeter defense mechanism** that establishes a barrier between a secured, trusted internal network and an untrusted external network (typically the public Internet).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCEOT602 Module 4):**  
> "A firewall is a security mechanism that enforces the security policy of an organization by mediating all traffic between the internal trusted network and the external untrusted network. It implements one or more of the following: packet filtering, stateful inspection, or application-layer proxying."

### Conceptual Analogy: The Bouncer at the Club
Imagine a high-end nightclub. The club is your **private network**, the city street is the **Internet**, and a stern bouncer stands at the entrance — that bouncer is the **firewall**.  
- People (packets) wanting to enter must show **ID** (comply with rules — valid source/destination IP, port, protocol).  
- The bouncer consults a **guest list** (Access Control List / ACL) to decide who is allowed in.  
- Troublemakers (malicious packets) carrying weapons (exploits, malware) are denied entry or thrown out.  
- The bouncer does **not** monitor what happens **inside** the club — for that, you need security cameras (the **IDS**).

---

## 1.2 The Intrusion Detection System (IDS): The Silent Watchdog

### Formal Definition
An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism (software or appliance) that **monitors network traffic or system activities** for malicious activity, policy violations, or anomalous behavior, and reports such events to a centralized management system (SIEM — Security Information and Event Management).

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Broad Categories of IDS:**
> 1. **NIDS (Network-based IDS):** Placed at strategic points (e.g., perimeter) to monitor all network traffic.
> 2. **HIDS (Host-based IDS):** Runs on individual hosts; monitors system calls, logs, file integrity.

When an IDS is configured to **actively block** detected threats, it becomes an **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)**.

### Conceptual Analogy: CCTV and Smoke Alarms
Think of an IDS as a network of **CCTV cameras** and **smoke detectors** installed inside the building:
- It **watches** ongoing activity silently.
- When it sees a fire (an intrusion) or an unusual movement (anomaly), it **raises the alarm** (alerts the admin).
- A smoke alarm that **automatically sprays water** is essentially an **IPS** — the active, preventive version of the IDS.

---

## 1.3 Why Are These Critical in Network Security?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why Firewalls & IDS are studied together:**
> - **Defense in Depth:** Firewalls are the *first line of defense*; IDS is the *second line*.  
> - **Complementary Roles:** A firewall blocks known bad traffic; an IDS detects sophisticated, novel attacks that bypass the firewall (e.g., insider threats, zero-day exploits).  
> - **Compliance:** Standards such as **PCI-DSS**, **HIPAA**, and the **ISO 27001** mandate the use of firewalls and intrusion monitoring in regulated environments.

### Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU)
- **Throughput / Bandwidth:** Measured in **Gbps** (Gigabits per second) — the volume of traffic the firewall/IDS can inspect without becoming a bottleneck.
- **Latency:** The **delay (in milliseconds, ms)** introduced while the packet is being inspected.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Legitimate traffic flagged as malicious. Measured as a **percentage (%)**.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR):** Actual attacks missed by the system. Measured as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Detection Rate / True Positive Rate (TPR):** Actual attacks correctly identified. Measured as a **percentage (%)**.

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where TP = True Positives, TN = True Negatives, FP = False Positives, FN = False Negatives.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Firewalls (KTU High-Priority Topic)

### 2.1.1 Packet-Filtering Firewall (Stateless)
- Operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** of the OSI model.
- Examines each packet **in isolation** (no memory of previous packets).
- Filters based on: **Source IP, Destination IP, Source Port, Destination Port, Protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP).**

**Advantages:** Simple, fast, low cost.  
**Disadvantages:** Cannot detect spoofed packets, vulnerable to IP spoofing, no application-layer awareness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A pure packet-filtering firewall is *stateless*. The moment a firewall remembers the state of a connection (e.g., tracks the TCP 3-way handshake), it becomes a **stateful inspection firewall**.

### 2.1.2 Stateful Inspection Firewall
- Operates at **Layers 3 and 4** but maintains a **State Table** that tracks every active connection.
- Verifies that incoming packets **belong to an established, legitimate session**.
- Example: An unsolicited inbound SYN packet is dropped, but the SYN-ACK reply to an outbound connection is allowed.

### 2.1.3 Application-Layer Gateway (Proxy Firewall)
- Operates at **Layer 7 (Application Layer)**.
- Acts as an **intermediary** between the client and the server — the client never talks directly to the server.
- Can inspect **HTTP headers, URLs, FTP commands, DNS queries** for malicious content.
- **Drawback:** High latency, single point of failure, requires one proxy per application.

### 2.1.4 Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)
- A modern, integrated firewall that combines:
  - Traditional stateful inspection.
  - **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI).**
  - **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS).**
  - Application awareness (Layer 7 visibility).
  - **SSL/TLS Inspection** (decrypts encrypted traffic to inspect it).
  - User-identity awareness (integrated with Active Directory/LDAP).

### 2.1.5 Circuit-Level Gateway
- Operates at the **Session Layer (Layer 5)** of the OSI model.
- Verifies the **TCP handshake** to ensure a legitimate session is established.
- Used as a **security mechanism for SOCKS proxies**.

---

## 2.2 Firewall Architectures (KTU Frequently Asked)

### 2.2.1 Bastion Host
- A **highly hardened computer** that is exposed to the public network.
- Runs a single application (e.g., a proxy) and is the only machine directly accessible from the Internet.

### 2.2.2 Screened Subnet (DMZ — Demilitarized Zone)
- A **buffer zone** between the untrusted Internet and the trusted internal LAN.
- Hosts public-facing servers (Web, Mail, DNS).
- Requires **two firewalls**: one between Internet and DMZ, another between DMZ and internal LAN.

### 2.2.3 Dual-Homed Host
- A host with **two network interfaces** (NICs): one to the Internet, one to the internal network.
- Acts as a gateway; **IP forwarding is disabled** to force traffic to be proxied.

---

## 2.3 Types of Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)

### 2.3.1 Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)
- Uses a database of known attack **signatures** (patterns).
- Examples: **Snort rules**, **Suricata rules**.
- **Analogy:** Like an antivirus scanner matching against a virus definition database.
- **Limitation:** Cannot detect **zero-day attacks** (attacks with no known signature).

### 2.3.2 Anomaly-Based Detection
- Builds a **statistical profile** of "normal" behavior.
- Any significant deviation from the baseline triggers an alert.
- **Methods Used:** Statistical analysis, Machine Learning (e.g., clustering, neural networks), Markov models.
- **Advantage:** Can detect zero-day attacks.  
- **Limitation:** Higher **False Positive Rate**.

### 2.3.3 Stateful Protocol Analysis
- Reconstructs protocol behavior from packet sequences and compares it to predefined vendor-developed profiles of normal protocol activity.
- E.g., detecting an HTTP request that doesn't conform to the HTTP RFC standard.

### 2.3.4 Hybrid Detection
- Combines **signature + anomaly** methods for higher accuracy.

---

## 2.4 Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) vs IDS

| Feature | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| Mode | Passive (monitors and alerts) | Active (monitors and blocks) |
| Placement | Out-of-band (taps traffic) | In-line (in the data path) |
| Failure Effect | Failure → No alerts only | Failure → Network outage |
| Reaction | Sends alert to admin | Drops packets, resets sessions, blocks IP |
| Typical Use | Audit, forensics, compliance | Real-time threat blocking |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Conceptual & Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a comprehensive cheat sheet covering all quantitative metrics a student must know for the KTU ESE on Firewalls and IDS.

| Concept | Formula / Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Packet Header Inspection** | Verify (Source IP, Dest IP, Source Port, Dest Port, Protocol) against ACL | ACL = Access Control List |
| **Stateful TCP Tracking** | Track 3-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK | Maintained in State Table |
| **Accuracy of IDS** | $\text{Accuracy} = (TP + TN) / (TP + TN + FP + FN)$ | Expressed as percentage (%) |
| **Detection Rate (TPR)** | $\text{TPR} = TP / (TP + FN)$ | Also called Sensitivity or Recall |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = FP / (FP + TN)$ | Should be minimized |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $\text{FNR} = FN / (TP + FN)$ | Critical — missed attacks |
| **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = TP / (TP + FP)$ | Reliability of an alert |
| **F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)** | $F1 = 2 \cdot (Precision \cdot Recall) / (Precision + Recall)$ | Balanced metric for IDS evaluation |
| **Throughput of Firewall** | $\text{Throughput} = \text{Bytes Processed} / \text{Time}$ | Measured in **Gbps** |
| **Latency of Firewall** | $\text{Latency} = T_{out} - T_{in}$ | Measured in **milliseconds (ms)** |
| **DMZ Packet Hop Count** | $\text{Hops} = 2$ (Internet $\rightarrow$ DMZ-FW $\rightarrow$ DMZ server) | DMZ = Demilitarized Zone |
| **Encryption Overhead (SSL Inspection)** | $\text{Overhead} \approx 10\% \text{ to } 30\%$ of throughput | Performance impact |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Note:** When writing the formula for FPR or TPR in your answer sheet, *always* clearly define the variables (TP, TN, FP, FN) before substituting numbers. Examiners deduct marks if the variables are not defined.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Enterprise Networks:** Every corporate office deploys a perimeter firewall (e.g., **Palo Alto NGFW, Cisco ASA, Fortinet FortiGate**) at the gateway to the Internet.
- **Cloud Environments:** **AWS Security Groups, Azure NSG (Network Security Group), GCP VPC Firewalls** are virtual firewalls.
- **Data Centers:** IDS/IPS solutions (e.g., **Snort, Suricata, Zeek**) are deployed on East-West traffic (server-to-server) to detect lateral movement.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA):** Specialized firewalls (e.g., **Claroty, Nozomi Networks**) protect OT networks in power plants and manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: How a Packet-Filtering Firewall Decides

> [!NOTE]
> While firewalls are conceptual, the **decision logic** is algorithmic. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **trace a packet through the firewall rule set**. We will do an exhaustive step-by-step analysis.

### Problem Statement
A packet-filtering firewall has the following **Access Control List (ACL)**:

| Rule # | Action | Source IP | Dest IP | Source Port | Dest Port | Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ALLOW | 192.168.1.0/24 | 10.0.0.5 | ANY | 80 | TCP |
| 2 | DENY | ANY | 10.0.0.0/8 | ANY | 22 | TCP |
| 3 | ALLOW | ANY | ANY | ANY | ANY | ICMP |
| 4 | DENY | ANY | ANY | ANY | ANY | ANY |

A packet arrives with the following attributes:
- Source IP: **192.168.1.50**
- Dest IP: **10.0.0.5**
- Source Port: **50000**
- Dest Port: **80**
- Protocol: **TCP**

**Trace the packet through the ACL step-by-step and state the firewall's final decision.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Initialize the Rule Pointer.**  
The firewall processes the rules in a **top-down, first-match-wins** order. The rule pointer starts at Rule 1.

**Step 2: Compare against Rule 1.**  
- Source IP **192.168.1.50** matches the network **192.168.1.0/24**.  
- Dest IP **10.0.0.5** matches **10.0.0.5**.  
- Source Port **ANY** matches **50000**.  
- Dest Port **80** matches **80**.  
- Protocol **TCP** matches **TCP**.  
All five fields match Rule 1.

**Step 3: Apply the Action.**  
The action for Rule 1 is **ALLOW**. The packet is forwarded. Subsequent rules are **not evaluated**.

**Final Decision: ALLOW.** The packet is permitted to pass.

---

### Analytical Variation (Negative Case)
Now consider a packet:
- Source IP: **203.0.113.45** (public IP)
- Dest IP: **10.0.0.10**
- Dest Port: **22**
- Protocol: **TCP**

**Step 1: Rule 1 Check.**  
- Source IP **203.0.113.45** does **NOT** match 192.168.1.0/24. → **No match.** Move to Rule 2.

**Step 2: Rule 2 Check.**  
- Source IP ANY matches.  
- Dest IP **10.0.0.10** matches 10.0.0.0/8.  
- Dest Port 22 matches 22.  
- Protocol TCP matches.  
- **Match found.**  
- **Action: DENY.** The packet is dropped silently.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example: Computing IDS Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a classic KTU 14-mark question pattern. We will work through a full numerical with derivation of every intermediate step.

### Problem Statement
An IDS was evaluated over **1000 network events**. The following observations were made:
- True Positives (TP) = **220** (correctly identified attacks)
- False Positives (FP) = **30** (legitimate events flagged as attacks)
- True Negatives (TN) = **700** (correctly identified legitimate events)
- False Negatives (FN) = **50** (missed attacks)

**Compute the following:** (a) Accuracy, (b) Detection Rate (TPR), (c) False Positive Rate (FPR), (d) Precision, (e) F1-Score.

---

### Step (a): Compute Accuracy

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

**Step 1: Sum the denominators (Total events).**

$$
TP + TN + FP + FN = 220 + 700 + 30 + 50 = 1000
$$

**Step 2: Sum the correct classifications (TP + TN).**

$$
TP + TN = 220 + 700 = 920
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the formula.**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{920}{1000} = 0.92
$$

**Final Result:** $\text{Accuracy} = 92\%$

---

### Step (b): Compute Detection Rate (TPR / Recall / Sensitivity)

$$
\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Step 1: Identify TP and FN.**  
- TP = 220 (actual attacks correctly identified).  
- FN = 50 (missed attacks).

**Step 2: Compute the denominator.**

$$
TP + FN = 220 + 50 = 270
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This denominator is the **total number of actual attacks** in the dataset. It is the ground truth for "attack events."

**Step 3: Substitute and compute.**

$$
\text{TPR} = \frac{220}{270} \approx 0.8148
$$

**Final Result:** $\text{TPR} \approx 81.48\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The IDS correctly catches about 81% of real attacks. The remaining 19% slip through undetected.

---

### Step (c): Compute False Positive Rate (FPR)

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

**Step 1: Identify FP and TN.**  
- FP = 30 (legitimate events wrongly flagged).  
- TN = 700 (legitimate events correctly ignored).

**Step 2: Compute the denominator (Total actual legitimate events).**

$$
FP + TN = 30 + 700 = 730
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and compute.**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{30}{730} \approx 0.0411
$$

**Final Result:** $\text{FPR} \approx 4.11\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Of every 100 legitimate events, the IDS falsely alarms on about 4.

---

### Step (d): Compute Precision

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Step 1: Identify TP and FP.**  
- TP = 220, FP = 30.

**Step 2: Compute the denominator.**

$$
TP + FP = 220 + 30 = 250
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This denominator is the **total number of events flagged by the IDS as attacks** (i.e., the total alerts generated).

**Step 3: Substitute and compute.**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{220}{250} = 0.88
$$

**Final Result:** $\text{Precision} = 88\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Of every 100 alerts raised by the IDS, 88 are real attacks. 12 are false alarms.

---

### Step (e): Compute the F1-Score

$$
F1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

**Step 1: Recall = TPR = 0.8148 (from Step b).**  
**Step 2: Precision = 0.88 (from Step d).**

**Step 3: Compute the numerator.**

$$
2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall} = 2 \cdot 0.88 \cdot 0.8148
$$

$$
= 2 \cdot 0.7170 \approx 1.4340
$$

**Step 4: Compute the denominator.**

$$
\text{Precision} + \text{Recall} = 0.88 + 0.8148 = 1.6948
$$

**Step 5: Divide.**

$$
F1 = \frac{1.4340}{1.6948} \approx 0.8461
$$

**Final Result:** $F1 \approx 84.61\%$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Interpretation:** A high F1-Score means the IDS is balanced — it neither misses too many attacks (high recall) nor generates excessive false alarms (high precision).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Firewall vs IDS Comparison

| Parameter | Firewall | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer(s) | L3, L4, L7 | L2 to L7 | L2 to L7 |
| Operating Mode | **Active** (blocks) | **Passive** (alerts) | **Active** (blocks) |
| In-line Placement? | Yes | No (uses TAP) | Yes |
| Database/Logic | ACL Rules | Signatures / Statistical Models | Signatures + Heuristics |
| Detects Zero-Day? | Limited | Yes (anomaly-based) | Yes (anomaly-based) |
| Failure Impact | Network Down | Silent (no alerts) | Network Down |
| Standards | NIST SP 800-41 | NIST SP 800-94 | NIST SP 800-94 |
| Example Products | Cisco ASA, pfSense | Snort, Suricata | Suricata (inline mode), Cisco Firepower |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Firewall Decision Flow (Packet Filtering)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders the **flowchart** used by a typical packet-filtering firewall to process an incoming packet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Packet Arrives at Firewall] --> B[Extract Header Fields]
    B --> C{Compare with Rule 1 of ACL}
    C -- Match Found --> D{Action of Rule 1}
    D -- ALLOW --> E[Forward Packet to Destination]
    D -- DENY --> F[Drop Packet Silently]
    C -- No Match --> G{More Rules in ACL?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Move to Next Rule]
    H --> C
    G -- No --> I{Default Policy?}
    I -- Default ALLOW --> E
    I -- Default DENY --> F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The firewall processes rules from top to bottom. The **first match** wins. If no rule matches, the **default policy** (either "default allow" or "default deny") is applied. Best practice is **default deny**.

---

## 4.2 Screened Subnet (DMZ) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InternetNode[Internet Untrusted Network] -->|Traffic 1| FW1[External Firewall Screening Router]
    FW1 -->|Traffic 2| DMZNode[DMZ Zone Public Web Mail DNS Servers]
    DMZNode -->|Traffic 3| FW2[Internal Firewall Bastion Host]
    FW2 -->|Traffic 4| LANNode[Internal Trusted LAN]
    InternetNode -.->|Direct Blocked| LANNode
    FW1 -.->|Blocks Spoofed Packets| InternetNode
    FW2 -.->|Blocks Lateral Movement| DMZNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**
> - The **External Firewall (FW1)** filters traffic between the Internet and the DMZ. It allows public services (HTTP/HTTPS/SMTP) to reach DMZ servers.
> - The **Internal Firewall (FW2)** separates the DMZ from the trusted LAN. It allows only *specific, vetted* traffic from DMZ to LAN (e.g., database queries from the Web server to the internal DB).
> - If a DMZ server is compromised, the attacker is **still isolated** from the internal LAN by FW2.

---

## 4.3 Stateful Inspection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Outbound SYN from Internal Host] --> Q1[State Table Entry Created SYN_SENT]
    Q1 --> P2[Remote Server Replies with SYN-ACK]
    P2 --> Q2{State Table Validates SYN-ACK Matches SYN_SENT Entry}
    Q2 -- Valid --> R1[State Updated to ESTABLISHED Packet Forwarded]
    Q2 -- Invalid --> R2[Packet Dropped and Logged]
    P3[Unsolicited Inbound SYN from Internet] --> Q3{State Table Has Matching Outbound SYN_SENT Entry?}
    Q3 -- No --> R2
    Q3 -- Yes --> R1
```

---

## 4.4 IDS / IPS Placement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InetNode[Internet] -->|Traffic| RouterNode[Edge Router]
    RouterNode -->|Mirrored Traffic| NIDSNode[NIDS Sensor]
    RouterNode -->|Live Inline Traffic| IPSNode[IPS Device]
    IPSNode --> FWNode[Perimeter Firewall]
    FWNode --> LANNet[Internal Corporate LAN]
    NIDSNode -->|Alerts| SIEMNode[Centralized SIEM Console]
    IPSNode -->|Blocks Threats Inline| FWNode
    HIDSNode[HIDS on Endpoints] -->|Logs| SIEMNode
    SIEMNode -->|Notifies| AdminNode[Security Administrator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Placement Insights (KTU high-yield content):**
> - **NIDS** uses a *network TAP* or *port mirroring (SPAN)* — it sees a copy of the traffic without being in-line.  
> - **IPS** is placed *in-line* (in the actual data path) so it can block threats in real-time.  
> - **HIDS** runs on individual hosts and forwards logs to the SIEM.

---

## 4.5 Signature vs Anomaly-Based Detection Comparison (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InPacket[Incoming Packet or Event] --> PreProc[Preprocessing Parsing Normalization]
    PreProc --> SigEng[Signature Engine]
    PreProc --> AnomEng[Anomaly Engine]
    SigEng --> SigDB[Signature Database Snort Rules]
    SigDB --> SigMatch{Signature Match Found?}
    SigMatch -- Yes --> AlertA[Alert Attack Detected]
    SigMatch -- No --> NoSigAlert[No Alert]
    AnomEng --> StatModel[Statistical Model of Normal Behavior]
    StatModel --> DevCheck{Deviation Score Above Threshold?}
    DevCheck -- Yes --> AlertB[Alert Anomaly Detected]
    DevCheck -- No --> NoAnomAlert[No Alert]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme | PCEOT602 | Module 4]**  
**Define a firewall. List and briefly explain any two types of firewalls.**

**Model Answer:**

A **firewall** is a network security device that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predefined security policies. It establishes a controlled barrier between a trusted internal network and an untrusted external network (such as the Internet), enforcing an organization's security perimeter.

**Two types of firewalls:**

**1. Packet-Filtering Firewall (Stateless):**  
This is the simplest type, operating at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and **Transport Layer (Layer 4)**. It inspects each packet individually — examining the source IP, destination IP, source port, destination port, and protocol — and either allows or denies the packet based on the rules in its Access Control List (ACL). It has **no memory of previous packets**, so it cannot detect stateful attacks. Example: **iptables** in Linux.

**2. Application-Layer Gateway (Proxy Firewall):**  
This operates at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** and acts as an **intermediary** between the client and the server. The client sends a request to the proxy, the proxy validates the request against the security policy, and then the proxy makes its own request to the destination server. This allows deep inspection of application data (e.g., HTTP headers, URLs, FTP commands). Example: **Squid Proxy**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme | PCEOT602 | Module 4]**  
**Differentiate between an IDS and an IPS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **IDS (Intrusion Detection System)** | **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode of Operation** | **Passive** — monitors traffic and raises alerts | **Active** — monitors and **blocks** malicious traffic inline |
| **Placement** | **Out-of-band** — connected via network TAP or SPAN port | **In-line** — sits directly in the data path |
| **Action on Threat** | Sends an alert to the admin / SIEM | Drops the packet, resets the TCP connection, or blocks the source IP |
| **Failure Impact** | If the IDS fails, the network continues to operate (only monitoring is lost) | If the IPS fails, the network may go down (single point of failure) |
| **Latency** | Lower (not in data path) | Higher (every packet must pass through it) |
| **Use Case** | Audit trails, forensic analysis, compliance reporting | Real-time threat mitigation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember:** *IDS Detects, IPS Prevents.* An IDS is the "watchdog that barks." An IPS is the "watchdog that bites."

---

## 📝 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B questions in this module carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as independent alternatives. Each is broken into sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme | PCEOT602 | Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **DMZ architecture** with a neat diagram. Discuss why a DMZ is used in modern networks and list the services typically hosted in a DMZ.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain the **stateful inspection firewall** mechanism. How does it overcome the limitations of a stateless packet-filtering firewall?

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (a): DMZ Architecture

**1. Definition of DMZ (2 marks):**  
A **Demilitarized Zone (DMZ)** is a **buffer network segment** that sits between an organization's trusted internal network (LAN) and an untrusted external network (Internet). It is created by placing **two firewalls** — an external firewall and an internal firewall — with public-facing servers hosted in the DMZ between them.

**2. Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inet1[Internet] -->|Allows HTTP HTTPS SMTP DNS| FW_A[External Firewall]
    FW_A -->|Allows Selected Traffic| DMZ1[DMZ Zone Web Server Mail Server DNS Server]
    DMZ1 -->|Restricts to Specific Ports| FW_B[Internal Firewall Bastion Host]
    FW_B -->|Allows Trusted Traffic| LAN1[Internal Corporate LAN Database File Servers]
    Inet1 -.->|Direct Traffic Blocked| LAN1
    DMZ1 -.->|Outbound to Internet Allowed| Inet1
```

**3. Why DMZ is Used (2 marks):**
- **Isolation of Public Services:** Public-facing servers (web, mail, DNS) are isolated from the internal LAN.  
- **Defense in Depth:** Even if a DMZ server is compromised by an attacker, the internal firewall (FW_B) prevents the attacker from moving into the internal LAN.  
- **Controlled Access:** The internal firewall can allow specific traffic from the DMZ to the LAN (e.g., the web server querying the internal database on port 3306 only).  
- **Compliance:** PCI-DSS requires cardholder data environments to be isolated using a DMZ.

**4. Services Hosted in DMZ (2 marks):**
- **Web Servers** (HTTP/HTTPS on ports 80/443)  
- **Mail Servers** (SMTP on port 25, IMAP/POP3 on 143/110)  
- **DNS Servers** (UDP/TCP port 53)  
- **FTP Servers** (port 21)  
- **Proxy Servers**  
- **VPN Gateways**

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (b): Stateful Inspection Firewall

**1. Definition (2 marks):**  
A **Stateful Inspection Firewall** (also called a stateful packet filter) operates at the **Network and Transport Layers** but maintains a **state table** to keep track of all active network connections. It makes filtering decisions not just on the current packet's header, but also on the **context of the connection**.

**2. Mechanism with Example (3 marks):**

**Example:** An internal host (192.168.1.10) initiates an HTTP connection to an external web server (203.0.113.50) on port 80.

- **Step 1:** The internal host sends a TCP **SYN** packet. The firewall records this in its state table as a new session: `{Source IP: 192.168.1.10, Source Port: 50000, Dest IP: 203.0.113.50, Dest Port: 80, State: SYN_SENT}`.  
- **Step 2:** The external server replies with a **SYN-ACK**. The firewall checks the state table — this packet is a **valid response** to the original SYN — and forwards it. The state is updated to `ESTABLISHED`.  
- **Step 3:** The internal host sends an **ACK**, completing the 3-way handshake. State remains `ESTABLISHED`.  
- **Step 4:** HTTP GET/POST packets flow freely because they belong to the established session.  
- **Step 5:** When the connection is closed (FIN packets), the state table entry is removed.

**3. How It Overcomes Stateless Limitations (2 marks):**

| Limitation of Stateless Firewall | How Stateful Firewall Solves It |
|---|---|
| Cannot prevent **SYN flood** attacks (it allows all SYN packets) | Tracks half-open connections; limits rate of SYNs |
| Cannot validate that incoming ACK/SYN-ACK is a **response to a legitimate outbound request** | Verifies the state table before allowing inbound responses |
| Vulnerable to **IP spoofing** of internal addresses | Cross-checks packet against active state table entries |
| No context — each packet treated in isolation | Maintains full **session context** across multiple packets |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning — Pitfall 1:**  
> A common mistake is to write that "stateful firewalls operate only at Layer 3." They actually operate at **Layers 3 and 4**, but with **state awareness**. Writing "Layer 7" is wrong. Writing "Layer 3 only" is incomplete.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme | PCEOT602 | Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **signature-based** and **anomaly-based** intrusion detection techniques. Compare their advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An IDS was tested on 2000 events. The results were: TP = 350, FP = 50, TN = 1400, FN = 200. Compute the **Accuracy, Detection Rate (TPR), False Positive Rate, Precision, and F1-Score** of the IDS.

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (a): Signature vs Anomaly Detection

**1. Signature-Based Detection (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Signature-based detection (also called **misuse detection** or **rule-based detection**) identifies attacks by comparing observed events against a database of **known attack patterns (signatures)**.

**How it Works:**
- Each known attack has a unique pattern called a signature. For example, a signature for a **SQL injection** attempt might be: a URL containing `' OR 1=1 --`.  
- Tools like **Snort** use rule sets:  
  `alert tcp any any -> 192.168.1.0/24 80 (msg:"SQL Injection Attempt"; content:"' OR 1=1"; sid:1000001;)`  
- If a packet matches a signature, an alert is generated.

**Advantages:**
- **Low False Positive Rate** — only known patterns trigger alerts.  
- **Fast and efficient** — pattern matching is computationally simple.  
- **Easy to understand** — signatures are human-readable.

**Limitations:**
- **Cannot detect zero-day attacks** — attacks with no known signature pass through.  
- **Signature database must be constantly updated.**  
- **Evasion techniques** like polymorphic malware can defeat signatures.

---

**2. Anomaly-Based Detection (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Anomaly-based detection builds a **statistical profile of normal behavior** and flags any significant deviation as a potential attack.

**How it Works:**
- During a **training phase**, the system observes normal traffic and builds a baseline model (e.g., average packet size, connection frequency, protocols used).  
- During **operation**, the system continuously compares live behavior against the baseline.  
- If a metric deviates beyond a configurable threshold (e.g., +3 standard deviations), an alert is raised.

**Methods Used:** Statistical analysis, **Machine Learning (Clustering, Neural Networks, Decision Trees)**, Markov models, Bayesian networks.

**Advantages:**
- Can detect **zero-day attacks** and previously unknown threats.  
- Detects **insider threats** and slow, low-and-slow attacks.  
- Adapts over time with retraining.

**Limitations:**
- **High False Positive Rate** — unusual but legitimate behavior triggers alerts.  
- **Training phase is critical** — a poorly trained model is ineffective.  
- **Computationally expensive** — especially for ML-based methods.

---

**3. Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Basis | Known patterns | Deviation from normal |
| Zero-Day Detection | **No** | **Yes** |
| False Positive Rate | Low | High |
| Update Requirement | Frequent signature updates | Periodic retraining |
| Computational Cost | Low | High |
| Example Tool | Snort, Suricata | OSSEC (anomaly mode), ML-based systems |

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (b): Numerical Computation

**Given Data:**  
- Total Events = 2000  
- **TP** (True Positives) = 350 (attacks correctly identified)  
- **FP** (False Positives) = 50 (legitimate flagged as attacks)  
- **TN** (True Negatives) = 1400 (legitimate correctly ignored)  
- **FN** (False Negatives) = 200 (attacks missed)

**Verification:** $TP + FP + TN + FN = 350 + 50 + 1400 + 200 = 2000$ ✓

---

**Step 1: Compute Accuracy (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{350 + 1400}{2000} = \frac{1750}{2000} = 0.875
$$

**Accuracy = 87.5%**

---

**Step 2: Compute Detection Rate (TPR) (2 marks)**

$$
\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{TPR} = \frac{350}{350 + 200} = \frac{350}{550} \approx 0.6364
$$

**TPR ≈ 63.64%**

---

**Step 3: Compute False Positive Rate (FPR) (1 mark)**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{50}{50 + 1400} = \frac{50}{1450} \approx 0.0345
$$

**FPR ≈ 3.45%**

---

**Step 4: Compute Precision (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{350}{350 + 50} = \frac{350}{400} = 0.875
$$

**Precision = 87.5%**

---

**Step 5: Compute F1-Score (1 mark)**

$$
F1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
F1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.875 \cdot 0.6364}{0.875 + 0.6364} = \frac{1.1137}{1.5114} \approx 0.7369
$$

**F1-Score ≈ 73.69%**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 2 (Numerical Errors):**  
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often use $TP + FP$ as the denominator for **Recall/TPR** instead of $TP + FN$. The denominator for TPR is the **total number of actual attacks** ($TP + FN$).  
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** For **Precision**, the denominator is $TP + FP$ (the total alerts raised). Do not confuse Precision and Recall denominators.  
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to **express results as percentages**. Always state "Accuracy = 87.5%" not just "0.875".  
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Not writing the **unit (fraction vs %)**, leading to a 0.5 mark deduction.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read before the exam):**

- ✅ **Firewall Definition:** A device that enforces security policy by filtering traffic between trusted and untrusted networks.

- ✅ **5 Types of Firewalls (in order of complexity):**  
  1. **Packet-Filtering (Stateless)** — Layer 3/4, no memory.  
  2. **Stateful Inspection** — Layer 3/4 with **state table**.  
  3. **Circuit-Level Gateway** — Layer 5 (Session), TCP handshake validation.  
  4. **Application-Layer Gateway (Proxy)** — Layer 7, deep inspection.  
  5. **NGFW (Next-Generation Firewall)** — DPI + IPS + Application awareness + User identity.

- ✅ **3 Firewall Architectures:**  
  1. **Bastion Host** — single hardened host.  
  2. **DMZ (Screened Subnet)** — two firewalls isolating public servers.  
  3. **Dual-Homed Host** — two NICs, IP forwarding disabled.

- ✅ **IDS = Passive / IPS = Active.** IDS alerts, IPS blocks.

- ✅ **3 IDS Detection Methods:**  
  1. **Signature-Based** (low FP, no zero-day).  
  2. **Anomaly-Based** (zero-day, high FP).  
  3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis** (RFC-conformance check).

- ✅ **5 Must-Know IDS Metrics:**  
  - **Accuracy** = (TP + TN) / Total  
  - **TPR (Recall)** = TP / (TP + FN)  
  - **FPR** = FP / (FP + TN)  
  - **Precision** = TP / (TP + FP)  
  - **F1-Score** = 2 × (P × R) / (P + R)

- ✅ **Standard Bodies / Documents (for 2-mark questions):**  
  - **NIST SP 800-41** — Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy.  
  - **NIST SP 800-94** — Guide to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems.

- ✅ **Real-World Tool Names (for 1-mark bonus marks):**  
  - **Snort, Suricata, Zeek** (IDS)  
  - **pfSense, iptables, Cisco ASA, Palo Alto, Fortinet FortiGate** (Firewall)  
  - **OSSEC, Tripwire** (HIDS)  
  - **Wireshark** (Packet analyzer, not an IDS but often used in IDS workflows)

- ✅ **Key Distinction to Memorize:**  
  - **"Stateless"** = no memory of previous packets.  
  - **"Stateful"** = remembers connection state via a state table.

- ✅ **One-Line Exam-Ready Definitions:**  
  - *DMZ:* A buffer network hosting public-facing servers, isolated by two firewalls.  
  - *Bastion Host:* A hardened computer that is the only host exposed to the Internet.  
  - *False Positive:* Legitimate event wrongly flagged as an attack.  
  - *False Negative:* Real attack missed by the IDS.  
  - *Zero-Day Attack:* A new, previously unknown attack with no signature.
